beats.title: Anukramanika Parva (The Table of Contents Parva)
beats.summary: This chapter introduces the Mahabharata, beginning with the invocation and the setting of the Naimisha forest where sages listen to the Suta Ugrashravas. He recounts the history of the Bharata lineage, the composition of the epic by Vyasa, and the narration by Vaishampayana to Janamejaya. The chapter covers the origins of the universe, the genealogy of kings, the birth of Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and Vidura, the upbringing of the Pandavas, their marriage to Draupadi, the Rajasuya sacrifice, the dice game, and the eventual war. It ends with Dhritarashtra lamenting the destruction and Samjaya consoling him with the inevitability of time.

beats.title: Parva 1, Chapter 2
beats.summary: This chapter (Mahabharata, Parva 1, Chapter 2) is a summary of the entire Mahabharata epic, known as the Parva-samgraha. It enumerates all 18 major parvas (books) and their sub-parvas, along with the number of chapters and verses in each. It also describes the origin of the name 'Samantapañcaka' for the battlefield of Kuruksetra, the composition of an akṣauhiṇī (military division), and the duration of the war. The chapter concludes with praise for the epic's greatness.

beats.title: The Story of Uttanka and the Snake Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Janamejaya's encounter with a dog, his subsequent search for a priest, and the tale of his priest Uttanka's quest to obtain earrings for his guru's wife. It includes the stories of the disciples of sage Dhaumya: Aruni, Upamanyu, Veda, and Uttanka, highlighting their devotion and trials. The chapter ends with Uttanka inciting Janamejaya to perform a snake sacrifice to avenge his father's death.

beats.title: The Sūta's Promise to Narrate the Mahābhārata
beats.summary: The sages in the Naimiṣāraṇya forest request the Sūta (Ugraśravas) to narrate the Mahābhārata when their guru Śaunaka arrives. The Sūta agrees to wait for Śaunaka and then speak.

beats.title: The Bhargava Lineage and the Birth of Cyavana
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the lineage of the Bhargava (Bhrigu) dynasty, starting with Bhrigu's son Cyavana, then Pramati, Ruru, and Śunaka. It also narrates the story of how Cyavana got his name: a rakshasa attempted to abduct Bhrigu's wife Puloma, but Agni refused to lie about her marital status, leading to the rakshasa's defeat and the birth of Cyavana.

beats.title: The Birth of Cyavana and the Curse on Agni
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth of Cyavana, son of Bhrigu, and the curse on Agni by Bhrigu. Puloma, Bhrigu's wife, is abducted by a rakshasa but saved by the unborn Cyavana's tejas. Bhrigu curses Agni to become sarvabhaksha (all-devouring) for revealing Puloma's location to the rakshasa.

beats.title: The Curse of Agni and Its Resolution
beats.summary: The sage Sūta narrates the story of Agni (fire) being cursed by Bhr̥gu for revealing a secret truth. Agni withdraws from sacrificial fires, causing chaos. The sages and gods appeal to Brahmā, who explains that Agni's flames purify everything, and the curse is modified: Agni will not be all-consuming in his physical form, but his flames will purify. Agni accepts and order is restored.

beats.title: The Death of Pramadvara
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Ruru and Pramadvara. The sage Sthūlakeśa finds an abandoned baby girl (Pramadvara) on a riverbank, raises her. Ruru, son of Pramati, falls in love with her. Their marriage is arranged, but on the wedding day, Pramadvara is bitten by a snake and dies. Ruru and the assembled sages mourn.

beats.title: Ruru and Pramadvarā
beats.summary: Ruru, grieving the death of his wife Pramadvarā, is visited by a divine messenger who offers a solution: Ruru can give half his life to revive her. Ruru agrees, and with the approval of Dharmarāja, Pramadvarā is restored. Later, Ruru vows to destroy all snakes and encounters a Ḍuṇḍubha, which he attempts to kill, but the snake protests its innocence.

beats.title: Ruru and the Snake
beats.summary: Ruru encounters a snake (Ḍuṇḍubha) and is about to kill it, but the snake speaks, arguing that not all snakes are harmful and that Ruru should not kill indiscriminately. The snake reveals it was once a sage named Ruru, cursed to become a snake. Ruru spares it and asks about the duration of the curse.

beats.title: The Curse of Khagama and the Dharma Discourse
beats.summary: The sage Ḍuṇḍubha recounts to Ruru his past life as a snake named Khagama, who was cursed by a brahmin friend to become a snake. The brahmin later relents and prophesies that Ruru, son of Pramati, will lift the curse. The sage then teaches Ruru about dharma: non-violence is the supreme duty for brahmins, while kṣatriyas have a different dharma involving protection and punishment.

beats.title: Ruru's Inquiry about Janamejaya's Snake Sacrifice
beats.summary: Ruru asks the sage about Janamejaya's snake sacrifice and the role of Astika. The sage promises to narrate the story and then disappears. Ruru searches for him but collapses, then recovers and asks his father to tell the story.

beats.title: The Story of Astika
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the sage Jaratkaru, who upon seeing his ancestors hanging upside down in a pit due to lack of progeny, agrees to marry under certain conditions. He marries a snake-princess named Jaratkaru (same name), and they beget a son named Astika. Astika later saves the snakes from the snake-sacrifice of King Janamejaya, thus fulfilling his father's promise to his ancestors.

beats.title: The Birth of Aruna and Garuda
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Kadru and Vinata, wives of sage Kashyapa, who receive boons for children. Kadru asks for a thousand serpent sons, Vinata for two sons stronger than Kadru's. Vinata's first son Aruna is born incomplete due to her impatience, curses her to become a slave, and becomes the dawn. Her second son Garuda is born as the enemy of serpents.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: The chapter describes the churning of the ocean by the gods and demons to obtain amrita. It begins with the appearance of Uccaihshravas, the divine horse, and then narrates how the gods, led by Narayana, decided to churn the ocean using Mount Mandara as the churning rod and Vasuki as the rope.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk
beats.summary: The gods and demons churn the ocean of milk to obtain amrita, using Mount Mandara as the churning rod and Vasuki as the rope. Lord Vishnu (Narayana) supports the process, and various beings and objects emerge, including the moon, Lakshmi, the horse Uchchaihshravas, the Kaustubha gem, and Dhanvantari with the amrita. Vishnu assumes the Mohini form to deceive the demons and secure the amrita for the gods.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean and the Battle for Amrita
beats.summary: Suta narrates the churning of the ocean and the battle between devas and asuras over the amrita. Vishnu, in his avatar as Narayana, along with Nara, defeats the asuras, beheads Rahu, and secures the amrita for the devas. The devas celebrate their victory.

beats.title: The Bet of Kadru and Vinata
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Kadru and Vinata, wives of Kashyapa, who bet on the color of the tail of the divine horse Uccaihshravas. Kadru, desiring to win, orders her serpent sons to cover the horse's tail, making it appear black. When they refuse, she curses them to be destroyed in Janamejaya's snake sacrifice. Brahma, seeing the curse, approves it for the welfare of creatures and grants the antidote knowledge to Kashyapa.

beats.title: The Sisters Behold the Ocean
beats.summary: Vinata and Kadru, sisters, go to see the horse Uccaihshravas after their wager. They behold the ocean, described in vivid detail as the abode of Varuna, source of gems, home of nagas and asuras, and the birthplace of the conch Panchajanya. The ocean is also linked to Vishnu's boar incarnation and the sage Atri's penance.

beats.title: Birth of Garuda and Enslavement of Vinata
beats.summary: Vinata loses a bet with Kadru and becomes her slave. Garuda is born, appearing as a massive, fiery bird. The gods and sages, fearing his power, praise him as a supreme being, and Garuda calms his radiance.

beats.title: Garuda's Journey and Kadru's Prayer
beats.summary: Garuda, the mighty bird, visits his mother Vinata who is in servitude due to a lost bet. Kadru, mother of serpents, asks Vinata to carry her to the serpent island. Garuda carries his mother and Kadru, but the serpents are scorched by the sun. Kadru prays to Indra for relief, praising him as the supreme deity.

beats.title: The Rain from the Clouds
beats.summary: Suta narrates how, after being praised by Kadru, the lord of the clouds (Harivahana) covered the entire sky with blue cloud masses. The clouds poured abundant rain, flashing lightning and roaring continuously. The sky became filled with waves of rain and the sound of thunder, bringing great joy to the Nagas, and the earth was filled with water everywhere.

beats.title: Garuḍa's Quest for Amrita
beats.summary: The chapter describes the journey of the snakes (nāgas) carried by Garuḍa to a beautiful island, and their request to be taken to another island. Garuḍa, distressed by his mother's servitude, asks the snakes how he can free her. The snakes demand amrita (nectar) as the price for liberation.

beats.title: Garuda's Mission to Fetch Amrita
beats.summary: Garuda, instructed by his mother Vinata, goes to fetch amrita. He asks how to recognize a Brahmana. Vinata explains that a Brahmana is like fire and should not be harmed; one who burns like a coal when swallowed is a Brahmana. She blesses him. Garuda then attacks the Nishadas, causing chaos.

beats.title: Garuda's Quest for Soma: The Elephant and Tortoise
beats.summary: Garuda, sent by his mother Vinata to fetch Soma, encounters a Brahmana and his Niṣādī wife inside his stomach. After they exit, Garuda visits his father Kaśyapa, who advises him to eat an elephant and a tortoise (the cursed brothers Vibhāvasu and Supratīka) before attempting to take Soma. Garuda seizes both and flies to a divine tree, where a branch offers to support him. Garuda breaks the branch and continues.

beats.title: Garuda's Journey and the Omens of War
beats.summary: Garuda, carrying a large branch with an elephant and a tortoise, seeks a place to drop it without harming the Valakhilya sages. Following his father Kashyapa's advice, he drops it on a remote mountain, then eats the elephant and tortoise. The gods see omens and prepare to defend the amrita, as Garuda approaches to steal it.

beats.title: The Birth of Garuda
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Garuda's birth. King Śaunaka asks Sūta about the origin of Garuda, his invincibility, and his powers. Sūta explains that during a sacrifice by Kaśyapa, Indra was assigned to bring firewood but mocked the Vālakhilya sages, who then performed a ritual to create a new Indra. Kaśyapa pacified them, and the sages agreed that the new being would be the king of birds. Meanwhile, Vinatā, Kaśyapa's wife, desired sons and was blessed by Kaśyapa that she would bear two sons, one of whom (Garuda) would become the lord of birds. Thus, Garuda and Aruṇa were born, and Garuda was anointed as the king of birds.

beats.title: Garuda's Battle for Amrita
beats.summary: This chapter describes Garuda's battle with the gods as he attempts to steal the amrita. Garuda defeats many gods, including a powerful being named Bhauvana, and creates chaos with his wings and beak. The gods, led by Indra, fight back but are overwhelmed. Garuda then extinguishes a fiery barrier using water from many rivers and shrinks his body to enter the amrita's location.

beats.title: Garuda Steals the Amrita
beats.summary: Garuda, the mighty bird, enters the region where the amrita is kept, overcomes a revolving sharp-edged wheel and two fierce serpents guarding the nectar, seizes the amrita, and flies away. On his way, he meets Vishnu, who grants him a boon; Garuda chooses to remain above Vishnu and to be immortal without drinking amrita. Vishnu chooses Garuda as his vehicle and banner. Indra strikes Garuda with his vajra, but Garuda is unharmed and drops a feather, which amazes all beings. Indra then seeks friendship with Garuda.

beats.title: Garuḍa and the Soma
beats.summary: Garuḍa approaches Indra to establish friendship and demonstrates his immense strength by claiming he can carry the entire earth with its mountains and forests, along with Indra himself, on a single feather. Indra accepts the friendship and asks Garuḍa to give him the soma he is carrying. Garuḍa refuses, stating he will place the soma somewhere and then Indra can take it. Indra is pleased and offers a boon. Garuḍa asks that serpents become his food. Indra agrees. Garuḍa then places the soma on kuśa grass, tells the serpents to bathe before drinking it, and while they are away, Indra takes the soma back to heaven. The serpents, upon returning, lick the grass, causing their tongues to become forked, and the grass becomes sacred.

beats.title: Enumeration of the Principal Nagas
beats.summary: Shaunaka asks Suta to narrate the names of the prominent serpents (nagas). Suta lists the principal nagas, mentioning that there are countless others, and explains that he will only name the foremost ones due to their vast numbers.

beats.title: The Penance of Śeṣa and His Appointment as Earth-Bearer
beats.summary: Śeṣa, the serpent, leaves his brothers due to their hostility and performs severe penance. Brahmā appears, grants him a boon, and appoints him to support the earth. Śeṣa descends to the underworld and holds the earth steady. Brahmā also gives him Garuḍa as a friend.

beats.title: The Serpents' Council
beats.summary: Vasuki, the king of serpents, learns of his mother's curse that his race will be destroyed in Janamejaya's snake sacrifice. He convenes a council of snakes to devise countermeasures. Various proposals are made, including bribing the priests, killing the officiants, or disrupting the sacrifice. The more righteous snakes argue against violence. Vasuki rejects the extreme plans and seeks a harmonious solution.

beats.title: The Prophecy of Āstīka's Birth
beats.summary: The chapter recounts a conversation among the serpent (naga) leaders, led by Elāpatra, who recalls a prophecy from Brahmā about the birth of the sage Jaratkāru and his son Āstīka, who will save the serpents from Janamejaya's snake sacrifice. Elāpatra advises Vāsuki to give his sister Jaratkāru in marriage to the sage Jaratkāru to ensure the birth of Āstīka.

beats.title: The Promise to Vasuki
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the serpent king Vasuki, who, after hearing the advice of the serpent Elapatra, protects his sister Jaratkaru. The gods, after churning the ocean, approach Brahma to relieve Vasuki's anxiety caused by his mother's curse. Brahma instructs Vasuki to give his sister in marriage to the sage Jaratkaru when the time comes, as foretold by Elapatra. Vasuki then appoints serpents to watch for Jaratkaru's desire to marry.

beats.title: The Story of Jaratkaru and King Parikshit
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the sage Jaratkaru, explaining the origin of his name. It also describes King Parikshit's encounter with a silent sage, his disrespectful act of placing a dead snake on the sage's shoulder, and the subsequent curse by the sage's son Shrngin.

beats.title: The Curse of Parikṣit
beats.summary: The sage Śr̥ṅgī curses King Parikṣit to die by snakebite within seven days after learning that the king had placed a dead snake on his father's shoulder. Śr̥ṅgī's father, the sage Śamīka, rebukes his son for the curse, explaining that the king protects them and deserves forgiveness.

beats.title: The Curse of Parikshit
beats.summary: King Parikshit is cursed by the sage Śr̥ṅgi to die from a snake bite within seven days, due to his disrespectful act of placing a dead snake on the sage Śamīka. Śamīka, the father, tries to mitigate the curse but cannot reverse it. He sends his disciple Gauramukha to inform the king. Parikshit, remorseful, builds a guarded palace and summons the sage Kāśyapa to cure him. On the way, the serpent king Takṣaka confronts Kāśyapa, claiming he cannot save the king.

beats.title: Takshaka's Revenge and the Death of Parikshit
beats.summary: Takshaka, the serpent king, challenges the sage Kashyapa to revive a tree he has bitten. Kashyapa succeeds, demonstrating his power. Takshaka then bribes Kashyapa to abandon his mission to save King Parikshit, who is doomed by a sage's curse. Takshaka sends disguised serpents with fruits, and Parikshit, ignoring warnings, eats a fruit containing a tiny worm that transforms into Takshaka and kills him.

beats.title: The Coronation and Marriage of Janamejaya
beats.summary: Suta narrates how after King Parikshit was killed by Takshaka, his ministers and priests performed his funeral rites and crowned his young son Janamejaya as king. Janamejaya ruled wisely with his ministers. Later, the ministers arranged his marriage to Vapushtama, daughter of King Suvarnavarman of Kashi. Janamejaya was delighted with his wife and remained faithful to her, enjoying pleasures like Pururavas with Urvashi.

beats.title: Jaratkaru and the Pitrs
beats.summary: The sage Jaratkaru, while wandering and performing severe austerities, sees his ancestors (pitrs) hanging upside down in a pit, clinging to a single remaining root of a grass clump, which is being gnawed by a rat. They explain that they are suffering due to the lack of progeny in their lineage, as Jaratkaru himself has not married or fathered children. They urge him to marry and produce offspring to save them from falling into hell.

beats.title: Jaratkaru's Marriage
beats.summary: Jaratkaru, a sage, encounters his ancestors hanging upside down in a pit. They reveal they are his ancestors and that his celibacy prevents them from attaining heaven. Jaratkaru agrees to marry, but only if he finds a woman with the same name who offers herself as alms. He searches in vain until the serpent king Vasuki offers his sister Jaratkaru. The sage initially refuses because her name is not exactly the same and he is uncertain about supporting her, but after learning her name is indeed Jaratkaru, he accepts.

beats.title: The Marriage and Separation of Jaratkaru
beats.summary: The sage Jaratkaru marries Vasuki's sister, also named Jaratkaru, on condition that she never displease him. She becomes pregnant but later wakes him for evening rituals, which he considers an insult. He leaves her, but she pleads for the sake of their unborn child. He acknowledges the child's divine nature and departs for austerities.

beats.title: The Birth of Astika
beats.summary: Vasuki, the king of serpents, learns from his sister Jaratkaru that her husband, the sage Jaratkaru, has blessed her with a son who will save the serpents from the snake sacrifice. The son, named Astika, is born and grows up in Vasuki's palace, studying the Vedas and bringing joy to the serpents.

beats.title: The Death of Parikshit
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks his ministers about the death of his father, King Parikshit. The ministers recount Parikshit's virtuous reign, his devotion to dharma, and his tragic death caused by a snake bite after he insulted a silent sage. The story highlights the consequences of anger and disrespect towards ascetics.

beats.title: The Death of Parikshit and Janamejaya's Vow
beats.summary: The ministers narrate to King Janamejaya the story of his father Parikshit's death: Parikshit placed a dead snake on the sage Shamika's shoulder; Shamika's son Shringi cursed Parikshit to die by Takshaka's bite within seven days; Parikshit took precautions but Takshaka bribed the sage Kashyapa to leave, then killed Parikshit. Janamejaya resolves to avenge his father by performing a snake sacrifice.

beats.title: The Snake Sacrifice of Janamejaya
beats.summary: King Janamejaya, son of Parikshit, resolves to perform a snake sacrifice (sarpasatra) to avenge his father's death by the serpent Takshaka. He consults his priests, who confirm the existence of such a sacrifice. The king orders preparations, and the ritual begins. Snakes of all kinds are summoned into the fire and perish.

beats.title: The Snake Sacrifice of Janamejaya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the preparations for the snake sacrifice of King Janamejaya, listing the priests and sages present, the horrific events of the sacrifice, and the plight of the serpent king Vasuki who seeks refuge with Indra. Vasuki, in despair, recalls a prophecy that his nephew Astika will stop the sacrifice and urges his sister Jaratkaru to send her son.

beats.title: Astika's Promise to Save the Snakes
beats.summary: Jaratkaru's wife explains to her son Astika the origin of the curse on the snakes: Kadru cursed her sons to be burned in Janamejaya's snake sacrifice, but Brahma decreed that a sage born from Jaratkaru's marriage would save them. Astika, that sage, reassures his uncle Vasuki and departs to intervene at the sacrifice.

beats.title: Āstīka's Praise of Janamejaya's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Āstīka praises King Janamejaya's snake sacrifice by comparing it to the great sacrifices of various deities and kings, and extols the king's virtues, likening him to many legendary figures.

beats.title: The Boon to Astika
beats.summary: King Janamejaya, while performing a snake sacrifice, is urged by the priests to grant a boon to the young sage Astika. Despite initial reluctance, Janamejaya agrees, and Astika requests the cessation of the sacrifice to save the snakes, including Takshaka. The king eventually grants the boon, ending the sacrifice.

beats.title: Parva 1, Chapter 52
beats.summary: This chapter lists the names of various serpent (nāga) lineages that were offered into the fire during Janamejaya's snake sacrifice. The sage Śaunaka asks the sūta to enumerate the serpents, and the sūta describes the major families: those born of Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Airāvata, Kaurava, and Dhṛtarāṣṭra, along with their physical descriptions and the vast number that perished.

beats.title: The Conclusion of the Snake Sacrifice and the Boon of Astika
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the conclusion of Janamejaya's snake sacrifice, where the sage Astika intervenes to save the snakes. The king grants Astika's wish to stop the sacrifice, and Astika returns to his mother and uncle. The snakes offer him a boon, and he asks that those who recite his story be free from snake fear. The chapter ends with the recitation of the Mahabharata by Vyasa at the sacrifice.

beats.title: Vyasa's Arrival at Janamejaya's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Suta narrates how the sage Krishna Dvaipayana Vyasa, the grandfather of the Pandavas and Kauravas, arrives at King Janamejaya's snake sacrifice. Janamejaya honors him and asks Vyasa to recount the history of the Kurus and Pandavas, including their conflict and the great war. Vyasa then instructs his disciple Vaishampayana to narrate the story.

beats.title: The Origins of the Kuru-Pandava Conflict
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the origins of the enmity between the Kauravas and Pandavas, detailing Duryodhana's attempts to kill the Pandavas, their escape, exile, marriage to Draupadi, establishment of Indraprastha, Arjuna's marriage to Subhadra, the burning of the Khandava forest, the construction of the Maya Sabha, the dice game, and the eventual war.

beats.title: The Glory of the Mahabharata
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana to narrate the Mahabharata in detail, especially the reasons behind the Pandavas' actions and their suffering. Vaishampayana praises the epic as a supreme purifier, containing dharma, artha, kama, and moksha, and describes its benefits for listeners.

beats.title: The Genealogies of the Principal Characters
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth stories of many key characters in the Mahabharata, starting with King Vasu (Uparicara) and his lineage, including the birth of Satyavati, Vyasa, and others. It also covers the births of Bhishma, Vidura, Karna, Krishna, Drona, Dhrishtadyumna, Draupadi, Shakuni, Dhritarashtra, Pandu, the Pandavas, the Kauravas, Abhimanyu, and others, culminating in the gathering of armies for the Kurukshetra war.

beats.title: The Descent of the Gods to Relieve Earth's Burden
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the origins of the great warriors mentioned. Vaishampayana recounts how after Parashurama (Jamadagnya) rid the earth of kshatriyas, brahmanas fathered kshatriya children with kshatriya women, restoring the warrior class. During the Krita Yuga, dharma flourished. Later, asuras (daityas, danavas) were born on earth as kings and beings, oppressing the world. Earth, burdened, sought refuge with Brahma. Brahma instructed the gods to incarnate on earth to relieve her burden. The gods, led by Indra, approached Narayana (Vishnu) to descend as well.

beats.title: Parva 1, Chapter 59
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the descent of gods and other celestial beings to Earth to relieve its burden, and provides a detailed genealogy of the divine, demonic, and other lineages, including the Adityas, Danavas, Gandharvas, Apsaras, Nagas, and Suparnas, as narrated by Vaishampayana to Janamejaya.

beats.title: Genealogy of Beings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Mahabharata (Parva 1, Chapter 60) provides a detailed genealogy of various divine and semi-divine beings, starting from the mind-born sons of Brahma, the eleven Rudras, and the six great sages. It then traces the lineages of Daksha, Dharma, Soma, Vasus, and others, including the births of Vishnu, Garuda, Bhrigu, and various creatures. The chapter serves as a comprehensive account of the origins of gods, sages, demons, animals, and other beings.

beats.title: Parva 1, Chapter 61
beats.summary: This chapter lists the incarnations (aṁśāvataraṇa) of various celestial beings—devas, asuras, gandharvas, apsaras, and others—as human kings and warriors on earth, primarily those involved in the Mahabharata war. It traces the births of key figures like Duryodhana (from Kali), the Pandavas (from Dharma, Vayu, Indra, Ashvins), Krishna (from Narayana), Balarama (from Shesha), and others, explaining their divine origins.

beats.title: The Lineage of the Kurus: King Duḥṣanta
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṁpāyana to narrate the lineage of the Kurus. Vaiśaṁpāyana describes King Duḥṣanta, a powerful and virtuous ruler who protected the earth, maintained dharma, and ensured prosperity without crime or fear. Duḥṣanta possessed immense strength and skill in warfare, comparable to Viṣṇu in power and the sun in brilliance.

beats.title: Dushyanta's Hunting Expedition
beats.summary: King Dushyanta goes on a grand hunting expedition in a dense forest, accompanied by his army and chariots. He kills many animals, causing chaos and destruction. The forest is described as arid and devoid of water, leading to exhaustion and death among the animals. The king's prowess is praised by the women of the city.

beats.title: Dushmanta's Entry into the Hermitage
beats.summary: King Dushmanta, while hunting, enters a beautiful forest and discovers the hermitage of sage Kashyapa (also called Kanva). He leaves his army at the forest entrance and approaches the ashram with his ministers and priest, marveling at the serene environment and the Vedic recitations.

beats.title: The Birth of Shakuntala
beats.summary: King Dushyanta arrives at Sage Kanva's hermitage and meets Shakuntala, who reveals she is Kanva's daughter. She narrates the story of her birth: the sage Vishvamitra, while performing severe penance, was tempted by the celestial nymph Menaka sent by Indra. Their union produced Shakuntala, who was later raised by Kanva.

beats.title: The Birth and Adoption of Śakuntalā
beats.summary: Menakā, sent by Indra, disturbs Viśvāmitra's penance. They have a daughter, Śakuntalā, whom Menakā abandons. Śakuntalā is protected by birds (śakuntas) and later found and adopted by Kaṇva. The story explains Śakuntalā's birth and adoption.

beats.title: The Marriage of Dushyanta and Shakuntala
beats.summary: King Dushyanta meets Shakuntala in the hermitage and proposes marriage through the Gandharva form. Shakuntala agrees on the condition that their son will be the heir. After the union, Dushyanta leaves, promising to send an army. Sage Kanva returns, divinely perceives the event, and blesses Shakuntala, foretelling a mighty son who will become a chakravartin. Shakuntala asks for the king's welfare and the stability of the Pauravas.

beats.title: Shakuntala's Plea and Dushyanta's Denial
beats.summary: Shakuntala brings her son Sarvadamana to King Dushyanta, reminding him of their marriage and his promise to make their son heir. Dushyanta denies knowing her, accusing her of lying. Shakuntala rebukes him with teachings on dharma, the nature of wife and son, and her own divine origin. She reveals her birth from Menaka and Vishvamitra. Dushyanta continues to reject her, questioning her character.

beats.title: The Acceptance of Bharata
beats.summary: Shakuntala confronts King Dushyanta, who denies their marriage and son. She argues with moral teachings on truth, parenthood, and dharma. A celestial voice confirms the son's paternity, leading Dushyanta to accept Shakuntala and their son Bharata, who becomes a great emperor.

beats.title: The Genealogy of the Paurava Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter traces the lineage from Daksha and Manu through the solar and lunar dynasties, focusing on the Paurava line. It describes the births of key figures including Yayati and his sons, and the story of Yayati's exchange of old age with his son Puru.

beats.title: The Story of Kaca and the Mṛtasañjīvanī Vidyā
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṃpāyana about Yayāti's marriage to Devayānī. Vaiśaṃpāyana narrates the story of Kaca, son of Bṛhaspati, who is sent by the gods to learn the Mṛtasañjīvanī (reviving) vidyā from Śukra. Kaca becomes a disciple of Śukra and wins the favor of Śukra's daughter Devayānī. The demons, enemies of the gods, kill Kaca twice, but Śukra revives him. The second time, the demons grind Kaca and mix him in wine, which Śukra drinks. When Śukra calls Kaca, Kaca speaks from Śukra's stomach. To save his daughter's grief and his own life, Śukra teaches Kaca the vidyā and then Kaca emerges from his stomach, killing Śukra temporarily. Kaca revives Śukra with the same vidyā. Śukra then curses that any brāhmaṇa who drinks wine will be a sinner. Kaca returns to the gods.

beats.title: Kacha's Departure and Devayani's Curse
beats.summary: Kacha, after completing his studies with Shukra, prepares to return to heaven. Devayani, Shukra's daughter, proposes marriage to him, but Kacha refuses, citing that she is like a sister as the daughter of his guru. Devayani curses him that his knowledge will not bear fruit, and Kacha retorts that no rishi's son will marry her. He then departs to heaven, where the gods honor him.

beats.title: The Quarrel of Devayani and Sharmishtha
beats.summary: Kacha, after learning the Mritasanjivani vidya from Shukra, returns to the gods. Indra, encouraged by the gods, prepares to attack the asuras. Meanwhile, Sharmishtha, daughter of Vrishaparva, and Devayani, daughter of Shukra, quarrel over clothes while bathing. Sharmishtha pushes Devayani into a well and leaves. King Yayati rescues Devayani, who then sends her maid Ghurnika to inform Shukra. Shukra arrives, consoles Devayani, and clarifies that she is not the daughter of a supplicant but of one who is praised, asserting the power of Brahman.

beats.title: Śukra's Teachings on Forbearance
beats.summary: Śukra teaches Devayānī about the power of forbearance and controlling anger, emphasizing that one who endures harsh words and suppresses anger conquers all. Devayānī responds that she understands the difference between anger and forbearance but refuses to live among those of mixed conduct who insult her. She declares that the wise should not associate with the wicked but with the virtuous.

beats.title: The Reconciliation of Shukracharya and Vrishaparva
beats.summary: Shukracharya, angry at Vrishaparva for the mistreatment of his daughter Devayani by Sharmishtha, threatens to leave the Asuras. Vrishaparva pleads with him to stay, offering all his wealth. Devayani demands that Sharmishtha become her servant. Vrishaparva agrees, and Sharmishtha accepts, thus resolving the conflict.

beats.title: Yayati Meets Devayani and Sharmishtha
beats.summary: King Yayati encounters Devayani and Sharmishtha in a forest. Devayani, daughter of Shukra, reveals that Sharmishtha is her maidservant. Yayati hesitates to marry Devayani due to caste differences, but Shukra permits the marriage and gives Sharmishtha as a companion, with the condition that Yayati never summon her to bed.

beats.title: Yayati and Sharmishtha
beats.summary: King Yayati returns to his capital, settles his two wives Devayani and Sharmishtha in separate residences, and lives happily with Devayani for many years. Devayani bears a son. After a thousand years, Sharmishtha, now of age, desires a child and approaches Yayati, arguing that as her friend's husband and king, he should grant her a child. Yayati initially hesitates due to a promise to Devayani's father Shukracharya, but Sharmishtha cites exceptions for lies in certain contexts. Yayati ultimately agrees, and Sharmishtha bears a son.

beats.title: The Birth of Yayati's Sons and the Curse of Old Age
beats.summary: Devayani discovers that Sharmishtha has secretly borne three sons by her husband Yayati. She confronts Sharmishtha, then leaves for her father Shukra's hermitage. Yayati follows, and Shukra curses him with premature old age. Yayati argues he acted to avoid the sin of refusing a woman's request for intercourse. Shukra relents, allowing Yayati to transfer his old age to any son who will exchange his youth.

beats.title: Yayāti's Exchange of Youth
beats.summary: King Yayāti, cursed with old age by Śukra, asks his sons to exchange their youth for his old age. Yadu, Turvasu, Druhyu, and Anu refuse, but his youngest son Pūru agrees. Yayāti blesses Pūru and curses the others, foretelling their degraded futures.

beats.title: Yayāti's Renunciation and Pūru's Coronation
beats.summary: Yayāti, having enjoyed royal pleasures for a thousand years, decides to retire and offers his youth and kingdom to his youngest son Pūru, who had always obeyed him. The elders object, but Yayāti justifies his choice based on the sons' obedience. Pūru is crowned, and Yayāti retires to the forest. The lineages of Yayāti's sons are described.

beats.title: The Fall and Rise of King Yayāti
beats.summary: King Yayāti, after installing his youngest son Pūru on the throne, retires to the forest to perform severe austerities for a thousand years, then attains heaven. However, due to some offense, he is cast down from heaven by Indra, but later returns to heaven along with other kings. Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana to narrate the full story of Yayāti's deeds that led to his return.

beats.title: Yayati's Advice to Puru
beats.summary: Yayati, after his death, resides in heaven and is honored by the gods. One day, Indra asks him about his conversation with his son Puru when he gave him his old age and kingdom. Yayati recounts his advice to Puru on the virtues of forbearance, gentle speech, and generosity.

beats.title: The Fall of Yayati
beats.summary: Indra questions Yayati about his tapas, and Yayati claims no equal. Indra reveals Yayati's fall from heaven due to pride. Yayati accepts his fate and wishes to fall among the virtuous. Indra assures him he will find refuge among the good. As Yayati falls, King Ashtaka and other sages see him and inquire about his identity and purpose, offering him protection.

beats.title: Yayāti's Fall and Discourse on Destiny
beats.summary: Yayāti, fallen from heaven due to pride, explains to King Aṣṭaka the nature of fate and equanimity, recounting his celestial sojourns and eventual fall. He teaches that one should neither rejoice nor grieve, as destiny is supreme.

beats.title: Yayāti's Discourse on Karma and Rebirth
beats.summary: King Yayāti explains to his grandson Aṣṭaka the nature of karma, rebirth, and the consequences of actions. He describes how beings fall into earthly hells, the process of transmigration, and the importance of humility and righteous deeds over pride and ostentation.

beats.title: The Four Ashramas and the Nature of a Sage
beats.summary: King Yayati instructs his grandson Ashtaka on the four ashramas (stages of life): brahmacharya (student), grihastha (householder), vanaprastha (hermit), and sannyasa (renunciate). He describes the qualities and practices of each, emphasizing detachment and self-control. Ashtaka asks about the number of sages and types of silence (mauna). Yayati explains that a true sage is one who is detached from both village and forest, and that mauna leads to liberation.

beats.title: Yayāti's Fall and the Dialogue on Dharma
beats.summary: King Yayāti, falling from heaven due to exhausted merit, is questioned by King Aṣṭaka and others about the path to heaven. Yayāti explains the superiority of the ascetic who renounces household life, and refuses to accept others' merits, emphasizing that a king should not act miserably or accept gifts from non-Brāhmaṇas. The dialogue explores dharma, karma, and the proper conduct of a king.

beats.title: The Ascent of Yayāti
beats.summary: King Vasumanā, Śibi, and Aṣṭaka question their grandfather Yayāti about the afterlife and the attainment of heavenly realms. Yayāti explains that the worlds are attained through truth, charity, and righteousness. Śibi is praised for his exceptional generosity, and Yayāti is eventually lifted to heaven by his grandsons' merits.

beats.title: The Lineage of Puru
beats.summary: This chapter traces the lineage of King Puru through the lunar dynasty, listing successive kings and their achievements, including Bharata, Samvarana, Kuru, and others, culminating in the ancestors of the Pandavas and Kauravas.

beats.title: Parva 1, Chapter 90
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the genealogy of the lunar dynasty, from Daksha through Manu, Pururavas, Yayati, and the Pauravas, culminating in the birth of the Pandavas and Kauravas. It details the lineage of King Janamejaya, including the story of Bharata, the founding of Hastinapura, and the births of Bhishma, Dhritarashtra, Pandu, Vidura, and the Pandava brothers. The chapter ends with the marriages and children of the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Curse of Mahabhisha and the Vasus
beats.summary: King Mahabhisha, after performing many sacrifices, goes to heaven but is cursed by Brahma for staring at Ganga. He is reborn as Shantanu. Ganga agrees to help the Vasus, who are cursed by Vasishtha, by becoming their mother and drowning them. The Vasus give a portion of their power to ensure Shantanu and Ganga's son will be mighty but childless.

beats.title: The Birth of Bhishma
beats.summary: King Pratipa encounters the goddess Ganga, who desires him. He refuses but arranges for her to marry his son Shantanu. Shantanu meets Ganga, who agrees to be his wife on condition that he never question her actions. She bears eight sons, drowning each after birth. At the eighth, Shantanu stops her, and she reveals she is Ganga, and the sons are the Vasus cursed to be born human. She leaves, taking the eighth son (Bhishma).

beats.title: The Curse of the Vasus and the Birth of Bhishma
beats.summary: Ganga explains to Shantanu the story of the Vasus' curse. The sage Vashistha (Apava) cursed the eight Vasus to be born as humans for stealing his wish-fulfilling cow. Seven Vasus were to be freed after a year, but Dyaus (the eighth) was to live a long human life. Ganga agreed to be their mother and drown them at birth to release them. She then disappears with the child, who becomes Devavrata (Bhishma).

beats.title: The Vow of Bhishma
beats.summary: This chapter describes the virtuous rule of King Shantanu, his son Devavrata (Bhishma), and the events leading to Shantanu's marriage to Satyavati. Devavrata takes a vow of celibacy to secure his father's marriage, earning the name Bhishma.

beats.title: The Succession of Shantanu's Sons
beats.summary: After Shantanu's marriage to Satyavati, they have two sons: Chitrangada and Vichitravirya. Shantanu dies when Chitrangada is young. Bhishma installs Chitrangada as king. Chitrangada becomes arrogant and challenges all beings, leading to a battle with a Gandharva of the same name. After a three-year war, the Gandharva kills Chitrangada. Bhishma performs his funeral rites and installs the young Vichitravirya as king. Vichitravirya rules under Bhishma's guidance.

beats.title: Bhishma Abducts the Princesses of Kashi
beats.summary: Bhishma, after his brother Chitrangada's death, rules the kingdom under Satyavati's guidance. He decides to get his younger brother Vichitravirya married. Hearing of the swayamvara of the three daughters of the king of Kashi, Bhishma goes there, abducts the princesses, defeats the assembled kings including Shalva, and brings them to Hastinapura. The eldest princess Amba reveals she has already chosen Shalva as her husband, so Bhishma releases her. He marries Ambika and Ambalika to Vichitravirya, who enjoys their company for seven years before dying of consumption. Bhishma performs his funeral rites.

beats.title: Satyavati's Request to Bhishma
beats.summary: Satyavati, grieving for her son Vicitravirya who died childless, asks Bhishma to father children with his brother's widows to continue the dynasty. Bhishma refuses, citing his vow of celibacy, but offers a solution through the practice of niyoga (levirate) with a brahmana. He advises her to consider the dharma of kings in times of distress.

beats.title: The Story of Dirghatamas and the Restoration of Kshatriya Lineage
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of the sage Dirghatamas, who was born from a curse of Brihaspati, abandoned by his sons, rescued by King Bali, and fathered children with a shudra woman and later with Queen Sudeshna, resulting in the birth of King Anga. The story illustrates the restoration of kshatriya lineage through brahminical intervention.

beats.title: The Birth of Vyasa and the Continuation of the Kuru Line
beats.summary: Satyavati reveals to Bhishma her past union with the sage Parashara, which produced Vyasa. She then summons Vyasa to father children on the wives of Vicitravirya to continue the Kuru lineage. Vyasa agrees on condition that the queens observe a year-long vow, but Satyavati insists on immediate conception. Vyasa then approaches Ambika (Kausalya) and instructs her.

beats.title: The Birth of Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and Vidura
beats.summary: Vyasa, at Satyavati's request, fathers Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and Vidura through niyoga with the wives of Vichitravirya. Dhritarashtra is born blind due to his mother closing her eyes in fear; Pandu is born pale because his mother turned pale; Vidura is born to a maid and is an incarnation of Dharma due to a sage's curse.

beats.title: The Curse of Māṇḍavya: Dharma's Birth as Vidura
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks how Dharma was cursed and born as Vidura. Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates the story of the sage Māṇḍavya, who was wrongly impaled on a stake by the king's men due to thieves hiding in his hermitage. Despite being impaled, he survived through his penance. Later, he confronted Dharma, who revealed that the punishment was for a childhood prank of piercing insects' tails. Māṇḍavya deemed the punishment disproportionate and cursed Dharma to be born as a śūdra (Vidura). He also decreed that for fourteen years, sins committed unknowingly would not incur punishment.

beats.title: The Prosperity of the Kuru Kingdom and the Upbringing of the Princes
beats.summary: This chapter describes the prosperity and virtuous rule in the Kuru kingdom after the birth of Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and Vidura. Under Bhishma's guardianship, the land flourishes with abundant crops, happy citizens, and moral conduct. The three princes are raised with care, educated in various arts and sciences, and grow into skilled warriors. Pandu becomes king due to Dhritarashtra's blindness and Vidura's birth status.

beats.title: The Marriage of Gandhari
beats.summary: Bhishma advises Dhritarashtra on the importance of continuing the Kuru lineage through marriage. He suggests suitable brides from the Yadava, Gandhara, and Madra kingdoms. Vidura defers to Bhishma's wisdom. Bhishma learns of Gandhari, daughter of Subala, who has a boon for a hundred sons. Despite Dhritarashtra's blindness, Subala agrees to the marriage. Gandhari, upon learning her husband is blind, blindfolds herself voluntarily to share his condition. Her brother Shakuni brings her to the Kauravas with great ceremony. Gandhari wins everyone's respect through her virtuous conduct.

beats.title: The Birth of Karna
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth of Karna, the son of Kunti and Surya. Kunti, as a maiden, received a mantra from the sage Durvasa to summon any god and obtain a child. Out of curiosity, she invoked the sun god Surya, who granted her a son born with natural armor and earrings. Fearing shame, she set the baby adrift in a river. The child was found and adopted by a charioteer named Adhiratha and his wife Radha, and named Vasushena. Later, due to his act of donating his natural armor and earrings to Indra (disguised as a Brahmin), he became known as Vaikartana Karna.

beats.title: Pandu's Marriage and Conquests
beats.summary: This chapter describes King Pandu's marriage to Kunti and Madri, his military conquests across various kingdoms, and his triumphant return to Hastinapura, where he is welcomed by Bhishma and the citizens.

beats.title: Pandu's Return and Forest Life
beats.summary: Pandu returns from conquest, distributes wealth to Bhishma, Satyavati, his mother, and Vidura. Dhritarashtra performs horse sacrifices. Pandu, with Kunti and Madri, lives in the forest, hunting. He hears of a maiden and arranges her marriage to Vidura, who begets sons.

beats.title: The Birth of the Kauravas
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana how Gandhari bore a hundred sons and one daughter, and how Dhritarashtra had a son from a Vaishya wife. Vaishampayana narrates: Vyasa blessed Gandhari with a hundred sons. She conceived but carried for two years. Hearing of Kunti's son, she aborted a lump of flesh. Vyasa divided it into a hundred and one pieces, placed them in pots, and after a month, Duryodhana and others were born. Dhritarashtra was advised to abandon Duryodhana due to evil omens but refused. Later, a son named Yuyutsu was born from a Vaishya woman.

beats.title: The Hundred Sons of Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana to list the names of Dhritarashtra's sons in order of birth. Vaishampayana enumerates the hundred sons (including Duryodhana, Yuyutsu, Duhshasana, etc.) and one daughter Duhshala. He describes them as all great warriors, learned in Vedas and statecraft, and notes that Dhritarashtra arranged their marriages appropriately, giving Duhshala to Jayadratha, king of Sindhu, with Shakuni's consent.

beats.title: The Curse of Pāṇḍu
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṁpāyana to narrate the birth of the Pāṇḍavas. Vaiśaṁpāyana recounts how King Pāṇḍu, while hunting in a forest, shot a deer that was actually the sage Kiṁdama in disguise, engaged in mating. The dying sage curses Pāṇḍu that he will die at the moment of union with his wife, due to his cruel act.

beats.title: Pandu's Renunciation
beats.summary: King Pandu, afflicted by grief and guilt over his father's death due to desire, resolves to renounce the world and adopt an ascetic life. He describes his rigorous vows of detachment, equanimity, and non-violence. His wives Kunti and Madri insist on accompanying him. Pandu distributes his wealth to brahmins and departs to the forest, eventually reaching Mount Satasringa where he practices severe austerities.

beats.title: Pandu's Lament and the Duty of Progeny
beats.summary: Pandu, afflicted by a sage's curse that prevents him from fathering children, discusses with his wife Kunti the importance of progeny for attaining heaven. He explains the concept of debts to gods, sages, ancestors, and humans, and the necessity of having a son to repay the ancestral debt. Citing the story of Sharadandayani, he urges Kunti to use the ancient practice of niyoga (levirate) to bear children from a worthy brahmana.

beats.title: Kunti's Persuasion: The Story of Vyuṣitāśva and Bhadrā
beats.summary: Kunti tells King Pandu the story of King Vyuṣitāśva and his wife Bhadrā to convince him to have children through niyoga. After Vyuṣitāśva's death, his spirit instructs Bhadrā to lie with his corpse on specific nights, resulting in sons. Kunti urges Pandu to similarly beget children through her by mental power or yogic means.

beats.title: Pandu's Request and Kunti's Boon
beats.summary: King Pandu, unable to father children due to a curse, asks his wife Kunti to use a boon from sage Durvasa to summon a deity and bear sons. Kunti agrees, and Pandu instructs her to invoke Dharma (the god of righteousness) to ensure the child is born of dharma. Kunti accepts and prepares to perform the rite.

beats.title: The Divine Births of the Pandavas
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the divine births of the three eldest Pandavas: Yudhishthira from Dharma, Bhima from Vayu, and Arjuna from Indra. It describes the circumstances of their conception, the celestial prophecies at their births, and the grand celebration by gods, sages, gandharvas, apsaras, and other beings. The chapter also includes Pandu's decision to perform penance to obtain a son from Indra, and Kunti's refusal to bear a fourth child.

beats.title: The Birth of Nakula and Sahadeva
beats.summary: Madri expresses her desire for children to Pandu, who then persuades Kunti to allow Madri to share the boon. Madri invokes the Ashvins and gives birth to Nakula and Sahadeva. The five Pandava brothers grow up in the Himalayas, astonishing the sages.

beats.title: The Death of Pandu and Madri's Sati
beats.summary: Pandu, while hunting in the forest, sees a deer coupling and shoots it. The deer is actually a sage in disguise who curses Pandu that he will die if he ever approaches his wife with desire. Later, overcome by passion for Madri, Pandu ignores the curse and dies. Madri blames herself and immolates herself on his funeral pyre, entrusting her sons to Kunti.

beats.title: The Return of the Pāṇḍavas to Hastinapura
beats.summary: The sage Vaiśampāyana narrates how the great sages, after performing the avabhr̥tha ceremony for Pāṇḍu, decided to bring his sons and wives to Hastinapura. They traveled with the family and the body of Pāṇḍu. Upon arrival, the citizens and Kauravas came out to receive them. Bhīṣma offered the kingdom to the sages. The eldest sage then recounted Pāṇḍu's story: his renunciation, the divine births of his sons (Yudhiṣṭhira from Dharma, Bhīma from Vāyu, Arjuna from Indra, and the twins from the Aśvins), Pāṇḍu's death, and Mādrī's self-immolation. He instructed the Kauravas to perform the funeral rites. After speaking, the sages and celestial beings vanished, leaving everyone in wonder.

beats.title: The Funeral of Pandu and Madri
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra instructs Vidura to perform the funeral rites for Pandu and Madri. Vidura, with Bhishma, conducts the cremation with full royal honors. The citizens mourn deeply, and the city remains in grief for twelve nights.

beats.title: The Childhood of the Pandavas and Duryodhana's Plots
beats.summary: After King Pandu's death, his sons the Pandavas return to Hastinapura. They grow up with the Kauravas, but Duryodhana becomes jealous of Bhima's strength and plots to kill him multiple times: by drowning, snakebite, and poison. Bhima survives all attempts due to his divine heritage. The chapter ends with the Pandavas aware of the plots but remaining patient, guided by Vidura's wisdom.

beats.title: The Birth of Kṛpa
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth of Kṛpa and his sister Kṛpī, the children of the sage Gautama (also known as Śaradvān). Śaradvān was a great archer who, through his penance, threatened Indra. Indra sent the celestial nymph Jālapadī to distract him. Śaradvān was tempted but controlled himself, though his seed fell on a clump of śara grass, producing twins. King Śantanu found them and raised them, naming them Kṛpa and Kṛpī because he raised them with compassion (kṛpā). Later, Gautama returned and taught Kṛpa the entire science of archery, making him a great teacher.

beats.title: The Birth and Training of Drona
beats.summary: Bhishma, seeking to ensure the Kauravas' excellence in archery, inquires about their teachers. The chapter narrates the birth of Drona from Bharadvaja and an apsara, his marriage to Kripi, the birth of Ashvatthama, and Drona's acquisition of divine weapons from Rama Jamadagnya. Drona then goes to his friend Drupada.

beats.title: Drona's Arrival and Appointment as Teacher
beats.summary: Drupada insults Drona, who then goes to Hastinapura, impresses the Kuru princes by retrieving a ball from a well, and is appointed as their teacher. Drona reveals his past friendship with Drupada and his desire for revenge. Arjuna pledges to fulfill Drona's wish, and Drona begins teaching the princes.

beats.title: The Training of the Princes and the Story of Ekalavya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Mahabharata (Parva 1, Chapter 123) narrates the training of the Kuru and Pandava princes under Drona, focusing on Arjuna's exceptional skill and dedication. It includes the story of Ekalavya, a Nishada prince who learns archery by making a clay image of Drona and surpasses Arjuna, leading Drona to demand his right thumb as guru-dakshina, thus crippling him. The chapter also describes a test where Arjuna alone correctly identifies the target (a bird's head) and shoots it, and later Drona saves Arjuna from a crocodile and bestows upon him the divine weapon Brahmashira.

beats.title: The Display of Princes' Skills
beats.summary: Droṇa, having taught the Kaurava and Pāṇḍava princes, requests King Dhṛtarāṣṭra to allow a public display of their skills. The king agrees, and a grand arena is prepared. The princes demonstrate their prowess in archery, swordplay, and other martial arts, culminating in a mace duel between Duryodhana and Bhīma. Vidura narrates the events to Dhṛtarāṣṭra and Gāndhārī.

beats.title: The Display of Skills in the Arena
beats.summary: In this chapter, the Kuru princes display their martial skills in the arena. Duryodhana and Bhima engage in a tense confrontation, stopped by Drona's son Aśvatthāmā. Arjuna then demonstrates his archery prowess, including mastery of divine weapons, to the amazement of the crowd. Dhṛtarāṣṭra, hearing the commotion, inquires of Vidura, who explains Arjuna's arrival. The chapter ends with Duryodhana surrounded by his brothers, resembling Indra surrounded by the gods.

beats.title: Karna's Arrival and Coronation
beats.summary: Karna enters the arena, displays his archery, challenges Arjuna, and is crowned king of Anga by Duryodhana, leading to a tense standoff.

beats.title: Karna's Adoption and Duryodhana's Defense
beats.summary: After Karna's public display of archery, Adhiratha enters the arena, revealing Karna's low birth. Bhima taunts Karna, but Duryodhana defends him, arguing that birth is irrelevant and citing examples of divine and noble births from humble origins. Duryodhana then challenges anyone who opposes Karna to a duel. The crowd reacts with mixed emotions, and Kunti secretly rejoices recognizing her son. Duryodhana's fear of Arjuna dissipates upon gaining Karna's alliance.

beats.title: Droṇa's Revenge and Drupada's Humiliation
beats.summary: Droṇa, after teaching the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas, demands a guru-dakṣiṇā: to capture King Drupada of Pāñcāla. The disciples attack Drupada, defeat him, and bring him before Droṇa. Droṇa reminds Drupada of their childhood friendship and offers half the kingdom, establishing a truce. Drupada, humiliated, nurses a grudge and later performs a sacrifice to obtain a son who will kill Droṇa.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Lament
beats.summary: Duryodhana, distressed by the popularity of the Pandavas and the citizens' desire to crown Yudhishthira, complains to his father Dhritarashtra about the situation, highlighting Bhishma's refusal to take the throne and the risk of the Kauravas being marginalized.

beats.title: The Conspiracy to Exile the Pandavas
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after hearing Duryodhana's words, reflects on Pandu's righteousness and the loyalty of his subjects. Duryodhana insists on exiling the Pandavas to Varanavata, arguing that the elders will not oppose it. Dhritarashtra hesitates due to moral concerns but eventually agrees, seeing it as a way to remove his anxiety.

beats.title: The Plot to Send the Pandavas to Varanavata
beats.summary: King Duryodhana, following Dhritarashtra's plan, sends the Pandavas to Varanavata under the pretext of a festival, but with the intention of harming them. Yudhishthira, aware of the danger, seeks blessings from elders before departing.

beats.title: The Plot of the Lacquer House
beats.summary: Duryodhana, enraged by the Pandavas' escape from the lacquer house, plots to kill them by sending Purocana to build a flammable house in Varanavata. He instructs Purocana to set it on fire when the Pandavas are asleep. Purocana agrees and departs.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Depart for Vāraṇāvata
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, after paying respects to elders and citizens, depart for Vāraṇāvata. Citizens lament the injustice of Dhṛtarāṣṭra. Yudhiṣṭhira reassures them. Vidura gives cryptic advice about survival and wisdom. Kuntī questions Yudhiṣṭhira about Vidura's words. They arrive at Vāraṇāvata on the eighth day of the month of Phālguna.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas in Vāraṇāvata
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas arrive in Vāraṇāvata and are welcomed by the townspeople. They stay in a house built by Purocana, which Yudhiṣṭhira suspects is made of flammable materials intended to burn them alive. He discusses the danger with Bhīma and devises a plan to escape by digging a tunnel.

beats.title: The House of Lac
beats.summary: Vidura sends a trusted miner to warn the Pandavas of Duryodhana's plot to burn them alive in a house of lac. The miner reveals the plan, and Yudhishthira acknowledges him. The miner digs a tunnel for escape, and the Pandavas live in hiding, pretending to trust Purocana while secretly preparing to flee.

beats.title: The Escape from the Lacquer House
beats.summary: Purocana, having housed the Pandavas for a year, becomes complacent. Yudhishthira, suspecting treachery, plans an escape by setting fire to the house and fleeing through a tunnel. Kunti hosts a feast for Brahmins, and a Niṣādī woman with five sons arrives drunk and falls asleep in the house. Bhima sets the house ablaze, killing Purocana and the Niṣādī family. The citizens lament the supposed death of the Pandavas, who escape unnoticed through the tunnel. Bhima carries his mother and brothers through the forest.

beats.title: The Escape from the House of Lac
beats.summary: After the fire in the house of lac, the citizens of Varanavata discover the burned house and the bodies of a Nishada woman and her five sons, mistakenly believing the Pandavas are dead. They send word to Dhritarashtra, who mourns publicly and performs funeral rites, though Vidura knows the truth. Meanwhile, the Pandavas escape through a tunnel and flee south through the forest. Exhausted and lost, they ask Bhima to carry them further.

beats.title: Bhima's Lament in the Forest
beats.summary: Bhima carries his exhausted mother and brothers through a forest, finds a large banyan tree, and goes to fetch water. Upon returning, he finds them asleep on the ground and laments their fallen state, reflecting on the hardships of exile and the value of kinship.

beats.title: Hiḍimba-vadha (The Killing of Hiḍimba)
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas are sleeping in a forest when a rākṣasa named Hiḍimba sends his sister to bring them as food. The sister, however, falls in love with Bhīmasena and warns him, offering to save them. Bhīma refuses to abandon his family and challenges Hiḍimba.

beats.title: The Slaying of Hidimba
beats.summary: The demon Hidimba, angered by his sister Hidimba's attraction to the Pandavas, attacks them. Bhima reassures Hidimba and confronts the demon, challenging him to battle.

beats.title: Bhima's encounter with Hidimba
beats.summary: Bhima confronts the rakshasa Hidimba, who is enraged at his sister Hidimba's affection for Bhima. Bhima defends her, challenges Hidimba to fight him instead, and after a fierce battle, drags him away to kill him, waking the other Pandavas.

beats.title: The Slaying of Hidimba
beats.summary: Bhima kills the rakshasa Hidimba, who had come to eat the Pandavas but was diverted by his sister Hidimba's love for Bhima. The Pandavas, with Kunti, witness the fight and then proceed onward.

beats.title: The Birth of Ghatotkaca
beats.summary: Bhima kills the rakshasa Hidimba, and his sister Hidimba asks to marry Bhima. Yudhishthira consents, and Hidimba bears a son, Ghatotkaca, who later leaves to serve his father's side in the war.

beats.title: The Pandavas Meet Vyasa
beats.summary: The Pandavas, disguised as ascetics and carrying their mother Kunti, travel through forests and regions including Matsya, Trigarta, Panchala, and Kicaka. They meet the sage Vyasa (Krishna Dvaipayana), who consoles them, foretells their future victory and restoration of their kingdom, and instructs them to stay hidden in a nearby city. Vyasa then departs.

beats.title: The Brāhmaṇa's Lament
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, after escaping the lac house, arrive in the city of Ekacakrā and live in a brāhmaṇa's house. One day, they hear loud lamentations from the brāhmaṇa's home. Kuntī sends Bhīma to investigate. The brāhmaṇa, overwhelmed by grief, laments his helplessness in the face of a calamity, contemplating abandoning his family or himself, but unable to choose. The chapter ends with his despair.

beats.title: The Brahmana Woman's Self-Sacrifice
beats.summary: A brahmana woman (the wife of a sage) argues with her husband that she should be allowed to sacrifice herself to save him and their children from a demon. She cites dharma, the duty of a wife to prioritize her husband's life, and the social dangers of being a widow. The husband, though reluctant, eventually agrees, and they both weep.

beats.title: The Daughter's Sacrifice
beats.summary: A young girl, facing abandonment by her parents due to a curse or threat, argues that she should be left behind to save the family. Her parents and she weep, but her younger brother, a child, bravely declares he will kill the demon with a blade of grass. Kunti arrives and revives them with hope.

beats.title: The Brahmana's Lament
beats.summary: Kunti asks a Brahmana about the source of his sorrow. The Brahmana explains that a powerful rakshasa named Baka terrorizes the kingdom, demanding a tribute of food and a human. The Brahmana's family is now in danger, and he sees no escape.

beats.title: Kunti's Offer to the Brahmana
beats.summary: Kunti advises a Brahmana not to sacrifice himself or his family to a demon, and offers her son Bhima to deliver the food and defeat the demon. The Brahmana initially refuses but is convinced by Kunti's confidence in Bhima's abilities.

beats.title: Kunti's Plan to Kill Baka
beats.summary: Kunti reveals to Yudhishthira that she instructed Bhima to kill the demon Baka to help a Brahmana, citing Bhima's strength and the duty of a Kshatriya to protect others. Yudhishthira agrees but urges secrecy.

beats.title: Bhima Kills the Demon Baka
beats.summary: Bhima goes to the forest where the rakshasa Baka lives, eats the food meant for Baka, and after a fierce wrestling match, kills Baka by breaking his back and twisting his neck.

beats.title: Bhima Slays Baka
beats.summary: Bhima kills the rakshasa Baka, who had been terrorizing the city of Ekacakra. After the killing, the rakshasas become peaceful. Bhima places the body at the city gate, and the citizens marvel at the feat. The brahmana family whose turn it was to be eaten by Baka is saved, and they attribute the deed to a mysterious brahmana. The Pāṇḍavas continue to live there.

beats.title: The Pandavas Hear of Draupadi's Origin
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana what the Pandavas did after killing Baka. Vaishampayana narrates that they stayed in a brahmin's house studying the supreme Brahman. A learned brahmin guest arrived and told them wonderful stories, including about the self-choice of Draupadi, the birth of Dhrishtadyumna and Shikhandi, and Draupadi's birth from the sacrificial altar. The Pandavas asked for details, and the brahmin narrated the full story of Draupadi's origin.

beats.title: The Birth and Early Life of Drona
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth of Dronacharya, his education under Rishi Bharadvaja and later under Parashurama, his friendship with King Drupada, the subsequent insult by Drupada, and Drona's revenge by capturing Drupada's kingdom through his students, the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Birth of Dhrishtadyumna and Draupadi
beats.summary: King Drupada, seeking revenge against Drona for past humiliation, performs a sacrifice to obtain a son capable of killing Drona. The sages Yaja and Upayaja conduct the ritual, resulting in the birth of Dhrishtadyumna from the fire and Draupadi from the altar. Dhrishtadyumna is later trained by Drona himself, who recognizes the inevitability of fate.

beats.title: Kunti's Counsel to Move to Panchala
beats.summary: Kunti, observing her sons' distress after hearing something, suggests they leave the Brahmana's house where they have been staying for a long time and go to the Panchala kingdom, which is prosperous and ruled by the Brahmana-devotee king Yudhishthira. Yudhishthira agrees, and after consulting his brothers, they depart for Panchala.

beats.title: Vyasa's Visit and the Boon of Five Husbands
beats.summary: Vyasa visits the Pandavas in hiding and tells them the story of a sage's daughter who, through penance, obtained a boon from Shiva to have five husbands. He reveals that she is Draupadi, destined to be their common wife, and instructs them to go to Panchala.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Meet the Gandharva Aṅgāraparṇa
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, led by Yudhiṣṭhira, set out with their mother Kuntī. At the Gaṅgā river, they encounter the gandharva Aṅgāraparṇa (Citraratha), who challenges them for entering his territory at twilight. Arjuna defeats him using the Āgneya astra, but spares his life at Yudhiṣṭhira's command. In gratitude, Citraratha offers friendship, the Cakṣuṣī vidyā, and a hundred divine horses to each Pāṇḍava. Arjuna accepts the horses and friendship but declines the vidyā unless given freely. Citraratha then gives the vidyā and they become friends.

beats.title: The Gandharva's Discourse on the Importance of Priests
beats.summary: The Gandharva explains to Arjuna why he attacked them: they lacked fire, oblations, and a priest, and were traveling at night with women. He then praises the Pandavas' lineage and reveals that Arjuna's victory is due to his celibacy and the importance of having a virtuous priest for a king's success.

beats.title: The Story of Tapatī and Samvarana
beats.summary: Arjuna asks the Gandharva about the meaning of 'Tapatya', and the Gandharva narrates the story of King Samvarana's encounter with Tapatī, the daughter of the Sun god Vivasvat. Samvarana, while hunting, sees Tapatī and becomes enamored, but she disappears. The story sets up the lineage of the Kauravas and Pandavas.

beats.title: Samvarana's Infatuation with Tapati
beats.summary: The Gandharva (a celestial musician) narrates a story to the Pandavas about King Samvarana who becomes enamored with Tapati, the daughter of Surya (the sun god). Tapati appears before him, disappears, and reappears, leading the king to fall unconscious. She then advises him to seek her father's consent for marriage through penance. The chapter ends with Tapati revealing her identity as the younger sister of Savitri and daughter of the sun.

beats.title: Saṃvaraṇa's Penance and Vasiṣṭha's Arrival
beats.summary: King Saṃvaraṇa, smitten by the celestial maiden, falls unconscious. His minister revives him and sends the army away. The king then performs penance to propitiate the sun god, and after twelve days, the sage Vasiṣṭha appears. Vasiṣṭha reveals his identity and offers to fulfill the king's wish.

beats.title: The Marriage of Saṁvaraṇa and Tapatī
beats.summary: Vasiṣṭha narrates the story of King Saṁvaraṇa and Tapatī, daughter of Sūrya (the Sun god). Saṁvaraṇa, a Kuru king, desired Tapatī. Vasiṣṭha mediated, and Sūrya agreed to the marriage. After a twelve-day austerity, Saṁvaraṇa married Tapatī with Vasiṣṭha's blessings. They lived in the mountains for twelve years, during which Indra withheld rain from the kingdom. Vasiṣṭha brought them back, rain returned, and the kingdom prospered. Their son was Kuru, ancestor of the Pāṇḍavas.

beats.title: The Glory of Vasistha
beats.summary: Arjuna asks the Gandharva about the sage Vasistha, who served as the purohita (priest) for the Ikshvaku kings. The Gandharva describes Vasistha's extraordinary self-control, forgiveness, and adherence to dharma, emphasizing the importance of having a virtuous brahmana as purohita for a king seeking to conquer the earth.

beats.title: The Origin of the Enmity between Viśvāmitra and Vasiṣṭha
beats.summary: The Gandharva narrates the origin of the enmity between Viśvāmitra and Vasiṣṭha. Viśvāmitra, a Kṣatriya king, covets Vasiṣṭha's wish-fulfilling cow Nandinī. When Vasiṣṭha refuses to give her, Viśvāmitra takes her by force. Nandinī returns to Vasiṣṭha and, empowered by his spiritual energy, creates armies of foreign warriors that rout Viśvāmitra's forces. Witnessing the supremacy of Brāhmaṇa power, Viśvāmitra renounces his kingdom and undertakes severe penance to become a Brāhmaṇa, eventually attaining that status.

beats.title: The Curse of Kalmāṣapāda
beats.summary: The Gandharva narrates the story of King Kalmāṣapāda (also known as Saudāsa), who was cursed by the sage Śakti to become a man-eater after striking the sage with a whip. The curse was instigated by Viśvāmitra due to a rivalry with Vasiṣṭha. A rākṣasa named Kinkara entered the king, leading him to eat human flesh, including Śakti himself. Vasiṣṭha's other sons were also killed by the rākṣasa. Vasiṣṭha attempted suicide multiple times but failed, eventually returning to his hermitage.

beats.title: Vasiṣṭha's Suicide Attempts and the Birth of Hope
beats.summary: The sage Vasiṣṭha, grieving the loss of his sons, attempts suicide by drowning but is saved by rivers. He then hears his grandson reciting the Vedas, which gives him a reason to live. He returns and encounters King Kalmāṣapāda possessed by a rākṣasa, who threatens to eat him and his daughter-in-law Adr̥śyantī.

beats.title: The Liberation of Kalmāṣapāda and the Birth of Aśmaka
beats.summary: Vasiṣṭha reassures his daughter-in-law that the approaching figure is not a demon but King Kalmāṣapāda, who had been possessed by a rākṣasa. Vasiṣṭha frees the king from the demon's grip. The grateful king asks Vasiṣṭha for a boon to obtain a worthy queen for the Ikṣvāku lineage. Vasiṣṭha agrees and accompanies the king to Ayodhyā, where he begets a son, Aśmaka, with the queen after a long gestation.

beats.title: The Birth of Parāśara and the Wrath of the Bhr̥gus
beats.summary: The Gandharva narrates the story of Parāśara's birth and his near-destruction of the world, which was averted by Vasiṣṭha. The story includes the persecution of the Bhr̥gus by Kṣatriyas and the miraculous birth of Parāśara from his mother's thigh, blinding the attackers.

beats.title: The Birth of Aurva and the Pacification of His Wrath
beats.summary: The Brahmana woman (wife of Bhrigu) explains to the Kshatriya kings that she did not take their eyesight; rather, her son Aurva, born from her thigh, blinded them due to anger over the killing of his ancestors. She advises them to seek his forgiveness. Aurva, appeased by their prostration, restores their sight. Later, Aurva contemplates destroying the entire world to avenge the Bhrigus, but his ancestors (Pitris) intervene, explaining that the Kshatriyas' violence was accepted by the Bhrigus themselves due to their desire for death and that Aurva should control his anger.

beats.title: Aurva's Anger and the Submarine Fire
beats.summary: Aurva recounts his anger at the massacre of the Bhrigus by Kshatriyas and his vow to destroy the world. His ancestors (Pitrs) advise him to release his anger-fire into the ocean, where it becomes the submarine fire (Vadavagni) that drinks the waters. Vasishtha concludes by advising Parashara not to destroy the worlds.

beats.title: Parāśara's Rākṣasa Sacrifice
beats.summary: The Gandharva narrates how the sage Parāśara, enraged by the killing of his father Śakti by a rākṣasa, performed a sacrifice to destroy all rākṣasas. The sages Vasiṣṭha, Atri, Pulastya, Pulaha, and Kratu intervened, and Pulastya convinced Parāśara to stop the sacrifice. Parāśara then cast the sacrificial fire into the northern Himalayas, where it still burns, consuming rākṣasas, trees, and stones.

beats.title: The Story of Kalmashapada and Vasistha
beats.summary: Arjuna asks the Gandharva why King Kalmashapada, despite knowing dharma, caused his wife to be approached by Vasistha. The Gandharva narrates the story: Kalmashapada, cursed by Sakti to become a cannibal, in a fit of hunger killed a Brahmin who was with his wife. The Brahmin's wife cursed him to die upon approaching his own wife at the proper time, but also prophesied that his wife would bear a son through Vasistha. Later, freed from the curse, Kalmashapada forgot it and approached his wife Madayanti, but remembering the curse, he refrained and instead arranged for Vasistha to beget a son on her.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Appoint Dhaumya as Their Priest
beats.summary: Arjuna asks the Gandharva for a suitable priest. The Gandharva recommends Dhaumya, the younger brother of Devala, who is performing penance at the Utkocaka tirtha. Arjuna gives the Gandharva the Agneya weapon as a gift. The Pāṇḍavas go to Dhaumya's hermitage and appoint him as their priest. Dhaumya accepts them with offerings. The Pāṇḍavas, with Dhaumya as their guide, then proceed to the Pañcāla kingdom for Draupadī's svayaṃvara.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Journey to Draupadī's Svayaṁvara
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, accompanied by their mother, set out to see Draupadī's svayaṁvara. On the way, they meet brāhmaṇas who inform them about the grand event at Drupada's court, describing Draupadī's beauty and the assembly of kings. Yudhiṣṭhira decides to go.

beats.title: The Svayaṁvara of Draupadī
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, having been sent away by King Drupada, travel to the Pāñcāla kingdom. On the way, they meet the sage Vyāsa, who comforts them. They eventually reach the city of the Pāñcālas and stay in a potter's house, living as brahmins. King Drupada, desiring to give his daughter Kṛṣṇā to Arjuna, arranges a svayaṁvara with a difficult test: stringing a bow and shooting an arrow through a rotating target. Many kings and sages gather for the event. On the sixteenth day, Draupadī enters the arena, and after a ritual fire ceremony, Dhṛṣṭadyumna announces the challenge.

beats.title: Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna's Enumeration of Warriors
beats.summary: Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna enumerates the vast array of warriors assembled on the Kaurava side, including many kings and heroes, all gathered for the sake of Duryodhana. He then addresses Draupadi, stating that these warriors will be targets for her to choose from in the svayamvara.

beats.title: Draupadi's Svayamvara
beats.summary: The chapter describes the svayamvara of Draupadi, where many kings and princes gather to win her hand by stringing a bow and shooting a target. After many fail, Arjuna succeeds, and the Pāṇḍavas are recognized. The assembly includes gods, sages, and celestial beings.

beats.title: Arjuna Wins Draupadi
beats.summary: Arjuna successfully strings the bow and shoots the target at Draupadi's svayamvara, winning her hand. The chapter describes the reactions of the assembled kings and brahmins, the divine signs, and Draupadi garlanding Arjuna.

beats.title: The Attack on Drupada and the Pāṇḍavas' Intervention
beats.summary: King Drupada, about to give his daughter Draupadi in marriage to a Brahmana (Arjuna in disguise), is attacked by enraged kings who feel insulted. Bhima uproots a tree to defend them, and Krishna identifies the Pāṇḍavas to Balarāma.

beats.title: The Brahmin Disguise and the Battle at the Arena
beats.summary: Arjuna and Bhima, disguised as brahmins, defend a sacrificial arena against attacking Kshatriyas led by Karna and Shalya. Arjuna fights Karna, who retreats upon recognizing Arjuna's divine prowess. Bhima defeats Shalya but spares him. Krishna intervenes to stop the battle. The Pandavas return to their mother Kunti, who had feared for their safety.

beats.title: The Marriage of Draupadi to the Five Pandavas
beats.summary: After winning Draupadi in the swayamvara, the Pandavas return home with her. Kunti, without seeing them, tells them to share whatever they have brought. Realizing her mistake, she is distressed. Yudhishthira, recalling Vyasa's prophecy that Draupadi would be the common wife of all five brothers, decides to marry her to all of them. Arjuna objects, but Yudhishthira insists, and the brothers accept.

beats.title: Krishna's Visit to the Pandavas in Hiding
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama visit the Pandavas in hiding after the lac-house fire. Krishna reassures them and advises continued secrecy.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Arrival at Drupada's Palace
beats.summary: After the Pāṇḍavas arrive at Drupada's palace, they go out to beg and bring back food. Kuntī instructs Draupadī to share the food among the brothers and herself, with a larger portion for Bhīma. They sleep on the ground with their heads to the east. Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna observes them and reports to Drupada, who anxiously inquires about Draupadī's suitor and the Pāṇḍavas' identity.

beats.title: The Report of the Svayamvara
beats.summary: Dhrishtadyumna reports to King Drupada about the successful completion of Draupadi's svayamvara, describing how Arjuna won her by stringing the bow and hitting the target. The Pandavas, disguised as brahmins, are living in a potter's house. Drupada sends his priest to invite them to the palace, and Yudhishthira responds, affirming that Draupadi was won fairly according to the conditions set by Drupada.

beats.title: The Pandavas Arrive at Drupada's Palace
beats.summary: The messenger announces that King Drupada has prepared food and a wedding ceremony, and invites the Pandavas to come. The Pandavas, along with Kunti and Krishna, travel to Drupada's palace. Dharmaraja Yudhishthira, out of curiosity, gathers various items including fruits, garlands, hides, armor, cattle, and tools. The palace is filled with luxurious items and weapons. Kunti enters the inner apartments with Krishna, where the women honor her. The Pandavas, looking like lions, are welcomed by King Drupada and his ministers. They are seated on high seats and served food on golden and silver plates.
