After eating, they examine the weapons and enter the armory. Drupada and his son Dhrishtadyumna honor the Pandavas.

beats.title: Drupada's Inquiry and the Marriage Agreement
beats.summary: King Drupada, after the svayamvara, questions Yudhishthira about the identity of the Pandavas. Yudhishthira reveals they are Kshatriyas, sons of Pandu. Drupada, overjoyed, offers his daughter Draupadi in marriage. Yudhishthira insists that Draupadi will be the common wife of all five brothers, citing their mother's command and their prior agreement. Drupada initially objects on grounds of dharma, but Yudhishthira defends the decision. They decide to consult further, and Vyasa arrives.

beats.title: The Debate on Draupadi's Marriage
beats.summary: King Drupada and others question the propriety of Draupadi marrying five husbands. Yudhishthira defends it citing the precedent of the sage Gautami who lived with seven sages, and his mother Kunti supports him. Vyasa confirms the righteousness of the polyandrous marriage and takes Drupada aside to explain the divine plan.

beats.title: The Origin of the Pandavas and Draupadi
beats.summary: Vyasa narrates the origin of the Pandavas and Draupadi. The gods, fearing human immortality due to Yama's absence during a sacrifice, approach Brahma. Brahma reveals that Yama's body will become death for humans. Later, the gods see a lotus in the Ganga, from which a weeping woman emerges. Indra follows her and meets a young man playing dice on a mountain. Indra claims lordship, but the man (Shiva) paralyzes him. Shiva reveals that the previous Indras are confined in a cave and must be born as humans. Shiva grants that Indra's son (Arjuna) will be born, and the previous Indras become the Pandavas. Vishnu plucks two hairs, which become Balarama and Krishna. Draupadi is the incarnation of Lakshmi and the goddess who desired five husbands. Vyasa gives Dhritarashtra divine sight to see the Pandavas' true forms.

beats.title: Marriage of Draupadi
beats.summary: Drupada, having heard the sage's words, reflects on destiny and the marriage of Draupadi to the five Pandavas. The wedding ceremony is performed by Dhaumya, and Drupada gives immense wealth as dowry. The Pandavas enjoy their new fortune in the city of the Panchala king.

beats.title: Kunti's Blessings and Krishna's Gifts
beats.summary: After the Pandavas' marriage to Draupadi, Kunti blesses her daughter-in-law with auspicious words, comparing her to divine consorts. Krishna sends lavish gifts to the Pandavas, which Yudhishthira accepts joyfully.

beats.title: The Return of the Pandavas and the Kaurava Reaction
beats.summary: After Draupadi's svayamvara, spies report to the Kauravas that the Pandavas are alive and have married Draupadi. Duryodhana and his allies return to Hastinapura in dismay. Vidura informs Dhritarashtra, who initially rejoices, but Duryodhana and Karna express their enmity and plot to destroy the Pandavas.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Counsel of Division
beats.summary: Duryodhana proposes various schemes to weaken the Pandavas, including bribing their allies, sowing discord among them, assassinating Bhima, and using Draupadi to create division. He urges immediate action before the Pandavas gain strength.

beats.title: Karna's Counsel for War
beats.summary: Karna advises Duryodhana that the Pandavas cannot be subdued by peaceful means, alliances, or bribery, but only by force. He urges immediate attack before the Pandavas gather more allies. Dhritarashtra praises Karna and then consults Bhishma, Drona, Vidura, and Karna for a plan.

beats.title: Bhishma's Plea for Peace
beats.summary: Bhishma advises Dhritarashtra and Duryodhana against war with the Pandavas, advocating for a peaceful resolution by giving half the kingdom. He emphasizes the importance of dharma, kirti (fame), and the rightful claim of the Pandavas.

beats.title: Droṇa's Peace Counsel and Karṇa's Rebuttal
beats.summary: Droṇa advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to make peace with the Pāṇḍavas by sending gifts and a conciliatory message to Drupada, and to invite the Pāṇḍavas back to share the kingdom. Karṇa objects, arguing that fate determines outcomes and that Droṇa's advice is biased. Droṇa warns that ignoring his counsel will lead to the destruction of the Kurus.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura advises King Dhritarashtra that Bhishma and Drona have spoken for his welfare, but Duryodhana and Karna reject their counsel. He warns that the Pandavas are invincible due to their allies (Krishna, Satyaki, Drupada, etc.) and urges peace through negotiation rather than war, blaming Duryodhana's folly for the impending destruction.

beats.title: Vidura's Mission to the Pandavas
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura to bring the Pandavas and Draupadi to Hastinapura after the lac house incident. Vidura goes to Drupada's court, conveys greetings from the Kauravas, and requests Drupada to allow the Pandavas to return. Drupada agrees.

beats.title: The Pandavas Establish Indraprastha
beats.summary: Drupada welcomes the Pandavas and Krishna, and after consultation, they return to Hastinapura. Dhritarashtra grants them half the kingdom, and they establish Indraprastha, a magnificent city. Krishna and Rama then depart for Dvaraka.

beats.title: Nārada's Warning and the Story of Sunda and Upasunda
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaiśampāyana about the Pāṇḍavas' life after gaining the kingdom of Indraprastha, especially how they shared Draupadī without conflict. Vaiśampāyana narrates that the Pāṇḍavas ruled righteously under Yudhiṣṭhira. The sage Nārada visits them and, after being honored, warns them about the danger of discord over a shared wife, citing the story of the asura brothers Sunda and Upasunda who killed each other over the apsarā Tilottamā. Yudhiṣṭhira then asks Nārada to narrate that story in detail.

beats.title: The Tale of Sunda and Upasunda
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of the demon brothers Sunda and Upasunda, who performed severe penance to gain power, received a boon from Brahma that they could only be killed by each other, and then ruled the world until they were tricked into fighting and killing each other over the celestial dancer Tilottama.

beats.title: The Reign of Sunda and Upasunda
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of the asura brothers Sunda and Upasunda, who, after receiving a boon from Brahma, conquer the three worlds, terrorize sages and kings, disrupt sacrifices, and eventually establish themselves in Kurukshetra.

beats.title: The Creation of Tilottama
beats.summary: Narada narrates how the gods and sages, distressed by the havoc caused by the demons Sunda and Upasunda, approach Brahma. Brahma instructs Vishvakarma to create a beautiful woman, Tilottama, to sow discord between the brothers. Tilottama circumambulates the assembly, causing even Shiva and Indra to manifest multiple faces and eyes to gaze upon her, while Brahma remains unaffected. She then goes to seduce Sunda and Upasunda.

beats.title: The Story of Sunda and Upasunda
beats.summary: Narada tells the Pandavas the story of the asura brothers Sunda and Upasunda, who were inseparable until they both desired the apsara Tilottama and killed each other. He warns them against a similar fate over Draupadi, leading the Pandavas to agree that no one will disturb Draupadi when she is with another brother, under penalty of exile.

beats.title: Arjuna's Vow and Exile
beats.summary: Arjuna, hearing a Brahmana's cows stolen, pursues the thieves, recovers the cows, and returns. He then reminds his brother Yudhishthira of their pact that if one enters the king's private quarters without permission, they must go on a twelve-year forest exile. Despite Yudhishthira's attempts to absolve him, Arjuna insists on keeping his vow and departs for the forest.

beats.title: Arjuna's Encounter with Ulupi
beats.summary: Arjuna, while on pilgrimage, is pulled underwater by Ulupi, the daughter of the Naga king Kauravya. She confesses her love and asks him to marry her. Arjuna initially refuses due to his vow of celibacy, but Ulupi argues that helping a distressed person is a higher dharma. Arjuna consents, spends the night with her, and then continues his journey.

beats.title: Arjuna's Marriage to Citrāṅgadā
beats.summary: Arjuna, after completing his pilgrimage, arrives in the kingdom of Maṇalūra and marries Citrāṅgadā, the daughter of King Citravāhana, under the condition that any son born will be the heir of the king.

beats.title: Arjuna and the Crocodile Woman
beats.summary: Arjuna visits the southern seashore tirthas, finds five tirthas avoided by ascetics due to crocodiles, dives into the Saubhadra tirtha, fights a crocodile, and rescues a woman who was cursed to be a crocodile for 100 years for disturbing a sage's penance.

beats.title: The Liberation of the Apsaras
beats.summary: A group of celestial nymphs (apsaras) cursed by a sage to become crocodiles in a river are rescued by Arjuna. The sage's curse is partially lifted: they will regain their original forms when a man pulls them from the water. After meeting sage Narada, they learn that Arjuna will be their liberator. Arjuna indeed rescues them, and they regain their celestial forms. The chapter also mentions Arjuna's son Babhruvahana with Citrangada.

beats.title: Arjuna's Visit to Dvaraka
beats.summary: Arjuna, after visiting various holy places, arrives at Prabhasa where he meets Krishna. They spend time together, then proceed to Raivataka mountain. Krishna's servants prepare a feast and entertainment. Arjuna sleeps, is awakened by music and praise, and then travels to Dvaraka where he is warmly welcomed by the Vrishnis and Andhakas. He stays with Krishna for many nights.

beats.title: Arjuna's Love for Subhadra
beats.summary: At a festival on Mount Raivataka, the Vrishnis and Andhakas celebrate. Arjuna and Krishna see Subhadra, Vasudeva's daughter, and Arjuna falls in love. Krishna suggests Arjuna abduct her, as it is a Kshatriya custom. They send word to Yudhishthira, who consents.

beats.title: The Abduction of Subhadra
beats.summary: Arjuna, with Krishna's permission, abducts Subhadra from Raivataka mountain. The Vrishnis and Andhakas, angered, prepare for battle, but Baladeva calms them, reminding them of Krishna's will. They then accept the marriage.

beats.title: The Marriage of Subhadra and Birth of Abhimanyu and Draupadi's Sons
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) addresses the Vrishnis about Arjuna's abduction of Subhadra, justifying it as a noble act. He advises them to pacify Arjuna rather than fight. The Vrishnis agree, and Arjuna marries Subhadra. After twelve years, Arjuna returns to Indraprastha with Subhadra. Draupadi initially feels jealous but is reassured. Krishna and the Vrishnis visit Indraprastha with gifts. Subhadra gives birth to Abhimanyu, who is trained by Arjuna. Draupadi also bears five sons, one from each Pandava.

beats.title: The Peaceful Reign and the Yamuna Outing
beats.summary: This chapter describes the peaceful and prosperous rule of Yudhishthira in Indraprastha, followed by a leisure outing of Krishna and Arjuna to the Yamuna riverbank. They enjoy various amusements with their retinue, and later a radiant sage approaches them.

beats.title: Agni's Request to Burn Khandava
beats.summary: Agni, disguised as a Brahmana, asks Arjuna and Krishna for food, revealing he wants to burn the Khandava forest. He explains that Indra protects it, especially for his friend Takshaka. Arjuna agrees to help but requests divine weapons, a bow, chariot, and horses. Krishna also needs a suitable weapon. Agni promises to provide them.

beats.title: The Burning of the Khandava Forest
beats.summary: Agni, disguised as a brahmin, asks Arjuna and Krishna for help in burning the Khandava forest. Varuna provides Arjuna with the Gandiva bow, two inexhaustible quivers, and a divine chariot with a monkey banner, and gives Krishna the Kaumodaki mace and the Sudarshana chakra. Agni then burns the forest, with Arjuna and Krishna protecting him from Indra and other gods.

beats.title: The Burning of the Khāṇḍava Forest
beats.summary: The Khāṇḍava forest is set ablaze by Agni with the help of Arjuna and Kṛṣṇa. Creatures flee in terror, many are killed. The gods, led by Indra, try to extinguish the fire but fail. The chapter describes the devastation and the helplessness of the beings.

beats.title: The Burning of the Khāṇḍava Forest
beats.summary: Arjuna and Krishna protect the Khāṇḍava forest from Indra and the gods, who attack with various weapons. Arjuna uses his archery to counter the divine assaults, while Krishna wields his discus. The gods are eventually repelled, and Indra, impressed by Arjuna's valor, becomes pleased. The forest is burned by Agni, with many creatures perishing.

beats.title: The Burning of the Khāṇḍava Forest
beats.summary: The Khāṇḍava forest is set ablaze by Agni with the help of Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna. They defeat all beings attempting to escape, including dānavas, rākṣasas, nāgas, and others. Indra and the gods try to intervene but are repelled. A celestial voice reveals that Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna are the ancient sages Nara and Nārāyaṇa, invincible in battle. Indra withdraws, and the burning continues. Maya, a dānava, is spared by Arjuna's protection.

beats.title: The Story of Mandapāla and the Śārṅgaka Birds
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks why the fire god Agni did not burn the Śārṅgaka birds during the Khāṇḍava forest fire. Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates the story of the sage Mandapāla, who after attaining tapas, was told by the gods that he needed offspring to enjoy the fruits of his penance. He then became a Śārṅgaka bird and fathered four sons with the bird Jaritā. Later, when Agni came to burn the Khāṇḍava forest, Mandapāla praised Agni and requested him to spare his sons. Agni granted the boon.

beats.title: The Śārṅgaka Birds and the Forest Fire
beats.summary: Jaritā, a female bird, laments as a forest fire approaches, unable to save her fledgling sons. Her sons, the Śārṅgaka birds, urge her to flee and preserve the lineage, arguing that their father's ascetic penance should not be in vain. Jaritā suggests hiding in a mouse hole, but the sons fear being eaten by the mouse and prefer death by fire. They choose cremation over being devoured, upholding their family honor.

beats.title: Jaritā and the Śārṅgakas
beats.summary: Jaritā, a female bird, tells her children (the Śārṅgakas) that a hawk took a mouse from their burrow, but they doubt her. They argue that uncertainty is worse than death and refuse to re-enter the burrow. Jaritā leaves them in the Khāṇḍava forest. Then Agni, the fire god, approaches the young birds. Jaritāri, one of the chicks, addresses the fire.

beats.title: The Forest Fire and the Hymns to Agni
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the sage Jaritāri and his sons (Sārisr̥kva, Stambamitra, and Droṇa) who are trapped in a forest fire. They praise Agni, the fire god, seeking protection. Agni, moved by their hymns, spares them and instead burns the Khāṇḍava forest.

beats.title: Mandapala's Lament and Reconciliation
beats.summary: Mandapala, a sage, worries about his sons trapped in a forest fire. His wife Lapita rebukes him for abandoning her and their children. After the fire passes, Jarita (another wife) finds their sons safe. Mandapala returns but his sons do not welcome him. Jarita criticizes him for his past abandonment. Mandapala defends himself, citing the untrustworthiness of women, and eventually reconciles with his sons.

beats.title: The Burning of Khandava Forest and Boons from Indra
beats.summary: Mandapala explains to his sons why he delayed coming to them, then departs with his wife. Agni, with Krishna and Arjuna, burns the Khandava forest, consuming animals and birds. Indra descends, pleased, and offers boons. Arjuna asks for all weapons, but Indra says he will receive them when Mahadeva is pleased. Vasudeva (Krishna) gains eternal friendship with Arjuna. Indra returns to heaven. Agni, satiated, allows Arjuna, Krishna, and Maya to depart. They sit by a riverbank.

beats.title: Maya Builds the Assembly Hall
beats.summary: Maya, the demon architect, thanks Arjuna for saving him from Krishna and Agni, and offers to build a grand assembly hall for Yudhishthira. Krishna suggests a divine hall, and Maya constructs a magnificent sabha with celestial features.

beats.title: Krishna's Departure from Khāṇḍavaprastha
beats.summary: Krishna, after a pleasant stay in Khāṇḍavaprastha with the Pāṇḍavas, decides to visit his father in Dvārakā. He takes leave of Yudhiṣṭhira, Kuntī, Subhadrā, Draupadī, and Dhaumya, and departs with honors. The Pāṇḍavas accompany him partway, and Krishna returns to Dvārakā.

beats.title: Maya Builds the Sabha
beats.summary: Maya the Asura, after being saved by Arjuna, promises to build a magnificent assembly hall for Yudhishthira. He travels to Bindusara lake, retrieves a divine mace for Bhima and a conch for Arjuna, and constructs an unparalleled sabha adorned with gems, a lotus pond, and gardens. The hall is guarded by eight thousand Rakshasas called Kinkaras.

beats.title: Consecration of the Assembly Hall
beats.summary: King Yudhiṣṭhira performs the consecration of his new assembly hall, hosting a grand feast for brahmins and distributing gifts. The hall is then adorned by the presence of numerous sages, kings, and warriors, including Vr̥ṣṇi heroes and Gandharvas, who entertain the assembly. The chapter describes the grandeur and the gathering of distinguished beings around Yudhiṣṭhira.

beats.title: Narada's Instructions on Kingly Duties
beats.summary: The sage Narada visits Yudhishthira's court and asks a series of questions about the king's duties, governance, and personal conduct, covering topics such as dharma, artha, kama, statecraft, military, justice, and welfare. Yudhishthira listens respectfully and vows to follow the advice.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Inquiry about Divine Assemblies
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, after being advised by the sage, asks Narada about the finest assemblies (sabhas) he has seen. Narada describes the divine assemblies of Yama, Varuna, Indra, Kubera, and Brahma, and offers to narrate them. Yudhishthira eagerly requests to hear about these assemblies.

beats.title: Description of Indra's Assembly Hall
beats.summary: Narada describes the divine assembly hall of Indra (Śakra), detailing its celestial features, the deities, sages, and beings who attend, and the activities that take place there.

beats.title: Parva 2, Chapter 8
beats.summary: Narada describes to Yudhishthira the divine assembly hall of Yama, the god of death, built by Vishvakarma. It is a splendid, comfortable hall where many righteous kings, sages, and beings gather to worship Yama. The hall is accessible only to those who have performed great austerities and virtuous deeds.

beats.title: Description of Varuna's Assembly Hall
beats.summary: Narada describes to Yudhishthira the divine assembly hall of Varuna, located in the waters, built by Vishvakarma with celestial trees, adorned with precious stones, and attended by various beings including Nagas, Daityas, Danavas, rivers, mountains, and celestial musicians, all worshipping Varuna.

beats.title: Description of Kubera's Assembly Hall
beats.summary: Narada describes to Dhritarashtra the magnificent celestial assembly hall of Kubera (Vaisravana), located on Mount Kailasa, adorned with divine features, attended by various beings including gods, gandharvas, apsaras, yakshas, and even Shiva. He then mentions that he will also describe the assembly hall of Brahma.

beats.title: Narada's Description of Brahma's Assembly and the Story of Harishchandra
beats.summary: Narada describes the celestial assembly hall of Brahma (Pitamaha Sabha) to Yudhishthira, detailing its indescribable beauty and the beings who attend. He then recounts the story of King Harishchandra, who performed the Rajasuya sacrifice and attained glory. Narada advises Yudhishthira to perform the Rajasuya, warning of its difficulties, and departs for Dvaraka.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Resolution to Perform the Rājasūya
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira, inspired by the accounts of great kings like Hariścandra, desires to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. He consults his ministers, brothers, and ṛtvijas, who encourage him. After deep deliberation, he sends a messenger to Kṛṣṇa, who comes to Indraprastha. Yudhiṣṭhira then asks Kṛṣṇa for his unbiased advice on whether he is worthy and capable of performing the Rājasūya.

beats.title: Krishna's Counsel on the Rajasuya and the Threat of Jarasandha
beats.summary: Krishna explains to Yudhishthira the political landscape of the time, focusing on the threat posed by Jarasandha, king of Magadha. He describes how many kings have been subjugated by Jarasandha and are imprisoned in Girivraja. Krishna recounts the history of the Yadava clan, their conflicts with Jarasandha, and their eventual migration to Dvaraka. He advises Yudhishthira that to perform the Rajasuya sacrifice, he must first defeat Jarasandha and free the captive kings.

beats.title: The Counsel on Conquest
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Krishna about the nature of kingship and conquest. Bhima advocates for aggressive action against Jarasandha. Krishna explains the history of Jarasandha's tyranny and proposes a plan to defeat him, which would establish Yudhishthira as emperor.

beats.title: Arjuna's Counsel on the Rājasūya
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses hesitation about the Rājasūya sacrifice due to the threat of Jarāsaṁdha. Arjuna encourages him, emphasizing the importance of valor over other qualities, and proposes to kill Jarāsaṁdha to free the kings and enable the sacrifice.

beats.title: The Birth of Jarasandha
beats.summary: Krishna narrates the story of Jarasandha's birth to Yudhishthira. King Brihadratha of Magadha, childless despite two wives, receives a mango fruit from the sage Chandakaushika. He divides it between his wives, who give birth to two half-bodies. A rakshasi named Jara joins the halves, forming a single child. She returns the child to the king, who names him Jarasandha.

beats.title: The Birth and Rise of Jarasandha
beats.summary: The demoness Jara explains how she joined two halves of a baby, creating Jarasandha. King Brihadratha names him and later, after a sage's prophecy, abdicates to become a hermit. Jarasandha grows powerful and subjugates many kings. The chapter ends noting that the Vrishnis tolerated him for political reasons.

beats.title: The Plan to Kill Jarasandha
beats.summary: Krishna proposes to Yudhishthira that Jarasandha must be killed for the Rajasuya sacrifice. He outlines a plan: Bhima will fight Jarasandha in single combat, with Krishna and Arjuna as support. Yudhishthira agrees, and the three heroes journey to Magadha, crossing various rivers and regions, eventually reaching the city of Magadha.

beats.title: The Entry into Magadha
beats.summary: Krishna describes the five mountains protecting Girivraja, the capital of Magadha, and recounts the history of the region. He, Bhima, and Arjuna enter the city disguised as brahmins to confront King Jarasandha. They break the mountain peak, enter through a non-gate, and are received by Jarasandha, who questions their disguise. Krishna explains the kshatriya dharma of strength over speech, and they refuse his hospitality as they come as enemies.

beats.title: Jarasandha's Confrontation
beats.summary: Jarasandha confronts Krishna, Bhima, and Arjuna, questioning their enmity. Krishna accuses Jarasandha of imprisoning kings for a human sacrifice to Rudra, violating dharma. Krishna declares that kṣatriyas attain heaven through battle, and challenges Jarasandha to fight or release the kings. Jarasandha refuses, asserting his right to rule and fight. The chapter ends with Jarasandha ordering his army and recalling generals Hamsa and Dimbhaka, while Krishna remembers that Jarasandha is destined to be killed by Bhima.

beats.title: Bhima Kills Jarasandha
beats.summary: Jarasandha chooses to fight Bhima in single combat. The duel lasts for days, with both warriors evenly matched. On the fourteenth night, Jarasandha becomes exhausted. Krishna advises Bhima not to press the attack but to fight fairly, and Bhima then exploits a weakness to kill Jarasandha.

beats.title: The Slaying of Jarasandha
beats.summary: Krishna, Bhima, and Arjuna travel to Girivraja to kill Jarasandha. Bhima wrestles Jarasandha to death. Krishna releases imprisoned kings, who pledge support for Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice. Krishna installs Jarasandha's son Sahadeva as king and returns to Indraprastha with tribute.

beats.title: Arjuna's Conquest of the Northern and Eastern Kingdoms
beats.summary: Arjuna, having obtained divine weapons and a celestial chariot, proposes to conquer kingdoms to amass wealth for Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice. Yudhishthira blesses him. Arjuna, Bhima, Nakula, Sahadeva, and their armies set out to conquer the four directions. Janamejaya asks for details. Vaisampayana narrates Arjuna's conquest: first the Kuninda kingdom, then Anarta, Kalakuta, and others, aided by Sumandala. He fights a great battle with Bhagadatta of Pragjyotisha, who after eight days surrenders and agrees to pay tribute to Yudhishthira.

beats.title: Arjuna's Northern Conquest
beats.summary: Arjuna's northern conquest: He defeats various kings and tribes including Br̥hanta, Senāvindu, Paurava, and others, collecting tribute and establishing dominance. The chapter ends with him camping near Śvetaparvata.

beats.title: Arjuna's Northern Conquest
beats.summary: Arjuna conquers the northern direction, defeating various kingdoms and collecting tribute for Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice. He encounters the guardians of the northern regions who advise him not to enter the land of the Uttara Kurus, as it is beyond human reach. They offer him tribute instead, and Arjuna accepts, returning to Indraprastha.

beats.title: Bhima's Eastern Campaign
beats.summary: Bhima, with permission from Yudhishthira, leads a military campaign to the eastern direction, conquering various kingdoms including Panchala, Gandaki, Videha, Dasharna, and Chedi. He spares Shishupala after receiving tribute, and returns to Indraprastha.

beats.title: Bhima's Eastern Conquests
beats.summary: Bhima's eastern conquests for the Rajasuya sacrifice: he subdues many kings and regions, including Kosala, Malla, Kashi, Matsya, Magadha, and finally defeats Karna and the Pauṇḍra king, collecting vast tribute for Yudhishthira.

beats.title: Sahadeva's Southern Campaign
beats.summary: Sahadeva's southern military campaign: he conquers various kingdoms, faces a crisis when Agni attacks his army due to a past curse on Mahishmati, pacifies Agni through devotion, receives submission from King Nila, and continues to subdue many southern and island kings, including sending envoys to Vibhishana, before returning to Yudhishthira.

beats.title: Nakula's Western Conquest
beats.summary: Nakula's conquest of the western direction, subduing various kingdoms and tribes, collecting tribute, and returning to Indraprastha.

beats.title: Preparation for the Rajasuya Sacrifice
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, under the rule of Dharma, ensures prosperity and justice. He decides to perform the Rajasuya sacrifice. Krishna visits, approves, and assists. Preparations begin: Sahadeva is appointed, Vyasa and other priests are summoned, and the sacrificial ground is prepared. Invitations are sent to kings and brahmins. The sacrifice proceeds with grand donations. Nakula is sent to Hastinapura to invite Bhishma, Drona, Dhritarashtra, Vidura, Kripa, and others.

beats.title: The Arrival of Kings for the Rajasuya
beats.summary: King Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice attracts numerous kings and dignitaries from across the land, who are welcomed and housed in grand residences. The assembly hall shines with the presence of kings and brahmanas.

beats.title: The Rājasūya Sacrifice: Assignments and Gathering
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira welcomes the elders and assigns duties for his Rājasūya sacrifice. Kings and sages gather, bringing gifts. The assembly hall is splendid, and Yudhiṣṭhira performs the sacrifice with great generosity.

beats.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Honor to Krishna
beats.summary: On the day of Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice, sages and kings gather. Narada observes the assembly and recalls the descent of the gods into the Kshatriya race, particularly Krishna as Narayana. Bhishma proposes offering the first honor to Krishna. Sahadeva presents the arghya to Krishna, but Shishupala objects angrily.

beats.title: Śiśupāla's Objection at the Rājasūya
beats.summary: Śiśupāla objects to Kṛṣṇa being honored first at Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya sacrifice, arguing that Kṛṣṇa is not a king, elder, teacher, or priest, and that the honor is misplaced. He accuses Bhīṣma and the Pāṇḍavas of acting out of partiality and fear, and leaves the assembly in anger.

beats.title: Bhishma's Defense of Krishna's Worship
beats.summary: Bhishma defends Krishna's preeminence at the Rajasuya sacrifice, arguing that Krishna deserves the first worship due to his divine nature and deeds, despite Shishupala's objections. Yudhishthira tries to pacify Shishupala, but Bhishma insists on Krishna's superiority.

beats.title: Sahadeva's Declaration and the Challenge of Sunitha
beats.summary: Sahadeva, after Bhishma's speech, declares that he has placed his foot on the heads of all mighty kings who cannot tolerate Krishna being honored. He challenges anyone to respond. A flower shower and celestial voices applaud him. Narada appears, and the kings, led by Sunitha, express anger at Yudhishthira's coronation and Krishna's worship. Sunitha declares himself commander and challenges the Vrishnis and Pandavas to battle.

beats.title: Bhīṣma's Counsel to Yudhiṣṭhira
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira, seeing the assembled kings agitated, seeks Bhīṣma's counsel on how to proceed without hindrance to the sacrifice and for the welfare of all. Bhīṣma reassures him, comparing the kings to dogs barking at a sleeping lion (Kṛṣṇa), and explains that Śiśupāla's foolishness will lead to his own destruction, as Kṛṣṇa is the origin and end of all beings.

beats.title: Śiśupāla's Rebuke of Bhīṣma
beats.summary: Śiśupāla rebukes Bhīṣma for praising Kṛṣṇa, accusing him of hypocrisy and recounting his past misdeeds, including the abduction of Ambā. He compares Bhīṣma to a hypocritical swan that preaches dharma but eats eggs, and warns that the kings will kill him.

beats.title: Śiśupāla's Rebuke and Bhīmasena's Anger
beats.summary: Śiśupāla criticizes Kṛṣṇa for the killing of Jarāsaṁdha, accusing him of deceit and impropriety. Bhīmasena becomes enraged, but Bhīṣma calms him. Śiśupāla remains unafraid and challenges Bhīmasena.

beats.title: The Birth and Prophecy of Shishupala
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Shishupala's birth, his unusual features (three eyes, four arms), the prophecy of his death at the hands of Krishna, and Krishna's promise to forgive a hundred offenses. The chapter sets up the enmity between Shishupala and Krishna.

beats.title: Bhishma's Defense of Krishna and Shishupala's Insults
beats.summary: Bhishma responds to Shishupala's insults, defending his praise of Krishna and asserting Krishna's divinity. Shishupala mocks Bhishma's constant glorification of Krishna, comparing him to a foolish bird. Bhishma remains steadfast, offering to be killed or burned, and challenges anyone who disputes Krishna's supremacy to face Krishna in battle.

beats.title: The Slaying of Shishupala and Conclusion of the Rajasuya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the events following the Rajasuya sacrifice of Yudhishthira. Shishupala, king of Chedi, insults Krishna and is slain by Krishna's discus. His soul merges into Krishna. The kings present react with shock or praise. Yudhishthira installs Shishupala's son on the Chedi throne. The sacrifice concludes successfully, protected by Krishna. The assembled kings depart, and Krishna takes leave of Yudhishthira and Kunti, returning to Dvaraka. Duryodhana and Shakuni remain in the assembly hall.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Envy at the Rajasuya
beats.summary: Duryodhana, after visiting the Pandavas' court during the Rajasuya sacrifice, becomes consumed with envy and humiliation. He recounts to Shakuni how he was mocked by Bhima and others, and how he witnessed the wealth and power of Yudhishthira. Overwhelmed by jealousy, he contemplates suicide and blames fate for his misfortune.

beats.title: Shakuni's Counsel and the Dice Game Plot
beats.summary: Shakuni advises Duryodhana that the Pandavas cannot be defeated in battle due to their divine allies and weapons, but suggests challenging Yudhishthira to a dice game, where Shakuni's skill ensures victory. Duryodhana agrees and asks Shakuni to inform Dhritarashtra.

beats.title: The Conspiracy of the Dice Game
beats.summary: Duryodhana, consumed by envy after witnessing Yudhishthira's magnificent Rajasuya sacrifice, confides in his father Dhritarashtra. Shakuni proposes a dice game to seize the Pandavas' wealth, and despite Vidura's warnings, Dhritarashtra agrees, ordering the construction of a grand assembly hall for the game.

beats.title: The Origin of the Dice Game
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the origin of the disastrous dice game and who supported or opposed it. Vaishampayana recounts how Dhritarashtra, heeding Vidura's advice, tried to dissuade Duryodhana from gambling, but Duryodhana expressed his envy and humiliation at the prosperity of the Pandavas, describing incidents at the Rajasuya sacrifice where he felt insulted by Bhima, Krishna, and Draupadi, and his accidental fall into a water tank.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Envy at the Rajasuya
beats.summary: Duryodhana describes to Dhritarashtra the immense wealth and tribute brought by various kings and peoples to Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice, expressing envy and despair at the Pandavas' prosperity.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Envy at the Rajasuya
beats.summary: Duryodhana describes to Dhritarashtra the immense wealth and tribute brought by various kings and tribes to Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice, highlighting the prosperity and grandeur of the Pandava court, which fuels his envy and despair.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Envy at the Rajasuya
beats.summary: Duryodhana describes the grand Rajasuya sacrifice of Yudhishthira, detailing the offerings brought by various kings and the ritual performed by sages. He expresses his envy and despair at Yudhishthira's prosperity, comparing it to that of Harishchandra, and laments his own diminished state.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Counsel and Duryodhana's Defiance
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra advises Duryodhana against hostility towards the Pandavas, emphasizing contentment and dharma. Duryodhana rejects this, arguing that a king's duty is to seek victory and expansion, even through deceit, and that discontent is the root of prosperity. He resolves to either win the Pandavas' wealth or die in battle.

beats.title: The Dice Game Proposal
beats.summary: Shakuni proposes a dice game to win the Pandavas' kingdom, Duryodhana persuades Dhritarashtra, who reluctantly agrees despite Vidura's warnings, and orders the construction of a grand assembly hall for the game.

beats.title: The Invitation to the Dice Game
beats.summary: Vidura is sent by Dhritarashtra to invite Yudhishthira to a game of dice in Hastinapura. Yudhishthira, despite knowing the dangers of gambling, accepts the invitation due to his sense of duty and the binding nature of a challenge. He travels to Hastinapura with his brothers and Draupadi, is welcomed by the Kuru elders, and the next day enters the assembly hall where the gamblers are gathered.

beats.title: The Dice Game Begins
beats.summary: Shakuni challenges Yudhishthira to a dice game. Yudhishthira initially hesitates, citing the deceitful nature of gambling, but is goaded by Duryodhana and ultimately agrees. The game begins with Yudhishthira staking a precious jewel, and Shakuni wins the first throw.

beats.title: The Dice Game: Yudhiṣṭhira's Losses
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira repeatedly stakes and loses vast wealth—including gold, a chariot, elephants, slave girls, servants, chariots, horses, and treasures—to Śakuni in a dice game, each time Śakuni declares 'won' after Yudhiṣṭhira's offer.

beats.title: Vidura's Warning to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura warns Dhritarashtra about the dangers of Duryodhana's actions, comparing him to a jackal and a madman addicted to dice. He advises sacrificing Duryodhana for the welfare of the family and kingdom, citing examples from history and mythology. Vidura urges Dhritarashtra to treat the Pandavas with care and not to destroy them like a foolish man who killed birds for immediate gain.

beats.title: Vidura's Warning on the Dice Game
beats.summary: Vidura warns Dhritarashtra that Duryodhana's addiction to gambling will lead to destruction. He advises peace with the Pandavas and criticizes the game of dice as a source of conflict.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Rebuke and Vidura's Response
beats.summary: Duryodhana rebukes Vidura for praising the Pandavas and criticizing the Kauravas, accusing him of disloyalty. Vidura defends his counsel as truthful and beneficial, warning against flattery and urging Duryodhana to heed wise advice. The exchange highlights themes of loyalty, truth, and the consequences of ignoring beneficial counsel.

beats.title: The Dice Game: Staking Draupadi
beats.summary: In this chapter, Shakuni challenges Yudhishthira to a dice game. Yudhishthira stakes and loses his wealth, kingdom, brothers, and finally himself. Then, at Shakuni's instigation, he stakes and loses Draupadi, causing outrage among the elders.

beats.title: Vidura's Warning
beats.summary: Duryodhana orders Vidura to bring Draupadi to the assembly as a slave, but Vidura rebukes him, warning that his actions will lead to destruction. Vidura advises against harsh speech and enmity with the Pandavas, but Duryodhana ignores his counsel.

beats.title: Draupadī Humiliated in the Assembly
beats.summary: Duryodhana sends the suta Prātikāmī to fetch Draupadī to the assembly after winning her in the dice game. Draupadī questions the validity of the wager, but is dragged by her hair by Duḥśāsana. She appeals to the elders, but Bhīṣma and others remain silent. Bhīma becomes enraged.

beats.title: The Disrobing of Draupadi and the Vow of Bhima
beats.summary: Bhima expresses fury at Draupadi being staked and lost in the dice game. Arjuna counsels restraint, reminding Bhima of dharma and their elder brother's authority. Vikarna argues that Draupadi was not legitimately won, but Karna refutes him, ordering Duḥśāsana to bring her as a slave. Duḥśāsana attempts to disrobe Draupadi, but she is miraculously protected by divine intervention. Bhima vows to kill Duḥśāsana and drink his blood. Vidura then recounts the story of Prahlāda and Sudhanvan to emphasize the duty of truth in a sabhā.

beats.title: Draupadi's Humiliation and Bhima's Vow
beats.summary: Draupadi, dragged into the Kuru assembly by Duhshasana after Yudhishthira lost her in a dice game, questions the legality of her enslavement. Bhishma, Drona, and others remain silent or equivocate. Duryodhana taunts her, and Bhima vows to kill the Kauravas. The chapter highlights the moral crisis and the breakdown of dharma.

beats.title: Draupadi's Humiliation and Boons
beats.summary: In this chapter, after the dice game, Karna insults Draupadi, calling her a slave. Bhima vows to break Duryodhana's thigh. Vidura warns of danger. Dhritarashtra grants Draupadi two boons: freedom for Yudhishthira and the other Pandavas. Draupadi refuses a third boon, citing dharma.

beats.title: Karṇa's Praise of Draupadī and Bhīma's Anger
beats.summary: Karṇa praises Draupadī as the savior of the Pāṇḍavas in their time of crisis. Bhīmasena becomes furious upon hearing this, but is calmed by Arjuna and Yudhiṣṭhira, who then approaches Dhṛtarāṣṭra with folded hands.

beats.title: Dhr̥tarāṣṭra's Blessing and Advice
beats.summary: Dhr̥tarāṣṭra blesses Yudhiṣṭhira and his brothers to return to Indraprastha, advising forgiveness of Duryodhana's harsh words and emphasizing the qualities of noble persons who remember virtues and ignore wrongs. He extols Yudhiṣṭhira's righteousness, Arjuna's valor, Bhīma's prowess, and the twins' devotion. Yudhiṣṭhira accepts and departs with his brothers and Draupadī.

beats.title: The Second Dice Game
beats.summary: Duryodhana, incensed that Dhritarashtra allowed the Pandavas to leave with wealth, conspires with Karna and Shakuni to invite them back for a second dice game. Despite opposition from elders like Vidura, Bhishma, and Drona, Dhritarashtra agrees, leading to the exile of the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Second Dice Game
beats.summary: Yudhishthira is summoned back to the dice game by Dhritarashtra's command. Despite knowing Shakuni's deceit, he feels compelled by dharma to accept the challenge. Shakuni proposes a single throw: the loser will go into exile for twelve years, with a thirteenth year incognito. Yudhishthira agrees and loses again.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Exile and Vows
beats.summary: After losing the dice game, the Pandavas prepare for exile. Duryodhana and his brothers mock them, especially Draupadi. Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva make fierce vows to kill their enemies in battle after the exile. They then approach Dhritarashtra.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Departure for Exile
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira takes leave of the Kuru elders and family before departing for exile after losing the dice game. Vidura advises him on dharma and the qualities he has imbibed from various sages and deities, blessing him for a safe return.

beats.title: The Departure to the Forest
beats.summary: Kunti consoles Draupadi and her sons as they depart for exile after losing the dice game. She laments their fate, praises the departed Pandu and Madri, and the Pandavas leave for the forest. Vidura comforts Kunti and is summoned by Dhritarashtra.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Departure into Exile
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura how the Pandavas are behaving as they leave for exile after losing the dice game. Vidura describes each Pandava's demeanor and explains the symbolic meaning behind their actions. He also recounts omens and Narada's prophecy of the Kaurava destruction. Drona then speaks to Duryodhana, warning him of the consequences and revealing his own fears about Dhrishtadyumna.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Vidura's Counsel
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the impending war caused by Duryodhana's humiliation of Draupadi. Sanjaya recounts the events and omens, and Vidura advises peace with the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Departure into Exile
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the Pandavas' life in exile after losing the dice game. Vaishampayana describes their departure from Hastinapura, the lamentations of the citizens, and the citizens' discourse on the importance of association with the virtuous. Yudhishthira requests the citizens to return and care for his family. The Pandavas proceed to the bank of the Ganga and spend the night there, accompanied by some brahmanas.

beats.title: The Discourse of Shaunaka on Detachment and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, about to enter the forest with his brothers, tries to dissuade the brahmins from accompanying them due to hardships. The brahmins insist on following. Sage Shaunaka then delivers a discourse on detachment, the dangers of desire and wealth, and the path to liberation through yoga and tapas.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Penance and the Sun Hymn
beats.summary: King Yudhiṣṭhira, distressed by his inability to support the brahmins who follow him into exile, seeks advice from his priest Dhaumya. Dhaumya explains that the sun (Sūrya) is the source of all food and life, and that kings have historically overcome difficulties through penance. He advises Yudhiṣṭhira to worship the sun. Yudhiṣṭhira performs a rigorous penance, and Dhaumya recites the 108 names of the sun, a hymn originally taught by Indra to Nārada and then to Dhaumya. The chapter concludes with the benefits of reciting this hymn.

beats.title: The Boon of the Sun God
beats.summary: After receiving a boon from the Sun god, Yudhishthira emerges from the water and greets his brothers and priest Dhaumya. He then uses the boon to provide abundant food and wealth, hosting brahmins and his brothers. Following rituals, the Pandavas proceed to Kamyaka forest.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel and Departure
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, blind and grieving, consults Vidura after the Pandavas enter the forest. Vidura advises him to restore the kingdom to Yudhishthira, warning that otherwise the Kauravas will be destroyed. Dhritarashtra rejects the advice, accusing Vidura of partiality, and Vidura leaves to join the Pandavas.

beats.title: Vidura's Visit to the Pandavas in Exile
beats.summary: Vidura visits the Pandavas in the Kamyaka forest after being banished by Dhritarashtra. He advises Yudhishthira on the importance of patience, sharing wealth with allies, and truthfulness. Yudhishthira accepts the counsel.

beats.title: The Reconciliation of Dhritarashtra and Vidura
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, remorseful after driving away Vidura, sends Sanjaya to bring him back. Sanjaya finds Vidura with the Pandavas in Kamyaka forest. Vidura returns to Hastinapura, and the brothers reconcile.

beats.title: The Conspiracy to Kill the Pandavas
beats.summary: Duryodhana, distressed by Vidura's departure to the Pandavas, consults with Shakuni, Dushasana, and Karna. They plot to kill the Pandavas in the forest. Karna advocates for a direct attack. As they set out, Vyasa arrives and restrains them.

beats.title: Vyasa's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vyasa advises Dhritarashtra to prevent Duryodhana's hostility towards the Pandavas, warning of dire consequences. He suggests reconciliation or sending Duryodhana to live with the Pandavas in the forest.

beats.title: The Story of Surabhi and Indra
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments his inability to control Duryodhana despite knowing the evils of gambling. Vyasa consoles him by narrating the story of Surabhi, the mother of cows, who wept for her weak son being mistreated by a farmer. Indra, moved by her maternal compassion, intervened to help the calf. Vyasa draws a parallel: just as Surabhi felt greater compassion for her weaker son, so should Dhritarashtra feel compassion for the suffering Pandavas, and advises him to make peace with them.

beats.title: Maitreya's Counsel and Curse
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vyasa about the welfare of the Pandavas. Vyasa informs him that the sage Maitreya is coming to counsel Duryodhana. Maitreya arrives, advises Duryodhana to make peace with the Pandavas, and recounts Bhima's slaying of the rakshasas Hidimba, Baka, and Kirmira, as well as the defeat of Jarasandha. Duryodhana disrespects Maitreya by tapping his thigh and drawing lines on the ground. Enraged, Maitreya curses that Bhima will break Duryodhana's thigh in battle. Dhritarashtra pleads for mercy, and Maitreya says the curse can be averted if Duryodhana makes peace. Duryodhana leaves, and Maitreya departs.

beats.title: The Slaying of Kirmira
beats.summary: Vidura narrates to Dhritarashtra the story of the Pandavas' encounter with the rakshasa Kirmira in the Kamyaka forest. Bhima kills Kirmira, avenging the deaths of Baka and Hidimba, and the Pandavas proceed to the Dvaita forest.

beats.title: The Visit of the Vrishnis and the Lament of Draupadi
beats.summary: In this chapter, after hearing of the Pandavas' exile, the Vrishnis, Bhojas, and other allies visit them in the forest. Krishna consoles them, and Arjuna recounts Krishna's past glories and incarnations. Draupadi then laments her humiliation in the Kaurava court and recounts the many attempts on the Pandavas' lives. Krishna promises that their enemies will be slain, and Dhrishtadyumna declares that they will kill Drona, Bhishma, Duryodhana, and Karna.

beats.title: Krishna's Lament and Explanation
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) tells Yudhishthira that if he had been present in Dvaraka, he would have prevented the dice game by pointing out its evils, and if Dhritarashtra's sons had not listened, he would have used force or divided the Kauravas. He expresses regret for his absence and sorrow at seeing Yudhishthira in distress.

beats.title: Krishna's Absence Explained: The Battle with Shalva
beats.summary: Krishna explains to Yudhishthira why he was absent during the dice game: he had gone to fight King Shalva of Saubha, who attacked Dvaraka in revenge for Krishna's killing of Shishupala. Krishna describes Shalva's rampage, his own decision to confront him, and the ensuing battle with Shalva's demonic forces, which he won.

beats.title: The Siege of Dwaraka by Shalva
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Krishna to describe the destruction of Saubha. Krishna recounts how Shalva attacked Dwaraka while he was away, and the city was heavily fortified and defended by the Vrishnis and Andhakas under King Ugrasena's orders.

beats.title: The Battle with Shalva
beats.summary: Vasudeva narrates to the Pandavas the battle between the Vrishnis and King Shalva of Saubha. Shalva besieges Dvaraka with a vast army. The Vrishni heroes, including Samba, Charudeshna, and Pradyumna, counterattack. Samba defeats Kshemavriddhi and kills the demon Vega. Charudeshna fights and kills the demon Vivindhya. Shalva returns with the flying city Saubha, causing confusion. Pradyumna then rallies the Vrishni forces and declares he will destroy Shalva and his army.

beats.title: Pradyumna's Battle with Shalva
beats.summary: Vasudeva narrates to Dhritarashtra the battle between Pradyumna and King Shalva. Pradyumna, son of Krishna and Rukmini, attacks Shalva's forces, and a fierce duel ensues. Pradyumna initially wounds Shalva, but Shalva recovers and strikes Pradyumna, causing him to lose consciousness. The battle is intense, with both warriors displaying great skill.

beats.title: Pradyumna's Rebuke to His Charioteer
beats.summary: Pradyumna, struck by Shalva's arrows, falls unconscious. His charioteer, Daruka's son, retreats from battle. Upon regaining consciousness, Pradyumna rebukes the charioteer for retreating, arguing that it is against the dharma of Vrishni warriors. He insists on returning to fight, fearing disgrace before his elders and peers, especially Krishna and Balarama. The charioteer explains his concern for Pradyumna's safety, but Pradyumna commands him to turn back.

beats.title: Pradyumna's Battle with Shalva
beats.summary: Vasudeva narrates to Dhritarashtra the battle between Pradyumna and Shalva. Pradyumna, son of Krishna and Rukmini, defeats Shalva but spares him upon divine intervention, as Shalva is destined to be killed by Krishna himself.

beats.title: Krishna's Battle with Shalva
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) narrates to Yudhishthira how he left Dvaraka after the Rajasuya sacrifice, found the city in a desolate state, learned from Hardikya (Kritavarma) about Shalva's siege, resolved to destroy Shalva, consoled the Vrishnis, and set out with an army. He pursued Shalva to the ocean, where Shalva used the flying city Saubha. A fierce battle ensued with magical illusions, but Krishna eventually overcame them using divine weapons and the Prajnastra.

beats.title: Krishna's Battle with Shalva and the Illusion of His Father's Death
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) narrates to Yudhishthira his battle with Shalva, who attacked Dwaraka while Krishna was away. During the fight, Krishna's charioteer Daruka is wounded, and a messenger from Ahuka reports that Shalva has killed Shurasuta (Krishna's father). Krishna is distraught, sees a vision of his father falling, and faints. He then realizes it was an illusion (maya) and recovers.

beats.title: Krishna's Battle with Shalva and Destruction of Saubha
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) narrates to Yudhishthira his battle with Shalva and the destruction of the flying city Saubha. After defeating Shalva, Krishna returns to Dvarka, then visits the Pandavas in Kamyaka forest, and finally departs with Subhadra and Abhimanyu. The other kings also take leave.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Depart for the Forest
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, led by Yudhiṣṭhira, depart for the forest after losing the dice game. The citizens of Kurujāṅgala lament their departure, and Arjuna reassures them that the king will return victorious. The people perform circumambulation and return home.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Enter Dvaitavana
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, led by Yudhiṣṭhira, decide to spend their twelve-year exile in the Dvaitavana forest. Arjuna praises Yudhiṣṭhira's wisdom and suggests Dvaitavana as a suitable place. They travel there with many brāhmaṇas and ascetics, and Yudhiṣṭhira enters the forest like Indra entering heaven, receiving honors from siddhas and forest-dwellers.

beats.title: Markandeya's Visit and the Example of Rama
beats.summary: In the Kamyaka forest, the Pandavas live in exile. Sage Markandeya visits them and, seeing Draupadi and the Pandavas, smiles. Yudhishthira asks why he smiles. Markandeya explains he was reminded of Rama's exile, who endured hardship without adharma. He recounts Rama's story and other kings who followed dharma despite suffering, concluding that Yudhishthira will regain his kingdom.

beats.title: Baka Dalbhya's Advice on Brahmana-Kshatriya Unity
beats.summary: In the Dvaitavana forest, the Pandavas reside with many Brahmanas. Sage Baka Dalbhya advises Yudhishthira on the symbiotic relationship between Brahmanas and Kshatriyas, emphasizing that a king's strength and prosperity depend on honoring and supporting Brahmanas. He cites the example of Bali, who lost his kingdom due to mistreating Brahmanas. The chapter lists many sages present and concludes with them praising Yudhishthira.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament and Exhortation
beats.summary: Draupadi laments the hardships of the Pandavas in exile, questioning Yudhishthira's lack of anger towards their enemies, and urges him to act with kshatriya valor.

beats.title: The Dialogue of Prahlada and Bali on Forgiveness and Severity
beats.summary: Draupadi recounts the ancient dialogue between Prahlada and Bali on the relative merits of forgiveness (kṣamā) and severity (tejas). Prahlada teaches that neither is always superior; one must apply each at the proper time. He lists the dangers of excessive forgiveness (loss of respect, exploitation) and excessive severity (alienation, loss of power). The proper times for forgiveness include when the offender has previously helped, is ignorant, or when public fear demands it. Draupadi then urges Yudhishthira to apply severity now against the Kauravas, as no occasion for forgiveness remains.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Discourse on Forgiveness
beats.summary: Yudhishthira explains to Draupadi the destructive nature of anger and the supreme value of forgiveness (kshama), citing the teachings of Kashyapa. He resolves to forgive even his enemies, hoping for a peaceful resolution.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament on Fate and Injustice
beats.summary: Draupadi rebukes Yudhishthira for his attachment to dharma despite his suffering, arguing that fate (Ishvara) controls all actions and outcomes. She laments the apparent injustice where the virtuous suffer and the wicked prosper, questioning the moral order.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Defense of Dharma
beats.summary: Yudhishthira responds to Draupadi's skepticism about dharma, arguing that dharma must be performed without attachment to results, citing examples of sages and the necessity of faith in dharma's efficacy. He warns against doubting dharma and emphasizes that actions have consequences, even if unseen.

beats.title: Draupadi's Discourse on Action and Destiny
beats.summary: Draupadi counsels Yudhishthira on the necessity of human effort (puruṣakāra) alongside destiny (daiva), arguing that action is essential for success and that laziness leads to ruin. She explains that results depend on a combination of fate, chance, effort, and nature, but emphasizes that one must always strive.

beats.title: Bhima's Exhortation to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Bhima urges Yudhishthira to abandon passive dharma and reclaim the kingdom through Kshatriya action, arguing that dharma, artha, and kama must be balanced and that wealth is necessary to perform dharma. He criticizes Yudhishthira's weakness and calls for war.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament and Defense
beats.summary: Yudhishthira responds to Bhimasena's criticism, recounting the events that led to their exile: the dice game with Shakuni, the loss of kingdom, Draupadi's humiliation, and the subsequent vow of exile. He defends his actions as bound by fate and dharma, and emphasizes the importance of patience and truth.

beats.title: Bhima's Exhortation to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Bhima urges Yudhishthira to abandon patience and take action against their enemies, arguing that time is fleeting and that a Kshatriya's duty is to fight. He criticizes Yudhishthira's leniency and emphasizes the need for vengeance and war.

beats.title: Vyasa's Consolation and the Pratismriti Vidya
beats.summary: Yudhishthira expresses fear of the Kaurava warriors to Bhima. Sage Vyasa arrives, consoles Yudhishthira, and teaches him the Pratismriti Vidya, then advises the Pandavas to move to the Kamyaka forest. The Pandavas follow his advice.

beats.title: Arjuna's Journey to Obtain Celestial Weapons
beats.summary: Yudhishthira advises Arjuna to seek divine weapons from Indra through severe penance. Arjuna journeys north, meets a sage who reveals himself as Indra, and requests celestial weapons. Indra directs him to first obtain the vision of Shiva.

beats.title: Arjuna's Penance and Shiva's Promise
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana to narrate how Arjuna obtained divine weapons and pleased Shiva. Vaishampayana describes Arjuna's journey to the Himalayas, his severe penance, and the gods' concern. Shiva assures the sages he will fulfill Arjuna's wish.

beats.title: Arjuna's Encounter with Śiva as a Kirāta
beats.summary: Arjuna, while hunting in the forest, encounters a Kirāta (hunter) who is actually Lord Śiva in disguise. They both claim the same boar (the demon Mūka) as their prey and engage in a fierce battle. After Arjuna exhausts all his weapons and is overpowered, Śiva reveals his true form, blesses Arjuna, and grants him divine weapons.

beats.title: Arjuna Receives the Pashupata Weapon from Shiva
beats.summary: Lord Shiva (Tryambaka) reveals to Arjuna that in a previous life he was the sage Nara, companion of Narayana, and together they sustain the world. He grants Arjuna the divine Pashupata weapon after Arjuna requests it to defeat powerful foes. Shiva instructs Arjuna on its use and cautions against misuse. Arjuna receives the weapon, and the earth trembles as a sign. Shiva then departs to Mount Kailasa.

beats.title: Arjuna Receives Celestial Weapons from the Lokapalas
beats.summary: Arjuna, after witnessing Shiva's disappearance, is visited by the Lokapalas (guardians of the directions) who reveal his divine origins and bestow celestial weapons upon him. Varuna gives the Varuna pasha, Kubera gives the Antardhana weapon, and Indra promises to send his chariot and more weapons. Yama explains Arjuna's purpose: to slay Karna and the Nivatakavachas, and to lighten the earth's burden.

beats.title: Arjuna's Ascent to Indra's Heaven
beats.summary: Arjuna, after the departure of the Lokapālas, contemplates the arrival of Indra's chariot. Mātali, Indra's charioteer, arrives and invites Arjuna to ascend the divine chariot to meet Indra in heaven. Arjuna, after bathing in the Ganges, performing rituals, and taking leave of Mount Mandara, ascends the chariot. He travels through the celestial realm, witnessing various divine beings and their luminous abodes, and finally sees Indra's city Amarāvatī.

beats.title: Arjuna's Journey to Indra's Realm
beats.summary: Arjuna, guided by Indra's command, travels to the celestial realm of Indra, witnessing divine sights and being honored by gods, sages, and celestial beings. He meets Indra, who embraces him and seats him on his throne, celebrating their reunion with music and dance.

beats.title: Indra's Revelation and Lomaśa's Mission
beats.summary: Indra reveals to the sage Lomaśa that Arjuna is the incarnation of Nara, and Kṛṣṇa is Nārāyaṇa. He explains that they have descended to relieve the Earth's burden, and that Arjuna will defeat the Nivātakavaca demons. Indra sends Lomaśa to comfort Yudhiṣṭhira and guide him on a pilgrimage.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and the Inevitable War
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after hearing about Arjuna's exploits in heaven, laments to Sanjaya about his sons' inevitable doom. He reflects on Arjuna's invincibility, the anger of the Pandavas due to Draupadi's humiliation, and the support of Krishna. Sanjaya confirms the dire situation, recounting Arjuna's encounter with Shiva and the Pandavas' resolve for revenge.

beats.title: Life of the Pāṇḍavas in the Forest
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the Pāṇḍavas' life in the forest, specifically their food and conduct. Vaishampayana describes how they hunted deer and other animals, offered the first portion to Brahmins, and then ate. They supported many Brahmins, and Yudhiṣṭhira cared for all like a father. Draupadī served the Brahmins first before eating herself. The Pāṇḍavas, except Arjuna who was away, spent five years in the Kāmyaka forest engaged in study, chanting, and sacrifices.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Krishna's Vow
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the impending war, recalling the strength of the Pandava forces and the support of Krishna. Samjaya recounts the assembly at Kamyaka forest where Krishna vowed to destroy the Kauravas and restore the Pandavas' kingdom. Dhritarashtra reflects on Vidura's warning about the destruction of the Kurus.

beats.title: The Lament of the Pandavas and the Arrival of Brihadasva
beats.summary: Yudhishthira and his brothers lament Arjuna's absence in the Kamyaka forest. Bhima urges Yudhishthira to abandon the vow of exile and attack the Kauravas, but Yudhishthira insists on keeping his promise. The sage Brihadasva arrives and consoles Yudhishthira by telling the story of King Nala, who suffered even greater misfortune.

beats.title: Nala and Damayantī: The Swan Messenger
beats.summary: This chapter introduces King Nala of Niṣadha and Princess Damayantī of Vidarbha, describing their virtues and beauty. It narrates how they hear of each other's qualities and fall in love without meeting. Nala captures a golden swan, which promises to speak of him to Damayantī. The swan then goes to Damayantī and praises Nala, conveying his love. Damayantī asks the swan to tell Nala the same, and the swan returns to Nala with her message.

beats.title: Damayanti's Svayamvara and the Meeting with Nala
beats.summary: Damayanti, having heard the swan's message about Nala, becomes lovesick and restless. Her father King Bhima arranges a svayamvara. Many kings and princes gather. Meanwhile, the sages Narada and Parvata visit Indra's court. Indra inquires about the kings, and Narada explains they are going to Damayanti's svayamvara. The Lokapalas (guardians of the quarters) decide to attend as well. On the way, they meet Nala and ask him to be their messenger.

beats.title: Nala as Messenger of the Gods
beats.summary: Nala, sent by the gods Indra, Agni, Varuna, and Yama as a messenger to Damayanti, enters her palace unseen and delivers their proposal of marriage. Damayanti, impressed by Nala's appearance, questions him, and he reveals his identity and mission.

beats.title: Nala's Report to the Gods
beats.summary: Nala returns to the gods after visiting Damayanti and reports her response: she will choose him as her husband in the presence of the gods at her svayamvara, thus avoiding any offense to them.

beats.title: Damayanti's Svayamvara
beats.summary: King Bhima of Vidarbha holds a svayamvara for his daughter Damayanti. Many kings and the four Lokapalas (Indra, Agni, Yama, Varuna) attend, disguised as Nala. Damayanti, unable to distinguish them, prays to the gods to reveal Nala. The gods grant her wish, and she chooses Nala. The Lokapalas bless Nala with boons, and the marriage is celebrated.

beats.title: The Encounter of Kali and Dvāpara with the Gods
beats.summary: Br̥hadaśva narrates how after Damayantī chose Nala as her husband, the gods Indra, Agni, Varuṇa, and Yama encountered Kali and Dvāpara on their way. Kali, angry that Damayantī chose a mortal, threatens to curse Nala. The gods warn him against it, praising Nala's virtues, and depart. Kali then plots with Dvāpara to destroy Nala's kingdom and separate him from Damayantī.

beats.title: Kali's Possession of Nala and the Dice Game
beats.summary: Br̥hadaśva narrates how Kali, having made a pact with Dvāpara, entered Nala after finding a lapse in his ritual purity. Kali then incited Puṣkara to challenge Nala to a dice game. Despite warnings from his subjects and wife Damayantī, Nala, possessed by Kali, continued playing and lost everything over many months.

beats.title: Damayanti's Plan to Save Her Children
beats.summary: Damayanti, seeing Nala obsessed with gambling and having lost everything, devises a plan to save her children and herself. She instructs the charioteer Varshneya to take her two children to her father's kingdom of Vidarbha, and then to seek service with King Rituparna of Ayodhya. Varshneya carries out her instructions, leaving the children with King Bhima and then entering the service of Rituparna.

beats.title: Nala's Fall and Wanderings
beats.summary: After losing his kingdom and wealth to his brother Pushkara in a dice game, King Nala, accompanied by his wife Damayanti, wanders in the forest. Suffering from hunger and humiliation, Nala attempts to catch birds with his only garment, but the birds (actually dice in disguise) steal it, leaving him naked. He then tries to persuade Damayanti to go to her father's kingdom for safety, but she refuses to abandon him, insisting they go together to Vidarbha.

beats.title: Nala's Abandonment of Damayantī
beats.summary: King Nala, having lost his kingdom in a dice game, wanders in the forest with his wife Damayanti. Overcome with grief and guilt, he decides to abandon her while she sleeps, cutting his half of their single garment. He struggles with his love for her and the compulsion of Kali, ultimately leaving her alone in the forest.

beats.title: Damayanti's Lament and Rescue
beats.summary: Damayanti, abandoned by Nala in the forest, laments and searches for him. She is attacked by a serpent but rescued by a hunter. The hunter, overcome by lust, tries to assault her, and she curses him to fall dead, which immediately happens.

beats.title: Damayanti's Search for Nala
beats.summary: Damayanti, searching for her husband Nala in the forest, encounters various dangers and speaks to animals, a mountain, and a tree, asking about Nala. She meets a caravan and learns it is heading to the kingdom of Cedi. The chapter ends with her continuing her search.

beats.title: Damayanti's Journey to Cedi
beats.summary: Damayanti, after being abandoned by Nala in the forest, wanders alone and encounters a caravan that is later destroyed by a herd of elephants. She laments her fate, reflecting on past actions and the role of destiny. She then reaches the city of Cedi, where the queen mother takes her in. Damayanti agrees to stay under certain conditions, including not eating leftovers, not washing feet, not speaking to other men, and that any man who propositions her should be punished. She also requests to meet with Brahmins to search for Nala.

beats.title: Nala and Karkotaka
beats.summary: King Nala, after abandoning Damayanti, encounters a forest fire and hears a voice calling him. He enters the fire and finds the serpent king Karkotaka, who explains he was cursed by a sage. Karkotaka asks Nala to carry him, then bites Nala, transforming his appearance. The serpent promises Nala that the poison will protect him and that he will regain his form later. Karkotaka instructs Nala to go to King Rituparna of Ayodhya, who will teach him dice skills, and gives him a divine garment to restore his original form.

beats.title: Nala's Disguise and Service
beats.summary: Nala, in disguise as Bāhuka, arrives at King Ṛtuparṇa's city and offers his services as a charioteer and cook. Ṛtuparṇa hires him, and Nala lives there, constantly lamenting his separation from Damayantī. He recites a verse each evening about a foolish man who lost his wife, which prompts Jīvala to question him. Nala obliquely tells his own story without revealing his identity.

beats.title: The Search for Nala and Damayanti
beats.summary: King Bhima sends brahmins to search for Nala and Damayanti, offering rewards. The brahmin Sudeva finds Damayanti in the kingdom of Chedi, living incognito as a servant. He recognizes her despite her emaciated state and grief, and reveals his identity. The queen mother of Chedi questions Sudeva about Damayanti's identity.

beats.title: Damayanti's Reunion and Return
beats.summary: Sudeva identifies Damayanti by a birthmark on her forehead, leading to her reunion with her maternal aunt, the queen of the Cedis. Damayanti then returns to Vidarbha, where she is joyfully welcomed by her family.

beats.title: Damayanti's Lament and Search for Nala
beats.summary: Damayanti, grieving for her lost husband Nala, asks her mother to help find him. The queen is speechless with sorrow. Damayanti then appeals to King Bhima, her father, to send brahmins in search of Nala, reciting a poignant message that reveals her abandonment and pleads for his return. The brahmins depart to search everywhere.

beats.title: Damayanti's Plan to Reunite with Nala
beats.summary: Damayanti, having heard from the Brahmin Parnada about Nala's whereabouts, sends the Brahmin Sudeva to King Rituparna with a false message that she will hold a second svayamvara, hoping to bring Nala (disguised as the charioteer Bahuka) to Ayodhya.

beats.title: Nala's Disguise and the Journey to Vidarbha
beats.summary: King Nala, disguised as the charioteer Bāhuka, agrees to drive King Ṛtuparṇa to Vidarbha for Damayantī's svayaṃvara in one day. He selects suitable horses, and despite initial doubts, Ṛtuparṇa is amazed by Nala's skill. Vārṣṇeya, the charioteer, suspects Bāhuka might be Nala due to his knowledge and appearance, but remains uncertain.

beats.title: Nala and Ṛtuparṇa: The Exchange of Knowledge
beats.summary: King Nala, disguised as the charioteer Bāhuka, demonstrates his skill in counting the leaves and fruits of a bibhītaka tree, leading to a deal with King Ṛtuparṇa: Nala teaches the aśvahṛdaya (horse-lore) in exchange for the akṣahṛdaya (dice-lore). Upon receiving the knowledge, the demon Kali, who had possessed Nala, is expelled and enters the bibhītaka tree, which becomes inauspicious. Nala regains his true form and proceeds to Vidarbha.

beats.title: Damayanti's Lament and Rtuparna's Arrival
beats.summary: Damayanti, hearing the chariot sound of King Rtuparna, mistakes it for Nala's and laments. She climbs to the palace and sees Rtuparna with Varshneya and Bahuka. Rtuparna meets King Bhima, who is puzzled by his sudden visit. Damayanti sends a messenger to investigate Nala's whereabouts.

beats.title: Damayanti's Inquiry and Nala's Agony
beats.summary: Damayanti sends her maid Keśinī to question the charioteer Bāhuka, suspecting he is her lost husband Nala. Bāhuka reveals he serves King Ṛtuparṇa and that Vārṣṇeya (Nala's former charioteer) is with them. Keśinī presses him with words from Damayanti, causing Nala (in disguise) great distress. He defends the virtue of women and breaks down in tears. Keśinī reports back to Damayanti.

beats.title: Damayanti's Recognition of Nala
beats.summary: Damayanti, suspecting that the charioteer Bāhuka is her lost husband Nala, sends her maid Keśinī to observe him. Keśinī reports miraculous feats: Bāhuka can create fire from grass, touch fire without burning, command water, and make crushed flowers more fragrant. Damayanti then tests him by having Keśinī bring meat he cooked; tasting it, she recognizes Nala's cooking. Overcome, she sends their children Indrasenā and her brother to Bāhuka, who embraces them and weeps, confirming his identity.

beats.title: Damayantī's Test and Nala's Revelation
beats.summary: Damayanti, suspecting that the charioteer Bāhuka is her lost husband Nala, sends her maid Keśinī to her mother to seek permission to bring him before her. With her parents' consent, she summons Nala (disguised as Bāhuka). Seeing him in a wretched state, she is overcome with grief and questions him about abandoning her in the forest. Nala explains that Kali was responsible for his downfall and that he has now overcome Kali through his efforts and penance. He expresses doubt about Damayanti's fidelity, but she reassures him.

beats.title: Damayanti's Reunion with Nala
beats.summary: Damayanti, reunited with Nala after years of separation, defends her chastity by invoking the elements as witnesses. The wind god testifies to her purity, and Nala regains his original form. They embrace and spend the night recounting their past sufferings. The next morning, Damayanti's mother informs King Bhima, who welcomes Nala. After four years, Nala and Damayanti enjoy happiness.

beats.title: Nala's Return to Kundina and Reconciliation with Rtuparna
beats.summary: King Nala, after spending the night, adorns himself and with Damayanti visits King Bhima. Bhima welcomes them joyfully. The city celebrates Nala's arrival. King Rtuparna hears that Nala (disguised as Bahuka) is with Damayanti and rejoices. Nala meets Rtuparna, exchanges forgiveness, and gives him the horse-knowledge (ashva-hridaya vidya). Rtuparna then departs with a new charioteer. Nala stays in Kundina for a short time.

beats.title: Nala Regains His Kingdom
beats.summary: Nala, having regained his kingdom from Pushkara through a single-throw dice match, forgives his brother and sends him back to his own city with honor. Pushkara acknowledges Nala's magnanimity and departs. Nala then enters his capital and comforts the citizens.

beats.title: Br̥hadaśva's Consolation and the Akṣahr̥daya
beats.summary: Br̥hadaśva narrates the story of Nala to Yudhiṣṭhira, describing Nala's recovery of his kingdom and happiness after suffering due to dice. He then consoles Yudhiṣṭhira, teaches him the heart of dice (akṣahr̥daya), and departs. Yudhiṣṭhira learns of Arjuna's severe penance and laments, seeking guidance from brāhmaṇas.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Lament for Arjuna
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana what the Pandavas did in the Kamyaka forest after Arjuna left. Vaishampayana describes their sorrow and longing for Arjuna, with Draupadi, Bhima, Nakula, and Sahadeva each expressing their grief and admiration for Arjuna's valor and achievements.

beats.title: The Glories of Tirtha Pilgrimage
beats.summary: Narada visits the Pandavas in the forest. Yudhishthira asks about the merit of circumambulating the earth and visiting tirthas. Narada recounts how Bhishma once asked the sage Pulastya the same question, and Pulastya explained the supreme merit of tirtha pilgrimage, especially to Pushkara, and described a series of sacred sites and their benefits.

beats.title: The Sacred Geography of Kurukṣetra
beats.summary: Pulastya instructs Yudhishthira on the sacred geography of Kurukṣetra, describing numerous tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) and the benefits of bathing, fasting, and performing rituals there. The chapter includes the story of sage Maṅkaṇaka and Mahādeva, and emphasizes the supreme sanctity of Kurukṣetra, especially the tīrtha Pr̥thūdaka.

beats.title: The Merits of Pilgrimage to Sacred Tirthas
beats.summary: Pulastya instructs Yudhishthira on the merits of visiting various tirthas (sacred sites), detailing the specific rituals, offerings, and fruits (spiritual rewards) such as attaining heaven, performing sacrifices like Ashvamedha, and purifying one's lineage. The chapter emphasizes the transformative power of pilgrimage and devotion.

beats.title: Pulastya's Discourse on Tirthas and Narada's Encouragement
beats.summary: Pulastya instructs Bhishma on the merits of various tirthas (sacred sites) across India, detailing the specific fruits of bathing, fasting, and performing rituals at each location. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of pilgrimage, especially at Prayaga (confluence of Ganga and Yamuna), and concludes with Narada encouraging Yudhishthira to undertake the pilgrimage with the rishis.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Explanation for Sending Arjuna
beats.summary: Yudhishthira explains to Dhaumya why he sent Arjuna to obtain celestial weapons from Indra, describing the threat of Karna and other Kaurava warriors, and expresses his desire to wait for Arjuna's return in a pleasant forest.

beats.title: Dhaumya's Discourse on Eastern Tirthas
beats.summary: Dhaumya, the priest of the Pandavas, describes the sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites) in the eastern direction to Yudhishthira, including rivers, mountains, and ashrams, and mentions the deeds of sages and kings associated with them.

beats.title: Description of Southern Tirthas
beats.summary: Dhaumya describes the sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites) in the southern direction, including rivers like Godavari, Venna, Bhimarathi, and Payoshni, and places like the hermitage of Matara, the yupa of Varuna, Kanva's ashrama, Jamadagni's vedi, Agastya's tirtha, Gokarna, and the mountain Ujjayanta in Surashtra. He extols Dvaravati where Krishna resides as the eternal dharma.

beats.title: Description of Western Sacred Places
beats.summary: Dhaumya describes the sacred places in the western direction, including the Narmada river, the hermitage of Viśravas where Kubera was born, the Vaiḍūryaśikhara mountain, the lake of Pitāmaha (Puṣkara), and various other holy sites and ashrams. He recounts that Yayāti fell at the bank of the Viśvāmitra river and regained higher worlds. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of these places, especially Puṣkara, where even mental desire for pilgrimage destroys sins and grants heaven.

beats.title: Dhaumya's Discourse on Northern Tirthas
beats.summary: Dhaumya describes to Yudhishthira the sacred tirthas and hermitages in the northern direction, including the Sarasvati and Yamuna rivers, the Plakshavatarana tirtha, the place where Sahadeva performed a sacrifice, the ashramas of Bharata, Jamadagni, and others, culminating in the supreme holy place of Badari where Narayana (Vishnu) resides.

beats.title: Lomasha's Arrival and Arjuna's Achievements
beats.summary: Lomasha the sage arrives and tells Yudhishthira that Arjuna has obtained divine weapons from various gods, including the Brahmashira from Rudra, and has mastered music from Vishvavasu's son. Indra sends a message that Arjuna will return soon after accomplishing a divine task, and advises Yudhishthira to practice tapas. Lomasha also reassures Yudhishthira about Karna and promises to guide him on pilgrimage.

beats.title: The Pilgrimage of Yudhishthira
