beats.summary: Lomasha recounts Arjuna's request to guide Yudhishthira on a pilgrimage to holy tirthas, emphasizing the purifying power of such journeys. Yudhishthira agrees, dismisses his retinue, and prepares to travel with Lomasha and a few brahmins.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Departure for Tirtha Pilgrimage
beats.summary: The chapter describes Yudhishthira's departure from the Kamyaka forest to visit holy tirthas, accompanied by his brothers, Draupadi, and the sage Lomasa. Before leaving, sages including Vyasa, Narada, and Parvata advise him on the importance of mental purity over external rituals. The Pandavas set out with their retinue, armed and determined.

beats.title: Lomasha's Discourse on Dharma and Adharma
beats.summary: Yudhishthira laments his suffering despite being virtuous, and asks why the wicked prosper. Lomasha explains that adharma leads to temporary success but eventual destruction, citing the example of the Daityas and Danavas who fell due to pride and loss of dharma. He advises Yudhishthira to perform pilgrimages and penances to regain his fortune, listing past kings who attained glory through such practices.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Pilgrimage and the Story of King Gaya
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas travel to various sacred tīrthas, performing rituals and giving gifts. They reach the Naimiṣāraṇya forest, bathe in the Gomati river, and visit holy sites including Kanyātīrtha, Aśvatīrtha, Gavāṁtīrtha, Vālakoṭī, Vr̥ṣaprastha, Bāhudā, Prayāga, and the confluence of Gaṅgā and Yamunā. They then go to the sacrificial ground of Prajāpati and later to Gayāśiras, where they perform a Cāturmāsya sacrifice. During this, the sage Śamaṭha narrates the story of King Gaya, a rājarṣi who performed an extraordinary sacrifice with immense food and gifts, leaving a lasting legacy.

beats.title: The Story of Agastya and Lopamudra
beats.summary: King Yudhishthira, while on pilgrimage, arrives at Agastya's hermitage and asks Lomasha why Agastya killed the demon Vatapi. Lomasha narrates the story of the demons Ilvala and Vatapi, who would kill Brahmins by trickery. Agastya, seeking to have a son to liberate his ancestors, creates a woman named Lopamudra from various creatures and gives her to King Vidarbha. She grows up beautiful and virtuous, but no one dares marry her due to Agastya's power.

beats.title: Agastya and Lopamudra
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Agastya and Lopamudra. King of Vidarbha, pressured by sage Agastya, gives his daughter Lopamudra in marriage. Lopamudra renounces finery, joins Agastya in asceticism. After long penance, Agastya desires union, but Lopamudra requests a luxurious setting. Agastya, lacking wealth, is reminded by Lopamudra of his tapas power. He agrees to acquire wealth without losing tapas, and Lopamudra suggests waiting for the proper season.

beats.title: Agastya's Quest for Wealth
beats.summary: Sage Lomasa narrates how Agastya, accompanied by King Srutarvana and later King Vadhryasva, went to various kings seeking wealth. Each king offered his income and expenditure, but Agastya found them equal and refused to take anything, as taking would cause harm to beings. Finally, they all approached King Trasadasyu, who also showed equal accounts. Then the kings suggested they go to the demon Ilvala, who had abundant wealth. They all agreed and proceeded to Ilvala.

beats.title: Agastya and the Demon Ilvala
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Agastya and the demon Ilvala. Ilvala and his brother Vatapi would invite sages, feed them Vatapi in the form of a goat, then Ilvala would call him back, causing the sage to burst. Agastya, however, consumes Vatapi and digests him, foiling Ilvala's plan. Agastya then demands wealth from Ilvala, who provides it. Later, Agastya's wife Lopamudra desires a son, and Agastya grants her a son equal to a thousand, named Drdhasyu, who becomes a great sage. The chapter ends with Lomasha showing the hermitage of Agastya and the holy Ganga.

beats.title: The Story of Dadhichi's Bones and the Vajra
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates to Yudhishthira the story of how the gods, led by Indra, sought the sage Dadhichi's bones to forge a thunderbolt (vajra) to kill the demon Vritra. Dadhichi willingly gave up his life, and Tvashtr fashioned the vajra, which Indra used to slay Vritra.

beats.title: The Slaying of Vritra and the Danavas' Conspiracy
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the battle between Indra and Vritra. The gods, led by Indra, fight the Danavas, but the Kālakeyas overpower them. Indra becomes despondent, but Vishnu and the sages infuse him with their tejas, strengthening him. Vritra roars terrifyingly, and Indra, though afraid, hurls his vajra, killing Vritra. The gods then attack the remaining Danavas, who flee into the ocean and plot to destroy the world by killing all ascetics, as the world is sustained by tapas.

beats.title: The Kāleya Demons and the Gods' Plea to Vishnu
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates how the Kāleya demons, dwelling in the ocean, terrorized the world by killing sages and disrupting sacrifices. The gods, led by Indra, approach Vishnu (Nārāyaṇa) for protection, recounting his previous avatars (Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana) and deeds, and beseech him to save the worlds from the Kāleyas.

beats.title: The Gods Seek Agastya's Aid Against the Kāleya Demons
beats.summary: The gods approach Vishnu, distressed by the nightly killing of brahmins by the Kāleya demons hiding in the ocean. Vishnu reveals that the demons, allies of Vritra, are invincible in the sea and suggests that only Agastya can dry up the ocean. The gods then seek Agastya's help.

beats.title: The Subduing of Vindhya and the Drinking of the Ocean
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Lomasa why the Vindhya mountain suddenly grew in anger. Lomasa narrates that the sun used to circumambulate Mount Meru, but Vindhya demanded the same treatment. When the sun refused, Vindhya began to grow to block the sun and moon's path. The gods sought help from the sage Agastya, who approached Vindhya and asked for a passage to the south, promising to return. Vindhya agreed and stopped growing, but Agastya never returned, so Vindhya remains subdued. Lomasa then recounts how the gods asked Agastya to drink the ocean to destroy the Kāleya demons, which he did.

beats.title: Agastya Drinks the Ocean
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates how the sage Agastya (Maitravaruni) drank the entire ocean to help the gods defeat the Kaleya demons. After the demons were slain, the gods requested Agastya to refill the ocean, but he said the water was digested. The gods then approached Brahma for a solution.

beats.title: The Birth of Sagara's Sons
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of King Sagara, who performed severe penance with his two wives to obtain sons. Lord Shiva granted him a boon: one wife would bear a single son, and the other would bear 60,000 sons, but they would all perish. The wives conceived: Vaidarbhi gave birth to a gourd, and Shaibya to a son. A divine voice instructed the king to preserve the gourd's seeds in warm ghee pots, from which 60,000 sons would be born.

beats.title: The Story of Sagara and His Sons
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of King Sagara and his 60,000 sons. The sons, born through Rudra's grace, become arrogant and torment the worlds. The gods seek Brahma's help, who prophesies their destruction by their own actions. Later, during Sagara's horse sacrifice, the horse is stolen and hidden near the sage Kapila. The sons dig up the earth to find it, causing great destruction, and eventually see the horse and Kapila.

beats.title: The Tale of Sagara's Sons and the Descent of Ganga
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of King Sagara's sons being burnt to ashes by Kapila's anger, and how Amshuman retrieves the sacrificial horse and secures blessings for the ocean's purification, leading to the lineage of Dilipa and Bhagiratha.

beats.title: The Descent of the Ganga
beats.summary: King Sagara's sons are killed by Kapila's wrath. Sagara's descendant Bhagiratha performs severe penance to bring the Ganga to earth to purify their ashes. Ganga appears but warns that only Shiva can hold her descent. Bhagiratha then propitiates Shiva, who agrees to catch Ganga.

beats.title: The Descent of the Ganges
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the descent of the Ganges. King Bhagiratha, after penance, convinces Shiva to hold the river on his head. Shiva agrees, and Ganga descends, is caught in Shiva's matted locks, and then flows to the sea, purifying the ashes of Bhagiratha's ancestors.

beats.title: The Journey to Hemakūṭa and the Curse of Ṛṣabha
beats.summary: The chapter describes the Pāṇḍavas' journey to the Hemakūṭa mountain, where they encounter strange phenomena such as clouds speaking and stones falling. The sage Lomaśa explains that these are due to a curse by the sage Ṛṣabha, who forbade speech and rain in the area. The gods, led by Indra, made the region inaccessible to ordinary humans. Lomaśa advises Yudhiṣṭhira to bathe in the river Nandā to purify himself, then proceed to the Kauśikī river where Viśvāmitra performed severe penance.

beats.title: The Birth and Luring of Rishyashringa
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Rishyashringa, born from a deer who drank the semen of the sage Vibhandaka. Rishyashringa grew up in the forest, never seeing another human. King Lomapada of Anga, abandoned by Brahmins due to a transgression, suffered a drought. On advice, he sent courtesans to lure Rishyashringa to his kingdom to bring rain. An old courtesan volunteered and succeeded.

beats.title: The Seduction of Rishyashringa
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates how a courtesan, sent by King Romapada, seduces the sage Rishyashringa, who had been raised in isolation by his father Vibhandaka. The courtesan creates a hermitage-like setting, gains Rishyashringa's trust, and then uses sensual pleasures to distract him from his ascetic practices. When Vibhandaka returns, he finds his son distraught and questions him.

beats.title: Rishyashringa's Encounter
beats.summary: Rishyashringa describes to his father a mysterious ascetic he met, detailing his divine appearance, ornaments, and actions, and expresses a desire to join him.

beats.title: The Story of R̥śyaśr̥ṅga
beats.summary: Vibhāṇḍaka warns his son R̥śyaśr̥ṅga about demons that tempt ascetics. Despite the warning, R̥śyaśr̥ṅga is lured by a courtesan sent by King Lomapāda to bring rain. The king marries his daughter Śāntā to R̥śyaśr̥ṅga. When Vibhāṇḍaka returns and finds his son gone, he angrily goes to the king's city but is pacified by gifts and hospitality. He blesses the couple and returns to the forest. R̥śyaśr̥ṅga later joins his father, and Śāntā serves him devotedly.

beats.title: The Journey to Vaitaraṇī and the Sacred Altar
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, guided by Lomaśa, travel to the Vaitaraṇī river, where they perform ancestral rites. Lomaśa narrates the story of Rudra claiming a sacrificial share, and the origin of the Vaitaraṇī river. He then shows Yudhiṣṭhira the sacred altar of Svayambhu, where Viśvakarmā performed a sacrifice and gave the earth to Kaśyapa. Lomaśa instructs Yudhiṣṭhira to ascend the altar for spiritual elevation.

beats.title: The Birth of Jamadagni
beats.summary: Yudhishthira and his brothers visit a hermitage and are greeted by sages. Lomasa introduces the sages. Yudhishthira asks Akritavrana about Rama Jamadagnya. Akritavrana narrates the story of Gadhi, his daughter Satyavati, and her marriage to Rcika. Rcika obtains horses from Varuna as bride price. Bhrigu blesses Satyavati and her mother, but due to a mix-up in the ritual, Satyavati's son Jamadagni becomes a Brahmana with Kshatriya conduct, while her mother's son becomes a Kshatriya with Brahmana conduct. Jamadagni excels in the Vedas and weapons.

beats.title: The Story of Jamadagni and Parashurama
beats.summary: Akritavrana narrates the story of Jamadagni and his son Parashurama. Jamadagni marries Renuka, who later lusts after a king and is cursed by Jamadagni. Parashurama beheads his mother at his father's command, then receives boons. Later, King Kartavirya steals Jamadagni's calf, and Parashurama kills him. Kartavirya's sons retaliate by killing Jamadagni while Parashurama is away.

beats.title: Parashurama's Vengeance and Retirement
beats.summary: Rama (Parashurama) laments the death of his father Jamadagni at the hands of Kartavirya's sons, performs funeral rites, vows to exterminate the Kshatriyas, fulfills the vow by killing them twenty-one times, creates lakes of blood, performs a sacrifice, gives the earth to Kashyapa, and retires to Mahendra mountain. Later, he appears to Yudhishthira and his brothers.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Pilgrimage to the Western Tirthas
beats.summary: Yudhishthira travels to various holy tirthas along the coast, performing ablutions, giving donations, and hearing about Arjuna's deeds. He visits the hermitage of Jamadagni (Parashurama), sees altars of various deities, and performs severe penance at Prabhasa. Krishna and Balarama, along with other Vrishnis, visit him, and Yudhishthira recounts their exile and Arjuna's journey to obtain weapons.

beats.title: Balarama's Lament at Prabhasa Tirtha
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the Vrishnis and Pandavas at Prabhasa Tirtha. Vaishampayana narrates that Balarama, seeing Yudhishthira suffering in exile while Duryodhana prospers, questions the value of dharma. He laments the injustice and describes the prowess of the Pandavas, especially Bhima and Arjuna, who endure hardship despite their strength.

beats.title: Satyaki's War Cry and Yudhishthira's Restraint
beats.summary: Satyaki urges Krishna and Balarama to lead the Vrishni army against the Kauravas, but Yudhishthira insists on patience and adherence to dharma. The Vrishni heroes depart, and Yudhishthira continues his pilgrimage.

beats.title: The Story of King Gaya and the Tirthas
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of King Gaya's seven horse sacrifices, where Indra was gratified with Soma and the earth was filled with sacrificial posts. He describes the immense wealth given to Brahmins and the resulting heavenly realms. He then advises Yudhishthira to bathe in the Payoshni river to purify himself. Yudhishthira does so and proceeds to the Vaidurya mountain and Narmada river. Lomasha explains that visiting these places grants liberation and tells the story of Cyavana's anger at Indra and his marriage to Sukanya.

beats.title: The Story of Cyavana and Sukanya
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Cyavana, son of Bhrigu, who performed severe penance and became covered by an anthill. King Sharyati's daughter Sukanya accidentally poked out Cyavana's eyes with a thorn. In anger, Cyavana caused the king's army to suffer from constipation. The king appeased Cyavana by offering his daughter in marriage. Sukanya served her ascetic husband faithfully.

beats.title: Sukanya and Cyavana
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Sukanya, daughter of King Sharyati, who marries the aged sage Cyavana. The Ashvins, divine twin physicians, see her and try to seduce her, offering to make her husband young and handsome. Sukanya remains faithful. Cyavana agrees to the Ashvins' proposal, and all three enter a lake. They emerge identical in youth and beauty. Sukanya chooses her husband Cyavana, who then grants the Ashvins the privilege of drinking Soma, which Indra had previously denied them.

beats.title: Cyavana's Sacrifice and the Creation of Mada
beats.summary: King Sharyati visits the ashram of sage Cyavana, who agrees to perform a sacrifice for him. During the sacrifice, Cyavana offers Soma to the Ashvins, but Indra objects, claiming they are unworthy. Cyavana insists, and when Indra threatens him with a thunderbolt, Cyavana paralyzes Indra's arm and creates a terrifying demon named Mada from his tapas. The demon rushes to devour Indra.

beats.title: Cyavana's Power and the Origin of Mad
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Cyavana and Sukanya, where Cyavana's anger at Indra leads to the creation of Mad (intoxication) and its distribution into wine, women, dice, and hunting. Cyavana then grants the Ashvins soma-drinking rights, performs a sacrifice, and lives with Sukanya in the forest. The chapter also describes sacred sites like the lake, Sikata-aksha, Saindhava forest, Arciika mountain, and the Yamuna river, urging Yudhishthira to perform rituals.

beats.title: The Birth and Glory of King Mandhatri
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Lomasa about the birth and deeds of King Mandhatri. Lomasa narrates how King Yuvanashva, childless, performed a sacrifice; the sage Bhrigu prepared consecrated water for his wife, but the king, thirsty, drank it. As a result, Mandhatri was born from his side. Indra gave him a finger to suck, naming him Mandhatri. He became a great emperor, conquered the three worlds, performed many sacrifices, and even received half of Indra's throne.

beats.title: The Story of King Somaka and the Sacrifice of Jantu
beats.summary: King Yudhishthira asks sage Lomasha about the power and deeds of King Somaka. Lomasha narrates that Somaka had a hundred wives but only one son named Jantu. When an ant bit Jantu, his mothers cried out, causing Somaka distress. He lamented the sorrow of having only one son and sought a way to have a hundred sons. The priest advised him to sacrifice Jantu in a fire ritual; the mothers would inhale the smoke and bear heroic sons, and Jantu would be reborn as his son with a golden mark.

beats.title: The Story of King Somaka and His Priest
beats.summary: King Somaka, desiring sons, performs a sacrifice with his priest, who advises him to sacrifice his son Jantu. The queens resist but the priest cuts out Jantu's fat and offers it. The queens faint and then conceive, and Somaka later has a hundred sons. After death, the priest suffers in hell for the act, and Somaka chooses to share his punishment. Dharma allows them to suffer together for a time and then attain heaven. The chapter ends with Lomasa telling Yudhishthira that staying at the hermitage for six nights brings merit.

beats.title: Sacred Sites along the Yamuna
beats.summary: Lomasha describes the sacred sites along the Yamuna, including the sacrificial grounds of Yayati, the hermitage of Narayana, and the holy tirtha of Plakshavatarana. He explains the significance of bathing there and recounts the story of a pisachi who spoke about the consequences of staying more than one night. The chapter highlights the purifying power of these places and the deeds of ancient kings and sages.

beats.title: Lomasha's Description of Sacred Tirthas
beats.summary: Lomasha describes sacred tirthas and rivers in the Himalayas, including Sarasvati, Sindhu, Prabhasa, Vishnupada, Vipasha, and the region of Kashmir. He recounts stories of sages and kings: Vasishtha's grief, Yavakri's penance, and King Ushinara's sacrifice where Indra and Agni tested him as a hawk and pigeon.

beats.title: The Hawk and the Dove (Shibi's Sacrifice)
beats.summary: King Ushinara (Shibi) protects a dove from a hawk. The hawk argues that depriving him of his natural food is unjust. The king offers other food, but the hawk insists on the dove. Finally, the king offers his own flesh in exchange. When weighed, the dove outweighs all his flesh, so the king himself mounts the scale. The hawk and dove reveal themselves as Indra and Agni, testing the king's dharma. They bless him with eternal glory.

beats.title: The Birth and Journey of Ashtavakra
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of the sage Ashtavakra, who was born with eight deformities due to a curse from his father Kahoda. Ashtavakra's uncle Shvetaketu raised him. When Ashtavakra learned of his father's defeat in a debate by the court scholar Bandi, he went to King Janaka's sacrifice with Shvetaketu. On the way, he encountered the king and spoke wisely.

beats.title: Aṣṭāvakra's Debate with the Gatekeeper and King Janaka
beats.summary: Aṣṭāvakra, a young sage, arrives at King Janaka's sacrifice and debates with the gatekeeper about his right to enter. He argues that true wisdom is not determined by age or appearance. After a series of riddles with the king, Janaka recognizes Aṣṭāvakra's spiritual maturity and grants him entry, offering him the chance to defeat the scholar Bandī.

beats.title: Aṣṭāvakra's Victory over Bandī
beats.summary: Aṣṭāvakra defeats the court scholar Bandī in a debate of numerical enigmas at King Janaka's sacrifice, revealing Bandī as Varuṇa's son and rescuing his father Kahoḍa and other sages from the ocean.

beats.title: The Story of Yavakrita
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates to Yudhishthira the story of Yavakrita, son of Bharadvaja, who performed severe penance to gain Vedic knowledge without a guru. Indra tried to dissuade him, but Yavakrita persisted. Indra then appeared as an old Brahmin trying to dam the Ganga with sand, illustrating the futility of Yavakrita's endeavor. Yavakrita eventually accepted Indra's boon that the Vedas would appear to him and his father.

beats.title: The Tale of Medhavi's Pride and Fall
beats.summary: Yavakri boasts to his father Bharadvaja about obtaining boons to surpass others in Vedic knowledge. Bharadvaja warns him that pride leads to destruction, citing the story of Balaadhi and his son Medhavi. Medhavi, proud of his boon of immortality tied to mountains, insulted sage Dhanushaksha, who then destroyed the mountains, causing Medhavi's death. Bharadvaja advises Yavakri to avoid arrogance and respect the sage Raibhya. Yavakri agrees but later continues to harass other sages.

beats.title: The Death of Yavakrīta
beats.summary: Yavakrīta, a fearless sage, approaches Raibhya's hermitage and attempts to seduce Raibhya's daughter-in-law. She resists and reports to Raibhya, who in anger creates a female entity and a demon from his matted hair. The demon kills Yavakrīta after a chase, and Raibhya then allows the demon to roam with the female entity.

beats.title: Bharadvaja's Lament and Self-immolation
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of Bharadvaja, whose son is killed by a rakshasa after disobeying his father's warning not to visit Raibhya. Bharadvaja laments, curses his elder son, and then cremates his dead son and enters the fire.

beats.title: The Story of Arvavasu and Paravasu
beats.summary: Lomasha narrates the story of King Brihaddyumna's sacrifice, where the sage Raibhya's sons Arvavasu and Paravasu are involved. Paravasu accidentally kills his father Raibhya, mistaking him for a deer. Arvavasu takes on the sin of brahmahatya to allow Paravasu to continue the sacrifice. The gods are pleased and grant boons, including the revival of Raibhya and others. The chapter ends with Lomasha telling Yudhishthira that staying at Raibhya's ashram will purify him.

beats.title: Lomasha's Guidance on the Himalayan Journey
beats.summary: Lomasha guides Yudhishthira through the Himalayan region, describing the sacred geography including the seven-streamed Ganga, the mountain Kailasa, and the abodes of Kubera and yakshas. He warns of dangers from supernatural beings and advises meditation and discipline. Yudhishthira instructs Bhima to protect Draupadi in Arjuna's absence, then comforts his twin brothers.

beats.title: The Journey to Gandhamadana
beats.summary: Yudhishthira and Bhima discuss the difficulty of the journey to Gandhamadana mountain to meet Arjuna. Bhima insists on accompanying Draupadi and his brothers, vowing to carry them if needed. They enter the kingdom of Subahu, who welcomes them, and then proceed on foot towards the Himalayas.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Longing for Arjuna
beats.summary: Yudhishthira expresses his deep longing to see Arjuna, who has been away for five years. He describes Arjuna's virtues, strength, and compassion, and decides to lead his brothers and Draupadi to Mount Gandhamadana, where Arjuna is believed to be. He emphasizes the need for austerity and self-control to reach that place.

beats.title: The Ascent of Gandhamādana and the Great Storm
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, accompanied by Draupadī and brahmins, ascend Mount Gandhamādana. They encounter a fierce storm with wind, dust, and rain, causing chaos. Bhīmasena protects Draupadī under a tree, while others take shelter. After the storm subsides, they continue their journey.

beats.title: Draupadi's Collapse and Ghatotkaca's Arrival
beats.summary: Draupadi, exhausted and distressed by the journey and harsh weather, collapses. Nakula catches her. Yudhishthira laments her suffering, blaming himself for their exile. Dhaumya and other brahmins perform rituals to revive her. Bhima offers to carry everyone himself or summon his son Ghatotkaca, who appears upon being remembered.

beats.title: Journey to Badari and Arrival at Nara-Narayana Ashrama
beats.summary: Yudhishthira instructs Bhima to have Ghatotkacha carry Draupadi and others to the hermitage of Nara-Narayana at Badari. The group travels swiftly with the help of rakshasas, crossing various lands and mountains, and finally arrives at the sacred ashrama. They are welcomed by sages and enjoy the divine surroundings.

beats.title: Bhima's Quest for the Saugandhika Flower
beats.summary: Bhima, seeking the Saugandhika flower for Draupadi, enters the Gandhamadana forest, encounters and defeats various wild animals, and finally meets Hanuman, who questions his actions and advises him to turn back.

beats.title: Bhima Meets Hanuman
beats.summary: Bhima encounters Hanuman, who blocks his path in the form of a monkey. After a failed attempt to move Hanuman's tail, Bhima realizes his brother's greatness and humbly asks for forgiveness. Hanuman reveals his identity, recounts the story of Rama and his own role in the Ramayana, and explains that the path is divine and not meant for mortals.

beats.title: Hanuman describes the four yugas
beats.summary: In this chapter, Hanuman describes the four yugas (ages) to Bhima, explaining how dharma declines and the characteristics of each age, including the changes in human behavior, lifespan, and the nature of the divine.

beats.title: Hanuman's Discourse on Dharma to Bhima
beats.summary: Bhima encounters Hanuman in the forest, who reveals his cosmic form. Hanuman then delivers a discourse on dharma, including the four varnas, statecraft, and the importance of righteous conduct.

beats.title: Bhima Meets Hanuman
beats.summary: Bhima meets Hanuman in the Gandhamadana forest. Hanuman embraces him, relieving his fatigue, and reveals his identity as the brother of Bhima (both sons of Vayu). Hanuman offers boons, and Bhima asks for his presence on Arjuna's chariot flag during the war. Hanuman agrees and disappears. Bhima continues his journey through the forest, seeking the Saugandhika flowers for Draupadi.

beats.title: Bhima's Encounter at the Lotus Lake
beats.summary: Bhima, while on a journey, reaches a beautiful lake on Mount Kailasa near Kubera's abode, guarded by Rakshasas. He approaches it to pick lotuses, and the Rakshasas confront him, asking his purpose.

beats.title: Bhima and the Saugandhika Flowers
beats.summary: Bhima, seeking a fragrant flower for Draupadi, enters a lake guarded by rakshasas who claim it belongs to Kubera. He fights and defeats them, then takes the flowers. The rakshasas report to Kubera, who laughs and allows Bhima to take the flowers.

beats.title: The Quest for the Saugandhika Flowers
beats.summary: Yudhishthira notices ominous portents and becomes anxious about Bhima's absence. Draupadi reveals that Bhima went to fetch the Saugandhika flowers for her. Yudhishthira, along with Nakula, Sahadeva, and the Rakshasas led by Ghatotkacha, follows Bhima to the lake of Kubera. They find Bhima standing guard over the lake after defeating the Yakshas. Yudhishthira admonishes Bhima for his rashness, then they all enjoy the lake. The guardians of the lake appear but bow to them, and the Pandavas stay there for a while.

beats.title: The Abduction by Jatasura
beats.summary: While the Pandavas are living in the forest, the rakshasa Jatasura, disguised as a brahmana, abducts Yudhishthira, Nakula, Sahadeva, and Draupadi. Sahadeva escapes and calls Bhima, who returns and fights Jatasura in a fierce wrestling match, ultimately killing him.

beats.title: The Journey to Gandhamadana
beats.summary: Yudhishthira and his brothers, accompanied by Draupadi and guided by sage Lomasha, journey through the Himalayas to Gandhamadana mountain. They visit the hermitage of King Vrishaparva, leave their belongings, and continue north. They traverse the White Mountain and enter the beautiful Gandhamadana forest, observing its diverse flora, fauna, and celestial beings. Yudhishthira points out the wonders to Bhima, and they eventually reach the hermitage of sage Arshtishena.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Meet a Sage on Gandhamādana
beats.summary: The sage Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates how Yudhiṣṭhira and his brothers, along with Draupadī and Dhaumya, approach the sage who has purified himself by penance. The sage blesses them and inquires about Yudhiṣṭhira's adherence to dharma, respect for elders, and proper conduct even in the forest. He describes the wonders of the Gandhamādana mountain, including celestial beings, music, and the presence of Vaiśravaṇa (Kubera). He advises the Pāṇḍavas to stay there without capriciousness until Arjuna returns, after which Yudhiṣṭhira will rule the earth.

beats.title: Bhīmasena's Ascent and Battle on Gandhamādana
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the Pāṇḍavas' stay on Gandhamādana mountain, their food, and Bhīmasena's exploits. Vaishampayana describes their life, the arrival of siddhas, and a great serpent being carried off by Suparṇa. Draupadī urges Bhīma to take her to the mountain peak. Bhīma ascends, encounters yakṣas and rākṣasas, fights them, and kills the rākṣasa Maṇimān, causing the rest to flee.

beats.title: Bhīma Slays Rākṣasas and Meets Kubera
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, having heard Bhīmasena's roars, climb the mountain and find him having slain many rākṣasas. Yudhiṣṭhira rebukes Bhīma for his rash act. The surviving rākṣasas report to Kubera, who becomes angry and approaches the Pāṇḍavas. Kubera, however, reveals that he was under a curse from Agastya, which is now lifted by Bhīma's actions, and he blesses them.

beats.title: Kubera's Instructions to Yudhiṣṭhira
beats.summary: Kubera (Vaiśravaṇa) instructs Yudhiṣṭhira on the fivefold path of kingship: patience, skill, place and time, valor, and knowledge of worldly governance. He praises Bhīmasena's valor but warns against his rashness, advises Yudhiṣṭhira to stay at Ārṣṭiṣeṇa's hermitage, and assures protection by yakṣas and rākṣasas. He extols Arjuna's virtues and predicts his success. Bhīma pays homage to Kubera, who then departs with his retinue. The rākṣasas' bodies are removed by Kubera's command, and the Pāṇḍavas spend a comfortable night.

beats.title: Dhaumya's Cosmological Discourse
beats.summary: Dhaumya explains to Yudhishthira the geography and cosmology of the world, describing the four directions and their presiding deities, the movement of the sun and moon, and the supreme abode of Vishnu beyond Brahmaloka.

beats.title: Arjuna's Return to the Pāṇḍavas
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, living on Mount Gandhamādana, eagerly await Arjuna's return. They experience joy and beauty of the mountain, but their happiness is incomplete without Arjuna. After a month, Arjuna returns in Indra's chariot driven by Mātali, having obtained divine weapons. He greets his brothers, Draupadī, and the sage Dhaumya, then recounts his journey and the boons received from the gods. The chapter ends with Arjuna resting.

beats.title: Indra's Visit to the Pandavas
beats.summary: Indra visits the Pandavas in the Kamyaka forest, blesses Yudhishthira, and confirms Arjuna has obtained divine weapons. He then returns to heaven.

beats.title: Arjuna's Receipt of the Pashupata Weapon
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Yudhishthira his encounter with Lord Shiva in the form of a Kirata hunter, his battle with him, and the eventual revelation of Shiva's true form. Pleased with Arjuna's valor, Shiva grants him the Pashupata weapon and other divine weapons, instructing him on their use.

beats.title: Arjuna's Journey to Heaven and Acquisition of Celestial Weapons
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Yudhishthira his meeting with Lord Shiva, the subsequent visit of the Lokapalas, and his journey to Indra's heaven where he receives celestial weapons and training.

beats.title: Arjuna's Divine Empowerment and Mission
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Yudhishthira how Indra blessed him with divine weapons, a celestial chariot, armor, and the conch Devadatta, and instructed him to slay the Nivatakavacha demons.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Nivatakavacha Demons
beats.summary: Arjuna describes his journey to the ocean, witnessing its vastness and the city of the Nivatakavacha demons. He blows his conch Devadatta, causing terror, and engages in a fierce battle with the demons, observed by celestial sages.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Nivatakavachas
beats.summary: Arjuna describes his battle against the Nivatakavacha demons, aided by Matali as his charioteer. He uses various celestial weapons, including the Madhava astra, to defeat them. The demons fight back with illusions and heavy weaponry, but Arjuna ultimately prevails.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Nivatakavacha Demons
beats.summary: Arjuna describes to Yudhishthira his battle with the Nivatakavacha demons, where he countered their magical illusions (maya) using divine weapons, including the drying astra and the mohini astra. The charioteer Matali became fearful during the darkness created by the demons, but Arjuna reassured him and ultimately defeated the demons.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Nivatakavachas
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Yudhishthira his battle against the Nivatakavacha demons, who used illusion and mountain-throwing tactics. With Matali's guidance, he used the Vajra astra to defeat them, then entered their city. Matali explained that the demons had obtained the city from Brahma and that Indra had destined Arjuna to be their destroyer.

beats.title: The Destruction of Hiranyapura
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Krishna his battle against the demons in the flying city Hiranyapura, which was granted invulnerability to gods and demons by Brahma. With the help of Matali, Arjuna attacks the city, but the demons prove difficult. He finally invokes the Raudra astra, destroying the city and the demons. Indra praises him.

beats.title: Arjuna's Return with Divine Weapons
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Yudhishthira his visit to Indra's abode, where he received divine weapons, armor, and gifts from the king of gods. Indra assured him of victory over his enemies. Yudhishthira rejoices at Arjuna's success and asks to see the divine weapons. Arjuna promises to show them the next morning.

beats.title: Arjuna's Display of Divine Weapons
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Arjuna to display the divine weapons he received from the gods. As Arjuna prepares to use them, the earth trembles, rivers and oceans are agitated, mountains shatter, the sun dims, and fire ceases to burn. Sages, gods, and creatures gather in distress. Brahma, the Lokapalas, and Shiva with his hosts arrive. Narada, sent by the gods, advises Arjuna not to use the weapons without a proper target, warning of great danger. The gods then depart, and the Pandavas remain in the forest with Draupadi.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Departure from Kubera's Abode
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, having received a boon from Kubera, spend time in the beautiful mountain region. Bhīma and Arjuna urge Yudhiṣṭhira to fulfill his vow and attack Duryodhana, citing their strength and allies. Yudhiṣṭhira agrees, circumambulates Kubera's abode, and departs with his brothers and Ghaṭotkaca, while the sage Lomaśa returns to heaven.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Journey to Dvaitavana
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas travel through various regions, including Kailāsa, Badarī, and the kingdom of Subāhu, eventually reaching the Dvaitavana forest near the Sarasvatī river, where they spend time in exile.

beats.title: Bhīma and the Python
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṁpāyana how the mighty Bhīmasena, who had defeated the Yakṣas and Rākṣasas, came to be terrified by a python. Vaiśaṁpāyana describes Bhīma's journey into the Himavat forest, where he encounters a massive python that seizes him. Despite Bhīma's strength of ten thousand elephants, the python's divine boon renders him powerless.

beats.title: Bhima and the Serpent Nahusha
beats.summary: Bhima is captured by a serpent who reveals himself as the king Nahusha, cursed to become a snake. Bhima laments his fate, worrying about his brothers and mother. Meanwhile, Yudhishthira sees omens and goes searching for Bhima, finding him trapped.

beats.title: Yudhishthira and the Serpent Nahusha
beats.summary: Yudhishthira encounters his brother Bhima trapped by the serpent king Nahusha, who was cursed by Agastya. Through a dialogue on the nature of a Brahmana, Yudhishthira's wisdom frees Bhima.

beats.title: The Liberation of Nahuṣa
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira questions a serpent (Nahuṣa) about the relative merits of charity, truthfulness, non-violence, and kind speech. The serpent explains that their importance varies depending on context. He then describes the threefold path of human, heavenly, and animal births based on actions, and discusses the nature of the self, mind, and intellect. Finally, he reveals his identity as the fallen king Nahuṣa, cursed by Agastya for his pride, and is liberated by Yudhiṣṭhira's questions.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Sojourn in the Forest
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, accompanied by Dhaumya and others, spend the rainy season and autumn in the forest, observing the beauty of nature and the changing seasons, and then proceed to Kāmyaka forest.

beats.title: Krishna's Visit to the Pandavas in Exile
beats.summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in the Kamyaka forest, accompanied by Satyabhama. He greets Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, the twins, Draupadi, and Dhaumya. Krishna praises Yudhishthira's adherence to dharma and recounts the welfare of the Pandavas' children in Dvaraka. He offers the support of the Vrishni army. The sage Markandeya and later Narada arrive, and they prepare to hear ancient stories.

beats.title: The Discourse on Karma and Rebirth
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Markandeya about the nature of karma and its effects in this life and the next. Markandeya explains the decline from a golden age of purity to a degraded age of karma-bound beings, and describes how karma determines rebirth and the fate of the wise versus the ignorant.

beats.title: The Haihaya Prince and the Revived Sage
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates to the Pāṇḍavas a story about a Haihaya prince who accidentally kills a sage in the forest. The prince and his kinsmen seek help from the sage Tārkṣya, who revives the dead sage through his spiritual power. Tārkṣya explains that sages are immune to death because they adhere to truth, follow their dharma, speak only good of Brahmins, and practice hospitality and austerity.

beats.title: The Dispute Over King Vainya's Status
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of the sage Atri who, desiring wealth, goes to King Vainya's Aśvamedha sacrifice. Atri praises Vainya as the supreme dharma-knower, but sage Gautama rebukes him, arguing that Indra is the first among gods. A dispute arises among the sages, who then consult Sanatkumāra. Sanatkumāra declares that the king is the foremost dharma, equal to Indra, Śukra, Dhātā, and Bṛhaspati, and that the king dispels adharma like the sun dispels darkness. Convinced, King Vainya rewards Atri with great wealth, and Atri distributes it to his sons and then proceeds to the forest for penance.

beats.title: Dialogue between Tārkṣya and Sarasvatī
beats.summary: Sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates a dialogue between the sage Tārkṣya and the goddess Sarasvatī on the banks of the Sarasvatī river. Tārkṣya asks about the highest good, how to uphold dharma, and the proper performance of Agnihotra. Sarasvatī explains the fruits of various gifts, the rules for Agnihotra, and describes the supreme state attained by the wise through Vedic study, charity, and austerity.

beats.title: The Matsya Purana: The Story of Manu and the Fish
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Markandeya to narrate the story of Manu Vaivasvata. Markandeya recounts how Manu, while performing severe penance, rescued a small fish from a pond. The fish grew rapidly, and Manu moved it to larger bodies of water. Eventually, the fish revealed itself as the god Brahma (or Vishnu in Matsya avatar) and warned Manu of an impending deluge. It instructed Manu to build a boat, take the seven sages and seeds of all creatures, and tie the boat to its horn. During the flood, the fish towed the boat to the Himalayas. After the flood, the fish revealed its divine identity and disappeared. Manu then created all beings through penance.

beats.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision of the Cosmic Child
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the cosmic cycles of yugas, the decline of dharma in Kali Yuga, and the great dissolution (pralaya). He describes witnessing a divine child (Viṣṇu) in a banyan tree during the deluge, entering his body, and seeing the entire universe within. The child reveals himself as the supreme Lord.

beats.title: Narayana's Revelation to Markandeya
beats.summary: Narayana (Vishnu) reveals his cosmic identity to the sage Markandeya, describing himself as the supreme source and controller of all beings, the creator and destroyer of the universe, and the one who incarnates to restore dharma. He explains his manifestations, the cycle of yugas, and grants Markandeya a boon.

beats.title: The Decline and Renewal of the Yugas
beats.summary: Markandeya describes the progressive decline of dharma through the yugas, culminating in the Kali Yuga with social chaos, moral decay, and natural disasters, followed by the eventual restoration of Krita Yuga with the advent of Kalki.

beats.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Prophecy and Advice
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya describes the future Kali Yuga and the advent of Kalki, who will destroy the barbarians and restore dharma. He then advises Yudhiṣṭhira on righteous conduct, emphasizing compassion, duty, and respect for brāhmaṇas. Yudhiṣṭhira accepts the counsel, and the Pāṇḍavas rejoice.

beats.title: The Tale of King Parikshit and the Frog Princess
beats.summary: King Parikshit of the Ikshvaku dynasty goes hunting, follows a deer into a forest, and meets a beautiful maiden who agrees to marry him on condition that he never shows her water. They live together, but eventually he breaks the promise, leading her to disappear into a pond. In anger, he orders the killing of all frogs. The frog king reveals she is his daughter, and they marry. Their sons are Shala, Dala, and Bala. Shala becomes king and later refuses to return horses borrowed from the sage Vamadeva, leading to a conflict where Shala is killed by rakshasas. Dala succeeds him and also refuses to return the horses, leading to a curse and eventual resolution through Dala's queen.

beats.title: The Restoration of King Indradyumna
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates to the Pāṇḍavas the story of King Indradyumna, who fell from heaven due to exhaustion of his merit. Through a chain of inquiries to progressively older beings (an owl named Ulūka, a crane named Nāḍījaṅgha, and a tortoise named Akūpāra), the king's identity is finally recognized by the tortoise, who recalls the king's pious acts. Indradyumna is then restored to heaven. The chapter concludes with a teaching on the power of good deeds and a reference to Kṛṣṇa rescuing King Nṛga.

beats.title: The Story of Dhundhumara
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks sage Markandeya about King Kuvalashva, who became Dhundhumara. Markandeya narrates the story of sage Uttanka, who pleased Vishnu with severe penance. Vishnu granted him a boon, and Uttanka chose devotion and wisdom. Vishnu then revealed that a demon named Dhundhu would threaten the world, and that King Brihadasva's son Kuvalashva would slay him, becoming Dhundhumara.

beats.title: The Slaying of Dhundhu
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates the lineage of Ikshvaku kings leading to Kuvalashva, and the story of the demon Dhundhu who terrorizes the sage Uttanka's ashram. Uttanka requests King Brhadasva to kill Dhundhu, but Brhadasva has retired to the forest, so his son Kuvalashva undertakes the task with the blessing of Vishnu.

beats.title: The Tale of King Kuvalashva and the Slaying of Madhu and Kaitabha
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates to Yudhishthira the story of King Kuvalashva, who was assigned by his father to assist sage Uttanka. The sage had been troubled by a powerful demon. The story then shifts to a cosmic account of Vishnu sleeping on the serpent Shesha in the cosmic ocean, the emergence of Brahma from a lotus, and the appearance of the demons Madhu and Kaitabha. They challenge Vishnu, who tricks them into choosing their own death, and ultimately kills them on his thighs.

beats.title: The Slaying of Dhundhu
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of Dhundhu, a powerful demon son of Madhu and Kaiṭabha, who after receiving a boon of invincibility from Brahmā, harassed the sage Uttanka's hermitage. King Kuvalāśva, aided by Viṣṇu's power and his twenty-one thousand sons, attacked Dhundhu. Dhundhu killed all but three of the sons with his fiery breath. Kuvalāśva then used a Brahmāstra and yogic water to slay Dhundhu, earning the name Dhundhumāra. The gods granted him boons, and the story is declared purifying and beneficial.

beats.title: The Greatness of Women
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Markandeya about the greatness of women, especially those devoted to their husbands, and the difficulty of their duties. Markandeya explains that serving one's husband is the highest dharma for a woman, surpassing all rituals.

beats.title: The Story of Kauśika and the Housewife
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of the brāhmaṇa Kauśika, who, after accidentally killing a crane with his angry glance, is humbled by a devoted housewife. She teaches him that true dharma lies in controlling anger and serving one's husband, and directs him to a hunter in Mithilā who will further instruct him.

beats.title: The Brāhmaṇa and the Dharma-Vyādha
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of a brāhmaṇa who, after hearing from a woman about a dharma-vyādha (virtuous hunter) in Mithilā, goes to meet him. The vyādha explains his dharma, emphasizing the importance of svadharma, truth, non-violence, and the conduct of the virtuous (śiṣṭācāra). He describes the characteristics of good people and the path to liberation.

beats.title: The Virtuous Hunter's Justification
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya tells Yudhiṣṭhira about a conversation with a dharmavyādha (virtuous hunter) who justifies his violent occupation by citing fate (vidhi), the necessity of svadharma, and the ubiquity of hiṃsā in life. He argues that even ascetics cause harm, and that one should not abandon one's prescribed duty.

beats.title: The Discourse on Dharma and Karma by the Virtuous Hunter
beats.summary: The chapter is a dialogue between the sage Mārkaṇḍeya and Yudhiṣṭhira, where Mārkaṇḍeya recounts a conversation between a brāhmaṇa and a dharmavyādha (virtuous hunter). The hunter explains the subtle nature of dharma, the law of karma, the cycle of rebirth, and the path to liberation through sense control, truthfulness, and detachment.

beats.title: The Discourse of Dharma-Vyadha on the Origin of Dharma and Adharma
beats.summary: The hunter Dharma-Vyadha explains to the Brahmin the process by which desire and aversion lead to adharma, and how wisdom and association with the virtuous lead to dharma. He then describes the 24-element cosmology (the unmanifest, five great elements, their qualities, mind, intellect, ego, five senses, and three gunas).

beats.title: The Dialogue on the Five Elements and Sense Control
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates a dialogue between a brāhmaṇa and a vyādha (hunter) named Dharmavyādha, who explains the nature of the five great elements (pañca mahābhūtas), their qualities, the role of the senses, and the path to self-realization through control of the senses, using the metaphor of a chariot.

beats.title: The Hunter's Discourse on Guṇas, Prāṇas, and Liberation
beats.summary: The hunter Vyādha explains the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas), the nature of the five prāṇas (vital airs), the digestive fire, and the path to liberation through self-knowledge, detachment, and control of desire and anger. He emphasizes non-violence, truthfulness, and renunciation as the highest dharma.

beats.title: The Righteous Hunter's Devotion to Parents
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates to Yudhiṣṭhira the story of a brāhmaṇa who visits a dharmavyādha (righteous hunter) and witnesses his devotion to his parents. The dharmavyādha explains that serving one's parents is the highest dharma, equivalent to worshiping gods and performing sacrifices.

beats.title: The Brāhmaṇa and the Hunter
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates a story about a virtuous brāhmaṇa who, after neglecting his parents, is instructed by a wise vyādha (hunter) to serve them. The vyādha reveals his past life as a brāhmaṇa who accidentally killed a sage and was cursed to be born as a hunter. The brāhmaṇa learns that serving parents is the highest dharma.

beats.title: The Story of the Hunter and the Brāhmaṇa
beats.summary: A hunter (vyādha) recounts how he was cursed by a sage to be born as a Śūdra, but through service to his parents he attained spiritual perfection. A brāhmaṇa then teaches that true brāhmaṇahood is determined by conduct, not birth, and that wisdom overcomes sorrow. The story concludes with Mārkaṇḍeya telling Yudhiṣṭhira that this illustrates the glory of a devoted wife, a brāhmaṇa, and the dharma of serving parents.

beats.title: The Story of Agni and Aṅgiras
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks sage Mārkaṇḍeya about the origin of fire, how Agni became many, and the birth of Kumāra (Skanda). Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of Agni losing his glory due to the penance of sage Aṅgiras, and how Aṅgiras became the fire-god, with Agni becoming his son Bṛhaspati. The chapter explains the multiplicity of fires.

beats.title: Daughters of Aṅgiras
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya describes the lineage of Aṅgiras, listing his seven daughters: Bhānumatī, Rāgā, Sinīvālī, Haviṣmatī, Mahāmatī, and Kuhū, along with their attributes and associations.

beats.title: Genealogy of Agni
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the genealogy of Agni (fire) and his various forms, including their origins, attributes, and roles in rituals. The chapter lists numerous manifestations of Agni, such as Śaṁyu, Bharadvāja, Bharata, Niścyavana, Vipāpa, Niṣkṛti, Svana, Viśvajit, Viśvabhuk, Gopati, Vaḍavāmukha, Ūrdhvabhāṅ, Sviṣṭakṛt, Kāma, Amogha, and Uktha, along with their associated deities and practices.

beats.title: The Origin of the Five Fires
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the origin of the five fires (pañcāgni) and the lineage of fire-gods. Sages including Kāśyapa, Vāsiṣṭha, Prāṇa, Agni Āṅgirasa, and Cyavana performed severe penance to obtain a son. From their meditation emerged a five-colored, radiant being named Pāñcajanya, who then created various fires and deities, including the yajñamuṣaḥ (ritual-stealing gods). The chapter describes how these fires are pacified by mantras and how Agni protects the sacrifice.

beats.title: The Many Forms of Agni
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya describes the various forms and manifestations of Agni (fire), including their names, origins, and associated rituals. The chapter lists many Agnis such as Bharata, Śiva, Manu, Manyumān, Viṣṇu, Āgrayaṇa, Vaiśvānara, Viśvapati, Kapila, and others, along with their attributes and the contexts in which they are worshipped. It also prescribes specific expiatory offerings (iṣṭi) for various transgressions involving the sacred fires.

beats.title: The Origin and Manifestations of Agni
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the origin and manifestations of Agni (fire), including the story of Agni hiding in the ocean, the creation of various metals and gems from his body, and the enumeration of sacred rivers associated with the dhiṣṇyas (altar fires). The chapter emphasizes the unity of Agni despite his many forms.

beats.title: The Birth of Skanda: The Divine General
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the birth of Kārttikeya (Skanda) to Yudhiṣṭhira. The story begins with the gods being defeated by the dānavas. Indra, seeking a leader for the divine army, encounters a maiden named Devasenā who is being harassed by the demon Keśin. Indra rescues her, and she reveals she is the daughter of Prajāpati and desires a husband who can conquer all beings. Indra, seeing celestial omens, realizes that a son born of Agni and the Kṛttikās (the wives of the Saptarṣis) will be that husband. Agni, smitten by the sages' wives, is approached by Svāhā, who assumes their forms to seduce him. The chapter sets the stage for Skanda's birth.

beats.title: The Birth of Skanda
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the birth of Skanda (Kumāra) from Agni's seed deposited in a golden kuṇḍa by Śivā, wife of Aṅgiras, who assumed the forms of the six Kṛttikās. Skanda is born with six heads, twelve eyes, and great strength, wielding a bow and spear, and later splits the Krauñca mountain.

beats.title: The Birth and Early Life of Skanda
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of Skanda's birth and early life. The seven sages' wives are falsely accused of adultery with Agni, leading to their abandonment. Viśvāmitra performs rituals for Skanda, who is revealed as the son of Agni and Svāhā. The gods, fearing Skanda's power, send the Mothers to kill him, but they instead adopt him. Skanda is protected by various beings and ultimately recognized as Mahāsena.

beats.title: The Birth of Viśākha and Skanda's Victory over Indra
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the battle between Skanda (Kārttikeya) and Indra. Indra, seeing the battle uncertain, mounts Airāvata and leads the gods against Skanda. Skanda emits fiery flames that burn the gods' army. The gods abandon Indra and seek refuge in Skanda. Indra then hurls his vajra at Skanda, striking his right side, from which a second being, Viśākha, is born. Fearing Skanda, Indra surrenders, and Skanda grants him and the gods safety.

beats.title: The Origin of Skanda's Attendants and the Matrgana
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates the origin of Skanda's attendants, including the fierce sons and daughters born from the blow of Skanda's vajra. The Matrgana (mothers) request to become mothers of the world, and Skanda grants their wish. The chapter lists the eight heroes (vīrāṣṭaka) born from the Matrgana, and describes Skanda's six heads, with the goat-faced head being the sixth. The story is set on the fifth and sixth lunar days of the bright fortnight of the month of Shukla.

beats.title: The Coronation of Skanda as Commander of the Divine Army
beats.summary: The chapter describes the coronation of Skanda as the commander-in-chief of the divine army by Indra and the gods. It details Skanda's birth from Rudra, Agni, and Svaha, his attributes, and his marriage to Devasena. The narrative emphasizes Skanda's power and the establishment of the sixth lunar day (Shashthi) as auspicious.

beats.title: The Grahas and Skanda's Boons
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of Skanda (Kumāra) and the various grahas (seizers) that afflict children. The chapter describes how Skanda grants boons to the six Kṛttikās, Vinatā, and the Mātṛgaṇa, and how various beings become grahas. It also lists different types of grahas (divine, ancestral, siddha, rākṣasa, gandharva, yakṣa, piśāca) and their effects, along with remedies including worship of Skanda and Maheśvara.

beats.title: The Birth of Skanda and the Origin of the Svāhā Mantra
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates the story of Skanda's birth and his relationship with Svaha. Skanda grants Svaha the boon that she will always be associated with Agni through the svaha mantra. Brahma instructs Skanda to approach Rudra, revealing that Skanda was born from Rudra's seed deposited in Uma's womb, which split into five parts. Skanda honors Rudra. The chapter also describes the various gaṇas (troops) born from the split seed, including the Miñjikāmiñjika and Vr̥ddhikā, and explains the origin of Skanda's bell and banner. The scene concludes with the gods and sages celebrating on the Śvetaparvata.

beats.title: The Birth and Victory of Skanda
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates the story of Skanda's birth and his appointment as general of the divine army. Rudra (Shiva) travels with his retinue to Bhadravata, appoints Skanda to protect the seventh Marut-skandha, and then dismisses him. A great battle ensues between the gods and the asuras, led by Mahisha. The gods are initially routed, but Skanda arrives, kills Mahisha with his spear, and defeats the asura army. Indra praises Skanda, and Rudra returns to Bhadravata. The chapter concludes with the phalaśruti that reciting Skanda's birth brings prosperity and union with Skanda.

beats.title: Draupadi's Discourse on Pativrata Dharma
beats.summary: Draupadi explains to Satyabhama the virtues and practices of a devoted wife, emphasizing humility, service, and self-control, and rejecting the use of spells or potions to control a husband.

beats.title: Draupadi's Advice to Satyabhama
beats.summary: Draupadi instructs Satyabhama on how to win and keep her husband Krishna's affection through devoted service, emphasizing the importance of truth, humility, and careful conduct.

beats.title: Satyabhama's Consolation to Draupadi
beats.summary: Satyabhama consoles Draupadi, assuring her that her hardships will end and she will enjoy the earth with her husbands after defeating the Kauravas. She also updates Draupadi on the well-being of her sons and Subhadra. Then Satyabhama and Krishna depart from the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Armies on the Battlefield of Kurukshetra
beats.summary: King Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas did on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes the armies, the warriors on both sides, and the blowing of conches signaling the start of the war.

beats.title: Karna's Exhortation to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Karna, accompanied by Shakuni, encourages Duryodhana to enjoy the earth conquered by his own valor, now that the Pandavas are exiled. He describes Duryodhana's prosperity and contrasts it with the Pandavas' suffering in the forest, urging him to flaunt his glory before them.

beats.title: The Conspiracy to Visit the Pandavas in Exile
beats.summary: Duryodhana, after hearing Karna's words, expresses his desire to see the Pandavas suffering in the forest. He discusses with Karna and Shakuni a plan to go to Dvaitavana under the pretext of a cattle expedition (ghoshayatra), hoping to get permission from his father Dhritarashtra. They agree on this plan and depart.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Expedition to Dvaita Forest
beats.summary: Duryodhana, with Karna and Shakuni, seeks permission from Dhritarashtra to go to the Dvaita forest for cattle counting and hunting, suspecting the Pandavas are nearby. Dhritarashtra warns against it, fearing conflict with the Pandavas, but reluctantly allows it after Shakuni's persuasion. Duryodhana sets out with a large retinue.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Confrontation with the Gandharvas
beats.summary: King Duryodhana, while staying in the forest, goes to the Dvaitavana lake for recreation. His advance party is stopped by Gandharvas led by their king, who had already occupied the lake. The Gandharvas refuse to let Duryodhana enter, leading to a confrontation.

beats.title: The Battle with the Gandharvas
beats.summary: The Kaurava army, led by Duryodhana, attempts to enter the forest where the Gandharvas are guarding. Despite initial resistance, they force their way in. The Gandharva king Citrasena orders his forces to attack the Kauravas. Karna fights fiercely, but the Gandharvas use their magical powers to overwhelm the Kaurava army. Many Kaurava soldiers flee, but Karna, Duryodhana, and others continue fighting. Eventually, Karna's chariot is destroyed, and he escapes to Vikarna's chariot.

beats.title: The Capture of Duryodhana by Gandharvas
beats.summary: Duryodhana and his army are attacked by Gandharvas led by Citrasena. Duryodhana is captured, and his brothers and wives are also taken. The Kaurava ministers seek help from the Pandavas. Bhima speaks harshly against Duryodhana, but Yudhishthira advises restraint.

beats.title: Rescue of Duryodhana
beats.summary: Yudhishthira instructs Bhima and Arjuna to rescue Duryodhana and the Kauravas who have been captured by Gandharvas. He emphasizes the duty to protect those who seek refuge, even enemies, and outlines a strategy of first using peaceful means, then gentle force, and finally all available methods. Arjuna vows to fight the Gandharvas if necessary.

beats.title: The Battle with the Gandharvas
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, led by Yudhiṣṭhira, prepare for battle against the Gandharvas who have captured the Kauravas. Arjuna attempts a peaceful resolution but ultimately engages in combat when the Gandharvas refuse to release the prisoners.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Gandharvas
beats.summary: Arjuna defeats the Gandharvas in battle, using divine weapons. He spares his friend Citrasena, the Gandharva king, after recognizing him.

beats.title: The Capture and Release of Duryodhana
beats.summary: Arjuna confronts Citrasena, who has captured Duryodhana on Indra's orders. Citrasena explains Duryodhana's evil intent to mock the Pandavas. Arjuna requests Duryodhana's release out of family duty. Citrasena refuses, but Yudhishthira, upon hearing, orders Duryodhana's release and praises the Gandharvas. The Gandharvas depart, and Yudhishthira advises Duryodhana against rashness. Duryodhana returns to Hastinapura ashamed, while the Pandavas continue their exile.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Return After Defeat
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana how Duryodhana, after being defeated and freed by the Pandavas, could enter Hastinapura. Vaishampayana describes Duryodhana's return, his camp, and Karna's speech praising Duryodhana's survival and victory over the Gandharvas, while expressing amazement at their escape.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Defeat and Rescue by the Pandavas
beats.summary: Duryodhana recounts to Karna his defeat by the Gandharvas, his rescue by the Pandavas, and the battle where Arjuna and Citrasena became friends.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Despair and Consolation
beats.summary: Duryodhana, humiliated after being captured by the Gandharva Citrasena and then freed by the Pandavas, contemplates suicide. He laments his shame and considers fasting to death. His brother Duhshasana and friend Karna console him, urging him to rise and rule. Karna argues that it is natural for subjects to rescue their king, and that Duryodhana should not despair.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Despair and Abduction by Danavas
beats.summary: Duryodhana, overwhelmed by defeat and humiliation, decides to fast unto death. His brother Duhshasana and friends try to dissuade him, but he remains firm. Meanwhile, the Danavas (demons) from the underworld, seeing an opportunity, perform a ritual to summon a Kritya (a magical entity) to bring Duryodhana to them. The Kritya succeeds, and Duryodhana is taken to the Danavas, who welcome him.

beats.title: The Danavas Console Duryodhana
beats.summary: The Danavas (demons) reveal to Duryodhana that he is not a mere mortal but a divine being created by them and Lord Shiva. They assure him that many warriors on both sides are possessed by demons or rakshasas, and that Karna will defeat Arjuna. They also mention that Indra will trick Karna out of his armor and earrings. Duryodhana is consoled and resolves to fight. Karna later encourages Duryodhana, promising to kill Arjuna. The Kaurava army then marches out.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Vaishnava Sacrifice
beats.summary: After the Pandavas leave Hastinapura and Duryodhana is freed by them from the Gandharvas, Bhishma rebukes Duryodhana for his arrogance and praises the Pandavas. Duryodhana, stung by the criticism, consults with Karna and others. Karna advises him to enjoy his sovereignty. Duryodhana expresses desire to perform the Rajasuya sacrifice like Yudhishthira. The priest explains that Rajasuya is not possible while Yudhishthira and Dhritarashtra live, and suggests a substitute Vaishnava sacrifice. Duryodhana agrees and orders preparations.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Grand Sacrifice
beats.summary: Duryodhana performs a grand sacrifice (Rajasuya-like) in Hastinapura. He sends an invitation to the Pandavas in exile, but Yudhishthira declines, citing their vow of thirteen years. Bhima angrily retorts that they will come only to a 'battle-sacrifice' after the exile ends. The sacrifice proceeds with great pomp, and after completion, Duryodhana returns to Hastinapura.

beats.title: Karna's Vow and Yudhishthira's Anxiety
beats.summary: After Duryodhana's Rajasuya sacrifice, Karna vows to kill Arjuna. Yudhishthira becomes anxious and considers leaving Dvaitavana forest. Duryodhana rules the earth with his brothers and Karna, performing sacrifices and pleasing brahmins.

beats.title: The Pandavas Move to Kamyaka Forest
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana what the Pandavas did after freeing Duryodhana. Vaishampayana narrates that while sleeping in Dvaitavana, Yudhishthira is approached in a dream by the surviving deer of the forest, who plead with him to leave because the Pandavas' hunting has decimated their families. Yudhishthira, compassionate, agrees and decides to move to Kamyaka forest. The Pandavas, along with brahmins and servants, travel to Kamyaka, a beautiful and holy hermitage.

beats.title: Vyasa's Consolation and Teaching on Austerity and Charity
beats.summary: Vyasa visits the Pandavas in the forest, consoles Yudhishthira, and teaches him about the importance of tapas (austerity), dharma, and charity, emphasizing that charity is the most difficult virtue. He narrates the story of Mudgala who attained great fruit by giving a measure of rice.

beats.title: The Story of Mudgala and the Test of Detachment
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Vyasa about the sage Mudgala who lived by gleaning grains (shilochchha) and how he attained heaven. Vyasa narrates the story of Mudgala, a virtuous sage who practiced the pigeon-vow, offering food to guests even when he had little. The sage Durvasa repeatedly visited him, eating all his food, yet Mudgala remained unperturbed. Pleased, Durvasa declared Mudgala's greatness. Later, a celestial messenger arrived with a divine chariot to take Mudgala to heaven, but Mudgala inquired about the qualities and defects of heaven before deciding.

beats.title: The Dialogue of Mudgala and the Celestial Messenger
beats.summary: The celestial messenger describes the heavenly realms to the sage Mudgala, detailing their pleasures and limitations, including the eventual fall from heaven due to exhaustion of merit. Mudgala rejects heaven, seeking instead the eternal state beyond sorrow, and attains liberation through meditation and yoga. The chapter concludes with Vyasa advising Yudhishthira to overcome grief and regain his kingdom.

beats.title: Jayadratha's Encounter with Draupadī
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, while living in the Kāmyaka forest, go hunting, leaving Draupadī alone in the hermitage. King Jayadratha of Sindhu, on his way to marry a princess, sees Draupadī and becomes infatuated. He sends his minister Koṭikāśya to inquire about her identity.

beats.title: Kotikashya Encounters a Mysterious Woman
beats.summary: King Kotikashya, accompanied by other kings, encounters a beautiful woman standing alone in a forest. He asks her identity, listing possible divine or demonic origins, and introduces himself and his companions: King Kshemankara of Trigarta, the son of the Kuninda king, Prince Subala of Ikshvaku, and King Jayadratha of Sauvira with his brothers. He asks her to reveal her husband and father.

beats.title: Draupadi meets the prince of the Shibis
beats.summary: Draupadi, while alone in the forest, encounters the prince of the Shibis, Kotikashya (also called Shaibya). She identifies herself as the daughter of Drupada and wife of the five Pandavas. She explains that her husbands have gone hunting in the four directions and are expected back soon. She invites him to stay as a guest, promising that Yudhishthira will honor him. She then enters her hut.

beats.title: Jayadratha's Attempt to Abduct Draupadi
beats.summary: Jayadratha, king of Sindhu and Sauvira, sees Draupadi in the forest and becomes infatuated. He approaches her in the hermitage, insults the Pandavas, and tries to persuade her to abandon them and marry him. Draupadi rebukes him and waits for her husbands to return.

beats.title: The Abduction of Draupadi
beats.summary: Draupadi, abducted by Jayadratha, rebukes him with fierce threats, invoking the prowess of her husbands, especially Arjuna and Bhima. Jayadratha dismisses her warnings, claiming superiority of his lineage. Draupadi is forcibly taken onto his chariot, but Dhaumya follows, warning Jayadratha of the consequences.

beats.title: The Abduction of Draupadī
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, while hunting in the forest, hear ominous animal cries. Yudhiṣṭhira senses disaster and they return to the hermitage to find Draupadī abducted by Jayadratha. The charioteer Indrasena learns from a maidservant that Jayadratha has taken her. The Pāṇḍavas, enraged, pursue Jayadratha's army.

beats.title: Draupadi's Captivity and the Approach of the Pandavas
beats.summary: In this chapter, Draupadi, while captured by Jayadratha, identifies the approaching five Pandava warriors to him, describing their virtues and prowess. She warns him of their invincibility and predicts the destruction of his army.

beats.title: The Rescue of Draupadi and the Defeat of Jayadratha
beats.summary: After Jayadratha abducts Draupadi, the Pandavas pursue and defeat the allied kings (Sibi, Trigarta, Sindhu, etc.). Bhima and Arjuna chase Jayadratha, but Yudhishthira spares him for Duhshala's sake. Draupadi demands his death, but Arjuna shows mercy. The Pandavas return to the hermitage.

beats.title: The Capture and Release of Jayadratha
beats.summary: Bhima captures Jayadratha after the latter attempts to abduct Draupadi. Bhima beats him, shaves his head, and forces him to declare himself a slave. Yudhishthira and Draupadi intervene to release him. Jayadratha then performs penance to Shiva, who grants him a boon to defeat all Pandavas except Arjuna, who is protected by Krishna.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana what the Pandavas did after Draupadi was rescued. Yudhishthira, surrounded by sages, laments the misfortunes they have suffered: Draupadi's abduction, their exile, and the violence of hunting. He asks Markandeya if there is anyone more unfortunate than him.

beats.title: The Story of Rama
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates the story of Rama to Yudhishthira, covering Rama's lineage, the abduction of Sita by Ravana, and Rama's alliance with Sugriva to rescue her. It also details the origins of Ravana and his brother Kubera.

beats.title: The Birth and Boons of Rāvaṇa and His Siblings
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the origin of Rāvaṇa and his siblings. Viśravā, born from Pulastya's anger, had sons by three wives given by Kubera. Rāvaṇa, Kumbhakarṇa, Vibhīṣaṇa, Khara, and Śūrpaṇakhā were born. They performed severe penance to please Brahmā, who granted boons: Rāvaṇa invincibility except from humans, Kumbhakarṇa a long sleep, and Vibhīṣaṇa devotion to dharma and the Brahmāstra. Rāvaṇa then defeated Kubera, seized Laṅkā and the Puṣpaka vimāna, and was cursed by Kubera. Vibhīṣaṇa remained righteous and was appointed general of the yakṣa and rākṣasa armies.

beats.title: The Descent of the Gods to Aid Viṣṇu
beats.summary: The sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates how the gods, led by Agni, sought refuge in Brahmā because of the oppression by Rāvaṇa, the ten-headed demon king. Brahmā revealed that Rāvaṇa was invincible to gods and demons due to a boon, but that Viṣṇu had incarnated as a four-armed being to deal with him. Brahmā instructed the gods to take birth on earth as allies of Viṣṇu, specifically as offspring of bears and monkeys. The gods agreed, and Brahmā also commanded the gandharvī Dundubhī to be born as the hunchback Mantharā to instigate conflict. The divine beings thus descended to earth, taking various forms.

beats.title: The Story of Rama's Exile
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Markandeya to narrate the story of Rama. Markandeya recounts Rama's birth, his virtues, the plan for his coronation, Kaikeyi's manipulation leading to Rama's exile, the death of Dasharatha, Bharata's refusal of the throne and his search for Rama, Rama's life in the forest, the killing of Khara and Dushana, and Ravana's decision to abduct Sita after hearing from Shurpanakha.

beats.title: The Abduction of Sītā
beats.summary: Mārīca warns Rāvaṇa against confronting Rāma, but Rāvaṇa insists. Mārīca transforms into a golden deer to lure Sītā. Rāma pursues the deer, leaving Lakṣmaṇa to guard Sītā. Mārīca, struck by Rāma's arrow, cries out in Rāma's voice. Sītā, hearing it, forces Lakṣmaṇa to go to Rāma's aid. Rāvaṇa, disguised as a mendicant, approaches Sītā, reveals his identity, and tries to abduct her. Jaṭāyu sees Sītā being taken.

beats.title: Jatayu's Fall and the Slaying of Kabandha
beats.summary: Jatayu, the vulture king and friend of Dasharatha, attempts to rescue Sita from Ravana but is fatally wounded. Rama and Lakshmana find him, learn of Sita's abduction, and perform his funeral rites. They then encounter the demon Kabandha, whom they slay, freeing the Gandharva Vishvavasu. He advises Rama to seek Sugriva's help.

beats.title: Rāma's Alliance with Sugrīva and the Imprisonment of Sītā
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates Rāma's journey after Sītā's abduction: he meets Sugrīva, makes an alliance, kills Vālī, installs Sugrīva as king, and spends four months on Malaya mountain. Meanwhile, Rāvaṇa keeps Sītā in Aśoka grove, guarded by rākṣasīs. Trijaṭā comforts Sītā with a dream prophecy of Rāma's victory.

beats.title: Ravana's Seduction Attempt and Sita's Rejection
beats.summary: Ravana approaches Sita in the Ashoka grove, attempts to seduce her with his power and wealth, but Sita rebukes him, remaining devoted to Rama. Ravana disappears after being rejected.

beats.title: Hanumān's Return and the Finding of Sītā
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates how Rāma, after a period of waiting, becomes impatient with Sugrīva's apparent delay in searching for Sītā. He sends Lakṣmaṇa to remind Sugrīva of his promise. Sugrīva reassures Lakṣmaṇa that he has already dispatched monkeys in all directions and that the month-long deadline is almost over. The monkeys return from the north, east, and west without success, but those sent south under Aṅgada and Hanumān have not yet returned. After two months, news arrives that Hanumān has found Sītā in Laṅkā. Hanumān recounts his journey: crossing the ocean, meeting Sampāti (who confirms Sītā's location), and finally finding Sītā in Rāvaṇa's palace. He gives Rāma a token from Sītā, confirming her identity.

beats.title: The March to Laṅkā and the Building of the Bridge
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates to Yudhiṣṭhira how Rāma, with Sugrīva's help, gathered a vast army of monkeys and bears, marched to the ocean, and after consulting with his army, decided to dry up the ocean. The ocean god appeared in a dream and advised Rāma to build a bridge with the help of Nala, son of Viśvakarman. Rāma built the bridge, crossed to Laṅkā, and received Vibhīṣaṇa, who defected from Rāvaṇa. Rāma crowned Vibhīṣaṇa king of the rākṣasas and sent Aṅgada as an envoy to Rāvaṇa.

beats.title: The Siege of Laṅkā
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the siege of Laṅkā. Rāma's army encamps in a forest. Rāvaṇa fortifies Laṅkā. Aṅgada delivers Rāma's ultimatum to Rāvaṇa, who reacts angrily. Aṅgada escapes, then Rāma orders a general assault. The monkeys breach the wall, but Rākṣasas counterattack. Fierce combat ensues. Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa use arrows to kill many Rākṣasas. Finally, Rāma orders a tactical withdrawal.

beats.title: The Battle of Rāma and Rāvaṇa
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the battle between Rāma and Rāvaṇa's forces. Rāvaṇa's demon allies attack but are killed by Vibhīṣaṇa's strategy. Rāvaṇa then leads his army in a formation, countered by Rāma. Individual duels ensue: Rāma vs Rāvaṇa, Lakṣmaṇa vs Indrajit, and others. The battle terrifies the three worlds.

beats.title: The Fall of Prahasta and Dhūmrākṣa, and the Awakening of Kumbhakarṇa
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the battle between Rāma's monkey army and Rāvaṇa's rākṣasas. Prahasta is killed by Vibhīṣaṇa, and Dhūmrākṣa is killed by Hanūmān. The surviving rākṣasas report to Rāvaṇa, who then awakens Kumbhakarṇa and orders him to fight Rāma, sending Vajravega and Pramāthin as his lieutenants.

beats.title: The Slaying of Kumbhakarna and His Lieutenants
beats.summary: Kumbhakarna, awakened from his long sleep, attacks the monkey army. He kills several monkeys including Panasa, Gavaksha, and Vajrabahu. Sugriva attacks him with a sala tree but is captured. Lakshmana rescues Sugriva by shooting arrows at Kumbhakarna, cutting off his hands, and finally using the Brahmastra to kill him. Then the two brothers of Dushana (Vajravega and Pramathin) attack Lakshmana. Hanuman kills Vajravega with a mountain peak, and Nila kills Pramathin with a boulder. The battle continues between the armies of Rama and Ravana.

beats.title: Indrajit's Battle with Lakshmana and Rama
beats.summary: Ravana, enraged by the deaths of Kumbhakarna and Prahasta, commands his son Indrajit to kill Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva. Indrajit engages Lakshmana in battle, using his magical powers to become invisible and shower arrows. Angada intervenes but is struck. Indrajit's chariot is destroyed by a tree thrown by Angada, but Indrajit disappears and continues attacking Rama and Lakshmana with divine arrows, causing them to fall unconscious.

beats.title: The Fall of Indrajit
beats.summary: Indrajit (Ravana's son) binds Rama and Lakshmana with a serpentine weapon. Vibhishana revives them with a divine herb and brings water from Kubera that grants the ability to see invisible beings. Lakshmana then fights and kills Indrajit. Ravana, enraged, attempts to kill Sita but is dissuaded by Avindhya.

beats.title: Rama's Return and Reunion
beats.summary: After killing Ravana, Rama is reunited with Sita but doubts her chastity. Sita's purity is affirmed by the gods, including Brahma, Agni, Vayu, and Varuna. Rama's father Dasharatha appears and blesses him. Rama returns to Ayodhya, is crowned king, and performs ashvamedha sacrifices.

beats.title: Markandeya's Consolation
beats.summary: Markandeya consoles Yudhishthira by recounting past hardships of heroes like Rama and Indra, emphasizing that with powerful allies like the Pandavas, he will overcome his enemies. He cites the rescue of Draupadi and capture of Jayadratha as evidence of their strength.

beats.title: The Story of Savitri
