beats.summary: Yudhishthira laments the suffering of Draupadi and his brothers, and asks Markandeya if there has ever been a woman as devoted as Draupadi. Markandeya responds by narrating the story of Savitri, a princess who chose her own husband, Satyavan, despite knowing he would die within a year. Through her unwavering devotion and penance, she wins his life back from Yama, the god of death.

beats.title: Sāvitrī's Choice
beats.summary: King Aśvapati asks Nārada about his daughter Sāvitrī's chosen husband Satyavān. Nārada reveals Satyavān's virtues but also that he will die in one year. Sāvitrī insists on marrying him despite his short lifespan, and Aśvapati agrees after Nārada's counsel.

beats.title: Sāvitrī's Marriage to Satyavān
beats.summary: King Aśvapati gives his daughter Sāvitrī in marriage to Satyavān, the son of the blind king Dyumatsena, who lives in a forest hermitage. Sāvitrī adapts to her new life, serving her in-laws and husband with devotion, while remembering the prophecy of Nārada.

beats.title: Savitri and Satyavan: The Vigil Before Death
beats.summary: Satyavan's death is imminent. Savitri undertakes a three-day fast and vigil, then accompanies Satyavan into the forest despite his protests. She observes the beauty of nature but is inwardly focused on the predicted moment of his death.

beats.title: Savitri and Satyavan
beats.summary: Savitri follows Yama as he takes her husband Satyavan's soul, and through her wisdom and devotion, she secures boons from Yama, ultimately restoring Satyavan's life.

beats.title: Sāvitrī's Devotion Restores Sight and Kingdom
beats.summary: Sage Mārkaṇḍeya narrates the story of King Dyumatsena, who regained his sight and found his son Satyavān alive due to the devotion and penance of his daughter-in-law Sāvitrī. The sages in the hermitage reassure the king about his son's fate, and later Sāvitrī returns with Satyavān, explaining how she secured his life from Yama through her truthfulness and austerities.

beats.title: Conclusion of Savitri's Story and Its Application to the Pandavas
beats.summary: Markandeya concludes the story of Savitri, describing how after the night passed, the sages gathered and repeatedly narrated Savitri's great fortune to Dyumatsena. Then the citizens of Salva arrived, announcing that the king had been killed by his minister, and the people desired Dyumatsena as their king. Dyumatsena, now with restored sight, returned to his kingdom with Savitri and his wife, was crowned king, and his son was made crown prince. In time, Savitri gave birth to a hundred heroic sons, and her brother also had a hundred sons. Thus Savitri rescued her family from distress. Markandeya then tells Yudhishthira that Draupadi, like Savitri, will deliver them all. Vaisampayana concludes that Yudhishthira, consoled, lived in Kamyaka forest without grief.

beats.title: Indra Begs Karna's Armor and Earrings
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the fear that Yudhishthira had regarding Karna, which he never revealed. Vaishampayana narrates the story of how Indra, disguised as a Brahmin, approached Karna to beg for his natural armor and earrings. Surya, Karna's father, warned him in a dream about Indra's plan and advised him not to give them. However, Karna, committed to his vow of never refusing a Brahmin's request, decides to give them to Indra despite knowing it would lead to his death. He values fame and honor above life.

beats.title: Surya's Warning to Karna
beats.summary: Surya, the sun god, advises Karna against giving away his divine earrings (kundalas) to Indra, who is approaching as a brahmin beggar. Surya emphasizes the importance of living to enjoy fame and warns that without the earrings, Karna will be vulnerable to Arjuna in battle. He urges Karna to refuse Indra's request.

beats.title: Karna's Vow and Surya's Counsel
beats.summary: Karna declares his unwavering devotion to Surya, who advises him to give his divine earrings to Indra in exchange for a boon. Karna agrees and awaits Indra.

beats.title: The Secret of Karna's Armor and Earrings
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṁpāyana about the secret of Karna's earrings and armor. Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates how a Brahmana (later revealed as Surya) came to Kuntibhoja's court and was entrusted to the care of Pritha (Kunti). Kuntibhoja instructs Kunti to serve the Brahmana diligently, warning of the dangers of angering Brahmins.

beats.title: Kunti's Vow to Serve the Brahmana
beats.summary: Kunti (Pritha) promises her father Kuntibhoja to serve a Brahmana guest with utmost devotion, regardless of when he arrives. Kuntibhoja entrusts her to the Brahmana, who accepts. Pritha diligently serves him, overcoming her own pride and fatigue.

beats.title: Kunti's Boon from the Brahmana
beats.summary: Kunti, as a young girl, serves a strict Brahmana with devotion for a year. Pleased, he offers her a boon, but she declines. He then gives her a mantra to invoke any god, who will then be under her control. Kunti accepts, fearing his curse. The Brahmana departs, leaving Kunti and her father amazed.

beats.title: The Birth of Karna: Kunti's Test of the Mantra
beats.summary: Kunti, curious about the power of the mantra given by the sage Durvasa, summons the sun god Surya. Surya appears and insists that she must accept him, as it is improper to summon a deity in vain. He threatens to curse her and her father if she refuses. Kunti, frightened, explains her maidenhood and that she only wanted to test the mantra. Surya reassures her that she will bear a son equal to him and become renowned. Kunti eventually agrees, and they unite, resulting in the birth of Karna.

beats.title: The Birth of Karna
beats.summary: Kunti, as a young maiden, is approached by the Sun god Surya. Initially frightened and reluctant due to fear of social stigma and her father's curse, she eventually agrees to a union after Surya assures her that she will remain a virgin and bear a son with divine armor and earrings. The union occurs through yogic power, and Kunti conceives Karna.

beats.title: The Abandonment of Karna
beats.summary: Kunti, fearing social stigma, abandons her newborn son Karna (born from Surya) in a basket on the river. She laments and prays for his protection. The basket floats from the Aśvanadī to the Gaṅgā and eventually reaches Campā, where the child is found and raised by a charioteer.

beats.title: The Birth and Early Life of Karna
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth and early life of Karna, the son of Surya, who was abandoned by his mother Kunti and found and raised by the suta Adhiratha and his wife Radha. It describes his growth, his friendship with Duryodhana, his training under Drona, Kripa, and Parashurama, and his rivalry with Arjuna. The chapter also mentions Indra's visit to Karna in the guise of a Brahmana to beg for his divine armor and earrings.

beats.title: Karna's Armor and Earrings
beats.summary: Indra, disguised as a Brahmana, asks Karna for his natural armor and earrings. Karna initially refuses but eventually agrees to exchange them for Indra's infallible spear (shakti), with the condition that it can only be used once and will return to Indra after killing a single powerful enemy. Karna cuts off his armor and earrings, impressing the gods. The Kauravas are dismayed, while the Pandavas rejoice upon hearing the news.

beats.title: The Pursuit of the Deer and Nakula's Lament
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana what the Pandavas did after recovering Draupadi. Vaishampayana narrates that they left Kamyaka forest and went to Dvaitavana, near the hermitage of Markandeya. A Brahmana's fire-sticks (araṇī) were carried off by a deer. The Pandavas pursued the deer but failed to catch it. Exhausted, they rested under a banyan tree. Nakula then lamented their misfortune, questioning how dharma could fail them.

beats.title: The Yaksha's Lake
beats.summary: Yudhishthira and his brothers, exhausted and thirsty during their exile, encounter a lake guarded by a Yaksha. One by one, Nakula, Sahadeva, Arjuna, and Bhima go to fetch water but ignore the Yaksha's condition to answer questions first, and they fall dead. Finally, Yudhishthira approaches, answers the Yaksha's questions, and revives his brothers.

beats.title: Yakṣa Praśna
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira finds his brothers dead by a lake, suspects foul play, but upon investigation encounters a Yakṣa who challenges him to answer philosophical questions. Yudhiṣṭhira answers correctly, and the Yakṣa revives his brothers, revealing the importance of dharma, compassion, and equanimity.

beats.title: Yaksha Prashna: The Meeting with Dharma
beats.summary: Yudhishthira meets the Yaksha, who reveals himself as Dharma (Yama). Dharma tests Yudhishthira, grants him boons, and promises the Pandavas will remain incognito during their thirteenth year of exile. The chapter ends with Dharma disappearing and the Pandavas returning the Brahmana's fire-sticks.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Prepare for Incognito Exile
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, having completed twelve years of exile, seek permission from the brāhmaṇas to begin their thirteenth year of incognito residence. Yudhiṣṭhira expresses grief and anxiety about the upcoming concealment, fearing the machinations of Duryodhana and his allies. The priest Dhaumya consoles him by citing examples of gods and sages who lived incognito to defeat their enemies. Bhīmasena encourages Yudhiṣṭhira, and the brāhmaṇas bless them before departing. The Pāṇḍavas then set out with Draupadī to begin their ajñātavāsa.

beats.title: The Pandavas Plan Their Incognito Year
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana how the Pandavas lived incognito in Virata's city. Vaishampayana recounts that after obtaining boons from Dharma, Yudhishthira told the Brahmins everything. Then he gathered his brothers and discussed where to spend the thirteenth year. Arjuna suggested various kingdoms, and Yudhishthira chose Virata's kingdom. He decided to become a brahmin named Kanka and serve as a courtier, while each brother would take a different role.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Disguises
beats.summary: In this chapter, the Pandavas discuss their disguises for the thirteenth year of exile. Bhima proposes to serve as a cook and wrestler, while Arjuna decides to pose as a eunuch named Brihannada, teaching dance and music in Virata's court. Yudhishthira praises Arjuna's prowess and recounts his past deeds.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Disguises in Virata's Court
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks his brothers and Draupadi how they will disguise themselves in the court of King Virata. Nakula chooses to be a horse-trainer, Sahadeva a cowherd, and Draupadi a maidservant skilled in hairdressing. Yudhishthira expresses concern for Draupadi's safety, but she reassures him.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Departure and Dhaumya's Advice
beats.summary: Yudhishthira instructs his brothers and servants to depart for Dwaraka and Panchala while he and his brothers go into hiding in the court of King Virata. Dhaumya gives extensive advice on how to behave as a servant in a king's court to avoid detection and danger. The chapter ends with the Pandavas performing rituals and departing.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Enter Virāṭa in Disguise
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, disguised as brāhmaṇas, travel through forests and mountains, hunting for food, and eventually approach the city of Virāṭa. They hide their weapons in a śamī tree near a cremation ground, tying a corpse to the tree to deter people. Yudhiṣṭhira assigns them secret names for their incognito year. They enter the city to begin their ajñātacarya.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Arrival at Virata's Court
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, disguised as a brahmin named Kanka, approaches King Virata in his court and requests shelter. Virata grants him a boon, and Yudhishthira asks to be allowed to stay and play dice. Virata accepts him as a friend and gives him authority in the kingdom.

beats.title: Bhima's Disguise as a Cook
beats.summary: Bhima, disguised as a cook named Ballava, approaches King Virata seeking employment. Despite his appearance, Virata recognizes his extraordinary qualities and appoints him as head cook, though Bhima's true identity remains hidden.

beats.title: Draupadi's Disguise as Sairandhrī
beats.summary: Draupadi, disguised as a maidservant named Malini, seeks employment in King Virata's palace. Queen Sudeṣṇā, suspicious of her divine appearance, questions her identity. Draupadi claims to be a sairandhrī (maidservant) skilled in hairdressing and garland-making, and says she served Satyabhāmā and Draupadi (herself). Sudeṣṇā fears her beauty will distract the king and men, but Draupadi assures her that she is protected by five Gandharva husbands. Sudeṣṇā agrees to keep her, with conditions.

beats.title: Sahadeva's Disguise as a Cowherd
beats.summary: Sahadeva, disguised as a cowherd named Arishtanemi, approaches King Virata seeking employment. He claims to be a Vaisya skilled in cattle management and forecasting. Virata, impressed by his appearance and knowledge, hires him as superintendent of cattle, providing him with wealth and protection.

beats.title: Arjuna's Disguise as Br̥hannaḍā
beats.summary: Arjuna, disguised as a eunuch named Br̥hannaḍā, approaches King Virāṭa and offers to teach dance and music to his daughter Uttarā. Virāṭa accepts and assigns him to the women's quarters, where Arjuna lives incognito.

beats.title: Nakula's Disguise as a Horse-Trainer
beats.summary: Nakula, disguised as a horse-trainer named Granthika, approaches King Virata and offers his services. Virata, impressed by his demeanor and skills, accepts him and places all his horses under Nakula's care. The Pandavas continue living incognito in Matsya, fulfilling their vow of exile.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Life in Virāṭa's Court and Bhīma's Wrestling Victory
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas continue their incognito life in Virāṭa's court, each serving in a disguised role. After four months, a grand festival occurs featuring wrestlers. Bhīma, as a cook, is compelled to fight and defeats a renowned wrestler, earning Virāṭa's favor. Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva also please the king through their respective skills.

beats.title: Kicaka's Lust for Draupadi
beats.summary: Kicaka, the commander-in-chief of King Virata, sees Draupadi (disguised as a maidservant named Sairandhri) and becomes infatuated. He approaches Queen Sudeshna to express his desire, then directly propositions Draupadi. Draupadi rebuffs him, warning him of the danger from her husbands (the Gandharvas), but Kicaka persists.

beats.title: Kicaka's Lust and Draupadi's Prayer
beats.summary: Kicaka, driven by lust for Draupadi (disguised as a maidservant), asks his sister Queen Sudeshna to arrange a meeting. Sudeshna, though reluctant, devises a plan: she orders Draupadi to fetch wine from Kicaka's house. Draupadi protests, citing Kicaka's shamelessness and her own vow of fidelity to her husbands. Forced to go, she prays to the Sun god for protection. The Sun god sends a rakshasa to guard her invisibly. Kicaka, seeing her, rises joyfully.

beats.title: Kicaka's Assault on Draupadi
beats.summary: Kicaka, the commander of King Virata's army, attempts to seduce Draupadi (disguised as a maidservant). When she refuses, he kicks her in the presence of the court. Bhima, enraged, is restrained by Yudhishthira. Draupadi laments the inaction of her husbands and Virata's weak response. She prophesies Kicaka's imminent death.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament and Bhima's Vow
beats.summary: Draupadi, humiliated by Kichaka (Sūtaputra), seeks Bhima's help. She wakes him and urges him to avenge her. Bhima reassures her of his support.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament to Bhima
beats.summary: Draupadi laments to Bhima about the repeated humiliations she has suffered, including being dragged into the court by Pratikami, being touched by Kichaka in Virata's palace, and the loss of their kingdom due to Yudhishthira's gambling addiction. She criticizes Yudhishthira's passivity and questions the value of her life amidst such suffering.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament in Virata's Court
beats.summary: Draupadi laments the degraded condition of the Pandavas living incognito in King Virata's court. She describes each brother's fall from glory: Arjuna as a dance teacher, Bhima as a cook, Yudhishthira as a dice player, Nakula as a horse trainer, and Sahadeva as a cowherd. She expresses her deep sorrow and humiliation, especially when observed by Queen Kaikeyi and other women.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament in Virata's Court
beats.summary: Draupadi, disguised as a maidservant in King Virata's court, laments her suffering to Bhima. She describes her servitude to Queen Sudeṣṇā, her calloused hands from grinding sandalwood, and her fear of being discovered. She reflects on the fickleness of fortune and the role of fate, while Bhima weeps with her.

beats.title: Bhima consoles Draupadi and vows to kill Kichaka
beats.summary: Bhima consoles Draupadi after Kichaka's harassment, recounting past grievances and citing examples of devoted wives. Draupadi urges Bhima to kill Kichaka, who has been pursuing her. Bhima vows to act.

beats.title: The Killing of Kicaka
beats.summary: Bhima, at Draupadi's request, arranges a meeting with Kicaka in the dance hall at night. Bhima hides there, and when Kicaka arrives, they fight. Bhima kills Kicaka by crushing his body into a ball. Draupadi then announces to the guards that her Gandharva husbands killed Kicaka.

beats.title: The Slaying of the Kichakas
beats.summary: Bhima kills the 105 Kichaka brothers after they attempt to burn Draupadi alive on the funeral pyre of Kichaka. Draupadi calls out to her husbands, and Bhima, disguised as a Gandharva, rescues her and slaughters the attackers.

beats.title: Draupadī's Return and Virāṭa's Fear
beats.summary: After the Kicaka brothers are killed by Bhimasena (disguised as a Gandharva), the charioteers report to King Virata. Fearing Gandharvas, Virata orders the cremation of the Kicakas and asks Queen Sudeṣṇa to send Draupadī (the sairandhrī) away. Draupadī, freed from danger, returns to the city, bathes, and meets Bhimasena at the kitchen door, thanking him. She then goes to the dance hall where Arjuna (disguised as Bṛhannaḍā) is teaching. The princesses greet her, and Bṛhannaḍā asks how she was freed. Draupadī evades, but later Sudeṣṇa conveys Virata's order to leave. Draupadī requests a thirteen-day stay, promising that the Gandharvas will then take her away and bring benefit to the king.

beats.title: The Spies' Report and Kicaka's Death
beats.summary: After Kicaka's death, the spies sent by Duryodhana report their failure to find the Pandavas despite extensive search. They also inform Duryodhana of Kicaka's death at the hands of Gandharvas, which pleases him.

beats.title: The Search for the Pāṇḍavas
beats.summary: In this chapter, King Duryodhana, after hearing the assembly's words, reflects on the difficulty of knowing the outcome of actions. He urges everyone to search for the Pāṇḍavas who are in their thirteenth year of exile. Karṇa suggests sending spies to various places to find them. Duḥśāsana supports Karṇa's plan and expresses doubt that the Pāṇḍavas may have perished. Duryodhana is advised to act resolutely.

beats.title: Droṇa's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Droṇa, a great warrior and teacher, speaks to Duryodhana about the Pāṇḍavas, praising their virtues and predicting their success. He advises careful planning and reconnaissance to counter them.

beats.title: Bhishma's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Bhishma, the grandsire of the Kurus, speaks to Duryodhana and the assembled Kauravas, praising the virtues of Yudhishthira and the Pandavas. He argues that the Pandavas, being righteous and protected by dharma, cannot be destroyed. He describes the prosperity and harmony that prevail wherever Yudhishthira rules, and advises Duryodhana to act wisely and not oppose them.

beats.title: Kripa's Counsel on Strategy
beats.summary: Kripa advises Dhritarashtra and the Kauravas on strategy regarding the Pandavas, who are in hiding after the dice game. He emphasizes the need to assess one's own strength and the enemy's, and to use a combination of diplomacy, gifts, punishment, and force to deal with allies and enemies. He counsels that the Pandavas, though hidden, are powerful and will return, so preparations should be made for war or peace accordingly.

beats.title: The Trigartas and Kauravas Plan to Attack Matsya
beats.summary: King Suśarman of the Trigartas, having been repeatedly harassed by King Virāṭa of Matsya and his commander Kīcaka (now dead), proposes to Duryodhana and Karṇa a joint attack on the Matsya kingdom to seize cattle and wealth. Karṇa agrees, and Duryodhana orders Duḥśāsana to prepare the army. The plan is to split forces: Suśarman and the Trigartas will attack first, followed by the Kauravas. Suśarman seizes cattle on the seventh day of the hot season, and the Kauravas take more on the eighth day.

beats.title: Virāṭa's Battle Preparation
beats.summary: King Virāṭa prepares to defend his kingdom against the Trigarta army that has stolen his cattle. He and his brothers, along with the Pāṇḍavas in disguise, arm themselves and march out with a large army.

beats.title: Battle of the Matsyas and Trigartas
beats.summary: The Matsya army, led by King Virāṭa, engages in a fierce battle with the Trigarta army. The combat is intense, with dust clouds obscuring vision, and many warriors fall. Virāṭa personally fights the Trigarta king Suśarman, wounding him with arrows, while Suśarman retaliates. The battle continues until dusk.

beats.title: The Rescue of Virāṭa
beats.summary: After a temporary darkness caused by dust, the moon rises and the battle resumes. King Virāṭa is captured by Suśarmā of the Trigartas. Yudhiṣṭhira urges Bhīma to rescue him, but advises using human weapons to avoid revealing their identity. Bhīma, Nakula, and Sahadeva attack, killing many. Yudhiṣṭhira wounds Suśarmā, Bhīma kills his horses and charioteer, and Virāṭa escapes. Bhīma captures Suśarmā. The Pāṇḍavas recover the cattle and spend the night victorious. Virāṭa offers them wealth and his kingdom, but Yudhiṣṭhira declines and suggests sending messengers to announce victory.

beats.title: The Cattle Raid on Matsya
beats.summary: The chapter describes the Kaurava army's raid on the Matsya kingdom's cattle herds while King Virāṭa is away. The cowherd superintendent reports to Prince Bhūmiṁjaya, who is then urged by the superintendent to defend the cattle. Bhūmiṁjaya boasts of his prowess and prepares to fight the Kurus.

beats.title: Uttara's Boast and Draupadi's Counsel
beats.summary: Uttara, the prince of Matsya, boasts that he could recover the cattle taken by the Kauravas if he had a skilled charioteer. Draupadi, disguised as a maidservant, suggests that the eunuch Brihannada (Arjuna in disguise) was Arjuna's charioteer and is skilled. Uttara sends his sister to fetch Brihannada.

beats.title: Arjuna as Charioteer for Uttara
beats.summary: In this chapter, the Pāṇḍava Arjuna, disguised as the eunuch Bṛhannaḍā, is requested by Princess Uttarā to serve as charioteer for her brother Prince Uttara, who is going to fight the Kauravas to recover their cattle. After initial reluctance, Arjuna agrees, dons armor, and sets out with Uttara to face the Kuru army.

beats.title: Uttara's Fear and Arjuna's Intervention
beats.summary: Uttara, the prince of Matsya, initially boasts of defeating the Kauravas but becomes terrified upon seeing their army. Arjuna, disguised as the eunuch Brihannada, encourages him to fight, but Uttara flees. Arjuna chases him, catches him, and after a brief struggle, convinces him to act as his charioteer. The Kauravas observe the scene and speculate about the identity of the disguised Arjuna.

beats.title: The Omens of War and the Arrival of Arjuna
beats.summary: Dronacharya perceives omens and deduces that Arjuna, disguised as a eunuch, is approaching. He warns the Kauravas of Arjuna's prowess, but Karna and Duryodhana dismiss his fears, with Duryodhana vowing to kill the intruder. Bhishma, Drona, Kripa, and Ashvatthama honor Duryodhana's courage.

beats.title: The Retrieval of the Pāṇḍava Weapons
beats.summary: Arjuna (disguised as Br̥hannaḍā) instructs Prince Uttara to retrieve the Pāṇḍava weapons hidden in a śamī tree. Uttara hesitates due to fear of touching a corpse, but Arjuna reassures him. Uttara climbs the tree, retrieves the weapons, and is awed by their splendor. Arjuna identifies each weapon as belonging to the Pāṇḍavas, recounting their divine origins and history.

beats.title: Arjuna Reveals His Ten Names
beats.summary: Uttara, the prince of Matsya, questions Arjuna about the identities of the Pandavas and Draupadi, who are disguised in the court. Arjuna reveals his own identity and explains the meanings behind his ten names, each reflecting a deed or attribute. Uttara then apologizes for his earlier ignorance and expresses joy and relief at meeting Arjuna.

beats.title: Uttara's Transformation and Arjuna's Revelation
beats.summary: Uttara, initially fearful, questions Arjuna about his eunuch disguise. Arjuna explains it was a vow for his elder brother's command. Uttara then gains confidence, praises Arjuna's valor, and offers to serve as his charioteer, describing the horses' qualities. Arjuna removes his ornaments, strings his bow Gāṇḍīva, and the sound terrifies the Kauravas.

beats.title: Arjuna's Preparation for Battle
beats.summary: Arjuna, disguised as a charioteer, prepares for battle with Uttara as his charioteer. He invokes divine power, blows his conch, and terrifies the Kaurava army. Drona interprets omens indicating the Pandavas' victory.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Battle Address
beats.summary: Duryodhana addresses Bhishma, Drona, and Kripa on the battlefield, reminding them of the Pandavas' exile terms and expressing suspicion that Arjuna has arrived. He urges the commanders to prepare for battle, criticizing their hesitation and emphasizing the need for decisive action.

beats.title: Karna's Boast
beats.summary: Karna boasts to Duryodhana about his prowess and vows to kill Arjuna, describing his arrows and skills, and recalling his training under Jamadagnya. He promises to relieve Duryodhana's grief by defeating Arjuna.

beats.title: Kripa's Counsel to Karna
beats.summary: Kripa advises Karna against fighting Arjuna alone, recounting Arjuna's past heroic deeds and warning that Arjuna is like a lion freed from a trap. He suggests they all fight together.

beats.title: Aśvatthāmā's Rebuke to Karṇa
beats.summary: Aśvatthāmā rebukes Karṇa for boasting about stealing the cows, reminding him that the Pāṇḍavas have not been truly defeated. He extols Arjuna's prowess and challenges Karṇa to face him in battle instead of boasting.

beats.title: Bhishma's Counsel and the Reconciliation with Drona
beats.summary: Bhishma advises Duryodhana to seek reconciliation with the Pandavas, emphasizing the importance of unity and the dangers of internal conflict. He praises Drona and Karna but warns against arrogance. Duryodhana, with Karna and others, appeases Drona. Drona agrees to fight but cautions against overconfidence.

beats.title: Bhishma's Counsel and Battle Formation
beats.summary: Bhishma advises Duryodhana on the cyclical nature of time and the inevitability of war, urging preparation. Duryodhana refuses to give up the kingdom and agrees to fight. Bhishma proposes a battle formation with specific commanders.

beats.title: Arjuna's Cattle Raid
beats.summary: Arjuna approaches the Kaurava army, blows his conch, and engages in battle to recover the cattle. Duryodhana is targeted, and Arjuna's prowess terrifies the enemy.

beats.title: Arjuna's Victory over the Kauravas
beats.summary: Arjuna defeats the Kaurava army, kills several warriors including Karna's brother, and engages Karna in battle, ultimately forcing Karna to retreat.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battlefield Instructions to Uttara
beats.summary: Arjuna instructs Uttara (Vairāṭi) to drive his chariot towards specific Kaurava commanders, identifying them by their banners and describing their qualities. He explains the order of engagement, showing respect for elders like Droṇa and Bhīṣma, and strategizing to avoid unnecessary conflict.

beats.title: The Celestial Assembly Witnesses the Battle
beats.summary: Vaiśaṁpāyana describes the celestial assembly of gods, sages, and kings who gathered in Indra's flying palace to witness the great battle between Bhīṣma and Arjuna. The scene is filled with divine beings, celestial chariots, and auspicious fragrances, highlighting the cosmic significance of the war.

beats.title: Arjuna's Duel with Kripacharya
beats.summary: Arjuna fights Kripacharya in a fierce duel. Arjuna demonstrates superior skill by cutting Kripacharya's bow multiple times, disabling his chariot, and finally sparing his life out of respect. Kripacharya is rescued by other warriors.

beats.title: Arjuna's Duel with Drona
beats.summary: Arjuna, eager to fight Drona, describes Drona's qualities and asks Uttara to drive his chariot towards Drona's army. The battle begins with Drona and Arjuna exchanging volleys of arrows, showcasing their divine weapons and skills. The fight is intense and evenly matched, with both warriors displaying extraordinary prowess. Eventually, Aśvatthāmā intervenes, and Arjuna creates an opening for Drona to retreat.

beats.title: The Duel of Arjuna and Aśvatthāmā
beats.summary: Arjuna and Aśvatthāmā engage in a fierce duel, with Arjuna eventually turning to face Karṇa, who had drawn his bow. The battle is intense, with divine quivers and extraordinary feats.

beats.title: Arjuna's Confrontation with Karna
beats.summary: Arjuna confronts Karna in battle, reminding him of his boasts in the assembly and his role in Draupadi's humiliation. They exchange harsh words and fight fiercely. Arjuna wounds Karna, who retreats from the battlefield.

beats.title: Arjuna's Boast and Battle with the Kauravas
beats.summary: Arjuna, having defeated the Kauravas in a previous encounter, boasts to Uttara (Vairāṭi) about his prowess and past exploits, then leads him into battle against Bhīṣma and other Kaurava warriors. Arjuna defeats several Kaurava princes, including Duḥśāsana, Vikarṇa, Duḥsaha, and Viviṁśati, demonstrating his superior archery and divine weapons.

beats.title: Arjuna's Solo Attack on the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: Arjuna single-handedly attacks the Kaurava army, causing massive destruction and creating a river of blood. He displays his archery prowess, terrifying the enemy forces, and then withdraws after fulfilling his vow.

beats.title: Arjuna's Counterattack with the Aindra Astra
beats.summary: Duryodhana, Karna, Duhshasana, Vivimshati, Drona with his son, and Kripa attack Arjuna, who counters with the divine Aindra astra, causing the Kaurava army to flee in terror.

beats.title: The Duel of Bhishma and Arjuna
beats.summary: Bhishma and Arjuna engage in a fierce duel, displaying mastery of divine weapons. Arjuna cuts Bhishma's bow and chariot, but Bhishma retaliates. The battle is witnessed by celestial beings, and Indra showers flowers. Bhishma is eventually wounded and his charioteer withdraws him.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Attack and Flight
beats.summary: Duryodhana attacks Arjuna after Bhishma retreats. Arjuna wounds Duryodhana, then kills Vikarna's elephant and wounds Duryodhana again. Duryodhana flees, and Arjuna taunts him, urging him to fight.

beats.title: Arjuna's Single-Handed Battle and Withdrawal
beats.summary: In this chapter, Arjuna (Partha) engages the Kaurava army in battle, uses a divine weapon to render them unconscious, instructs Uttara (the Matsya prince) to take their garments, then withdraws after honoring the elders. Bhishma criticizes Duryodhana's panic and advises retreat. Arjuna cuts Duryodhana's crown and departs.

beats.title: After the Battle: Arjuna's Mercy
beats.summary: After defeating the Kurus in battle, Arjuna reassures the frightened enemy soldiers, grants them safety, and instructs Uttara to send messengers to announce victory in Virāṭa's city.

beats.title: Uttara's Victory and Virāṭa's Anger
beats.summary: After defeating the Trigartas and recovering the cattle, King Virāṭa returns to the city with the Pāṇḍavas. He learns that his son Uttara has gone alone with Bṛhannaḍā (Arjuna in disguise) to fight the Kauravas. Despite initial despair, Yudhiṣṭhira reassures him. Uttara returns victorious, and Virāṭa orders celebrations. In his joy, he insists on gambling with Yudhiṣṭhira, who reluctantly agrees. When Yudhiṣṭhira praises Bṛhannaḍā, Virāṭa angrily strikes him, causing blood. Draupadī collects the blood in a vessel. Uttara arrives and is welcomed, but Yudhiṣṭhira (as Kaṅka) warns that Bṛhannaḍā should not see the blood, else he will kill Virāṭa.

beats.title: Uttara's Return and the Revelation of the Divine Youth
beats.summary: Uttara returns to Virāṭa's court and recounts how a divine youth (Arjuna in disguise) defeated the Kauravas and recovered the cattle. Virāṭa, unaware that the disguised Pāṇḍava is Arjuna, praises Uttara. Arjuna later gives the captured garments to Uttarā.

beats.title: The Revelation of Yudhiṣṭhira's Identity
beats.summary: On the third day of the Virāṭa court, Yudhiṣṭhira and his brothers, bathed and dressed in white, enter the assembly. King Virāṭa sees Yudhiṣṭhira seated on a royal seat and questions him. Arjuna defends Yudhiṣṭhira, revealing his true identity as the eldest Pāṇḍava, describing his virtues, wealth, and past glory.

beats.title: Revelation and Alliance
beats.summary: King Virāṭa, upon learning the true identities of the Pāṇḍavas, expresses gratitude and offers his kingdom and daughter Uttarā to Arjuna. Arjuna accepts Uttarā as his daughter-in-law, formalizing the alliance.

beats.title: The Marriage of Abhimanyu and Uttarā
beats.summary: Arjuna explains to King Virāṭa that he seeks to accept Virāṭa's daughter Uttarā as a daughter-in-law, not as a wife, to avoid scandal. He proposes marriage to his son Abhimanyu. Virāṭa agrees, and the marriage is celebrated with great festivities. The Pāṇḍavas, having completed their exile, gather allies including Kṛṣṇa and the Vṛṣṇis, and the wedding takes place with Yudhiṣṭhira's approval.

beats.title: The Assembly at Virāṭa's Court
beats.summary: In this chapter, after Abhimanyu's marriage, the Pandavas and their allies gather in King Virata's court. Krishna addresses the assembly, recounting how Yudhishthira lost his kingdom in a dice game and endured exile. He urges the kings to decide on a course of action that is righteous and beneficial for both the Pandavas and Kauravas, suggesting a peace mission offering half the kingdom to Yudhishthira.

beats.title: Baladeva's Peace Proposal and Satyaki's Rebuttal
beats.summary: Baladeva addresses the assembly, recounting the history of the conflict and urging a peaceful resolution. He suggests that Duryodhana should give half the kingdom to the Pandavas, and criticizes the deceitful gambling match. He advises sending a messenger to Duryodhana with a conciliatory proposal. In response, Satyaki (Shinipravira) angrily rejects Baladeva's stance.

beats.title: Satyaki's Defense of Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Satyaki (Yuyudhana) rebukes Balarama for criticizing Yudhishthira, defending the Pandavas' righteousness and vowing to defeat the Kauravas in battle.

beats.title: Drupada's Rejection of Baladeva's Advice and Mobilization for War
beats.summary: Drupada, after hearing Baladeva's advice, rejects it as unsuitable. He argues that Duryodhana will not give up the kingdom peacefully, and that harsh measures are needed. He orders messengers to be sent to various kings and allies to gather forces for war, and instructs his priest to deliver a firm message to Dhritarashtra and the Kauravas.

beats.title: The Pandavas Prepare for War
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) advises Virata and Drupada on diplomacy, suggesting they first send a peace proposal to Dhritarashtra. After Krishna departs for Dvaraka, the Pandavas and their allies prepare for war, gathering armies. Dhritarashtra's son Duryodhana also assembles his forces. The chapter ends with Drupada sending his priest to the Kurus with a message.

beats.title: Drupada's Counsel to the Priest
beats.summary: Drupada instructs the priest to go to Dhritarashtra and use wise counsel to sow division among the Kaurava forces, especially Bhishma, Drona, and Kripa, while the Pandavas prepare for war. He emphasizes the priest's qualifications and the auspicious timing for the mission.

beats.title: The Choice of Krishna
beats.summary: After Krishna and Balarama leave for Dwaraka, Duryodhana sends spies to learn the Pandavas' plans. He then goes to Dwaraka to seek Krishna's alliance. Arjuna also arrives. Krishna offers a choice: himself as a non-combatant or his army of Narayanas. Arjuna chooses Krishna, Duryodhana takes the army. Duryodhana then approaches Balarama, who refuses to side with either party. Duryodhana secures an army from Kritavarma and returns to Hastinapura. Krishna agrees to be Arjuna's charioteer.

beats.title: Shalya's Arrival and Alliance
beats.summary: Shalya, the king of Madra, arrives with a large army to join the Pandavas. Duryodhana intercepts him and secures a boon that Shalya will lead his forces. Later, Shalya goes to the Pandava camp, where Yudhishthira asks him to act as Karna's charioteer in the upcoming duel with Arjuna and to demoralize Karna. Shalya agrees.

beats.title: The Slaying of Trisiras and the Birth of Vritra
beats.summary: Shalya narrates to Yudhishthira the story of Indra's killing of Trisiras (Vishvarupa), the son of Tvashtr, and the subsequent creation of Vritra by Tvashtr to avenge his son. Indra, with the help of a carpenter (Taksha), beheads Trisiras, but Tvashtr creates Vritra, who nearly defeats Indra until the gods create a yawn (jrimbhika) to release Indra. The battle continues, and the gods seek Vishnu's help.

beats.title: The Killing of Vritra
beats.summary: Indra, fearful of Vritra, seeks counsel from the gods and then from Vishnu. Vishnu advises making peace with Vritra. The gods and sages approach Vritra, who agrees to a truce under the condition that he cannot be killed by anything dry, wet, stone, wood, weapon, thunderbolt, by day or by night. Later, Indra, remembering Vishnu, sees foam at the seashore at twilight, which is neither dry nor wet, and hurls it at Vritra. Vishnu enters the foam and kills Vritra. Indra is then overcome by guilt for the deceit and the sin of brahminicide, hides in the waters, and the world suffers from anarchy.

beats.title: The Fall of Nahusha
beats.summary: Nahusha is appointed as Indra by the gods and sages, but becomes arrogant and demands Shachi, Indra's wife. Shachi seeks refuge with Brihaspati, who reassures her that Indra will return.

beats.title: The Story of Nahusha and Indrani
beats.summary: Shalya narrates the story of Nahusha's attempt to claim Indrani, Indra's wife, after Indra's fall. The gods and sages, fearing Nahusha's wrath, try to persuade Brihaspati to hand over Indrani, but Brihaspati refuses, citing the sin of betraying a refugee. He advises the gods to ask Indrani to request a delay from Nahusha, trusting that time will resolve the issue. Indrani reluctantly agrees and approaches Nahusha.

beats.title: The Fall of Nahusha and the Vow of Shachi
beats.summary: Nahusha, having become Indra, desires Shachi (Indra's wife). Shachi, a devoted wife, seeks time and consults Brihaspati. The gods approach Vishnu for help. Vishnu advises that Indra perform an Ashvamedha sacrifice to purify himself from the sin of killing Vritra. Indra does so, distributes the sin, and regains purity. Nahusha is cursed to fall. Shachi, distressed, prays to the night goddess and performs a vow to be reunited with Indra.

beats.title: Indrani's Plea to Indra
beats.summary: Shalya narrates how Indrani, distressed by Nahusha's demand, seeks Indra's help. Guided by Upaśruti, she travels to a divine lake, enters a lotus, and finds Indra in a subtle form. She praises him and recounts Nahusha's arrogance, pleading for protection.

beats.title: The Downfall of Nahusha
beats.summary: Shalya narrates the story of Nahusha's downfall. Indra, after losing his kingdom to Nahusha, instructs his wife Shachi to approach Nahusha and request a unique palanquin carried by sages. Nahusha, intoxicated by power, agrees and forces the sages to carry him. Shachi then seeks help from Brihaspati, who performs a fire sacrifice to locate Indra. Agni searches but cannot find Indra, and Brihaspati advises him to enter the waters. Agni hesitates, citing the principle that fire's origin from water causes its extinction.

beats.title: The Praise of Agni and the Restoration of Indra
beats.summary: Bṛhaspati praises Agni as the mouth of the gods, the carrier of oblations, and the inner witness of all beings. Agni agrees to show Indra, who is hiding in a lotus stalk in a lake. Bṛhaspati, along with gods and sages, praises Indra, recounting his victories over demons like Namuci, Śambara, Bala, and Vṛtra. Indra emerges, and the gods request his help against the usurper Nahuṣa, who has taken over heaven. Indra agrees, and the gods assign shares of sacrifices to Agni, Kubera, Yama, and Varuṇa.

beats.title: The Fall of Nahusha and the Restoration of Indra
beats.summary: King Shalya narrates to Yudhishthira how the sage Agastya cursed King Nahusha to become a serpent for ten thousand years after Nahusha, puffed with pride, used sages as carriers and kicked Agastya on the head. Agastya then restored Indra to heaven.

beats.title: Shalya's Consolation and the Story of Indra's Victory
beats.summary: Shalya consoles Yudhishthira by narrating the story of Indra's victory over Vritra and the downfall of Nahusha, assuring him that his enemies will be destroyed. He agrees to serve as Karna's charioteer and promises to demoralize Karna. Yudhishthira honors Shalya.

beats.title: The Gathering of Armies
beats.summary: This chapter describes the arrival of various kings and their armies to join either the Pandavas or the Kauravas before the Kurukshetra war. It lists the forces of Yuyudhana, Dhrishtaketu, Jayatsena, Pandya, Drupada, Virata, and others on the Pandava side, and Bhagadatta, Bhurishravas, Shalya, Kritavarma, Jayadratha, Sudakshina, Nila, the Avanti kings, and the Kekayas on the Kaurava side. The total forces are seven akshauhinis for the Pandavas and eleven for the Kauravas, with the armies covering the entire region from Hastinapura to the Yamuna.

beats.title: The Envoy's Plea for Peace
beats.summary: The priest of Drupada, sent as an envoy to the Kauravas, addresses the Kuru assembly. He recounts the history of the conflict: the Pandavas were deprived of their paternal share of the kingdom, endured exile and hardships, and now seek peace through negotiation. He warns that the Pandavas are powerful, with a vast army and the support of Krishna, and urges the Kauravas to give them their due to avoid war.

beats.title: Bhishma's Counsel and Karna's Retort
beats.summary: Bhishma responds to a speech by a brahmana (likely Krishna), praising the Pandavas for their virtue and desire for peace, and criticizing Duryodhana's harsh words. Karna retorts, defending Duryodhana and accusing the Pandavas of breaking their exile agreement. Bhishma rebukes Karna, reminding him of Arjuna's prowess. Dhritarashtra then decides to send Sanjaya as a messenger to the Pandavas.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Instructions to Sanjaya
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the Pandavas' army and expresses his fear and anxiety over the impending war, listing the strengths of the Pandava forces and their allies, and acknowledging the invincibility of Krishna and Arjuna together. He instructs Sanjaya to go to the Pandava camp and convey his desire for peace.

beats.title: Sanjaya's Visit and Yudhishthira's Inquiry
beats.summary: Sanjaya visits the Pandavas in Upaplavya and conveys Dhritarashtra's greetings. Yudhishthira inquires about the well-being of the Kauravas and their allies, and recalls past exploits of the Pandavas, hinting at the futility of war.

beats.title: Samjaya's Report on the Kaurava Court
beats.summary: Samjaya reports to Dhritarashtra the conversation with Yudhishthira, describing the Kaurava court's mixed character, Dhritarashtra's grief over the impending war, and the Pāṇḍavas' resolve. He urges Yudhishthira to use wisdom to bring peace.

beats.title: Sañjaya's Report and Plea for Peace
beats.summary: Sañjaya reports to Yudhiṣṭhira the message from Dhṛtarāṣṭra urging peace and warning of the destruction of war. He praises the strength of both armies and expresses his own uncertainty about victory or defeat, ultimately pleading for reconciliation.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament and Counsel
beats.summary: Yudhishthira criticizes Duryodhana's greed and Dhritarashtra's blind attachment to his son, arguing that war is futile and that peace should be sought. He recounts the wise counsel of Vidura, which was ignored, and warns of the destruction that will follow if the Kauravas persist in their hostility.

beats.title: The Advisor's Plea for Peace
beats.summary: Samjaya continues his report to Dhritarashtra, recounting the words of an advisor (likely Vidura or a sage) who urges Yudhishthira to avoid war, emphasizing the impermanence of life, the supremacy of dharma over kama and artha, and the futility of violence against kinsmen. The advisor warns that victory in such a war brings no lasting happiness and that even winning the earth cannot overcome old age and death.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Discourse on Dharma and Adharma
beats.summary: Yudhishthira questions the nature of dharma and adharma, especially in times of distress (āpaddharma). He argues that dharma and adharma can appear in each other's forms, and that the wise discern them through intellect. He emphasizes that even in adversity, one should strive to preserve dharma, and that he does not desire any heavenly reward obtained through adharma. He praises Krishna's wisdom and leadership, and declares that he will not disregard Krishna's words.

beats.title: Krishna's Discourse on Karma and the Kaurava-Pandava Conflict
beats.summary: Vasudeva (Krishna) instructs Samjaya on the eternal nature of karma and dharma, emphasizing that all beings—gods, sages, kings, and even natural phenomena—achieve their greatness through diligent action. He criticizes the Kauravas for their adharma, recounts the humiliation of Draupadi, and urges Samjaya to convey the truth to Dhritarashtra. Krishna uses metaphors of a tree and a forest to describe the two sides, advocating for peace but warning of destruction if war ensues.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Peace Mission
beats.summary: Yudhishthira instructs Sanjaya to convey a message of peace to Dhritarashtra, Bhishma, Vidura, and Duryodhana, recounting past sufferings and requesting at least five villages to avoid war.

beats.title: Sanjaya's Report to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Sanjaya returns from the Pandavas and reports to Dhritarashtra. He conveys Yudhishthira's virtues and criticizes Dhritarashtra's attachment to his sons, which leads to the destruction of the Kurus. Sanjaya emphasizes the role of destiny and the consequences of adharma.

beats.title: Vidura's Discourse on Wisdom
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra summons Vidura and expresses anxiety about Samjaya's return from the Pandavas. Vidura responds with a lengthy discourse on the characteristics of the wise (pandita) and the foolish (mudha), enumerating virtues and vices, and advising Dhritarashtra to act righteously. He urges the king to give the Pandavas their rightful share of the kingdom.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel on Statecraft and Self-Control
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura for counsel on the impending war. Vidura advises on statecraft, self-control, and the dangers of unbridled senses, emphasizing that true victory comes from mastering oneself. He warns against the Kauravas' greed and urges reconciliation with the Pandavas.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel and the Story of Sudhanva and Virocana
beats.summary: Vidura advises Dhritarashtra on the importance of truthfulness and righteousness, illustrating with the story of Sudhanva and Virocana. He warns against falsehood and lists virtues and vices, urging Dhritarashtra to treat the Pandavas fairly.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel on Dharma and Family Unity
beats.summary: Vidura advises Dhritarashtra on ethics, self-control, and the importance of family unity, using the ancient dialogue between the sage Atreya (in the form of a swan) and the Sadhyas. He warns against anger, harsh speech, and bad company, and extols virtues like truthfulness, patience, and forgiveness. He also discusses the qualities of noble families and the dangers of internal discord, urging Dhritarashtra to reconcile with the Pandavas.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura advises Dhritarashtra on statecraft, ethics, and the dangers of war with the Pandavas, emphasizing dharma, wise counsel, and the futility of conflict.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura imparts extensive wisdom to Dhritarashtra on statecraft, ethics, and personal conduct, emphasizing the importance of self-control, respect for elders, proper treatment of guests and women, secrecy in counsel, and the dangers of anger and greed. He warns that Dhritarashtra's attachment to Duryodhana will lead to ruin.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura advises Dhritarashtra on statecraft, ethics, and personal conduct, urging him to treat the Pandavas fairly and avoid war. He emphasizes the importance of forgiveness, self-control, and righteous action.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura instructs Dhritarashtra on dharma, emphasizing truth, control of senses, and the transient nature of life. He describes the river of self with holy banks, and advises crossing the river of desire and anger with the boat of fortitude. He outlines duties for each varna and urges Dhritarashtra to guide Duryodhana towards righteousness. Dhritarashtra acknowledges the advice but laments his attachment to his son.

beats.title: The Summoning of Sanatsujata
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura to reveal the teachings of the ancient sage Sanatsujata, who declared that death does not exist. Vidura, though born from a Shudra womb, summons Sanatsujata, who appears and is received with due rites. Vidura then requests Sanatsujata to answer Dhritarashtra's doubts about transcending dualities.

beats.title: Sanatsujata's Discourse on Death and Immortality
beats.summary: Sanatsujata explains to Dhritarashtra the nature of death and immortality, emphasizing that death is not a physical event but a state of ignorance and heedlessness (pramada). He discusses the role of desire, anger, and delusion in causing spiritual death, and contrasts the paths of the wise and the ignorant. The dialogue also covers the conduct of Brahmins and the relationship between dharma and adharma.

beats.title: Sanatsujāta's Discourse on True Knowledge
beats.summary: Sanatsujāta teaches Dhṛtarāṣṭra that the Vedas alone do not protect one from sin; true knowledge lies in truthfulness, self-control, and inner purity. He enumerates twelve vices and twelve virtues, emphasizing that a brāhmaṇa is defined by adherence to truth, not by mere study of scriptures.

beats.title: Sanatsujāta's Discourse on Brahmacarya and Brahman
beats.summary: Sanatsujāta instructs Dhṛtarāṣṭra on the nature of Brahman and the path of brahmacarya, emphasizing that the ultimate reality is beyond sensory perception and can be attained through disciplined celibacy and devotion to the guru.

beats.title: Sanatsujata's Discourse on the Supreme Light
beats.summary: Sanatsujata describes the supreme, eternal, luminous being (Shukra/Brahman) that yogis perceive. He explains how this being pervades all elements, sustains creation, and is known through inner realization. The chapter emphasizes that those who know this being become immortal, and it concludes with the teaching that the Self is all—father, mother, son, and the substratum of existence.

beats.title: The Morning Assembly and Sanjaya's Return
beats.summary: Sanatsujata and Vidura converse with Dhritarashtra through the night. The next morning, all kings assemble in the court, eager to hear Sanjaya's report from the Pandavas. Sanjaya arrives and conveys the Pandavas' respectful greetings.

beats.title: Arjuna's Ultimatum to Duryodhana
beats.summary: In this chapter, Arjuna, through Samjaya's narration to Dhritarashtra, delivers a fierce ultimatum to Duryodhana. He lists the Pāṇḍava warriors—Bhīma, Nakula, Sahadeva, the Draupadeyas, Abhimanyu, and allies like Virāṭa, Drupada, Śikhaṇḍin, Dhṛṣṭadyumna, and Sātyaki—and warns that they will annihilate the Kauravas in battle. Arjuna extols Kṛṣṇa's divine exploits, including the slaying of Naraka, Mura, and others, and declares that with Kṛṣṇa as his ally, victory is certain. He rebukes Duryodhana's folly and urges the Kauravas to avoid war.

beats.title: Bhishma's Warning and the Divine Origins of Krishna and Arjuna
beats.summary: Bhishma, Drona, and Karna debate the prowess of the Pandavas and the Kauravas. Bhishma recounts the divine origins of Krishna and Arjuna as Nara-Narayana, their past deeds, and warns Duryodhana of impending doom. Karna defends himself, but Bhishma and Drona urge peace.

beats.title: Sanjaya's Report on the Pandava Army
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the Pandavas' actions and intentions after seeing the Kaurava army. Sanjaya describes the Pandava forces, highlighting each warrior's prowess and past deeds, and explains that they are arrayed for battle under Yudhishthira's command.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Fear of Bhima
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra expresses his deep fear of Bhima, describing his immense strength, wrath, and prowess in battle. He laments that his sons, blinded by pride, ignore his warnings and are heading towards destruction. He recalls Bhima's past feats, including the killing of Jarasandha, and foresees Bhima's devastating impact on the Kaurava army. The chapter ends with Dhritarashtra's despair and acceptance of inevitable doom.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra expresses his anxiety about the war, acknowledging Arjuna's invincibility with Krishna as his charioteer, and laments the impending destruction of his sons due to their ignorance.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra expresses his anxiety about the Pandavas' strength and the impending war, listing the powerful warriors on their side and lamenting the inevitable destruction of his own family.

beats.title: Saṁjaya's Rebuke and Prophecy
beats.summary: Saṁjaya rebukes Dhṛtarāṣṭra for his past injustices and warns that Arjuna, with Kṛṣṇa's support, will destroy the Kauravas in battle.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Boast and Reassurance to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Duryodhana, in a conversation with his father Dhritarashtra via Sanjaya, boasts about his military strength and belittles the Pandavas. He recounts how Krishna and other kings joined the Pandavas, but asserts that his own army, led by Bhishma, Drona, Karna, and others, is invincible. He dismisses the Pandavas' request for five villages and claims that Bhima is no match for him in mace-fighting. He lists his powerful warriors and argues that victory is certain due to numerical superiority and the prowess of his commanders.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Inquiry and the Divine Chariot
beats.summary: Duryodhana asks Sanjaya about Yudhishthira's intentions after gathering seven akshauhinis. Sanjaya describes the Pandavas' readiness, Arjuna's divine chariot and banner crafted by Tvashtr and Dhata, and the horses of each Pandava brother, emphasizing their divine origins and prowess.

beats.title: The Army Divisions and Battle Formations
beats.summary: Samjaya describes to Dhritarashtra the armies assembled on the Kurukshetra battlefield, listing the warriors on both sides and their battle formations. Dhritarashtra laments his sons' folly, while Duryodhana boasts of his forces. Yudhishthira and Dhrishtadyumna encourage the Pandava troops.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Duryodhana's Defiance
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the impending war and urges Duryodhana to make peace with the Pandavas, but Duryodhana refuses, vowing to fight. Dhritarashtra then predicts the destruction of the Kauravas by Bhima and others.

beats.title: Sañjaya's Audience with Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna
beats.summary: Sañjaya recounts to Dhṛtarāṣṭra his visit to Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna in their private chamber, where he witnessed their intimate bond and heard Kṛṣṇa's fierce declaration of Arjuna's invincibility and the impending doom of the Kauravas.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Anxiety and Analysis
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after hearing Samjaya's report, analyzes the strengths and weaknesses of both armies. He expresses his anxiety about the impending war, highlighting Arjuna's divine weapons and invincibility, and concludes that peace with the Pandavas is preferable to war.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Boast
beats.summary: Duryodhana responds to his father Dhritarashtra's fears by asserting his own power and the limitations of the gods. He claims that the gods are not like humans, being free from desire and hatred, and that his own ascetic power surpasses even theirs. He boasts of his ability to control natural elements, protect his kingdom, and defeat the Pandavas and their allies. He concludes by declaring his superiority in intellect, energy, and yoga, and asks Samjaya to report his words.

beats.title: Karna's Boast and Bhishma's Rebuke
beats.summary: Karna boasts of his prowess and vows to defeat the Pandavas, but Bhishma rebukes him, reminding him of Arjuna's feats and predicting Karna's weapons will be destroyed. Karna leaves the assembly in anger, and Bhishma then speaks to Duryodhana, criticizing Karna and declaring his own resolve to fight.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel and Parables
beats.summary: Vidura narrates a parable of two birds caught by a fowler when they quarrel, illustrating that disunity among kinsmen leads to destruction. He advises Duryodhana against war, warning that the Pandavas are invincible when united. He also tells a story of a honey trap in the mountains to caution against greed.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra advises Duryodhana to heed the counsel of elders and avoid war with the Pandavas, citing the strength of Arjuna, Bhima, Krishna, and others, and recalling a past incident in Virata's city where the Pandavas' might was demonstrated.

beats.title: Arjuna's Message to the Kauravas
beats.summary: Sanjaya recounts to Dhritarashtra how Arjuna, after hearing Krishna's words, instructed him to convey a message to the Kaurava assembly. Arjuna lists the Kaurava warriors and declares that if they do not yield the Pandavas' rightful share, he will send them to the abode of death. Sanjaya then departs to deliver the message.

beats.title: Sanjaya's Reluctance and Vyasa's Intervention
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the relative strength of the two armies, but Sanjaya hesitates to speak in private, fearing the king's anger. He requests that Dhritarashtra summon his father Vyasa and mother Gandhari, in whose presence he will reveal the truth about Krishna and Arjuna. Vyasa then arrives and instructs Sanjaya to answer all questions truthfully.

beats.title: The Supreme Power of Krishna
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the supreme power of Krishna (Vasudeva) as the controller of all worlds, time, and destiny. He explains that Krishna, though capable of destroying everything, acts with restraint and uses his maya to delude the wicked. The Pandavas are protected by Krishna, and their victory is assured because where Krishna is, there is dharma and victory.

beats.title: The Path to Krishna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya how he knows Krishna as the supreme lord. Sanjaya explains that his knowledge comes from devotion and purity, not from illusion. Dhritarashtra urges Duryodhana to surrender to Krishna, but Duryodhana refuses due to pride and enmity. Gandhari rebukes Duryodhana. Vyasa tells Dhritarashtra that Sanjaya, as a devoted messenger, will lead him to the path of liberation. Sanjaya teaches that control of the senses and non-violence are the foundations of knowledge, and that Krishna is attainable only through disciplined senses and yoga.

beats.title: The Names of Krishna
beats.summary: Samjaya explains the meanings of various names of Krishna/Vishnu to Dhritarashtra, including Vasudeva, Madhava, Madhusudana, Krishna, Pundarikaksha, Janardana, Satvata, Vrishabhekshana, Anikajit, Damodara, Hrishikesha, Mahabahu, Adhokshaja, Narayana, Purushottama, Govinda, Vishnu, Jishnu, Ananta, and Acyuta. The chapter emphasizes the eternal and supreme nature of Krishna.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Praise of Krishna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra expresses envy towards those who can see Vasudeva (Krishna) on the battlefield, describing Krishna's divine attributes and taking refuge in him as the supreme being.

beats.title: Krishna's Decision to Go as Peace Envoy
beats.summary: Yudhishthira expresses his distress to Krishna about the impending war, detailing Dhritarashtra's greed and refusal to give even five villages. He reflects on the nature of wealth, honor, and the cycle of greed leading to downfall. Krishna agrees to go to the Kuru court as a peace envoy, despite Yudhishthira's doubts about Duryodhana's willingness to listen.

beats.title: Krishna's Counsel to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Lord Krishna advises Yudhishthira on the necessity of war, condemns Duryodhana's evil deeds, and declares his intention to seek peace but prepare for battle.

beats.title: Bhima's Counsel on Peace
beats.summary: Bhima advises Krishna on how to approach Duryodhana for peace, describing Duryodhana's stubborn and evil nature, and listing past wicked kings who destroyed their families. He urges gentle persuasion and warns against war.

beats.title: Krishna's Exhortation to Bhima
beats.summary: Krishna chides Bhima for his sudden pacifism and despondency, reminding him of his warrior nature and urging him to rise above weakness. He interprets Bhima's unusual behavior as a sign of inner turmoil and lack of resolve, and exhorts him to act according to his kshatriya dharma.

beats.title: Bhima's Boast
beats.summary: Bhima responds to Krishna's earlier words with anger and pride, asserting his immense strength and vowing to crush the Kaurava army. He boasts that he could even restrain the earth and sky, and that no one can escape his grasp. He asks Krishna not to belittle him, promising to prove his might in battle.

beats.title: Krishna's Counsel to Bhima
beats.summary: Lord Krishna, addressing Bhima, explains the interplay of divine will (daiva) and human effort (pauruṣa), emphasizing that both are necessary for success. He advises Bhima not to be despondent and assures him of his support in the upcoming war, while also revealing his intention to seek peace with the Kauravas first.

beats.title: Arjuna's Call to Action
beats.summary: Arjuna urges Krishna to take decisive action against the Kauravas, arguing that peace is impossible due to Duryodhana's greed and past injustices, and that war is the only recourse for a Kshatriya.

beats.title: Krishna's Discourse on Effort and Destiny
beats.summary: Krishna explains to Arjuna that both human effort and divine will are necessary for success. He criticizes Duryodhana's stubbornness and evil nature, stating that war is inevitable. Krishna affirms his commitment to Dharma and to Yudhishthira's cause, and declares Duryodhana worthy of death.

beats.title: Nakula's Address to Krishna
beats.summary: Nakula speaks to Krishna, summarizing the views of Yudhishthira, Bhima, and Arjuna, and urges Krishna to act according to the present situation. He lists the powerful warriors on the Pandava side and suggests that Krishna's mere presence will achieve the desired goal. He also mentions that Vidura, Bhishma, Droṇa, and others can persuade Dhritarashtra and Duryodhana.

beats.title: Sahadeva and Sātyaki's War Cry
beats.summary: Sahadeva expresses his anger at the Kauravas' treatment of Draupadi and insists on war, even if the Kauravas seek peace. Sātyaki agrees, stating that only Duryodhana's death can quell his anger. The warriors applaud Sātyaki's words.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament and Krishna's Assurance
beats.summary: Draupadi, distressed by the injustices suffered by the Pandavas, appeals to Krishna for vengeance against the Kauravas, recounting her humiliation in the court and the failed peace negotiations. Krishna consoles her, promising the destruction of the Kauravas.

beats.title: Krishna's Mission to the Kuru Court
beats.summary: Arjuna requests Krishna to act as a peacemaker and go to the Kuru court to negotiate. Krishna agrees, prepares his chariot, and sets off with Sātyaki. Yudhiṣṭhira and other Pāṇḍavas accompany him partway, then Yudhiṣṭhira gives Krishna messages for his mother Kuntī and the elders. Arjuna expresses his willingness for peace but also his resolve for war if necessary. Bhīma reacts fiercely. On the way, Krishna meets a group of sages led by Jamadagnya (Paraśurāma), who express their desire to hear the peace talks.

beats.title: Krishna's Journey to Hastinapura
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about Krishna's journey to Hastinapura as a peace envoy. Vaishampayana describes the miraculous omens accompanying Krishna's departure from Upaplavya, his journey through prosperous lands, and his arrival at Vr̥kasthala where he performs evening rituals and is honored by local brahmins.

beats.title: Preparations for Krishna's Arrival
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, upon hearing that Krishna is approaching, instructs Bhishma, Drona, Sanjaya, Vidura, and Duryodhana to prepare a grand reception. He emphasizes the importance of honoring Krishna, as he is the lord of all beings and the embodiment of dharma. Duryodhana then arranges luxurious pavilions and offerings along the route, including a special residence in Vrkasthala. Krishna, however, passes by all these preparations without noticing them and proceeds directly to the Kuru court.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Preparations for Krishna's Visit
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra describes to Sanjaya his preparations to welcome Krishna, who is arriving from Upaplavya. He lists the gifts he will offer: sixteen chariots, eight elephants, hundreds of slave girls and slaves, woolen blankets, deer skins, a precious gem, a mule-drawn vehicle, and abundant food. He also orders that the city be decorated, the road cleaned, and that his sons (except Duryodhana) and courtesans go out to greet Krishna. He instructs that Duhshasana's house, which is better than Duryodhana's, be prepared for Krishna's stay, and that all jewels be given to him.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vidura advises King Dhritarashtra to act righteously, warning him against deceitful intentions toward Krishna and the Pandavas. He emphasizes Krishna's greatness and urges peace.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Plot to Capture Krishna
beats.summary: Duryodhana expresses his resentment towards Krishna and refuses to honor him as a messenger, proposing to capture him instead. Bhishma and Dhritarashtra rebuke Duryodhana for his foolishness and disrespect, warning that his actions will lead to destruction. Bhishma leaves the assembly in anger.

beats.title: Krishna's Arrival in Hastinapura
beats.summary: Krishna arrives in Hastinapura as a peace envoy. He is welcomed by the Kuru elders except Duryodhana, and after formalities, he stays at Vidura's house, where he recounts the activities of the Pandavas.

beats.title: Kunti's Lament and Krishna's Consolation
beats.summary: Kunti laments to Krishna about the sufferings of her sons, the Pandavas, during their exile, recalling their past luxuries and the humiliation of Draupadi. Krishna consoles her, assuring her of their eventual victory and restoration.

beats.title: Krishna's Visit to Duryodhana and Vidura
beats.summary: Krishna visits Duryodhana's palace as a peace envoy, refuses his hospitality, and explains his allegiance to the Pandavas. He then goes to Vidura's house, where he is honored and feasted.

beats.title: Vidura's Warning to Krishna
beats.summary: Vidura advises Krishna not to go to the Kaurava court, arguing that Duryodhana is too arrogant and foolish to heed wise counsel, and that entering the midst of such wicked enemies is dangerous. He expresses concern that Krishna's words will be wasted on Duryodhana and his allies, who are determined on war.

beats.title: Krishna's Mission for Peace
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains to Vidura why he has come to the Kauravas: to attempt peace between the warring cousins. He acknowledges Duryodhana's wickedness but feels obligated to try, as a friend and well-wisher. He warns that if his words are rejected, fate will take its course.

beats.title: Krishna's Journey to Hastinapura
beats.summary: Krishna departs from Upaplavya to Hastinapura as a peace envoy, accompanied by Vidura, Duryodhana, Shakuni, and others. He is received with great honor by Dhritarashtra, Bhishma, Drona, and the assembled kings. Sages including Narada appear, and Krishna instructs Bhishma to honor them. The chapter describes the grandeur of Krishna's arrival and the assembly.

beats.title: Krishna's Peace Mission
beats.summary: Krishna addresses the Kuru assembly, urging Dhritarashtra to restrain his sons and make peace with the Pandavas to avert a catastrophic war. He emphasizes the righteousness of the Pandavas and the folly of Duryodhana's greed, warning that the war will destroy both sides. Krishna offers to mediate and calls for a peaceful resolution.

beats.title: The Tale of King Dambhodbhava
beats.summary: Jamadagnya Rama narrates the story of King Dambhodbhava, who, driven by pride, sought a worthy opponent and was humbled by the sages Nara and Narayana. The story illustrates the futility of arrogance and the importance of humility, dharma, and respect for brahmanas. It concludes with the identification of Nara-Narayana as Arjuna and Krishna, urging Duryodhana to seek peace.

beats.title: Kaṇva's Counsel and the Tale of Mātali
beats.summary: Kaṇva ṛṣi advises Duryodhana to make peace with the Pāṇḍavas, citing the impermanence of all beings and the story of Mātali seeking a husband for his daughter Guṇakeśī in the Nāga realm.

beats.title: Narada and Matali Visit Varuna's Realm
beats.summary: Narada and Matali visit Varuna's realm, where Narada describes various divine objects and beings, including Varuna's sons, the conch of Vishnu, the Gandiva bow, and the divine staff of Brahma.

beats.title: Narada and Matali in Patala
beats.summary: Narada describes the wonders of Patala (the netherworld) to Matali, including the city of the Daityas and Danavas, the submarine fire, the origin of the moon's waxing and waning, the divine horse-headed being, the source of rain, the abode of the sun's rays at night, the rejuvenating touch of the moon, the suffering of adharmic Daityas, the penance of Bhutapati, the cow-observing sages, and the great elephants. They then see a mysterious egg in the water, which Narada says is the source of a great fire at the end of time. Matali declines to choose any of the beings there and urges Narada to go elsewhere.

beats.title: Narada Shows Matali the Splendor of Hiranyapura
beats.summary: Narada describes the magnificent and impenetrable golden city of Hiranyapura, built by Vishvakarma and Mayasura, inhabited by powerful danavas and asuras. Matali, the charioteer of Indra, is invited to choose a boon but declines, citing his loyalty to the devas and the eternal enmity between devas and danavas.

beats.title: Narada describes the lineage of Garuda's sons
beats.summary: Narada describes to Matali the lineage of Garuda's sons, the Suparnas, who are powerful birds feeding on snakes. He lists many names of these Garuda offspring, emphasizing their devotion to Vishnu. Narada offers to take Matali elsewhere if he is not interested.

beats.title: Description of Rasatala and the Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: Narada describes the seventh underworld Rasatala, where the divine cow Surabhi resides, providing milk that forms a lake of nectar. He also describes four guardian cows of the directions and recounts the churning of the ocean by gods and demons, which produced Varuni, Lakshmi, amrita, Uccaihsravas, and Kaustubha. A verse extols the happiness of living in Rasatala.

beats.title: Matali's Quest for a Son-in-Law
beats.summary: Narada shows Matali the serpent city Bhogavati, describing its ruler Vasuki and many nagas. Matali is attracted to a young naga named Sumukha, and asks Narada about him. Narada explains Sumukha's lineage: he is the grandson of Aryaka and son of Cikura, who was killed by Garuda. Matali expresses desire to give his daughter to Sumukha.

beats.title: The Marriage of Sumukha and Gunakeshi
beats.summary: Narada approaches the serpent king Aryaka to propose a marriage between his granddaughter Gunakeshi and Sumukha, son of Aryaka's deceased son. Aryaka is initially hesitant because Sumukha is destined to be eaten by Garuda. Matali, Indra's charioteer, intervenes and takes Sumukha to Indra. With Vishnu's counsel, Indra grants Sumukha long life but not immortality, resolving the crisis. The marriage proceeds, and all parties return satisfied.

beats.title: The Humility of Garuḍa and the Warning to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Kaṇva narrates to Duryodhana the story of Garuḍa's pride being humbled by Viṣṇu, drawing a parallel to Duryodhana's own arrogance. Garuḍa, angered by Indra's interference with his food (a serpent), confronts Indra but is subdued by Viṣṇu's mere arm. Viṣṇu reveals his supreme power, and Garuḍa submits. Kaṇva warns Duryodhana not to fight the Pāṇḍavas, who are incarnations of gods, but Duryodhana dismisses the advice.

beats.title: The Perils of Persistence: The Story of Viśvāmitra and Gālava
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks how Duryodhana's relatives failed to restrain him from his destructive path. Vaiśampāyana recounts Nārada's story of Viśvāmitra and Gālava, illustrating the dangers of persistence (nirbandha) and the importance of guru-dakṣiṇā.

beats.title: Galava's Despair and Garuda's Counsel
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Galava, who, after being asked by his guru Vishvamitra to procure eight hundred white horses as guru-dakshina, falls into deep despair. He contemplates suicide, feeling unable to fulfill the promise. His friend Garuda consoles him and offers to take him to Lord Vishnu for help.

beats.title: Suparṇa Describes the Eastern Direction
beats.summary: Suparṇa (Garuda) tells Gālava that he has been instructed by the god Brahmā (born from an unknown womb) to go to the eastern direction first. He describes the glories of the eastern direction: it is where the sun rises, where dharma is established, where the first thought arose, where the daughters of Dakṣa were born, where the Kaśyapas multiplied, where Indra was anointed, where the Vedas were first sung, where Sāvitrī was spoken, where the Yajus were given to the sun, where Soma is drunk by the gods, where the fire gods are satisfied, where Varuṇa obtained glory, where Vasiṣṭha's birth and death are manifest, where the Oṃkāra's ten tens of sounds arise, where the sages drink Soma, where boars and other animals were consecrated by Indra, where the ungrateful humans and asuras are destroyed by fire. He concludes that this is the door to the three worlds and asks Gālava to command him.

beats.title: The Southern Direction and Its Significance
beats.summary: The chapter describes the southern direction (dakṣiṇā dik) as a realm associated with ancestors (pitṛs), deities, and sages. It is a place of dharma, truth, and karma, where beings experience the results of their actions. The sage Suparṇa explains to Gālava the significance of this direction, including the river Vaitaraṇī, the city Bhogavatī, and various beings and phenomena.

beats.title: Suparṇa Describes the Western Direction
beats.summary: Suparṇa describes the western direction to Gālava, detailing its features, including the domain of Varuṇa, the setting of the sun, the origin of night and sleep, the story of Indra removing Diti's embryo, the location of Mount Mandara, the ocean where Surabhi yields milk, the appearance of Svarbhānu's headless trunk, the singing of a golden-headed being, the maiden Dhvajavatī, the release of elements, the path of the sun, the entrance of stars into the sun's disc, the source of rivers, the abode of Ananta and Viṣṇu, and the dwellings of Pavana, Kaśyapa, and Mārīca.

beats.title: The Glories of the Northern Direction
beats.summary: Suparṇa describes the glories of the northern direction (Uttara) to Gālava, including sacred places, deities, sages, and the path to liberation.

beats.title: Gālava's Flight with Garuḍa
beats.summary: The sage Gālava asks Garuḍa to take him to the eastern direction where the eyes of Dharma reside. During the flight, Gālava experiences disorientation and fear due to the speed and sights, and requests to return. Garuḍa chides him for wanting to give up life and reveals a plan involving the mountain R̥ṣabha.

beats.title: The Story of Shandili and the Lesson of Acara
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Garuda and Galava visiting the sage Shandili, who through her ascetic power transforms Garuda's body as a lesson in humility and the importance of proper conduct (acara). After being forgiven, Garuda regains his strength. Galava then meets Vishvamitra, who reminds him of his promise to bring eight hundred white horses as guru-dakshina.

beats.title: Garuda and Galava seek wealth from Yayati
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Garuda and Galava seeking wealth from King Yayati to fulfill a guru-dakshina demand of eight hundred white horses with one black ear each, as demanded by Vishwamitra. Garuda explains the nature of wealth and its guardians, and Yayati agrees to help.

beats.title: Yayati Offers His Daughter Madhavi
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of King Yayati, who, upon being approached by Garuda and the sage Galava seeking horses as a gift, offers his daughter Madhavi as a bride, whose bride-price will be provided by kings. Galava accepts and departs with Madhavi, later approaching King Haryashva of Ayodhya with the proposal.

beats.title: The Story of Haryashva and Madhavi
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of King Haryashva and the sage Galava. Haryashva desires a son and approaches Galava for a bride. Galava demands 800 horses with one black ear as bride price. Haryashva has only 200 such horses. The princess Madhavi reveals a boon that she will always remain a virgin and that four kings will each father a son through her, providing 200 horses each. Haryashva accepts her, fathers Vasumana, and returns Madhavi to Galava, who then goes to King Divodasa.

beats.title: Divodāsa and the Birth of Pratardana
