beats.summary: Gālava approaches King Divodāsa of Kāśī to request a bride for his disciple. Divodāsa agrees and marries Mādhavī, who bears him a son named Pratardana. After the agreed time, Gālava returns and Divodāsa returns the maiden, keeping his promise.

beats.title: The Story of Mādhavī and King Uśīnara
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Mādhavī, who after renouncing her royal status, follows the sage Gālava. Gālava approaches King Uśīnara to offer Mādhavī in marriage in exchange for horses as guru-dakṣiṇā. Uśīnara agrees to marry her for one son, and they beget Śibi. Gālava then continues his journey.

beats.title: Galava's Guru-Dakshina and the Birth of Ashtaka
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Galava, who, after failing to obtain eight hundred white horses with one black ear as guru-dakshina for Vishvamitra, is advised by Garuda to offer two hundred horses and a maiden instead. Galava presents the maiden Madhavi to Vishvamitra, who fathers a son named Ashtaka with her. Vishvamitra then gives Ashtaka the horses and sends him to his kingdom, while Galava releases Madhavi and retires to the forest.

beats.title: The Story of Yayati and Madhavi
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Yayati's daughter Madhavi, who after a svayamvara chooses the forest over kings, performing severe penance. Yayati, after a long reign, becomes arrogant in heaven and is questioned by the celestial beings, eventually losing his luster.

beats.title: The Fall of Yayati and His Rescue by Grandsons
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of King Yayati who, due to pride, falls from heaven. He lands among four kings performing a Vajapeya sacrifice. His daughter Madhavi and her sons (his grandsons) offer their merit to restore him to heaven. Galava also offers a portion of his tapas.

beats.title: Yayati's Ascent to Heaven
beats.summary: Narada narrates how King Yayati, who had fallen from heaven, was rescued by his four grandsons (Vasumana, Pratardana, Shibi, and Ashtaka) through their accumulated merits of charity, truthfulness, and sacrifices. Each grandson offers his own virtuous deeds to Yayati, enabling him to ascend to heaven.

beats.title: The Fall of Yayati
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of King Yayati, who ascended to heaven due to his meritorious deeds but later fell due to pride (abhimana). Brahma explains that pride diminishes the fruits of one's actions. Narada advises Dhritarashtra to abandon pride and anger and make peace with the Pandavas.

beats.title: Krishna's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: In this chapter, Dhritarashtra expresses his helplessness to Krishna, who then advises Duryodhana to make peace with the Pandavas. Krishna warns Duryodhana of the dire consequences of war, extols the virtues of the Pandavas, and urges him to follow dharma and the counsel of elders.

beats.title: Bhishma, Drona, Vidura, and Dhritarashtra counsel Duryodhana
beats.summary: Bhishma, Drona, Vidura, and Dhritarashtra each advise Duryodhana to accept Krishna's peace proposal and avoid war, but Duryodhana remains obstinate.

beats.title: Bhīṣma and Droṇa's Plea for Peace
beats.summary: Bhishma and Drona advise Duryodhana to seek peace before the battle begins, listing the formidable Pāṇḍava warriors and urging him to reconcile with Yudhiṣṭhira.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Defiance
beats.summary: Duryodhana responds to Krishna's earlier speech in the Kuru assembly, defending his actions and refusing to compromise. He argues that the Pāṇḍavas were defeated fairly in the dice game, that he has committed no wrong, and that as a Kṣatriya he must fight rather than submit. He declares that he will not give up even a needle-point of land to the Pāṇḍavas as long as he lives.

beats.title: Krishna's Rebuke and the Counsel to Bind Duryodhana
beats.summary: In this chapter, Krishna (Dāśārha) rebukes Duryodhana for his past misdeeds against the Pāṇḍavas, including the dice game, the attempted poisoning, and the exile. Duḥśāsana interrupts, threatening to bind and hand over Duryodhana, Karṇa, and himself to the Pāṇḍavas. Duryodhana leaves the assembly in anger, followed by his brothers and allies. Bhīṣma comments on Duryodhana's folly, and Krishna advises the elders to restrain the king, citing the precedent of Kamsa's slaying for the good of the clan. He urges them to bind Duryodhana, Karṇa, Śakuni, and Duḥśāsana and hand them over to the Pāṇḍavas to prevent the destruction of the Kṣatriyas.

beats.title: Gandhari's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after hearing Krishna's words, sends Vidura to bring Gandhari. Gandhari rebukes Dhritarashtra for indulging Duryodhana and advises Duryodhana to control his senses, make peace with the Pandavas, and give them their rightful share. She warns that war will bring destruction and that the Pandavas, with Krishna's support, are invincible.

beats.title: The Plot to Capture Kṛṣṇa
beats.summary: Duryodhana, with Karṇa, Śakuni, and Duḥśāsana, plots to capture Kṛṣṇa by force. Sātyaki learns of the plan and informs Kṛṣṇa and Vidura. Vidura rebukes Duryodhana, and Dhṛtarāṣṭra orders him brought back. Vidura recounts Kṛṣṇa's past exploits to dissuade Duryodhana.

beats.title: Krishna's Universal Form and Departure
beats.summary: In the Kuru court, after Vidura's speech, Krishna reveals his universal form to Duryodhana and the assembled kings, displaying all the gods, the Pandavas, and his weapons. He then departs for the Pandava camp, accompanied by Satyaki and Kritavarma. Dhritarashtra speaks to Krishna, expressing his desire for peace but his helplessness. Krishna takes leave of the elders and sets out to see his aunt Pritha.

beats.title: Kunti's Counsel to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Kunti instructs Krishna to convey a message to Yudhishthira, emphasizing the kshatriya dharma of protecting the people through strength and action, not passive non-violence. She recounts the story of King Mucukunda who refused a kingdom gifted by Kubera, preferring to earn it through his own valor. She explains that a king's duty is to uphold dharma through danda-niti (rule of law), which creates the golden age. She warns against the path of weakness and urges Yudhishthira to fight to reclaim his ancestral kingdom.

beats.title: Vidura's Rebuke to Her Defeated Son
beats.summary: Kunti recounts the story of Vidura, a wise woman who rebukes her defeated son for his cowardice and lack of spirit, urging him to rise, fight, and uphold dharma. The chapter emphasizes the importance of courage, self-reliance, and righteous action over passive despair.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel to Samjaya
beats.summary: Vidura advises Samjaya, who is despondent and considering abandoning his duty, to instead act with courage and follow the Kshatriya dharma. He warns against cowardice and poverty, recounts the story of a queen who suffered due to her husband's inaction, and urges Samjaya to fight, citing Indra's victory over Vritra as an example. Vidura emphasizes that a Kshatriya should never bow to anyone except Brahmins and should always strive, even if it leads to death.

beats.title: Mother's Counsel to a Despondent Son
beats.summary: A dialogue between a mother (likely Gandhari or a similar figure) and her son (likely Duryodhana or a Kaurava prince) where the mother urges him to fight, emphasizing the duties of a kshatriya and the importance of action, perseverance, and strategic alliances. The son expresses despair over lack of resources and allies, but the mother counsels him to not underestimate himself, to act with courage, and to win over others through generosity and leadership.

beats.title: Kunti's Counsel to Her Son
beats.summary: Queen Kunti counsels her son, King Dhritarashtra (or Sanjaya? Actually the text has 'mātovāca' and 'putra uvāca', likely Kunti speaking to her son Sanjaya? But Sanjaya is not a king. The chapter is from the Mahabharata, Parva 5 (Udyoga Parva), Chapter 134. It features a mother (Kunti) advising her son (Sanjaya? Or perhaps Dhritarashtra? The text says 'putra uvāca' and 'kunty uvāca', so Kunti and her son. But Sanjaya is a suta, not a king. However, the content is about a king not showing fear, and the son responds. Likely it is Kunti advising her son, who is a king? Possibly it is Kunti advising Dhritarashtra? But the text says 'mātovāca' and 'putra uvāca', and later 'kunty uvāca'. The son is addressed as 'rājan' in verse 1? Actually verse 1 says 'naiva rājñā daraḥ kāryo' meaning 'a king should never fear'. So the mother is addressing a king. In the Mahabharata, Kunti is the mother of the Pandavas, but she also advises Dhritarashtra? Or perhaps this is a different context. Given the ambiguity, I will treat the mother as Kunti and the son as Sanjaya? But Sanjaya is not a king. Alternatively, it could be Kunti advising her son Yudhishthira? However, the chapter is from Udyoga Parva, and this particular chapter (134) is known as 'Jaya' or 'Sanjaya's victory'? Actually, the chapter is titled 'Jaya' and is recited to inspire a king. The mother (Kunti) is advising her son (Sanjaya? No, Sanjaya is a charioteer's son). I think it's safer to note the ambiguity. The summary: A mother advises her son, a king, not to show fear even in adversity, as it causes the kingdom and allies to lose heart. She encourages him to rise and fight, promising hidden resources and many loyal friends. The son responds that even a dullard would be inspired by such words, and he resolves to conquer his enemies. Kunti then praises the power of such inspiring speech, and describes the ideal warrior king.

beats.title: Kunti's Message to the Pandavas
beats.summary: Kunti instructs Krishna to deliver messages to her sons and Draupadi, reminding them of their duty and the insults they suffered. She emphasizes the importance of righteous action and vengeance. Krishna then departs, meets with Karna, and travels to the Pandavas.

beats.title: Bhishma and Drona's Plea for Peace
beats.summary: Bhishma and Drona advise Duryodhana to make peace with the Pandavas, citing their strength, past defeats, and ominous omens. They urge him to seek reconciliation with Yudhishthira and his brothers.

beats.title: Bhishma and Drona's Warning to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Bhishma and Drona express their reluctance to fight Arjuna, whom they hold dear, but are bound by their kshatriya duty. They warn Duryodhana of the consequences of war, citing the strength of the Pandavas and their allies, including Krishna, Draupadi, and the sage Kubera's son. They urge Duryodhana to make peace.

beats.title: Krishna's Appeal to Karna
beats.summary: In this chapter, Krishna attempts to persuade Karna to join the Pandavas by revealing his true birth as the eldest son of Kunti and Pandu, offering him kingship and alliance with his brothers. Karna is urged to abandon Duryodhana and accept his rightful place among the Pandavas.

beats.title: Karna's Revelation and the War as Sacrifice
beats.summary: Karna reveals his true birth to Krishna, expresses loyalty to Duryodhana, and describes the upcoming war as a sacrificial ritual where the Kauravas are the offerings.

beats.title: Krishna's Prophecy and Message to the Kauravas
beats.summary: Krishna responds to Karna's words with a smile, declaring that the Pandavas' victory is certain. He describes Arjuna's divine standard, the might of the Pandava warriors, and predicts the destruction of the Kauravas. He instructs Karna to convey a message to Drona, Bhishma, Kripa, Duryodhana, and Jayadratha, urging them to fight on the upcoming new moon day, promising to fulfill their desires. He concludes that the kings following Duryodhana will attain a noble end in battle.

beats.title: Karna's Foreboding and Omens
beats.summary: Karna, after hearing Krishna's words, acknowledges the impending war and describes various omens and dreams indicating the defeat of the Kauravas and victory of the Pandavas. He predicts his own death and that of other warriors. Krishna responds that when destruction is imminent, reason departs. Karna then departs sadly.

beats.title: Kunti's Appeal to Karna
beats.summary: Vidura approaches Kunti after Krishna's failed peace mission, expressing despair over the impending war. Kunti decides to approach Karna, her firstborn, to persuade him to side with the Pandavas, revealing his birth secret. She goes to the Ganga where Karna is performing his daily prayers and waits for him to finish.

beats.title: Kunti's Revelation to Karna
beats.summary: Kunti reveals to Karna that he is her firstborn son, born from the sun god Surya, and not a suta's son. She urges him to join his brothers, the Pandavas, and enjoy the kingdom. Karna is advised to abandon Duryodhana and fight alongside Arjuna.

beats.title: Karna's Refusal to Kunti
beats.summary: Karna refuses Kunti's request to join the Pandavas, citing his loyalty to Duryodhana and the dishonor he suffered due to his abandonment. He promises not to kill any of Kunti's sons except Arjuna, and Kunti accepts his vow.

beats.title: Krishna's Report on the Peace Mission
beats.summary: After returning from Hastinapura, Krishna reports to Yudhishthira about his peace mission. Yudhishthira asks what Duryodhana, Bhishma, Drona, Vidura, and others said. Krishna recounts Bhishma's speech, where he narrates his own history of renouncing the throne, his vow of celibacy, and his efforts to secure the Kuru lineage. Bhishma advises Duryodhana to give half the kingdom to the Pandavas and avoid war.

beats.title: The Counsel of Drona and Vidura
beats.summary: In this chapter, Vasudeva narrates the events leading up to the Kurukshetra war, focusing on the lineage and duties of the Kuru dynasty. Drona advises Duryodhana to share the kingdom with the Pandavas, Vidura pleads for peace, and Gandhari rebukes Duryodhana for his greed. The chapter emphasizes dharma, the importance of rightful succession, and the impending destruction due to Duryodhana's actions.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, through Sanjaya, recounts the story of Yayati's curse on his son Yadu and the subsequent lineage, including Pratipa, Devapi, Bahlika, and Shantanu. He uses these examples to argue that the eldest son is not always entitled to the throne, and that Yudhishthira, though younger, is more worthy than Duryodhana. He advises Duryodhana to give half the kingdom to the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Final Attempt at Peace and the March to War
beats.summary: Vasudeva recounts to Dhritarashtra the events leading to the war: despite his efforts at peace through conciliation, bribery, and division, Duryodhana refused to yield even five villages, forcing the Pandavas to march to Kurukshetra for battle.

beats.title: Selection of the Commander-in-Chief and March to Kurukshetra
beats.summary: Yudhishthira consults his brothers and Krishna on choosing a commander-in-chief for the Pandava army. After hearing suggestions for Virata, Drupada, Dhrishtadyumna, and Shikhandi, Yudhishthira defers to Krishna, who confirms all are capable. The Pandava army marches to Kurukshetra, sets up camp, and prepares for battle.

beats.title: Duryodhana's War Preparations
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the events when Duryodhana heard of the Pandava army assembled at Kurukshetra with Vasudeva, Virata, and Drupada. Duryodhana, after Krishna's departure, orders preparations for war, and the Kaurava capital becomes bustling with military activity.

beats.title: Krishna's Counsel and the Pandavas' Preparation for War
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, recalling Krishna's earlier words, asks Krishna for guidance on how to act without deviating from dharma. Krishna explains that Duryodhana, influenced by Karna and others, rejects all wise counsel and is set on war. The Pandava army prepares for battle, and Yudhishthira laments the necessity of fighting elders. Arjuna reminds him of Krishna's and Vidura's advice, and Krishna confirms that Arjuna's words are correct. The Pandavas spend the night before battle peacefully.

beats.title: The Arrangement of the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: King Duryodhana organizes the Kaurava army into eleven akshauhinis, appoints commanders for each division, and describes the detailed structure of the forces including elephants, chariots, horses, and infantry.

beats.title: Appointment of Bhishma as Commander-in-Chief
beats.summary: Duryodhana requests Bhishma to be the commander-in-chief of the Kaurava army, citing the importance of a single leader. Bhishma accepts but notes his affection for the Pandavas and his inability to kill them directly. Karna refuses to fight while Bhishma is alive. Bhishma is consecrated as general, and the army marches to Kurukshetra, where ominous portents appear.

beats.title: Appointment of Commanders and Balarama's Departure
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about Yudhishthira's response to Bhishma's vow to fight. Yudhishthira appoints seven commanders for his army, with Dhrishtadyumna as supreme commander and Arjuna as overall leader. Balarama arrives, expresses his sorrow over the war, and decides to go on a pilgrimage to avoid witnessing the destruction.

beats.title: Rukmi's Arrival and Rejection
beats.summary: Rukmi, son of Bhishmaka and king of the Bhojas, arrives at the Pandava camp with a large army, offering to ally with Arjuna in the upcoming war. Arjuna proudly refuses, recounting his past victories without Rukmi's help. Rukmi then approaches Duryodhana, who also rejects him. Both Rukmi and Balarama (Rauhiṇeya) leave the battlefield. The Pandavas then hold a council.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Sanjaya's Response
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the events on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, lamenting his inability to control his son Duryodhana. Sanjaya responds that humans are not independent agents but are driven by fate, chance, or past actions, and advises Dhritarashtra to hear the account of the war without distress.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Taunt
beats.summary: Duryodhana sends Uluka as a messenger to the Pandavas, taunting them to fulfill their vows and fight. He reminds them of their sufferings and challenges Bhima to drink Duhshasana's blood.

beats.title: Ulūka's Taunt
beats.summary: Ulūka, the messenger of Duryodhana, delivers a provocative speech to Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas, taunting them about their past defeats, exile, and the humiliation of Draupadī. He challenges Arjuna and Bhīma to fulfill their vows, boasts of the Kaurava army's strength, and declares that he will not return the kingdom without war.

beats.title: Uluka's Embassy and Krishna's Response
beats.summary: Uluka delivers a provocative message from Duryodhana to the Pandavas, angering them. Krishna responds, asserting his role as Arjuna's charioteer and warning of the consequences.

beats.title: Arjuna's Rebuke and Vow
beats.summary: Arjuna, angered by Duryodhana's boastful words, rebukes him for relying on others' valor and declares that he will kill Bhishma first. He also reminds Duryodhana of Bhima's vow to kill Dushasana. Duryodhana then orders the army to prepare for battle at sunrise.

beats.title: Pandava Army Deployment
beats.summary: Samjaya describes how Yudhishthira, after hearing Uluka's message, orders his army to march out under the command of Dhrishtadyumna. Dhrishtadyumna arranges the Pandava forces in battle formation, assigning specific warriors to counter key Kaurava fighters.

beats.title: Bhishma's Appointment as Commander and Enumeration of Warriors
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the events after Arjuna's vow to kill Bhishma. Sanjaya reports that Bhishma, appointed as commander-in-chief, assures Duryodhana of victory and enumerates the great warriors on the Kaurava side, including Duryodhana, his brothers, Kritavarma, Shalya, Bhurishravas, and Jayadratha.

beats.title: Bhishma's Description of Kaurava Warriors
beats.summary: Bhishma describes the warriors on the Kaurava side to Duryodhana, listing their strengths and battle formations.

beats.title: Bhishma's Assessment of Kaurava Warriors
beats.summary: Bhishma describes the key warriors on the Kaurava side to Duryodhana, praising their prowess and predicting their roles in the battle. He highlights Aśvatthāmā, Drona, Paurava, Satyavrata, Vṛṣasena, Jalasandha, Bāhlīka, Senāpati, Alāyudha, and Bhagadatta, noting their strengths and potential impact.

beats.title: Bhishma and Karna's Confrontation
beats.summary: Bhishma evaluates Karna as an ardharatha (half-chariot-warrior) due to his lack of natural armor and earrings, and his curse. Karna angrily retorts, criticizing Bhishma's old age and bias, and boasts he alone can defeat the Pandavas. Duryodhana is urged to reconcile the rift.

beats.title: Bhishma's Assessment of the Pandava Army
beats.summary: Bhishma describes the prowess of the Pandava warriors to Duryodhana, emphasizing Arjuna's invincibility with Krishna as his charioteer, and warns against engaging them in battle.

beats.title: Bhishma's Assessment of Pandava Forces
beats.summary: Bhishma describes the key warriors on the Pandava side to Duryodhana, praising their valor and determination. He highlights Abhimanyu, Satyaki, Uttamaujas, Yudhamanyu, Virata, and Drupada, noting their loyalty to the Pandavas and their readiness to sacrifice their lives.

beats.title: Bhishma's Assessment of Pandava Warriors
beats.summary: Bhishma describes the key warriors on the Pandava side to Dhritarashtra, praising their skills and valor. He mentions Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Kshatradharma, Dhrishtaketu, Kshatradeva, Jayanta, Amitaujas, Satyajit, Aja, Bhoja, the five Kekaya brothers, Kashika, Sukumara, Nila, Suryadatta, Shankha, Madirashva, Vardhakshemi, Chitrayudha, Chekitana, Satyadhriti, Vyaghradatta, Candrasena, Senabindu, Krodhahanta, Kashya, Satyajit (son of Drupada), Pandyaraja, Dridhadhanva, Shreniman, and Vasudana.

beats.title: Bhishma's Assessment of the Pandava Army
beats.summary: Bhishma enumerates the great warriors on the Pandava side to Dhritarashtra, describing their prowess and his own resolve to fight, except against Shikhandi whom he refuses to strike due to his former female nature.

beats.title: Bhishma's Vow and the Abduction of the Kashi Princesses
beats.summary: Duryodhana asks Bhishma why he does not kill Shikhandi, despite having vowed to slay all Pandavas. Bhishma recounts his past: after his father Shantanu's death, he placed his brother Chitrangada on the throne, then Vichitravirya. To get wives for Vichitravirya, he abducted the three daughters of the king of Kashi—Amba, Ambika, and Ambalika—defeating all rival kings in battle. He then reported the deed to his mother Satyavati.

beats.title: Bhishma's Abduction of the Kashi Princesses
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts to Dhritarashtra how he won the daughters of the king of Kashi for his brother Vicitravirya, but the eldest princess reveals she had already chosen the king of Shalva as her husband and requests Bhishma to let her go.

beats.title: The Rejection of Amba
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates to Dhritarashtra the story of Amba, the eldest daughter of the king of Kashi. She was abducted by Bhishma for his brother Vichitravirya, but she had already chosen King Shalva as her husband. Bhishma allowed her to go to Shalva, but Shalva rejected her, fearing Bhishma and believing she was already taken. Despite her protests of purity and devotion, Shalva abandoned her. Amba, heartbroken and angry, curses her fate and leaves the city in distress.

beats.title: Amba's Lament and Resolve
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Amba, who was abducted by Bhishma for his brother Vichitravirya but later rejected by Shalva. She curses Bhishma and seeks refuge with ascetics, resolving to perform severe penance.

beats.title: Amba's Lament and Hotravahana's Intervention
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Amba, who after being rejected by Shalva, seeks refuge with sages in the forest. They advise her to return to her father, but she refuses, fearing disgrace. The sage Hotravahana, her maternal grandfather, arrives and promises to help her by sending her to Parashurama to seek justice against Bhishma.

beats.title: Ambā's Plight and the Quest for Rāma Jāmadagnya
beats.summary: Hotravāhana tells the story of Ambā, who was abducted by Bhīṣma for his brother Vicitravīrya but had already chosen Śālva as her husband. Śālva rejected her, and she seeks refuge in the forest, hoping to meet Rāma Jāmadagnya for help.

beats.title: Amba's Plea to Rama Jamadagnya
beats.summary: Akritavrana and Srnjaya discuss Amba's plight. Amba, abducted by Bhishma, seeks revenge. She approaches Rama Jamadagnya for help, recounting her story and requesting Bhishma's death.

beats.title: Parasurama's Intervention for Amba
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates to Dhritarashtra the story of how Parasurama (Rama Jamadagnya) was persuaded by the sage Akritavrana to fight Bhishma on behalf of Princess Amba. Parasurama, bound by his vow to protect Brahmins and the helpless, agrees to confront Bhishma. The chapter ends with Parasurama and the sages traveling to Kurukshetra with Amba.

beats.title: Bhishma's Confrontation with Parasurama
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts his confrontation with Parasurama (Jamadagnya) over the abducted princess of Kashi. Bhishma refuses to marry her or return her, citing kshatriya dharma. Parasurama challenges him, and Bhishma, despite respecting him as guru, defends his stance, leading to a battle at Kurukshetra.

beats.title: Bhishma's Account of the Encounter with Parashurama
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates his past encounter with Parashurama (Rama Jamadagnya) on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Parashurama challenges Bhishma to a duel, but Bhishma's mother Ganga intervenes and reconciles them, preventing the fight.

beats.title: Bhishma's Battle with Parashurama
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts his battle with Parashurama (Jamadagnya Rama), describing how he refused to fight from the ground, the divine nature of Rama's chariot, the exchange of arrows, and his eventual remorse for harming his guru.

beats.title: Bhishma's Duel with Parashurama
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts his fierce battle with Parashurama (Jamadagnya) to Dhritarashtra. The duel involves divine weapons, mutual wounding, and ends inconclusively at dusk when Parashurama withdraws.

beats.title: Bhishma's Battle with Parashurama
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts a fierce battle with Parashurama (Rama Jamadagnya), describing the exchange of divine weapons, the destruction of his chariot, and the eventual cessation of combat at sunset.

beats.title: Bhishma's Battle with Parashurama
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates his battle with Parashurama (Jamadagnya). After his charioteer is killed, Bhishma is struck down but saved by eight Brahmins and the river goddess Ganga. He recovers, fights back, and fells Parashurama with an arrow. Omens appear. The battle is suspended at sunset and resumed over many days.

beats.title: Bhishma's Dream and the Prasvapa Astra
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts a dream in which eight brahmins (the Vasus) instruct him to use the Prasvapa astra (a sleep-inducing weapon) against Parashurama (Jamadagnya) in their ongoing battle. They assure Bhishma that Parashurama will be put to sleep, not killed, and that Bhishma will later revive him. The dream ends with the brahmins vanishing.

beats.title: Bhishma's Battle with Parashurama
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts to Dhritarashtra a past battle with Parashurama (Jamadagnya), where both used divine weapons, culminating in a stalemate and the use of the Brahmastra, which threatened the universe. The conflict was resolved by the intervention of sages and the use of a sleep-inducing weapon.

beats.title: Bhishma's Battle with Parashurama and Reconciliation
beats.summary: Bhishma recounts to Dhritarashtra his past battle with Parashurama, where he was prevented from using the Prasvapa astra by divine sages and ultimately reconciled with his guru, who blessed him.

beats.title: The Story of the Kashi Princess and the River Vārṣikī
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates to King Dhritarashtra the story of the Kashi princess who, after being rejected by Bhishma, performed severe penance to become a river. Despite the efforts of Rama (Parashurama) and others, she could not defeat Bhishma in battle. She then undertook extreme austerities, eventually transforming into the river Vārṣikī in the Vatsabhumi region, while also remaining a maiden.

beats.title: The Vow of the Princess and the Boon of Shiva
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of a princess who, rejected by him, undertakes severe penance to gain the power to kill him. Lord Shiva appears, grants her boon that she will be reborn as a man (Shikhandi) and kill Bhishma in battle. She then immolates herself.

beats.title: The Birth and Transformation of Shikhandi
beats.summary: Duryodhana asks Bhishma how Shikhandi, who was born as a woman, became a man. Bhishma narrates that King Drupada, desiring a son to kill Bhishma, performed severe penance to please Shiva. Shiva granted him a child who would be a female first and then become a male. Drupada's queen gave birth to a daughter, but Drupada concealed her gender and raised her as a son named Shikhandi. Only Drupada and his queen knew the secret; others believed Shikhandi was male. Bhishma learned the truth through spies, Narada's words, and the penance of Amba.

beats.title: The Origin of Shikhandi
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Shikhandi's origin: Drupada, having a daughter raised as a son due to a boon from Shiva, marries her to the daughter of Hiranyavarma, king of Dasharnas. The deception is discovered, leading to conflict.

beats.title: The Deception of Shikhandini
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Shikhandini's birth and the conflict between King Drupada and King Hiranyavarma of the Dasharnas. Drupada, fearing Hiranyavarma's wrath over the deception that his child Shikhandini is a girl, consults his wife to ascertain the truth and decide on a course of action.

beats.title: The Birth and Transformation of Shikhandini
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Shikhandini's birth and transformation. King Drupada, fearing war with the king of Dasharna over his daughter being married as a woman, prays to the gods. His daughter Shikhandini, distressed by her parents' sorrow, goes to a forest inhabited by a yaksha named Sthunakarna. She fasts and the yaksha appears, offering a boon. She requests to become a man to save her family from conflict.

beats.title: The Origin of Shikhandi
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates the story of Shikhandi's origin: born as a female (Shikhandini) to Drupada, she exchanged genders with a yaksha named Sthuna, later cursed by Kubera to remain female until Shikhandi's death in battle. Bhishma explains his vow not to fight Shikhandi because of her former female nature.

beats.title: The Warriors' Estimates
beats.summary: Duryodhana asks Bhishma, Drona, Kripa, Ashvatthama, and Karna how long each would take to destroy the Pandava army. Bhishma says he could kill 10,000 soldiers daily or, using divine weapons, finish in a month. Drona says a month, Kripa two months, Ashvatthama ten days, and Karna five days. Bhishma laughs at Karna's boast, noting he hasn't faced Arjuna with Krishna.

beats.title: Arjuna's Declaration of Invincibility
beats.summary: Yudhishthira learns from spies that Duryodhana asked Bhishma, Drona, Kripacharya, Ashvatthama, and Karna how long it would take them to destroy the Pandava army. Arjuna, with Krishna's approval, declares he could destroy all three worlds in an instant with the Pashupata weapon, but chooses to fight fairly. He also lists the Pandava warriors who are capable of defeating even the gods.

beats.title: Marshaling of the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: This chapter describes the marshaling of the Kaurava army on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. King Duryodhana, after consulting with his commanders, organizes the troops into divisions led by various kings and warriors. The army is arrayed in a grand formation, and Duryodhana ensures provisions and accommodations for all, including soldiers, animals, and camp followers.

beats.title: Arrangement of the Pandava Army
beats.summary: Yudhishthira organizes the Pandava army for battle, assigning commanders and arranging troops in formation. The army marches forth with great noise and enthusiasm, led by Dhrishtadyumna, Bhima, Arjuna, and others.

beats.title: The Assembly of Armies at Kurukṣetra
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṁpāyana how the war was fought. Vaiśaṁpāyana describes the armies assembling at Kurukṣetra, Yudhiṣṭhira's arrangements, Duryodhana's formation, the blowing of conches, and the establishment of rules of engagement.

beats.title: Vyasa's Boon to Sanjaya and Omens of War
beats.summary: Vyasa grants divine vision to Sanjaya so he can narrate the war to Dhritarashtra, and describes ominous portents foretelling the destruction of the Kuru dynasty.

beats.title: Omens of War
beats.summary: Vyasa describes a series of terrifying omens and portents to Dhritarashtra, foretelling the destruction of the Kauravas in the upcoming war. Dhritarashtra accepts these as fate and expresses that Kshatriyas dying in battle will attain glory and heaven.

beats.title: Vyasa's Discourse on Omens and Strategy
beats.summary: Vyasa explains to Dhritarashtra the omens and signs of victory and defeat in battle, emphasizing that courage and morale are more decisive than numbers, and that wise kings should seek victory through strategy and negotiation rather than war.

beats.title: The Earth and the Beings
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after hearing Vyasa's words, falls into contemplation and then asks Sanjaya about the kings gathered for battle, their numbers, and the qualities of the earth. Sanjaya responds by describing the categories of beings, the fourteen types of domestic and wild animals, and the importance of the earth as the foundation of all. He also mentions the Gayatri mantra and concludes that kings fight over the earth.

beats.title: Description of the Earth and the Island of Sudarshana
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya to describe the names of rivers, mountains, countries, and other features of the earth, as well as its extent. Sanjaya begins by explaining the five great elements (earth, water, fire, air, ether) and their qualities, then describes the island of Sudarshana, which is circular and surrounded by the salt ocean, and is seen in the moon's disc.

beats.title: Geography of Jambudvipa
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the geography of Jambudvipa, including its seven main mountains (Himavan, Hemakuta, Nishadha, Nila, Shveta, Shringavan, and Malyavan), the central Mount Meru, and the surrounding regions (Bhadrashva, Ketumala, Jambudvipa, and Uttarakurus). He details the divine beings, rivers, and the seven Ganges, concluding with the shape of the continent resembling a hare.

beats.title: Description of Meru's Regions and Jambūdvīpa
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the northern and eastern regions of Mount Meru, including the Uttara Kurus, Bhadrāśva, and the Jambūdvīpa with its great Jambū tree. He details the inhabitants, their lifespan, and the natural features like rivers of fruit juice and gold. The chapter also mentions the mountain Mālyavān and the sun's retinue.

beats.title: Description of the Regions and the Supreme Lord
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the names of the rainy seasons, mountains, and their inhabitants. Sanjaya describes the regions of Ramanaka, Hairanvata, and Airavata, detailing the lifespan and characteristics of their people. He then describes the divine abode of Hari (Vishnu) in Vaikuntha, north of the ocean of milk, and his cosmic role. Dhritarashtra reflects on the impermanence of the world and identifies Hari as Narayana, the all-knowing sustainer.

beats.title: The Description of Bharata Varsha
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the land of Bharata (India) to Dhritarashtra, listing its mountains, rivers, and numerous kingdoms and tribes, and explains that the greed for this land drives the Kauravas and Pandavas to war.

beats.title: Description of Yugas and Regions
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the dimensions, lifespan, and fruits of the Bharata, Haimavata, and Harivarsha regions. Sanjaya describes the four yugas (Krita, Treta, Dvapara, Pushya) in the Bharata varsha, their durations, and the characteristics of beings born in each yuga, noting the decline in virtue and increase in vice over time.

beats.title: Geography of the Continents
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the dimensions of Jambudvipa and other continents, and Sanjaya describes the geography of Shakadvipa, including its mountains, rivers, and inhabitants.

beats.title: Geography of the Northern Islands and Celestial Bodies
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the geography of the northern islands (dvīpas) beyond Jambūdvīpa, including their mountains, rivers, and inhabitants. He details the seven islands (Kuśa, Śālmalī, Krauñca, Puṣkara, etc.), their features, and the presence of divine beings. He also describes the dimensions of the sun, moon, and the demon Rāhu, and explains how the elephants of the directions regulate the winds. The chapter concludes with the benefits of hearing this Bhūmiparva.

beats.title: The Fall of Bhishma
beats.summary: Sanjaya returns from the battlefield and reports to Dhritarashtra that Bhishma has been slain by Shikhandin after ten days of fighting. He describes Bhishma's valor and his fall, blaming Dhritarashtra's evil counsel.

beats.title: The Fall of Bhishma
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, grieving the death of Bhishma, asks Sanjaya to recount in detail how Bhishma fell in battle, who surrounded him, and how the Pandavas managed to defeat him despite his invincibility. He laments the loss and reflects on the inevitability of fate.

beats.title: The Armies Arrayed
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the Kurukshetra war preparations: Duryodhana's instructions to protect Bhishma, the array of armies, and the fearsome sight of Bhishma leading the Kaurava forces.

beats.title: The Kaurava Army's Deployment
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the Kaurava army's formation on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, including omens, Bhishma's speech to the kings about the warrior's duty, and the deployment of various warriors and their standards.

beats.title: The Kaurava Army Arrayed for Battle
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the tumultuous sounds and sights of the Kaurava army arrayed for battle, including the divisions led by Duryodhana's brothers and allied kings, with Bhishma as the commander protected by a vast force.

beats.title: The Vajra Formation
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya how Yudhishthira, with a smaller army, arrayed his forces against the Kaurava army. Sanjaya describes the Vajra formation (also called Acala) devised by Arjuna, with Bhima as the vanguard. The chapter details the positions of key warriors, including Bhima, Arjuna, Dhrishtadyumna, Shikhandi, and others, and ends with ominous omens like meteors, earthquakes, and dust storms.

beats.title: The Armies Arrayed at Sunrise
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the disposition of the armies at sunrise. Sanjaya describes both armies as equally vast and fierce, with the Kauravas facing west and the Pandavas east. He details the Kaurava formation led by Bhishma, with Duryodhana on an elephant, and notes the presence of various allies. The Pandava army is led by Krishna and Arjuna, and Sanjaya considers it invincible due to their leadership.

beats.title: Arjuna's Consolation to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Yudhishthira despairs upon seeing the Kaurava army's invincible formation led by Bhishma. Arjuna consoles him, recounting the teaching of Narada and the ancient devasura war: victory comes not from strength but from truth, compassion, dharma, and effort, and ultimately from Krishna. He asserts that where Krishna is, there is victory.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍava Army Formation and Bhīṣma's Threat
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the Pāṇḍava army formation, with Yudhiṣṭhira at the center, Arjuna and Kṛṣṇa in their chariot, Bhīma and the twins as protectors, and Kṛṣṇa pointing out Bhīṣma as the chief threat. Dhṛtarāṣṭra asks about the morale and initial actions of both armies.

beats.title: Arjuna's Grief on the Battlefield
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas are doing on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes Duryodhana's speech to Drona, listing the prominent warriors on both sides. Bhishma blows his conch, followed by others, and Arjuna asks Krishna to place the chariot between the armies. Seeing relatives on both sides, Arjuna is overcome with compassion and grief, expressing his unwillingness to fight.

beats.title: The Yoga of Arjuna's Dejection and the Path of Wisdom
beats.summary: Krishna (Madhusudana) rebukes Arjuna for his pity and delusion, teaching him about the eternal nature of the self (atman), the duty of a warrior, and the path of yoga (equanimity). Arjuna asks about the characteristics of a stable-minded person, and Krishna describes the sage of steady wisdom.

beats.title: Karma Yoga: The Path of Action
beats.summary: Krishna explains the superiority of action (karma-yoga) over renunciation, emphasizing that everyone must act according to their nature. He describes the cycle of sacrifice and the importance of performing actions without attachment for the welfare of the world. Arjuna asks what drives a person to sin, and Krishna identifies desire and anger as the enemies, urging control of the senses and mind.

beats.title: The Yoga of Knowledge and Action
beats.summary: Krishna reveals the eternal yoga to Arjuna, explaining his divine incarnations, the nature of action and inaction, and the superiority of knowledge. He describes various types of sacrifice and emphasizes that knowledge purifies all actions.

beats.title: Karma Yoga and Sannyasa
beats.summary: Krishna explains the difference between sannyasa (renunciation of actions) and karma yoga (yoga of action), asserting that karma yoga is superior. He describes the characteristics of a true renunciant, the unity of sankhya and yoga, and the path to liberation through detached action, equanimity, and knowledge. The chapter culminates in the vision of the supreme Lord as the enjoyer of all sacrifices and the friend of all beings, leading to peace.

beats.title: The Yoga of Meditation
beats.summary: Krishna teaches Arjuna the path of yoga, emphasizing self-discipline, detachment from fruits of actions, and meditation. He describes the characteristics of a perfected yogi, the practice of meditation, and the fate of one who falls from yoga. The chapter concludes with Krishna declaring that the yogi who worships him with faith is the highest.

beats.title: Krishna's Discourse on Divine Manifestation
beats.summary: Krishna reveals his divine nature as the supreme reality, describing his eightfold material nature and higher spiritual nature. He explains that he is the source of all, the essence in all elements, and the goal of all knowledge. He distinguishes between those who are deluded by the three gunas and those who surrender to him. He categorizes four types of virtuous devotees and extols the jnani as the highest. He also explains how he supports the faith of those who worship other deities, but emphasizes that their rewards are temporary, while his devotees attain him.

beats.title: The Yoga of the Supreme Spirit
beats.summary: Arjuna asks Krishna about the nature of Brahman, Adhyatma, Karma, Adhibhuta, Adhidaiva, and Adhiyajna. Krishna explains these concepts, emphasizing the importance of remembering Him at the time of death to attain His supreme state. He describes the two paths of departure (light and dark) and the eternal nature of the supreme being.

beats.title: The Yoga of the Supreme Secret
beats.summary: Krishna reveals the most secret knowledge of devotion and surrender to him as the supreme reality, explaining his immanence and transcendence, the futility of mere ritual without devotion, and the promise of liberation for all who take refuge in him.

beats.title: The Yoga of Divine Manifestations
beats.summary: Krishna reveals his divine manifestations (vibhutis) to Arjuna, explaining that all beings and powers originate from him. He lists his primary forms among various categories of existence, emphasizing that he is the source of everything and that knowing this leads to liberation.

beats.title: The Vision of the Universal Form
beats.summary: Arjuna requests Krishna to show His universal form. Krishna grants Arjuna divine sight and reveals His cosmic form, containing all beings, gods, and the universe. Arjuna sees the terrifying form with many mouths and weapons, and the Kauravas entering His mouths. Krishna declares He is Time, the destroyer of worlds, and that the warriors are already slain by Him; Arjuna is merely an instrument. Arjuna prays for forgiveness and asks to see the four-armed form again. Krishna resumes His gentle form and teaches that He can be known only through single-minded devotion.

beats.title: The Path of Devotion and the Qualities of a Devotee
beats.summary: Arjuna asks Krishna which is better: worshipping the manifest personal God or the unmanifest Brahman. Krishna explains that those who fix their minds on Him with supreme faith are the highest yogis. He describes the difficulty of worshipping the unmanifest for embodied beings. He then outlines a progressive path: fixing the mind on Him, practice, performing actions for Him, and renouncing fruits of actions. He extols the qualities of a beloved devotee: equal-minded, compassionate, detached, and devoted.

beats.title: The Field and the Knower of the Field
beats.summary: Krishna explains the distinction between the field (kṣetra) and the knower of the field (kṣetrajña), describing the nature of the body, the process of knowledge, and the supreme Brahman. He emphasizes that true knowledge leads to liberation and that the supreme self, though dwelling in the body, remains unaffected.

beats.title: The Three Guṇas and Transcendence
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) that bind the soul to the body, their characteristics, effects, and how to transcend them through detachment and devotion to Krishna.

beats.title: The Supreme Person and the Cosmic Tree
beats.summary: Lord Krishna reveals the eternal cosmic tree (ashvattha) with roots above and branches below, symbolizing the material world. He explains the nature of the self, the process of liberation through detachment, and his own supreme position as the Purushottama beyond the perishable and imperishable. He describes how the jiva (individual soul) is an eternal part of him, entangled in the senses and mind, and how the enlightened perceive the soul while the ignorant do not. He concludes by stating that knowing this secret scripture leads to wisdom and fulfillment.

beats.title: The Divine and Demoniac Natures
beats.summary: Lord Krishna describes the divine and demoniac natures, listing virtues of the divine and vices of the demoniac, and explains their consequences. He urges Arjuna to follow scripture and cultivate divine qualities.

beats.title: The Threefold Division of Faith
beats.summary: Krishna explains the threefold nature of faith (śraddhā) based on the three guṇas: sattva, rajas, and tamas. He describes how faith determines a person's character and actions, including worship, food, sacrifices, austerities, and charity. The chapter concludes with the significance of the syllable Om and the words Tat and Sat as symbols of Brahman.

beats.title: The Yoga of Renunciation and the Three Gunas
beats.summary: Krishna explains the difference between sannyasa (renunciation of actions) and tyaga (renunciation of the fruits of actions), discusses the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) as they apply to knowledge, action, doer, intellect, determination, and happiness, describes the duties of the four varnas based on svabhava, and concludes with the path of devotion and surrender to Krishna as the ultimate means to liberation.

beats.title: Yudhishthira Seeks Blessings from Elders
beats.summary: Yudhishthira approaches Bhishma, Drona, Kripa, and Shalya to seek their blessings before battle. Each guru blesses him but explains they are bound by duty to the Kauravas. Yudhishthira also asks how to defeat them, receiving hints of their vulnerabilities. Krishna attempts to recruit Karna, who refuses. Yuyutsu defects to the Pandavas. The chapter ends with the armies readying for battle.

beats.title: The First Clash
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya who struck first in the battle. Sanjaya describes the initial clash: Duryodhana leads the Kauravas with Bhishma at the front; the Pandavas advance with Bhima leading. A tumultuous noise arises from conches, drums, and war cries. Bhima roars like a lion, terrifying the Kaurava army. Duryodhana and his brothers attack Bhima, but the Pandava brothers and sons of Draupadi counter. The battle becomes fierce, with no one retreating. The sun is obscured by dust. Bhishma shines above all.

beats.title: The First Day's Battle
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce and chaotic battle on the first day of the Kurukshetra war, with numerous duels between warriors from both sides, including Bhishma vs Arjuna, Satyaki vs Kritavarma, Abhimanyu vs Brihadbala, Bhima vs Duryodhana, and many others. The battle is marked by intense fighting, mutual wounding, and the involvement of divine beings observing the carnage.

beats.title: The Great Battle Unfolds
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the chaotic and brutal battle of Kurukṣetra, where warriors fight indiscriminately, ignoring familial bonds. The chapter depicts the fierce combat between the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas, with vivid imagery of elephants, chariots, horses, and foot soldiers. Bhīṣma's presence terrifies the Pāṇḍava army.

beats.title: Bhishma's Onslaught and Abhimanyu's Valor
beats.summary: Bhishma leads the Kaurava attack, causing massive destruction. Abhimanyu fights valiantly, but is overwhelmed. Shalya kills Uttara. Bhishma's onslaught forces the Pandava army to retreat as sunset ends the day's battle.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Despair and the Krauncha Formation
beats.summary: Yudhishthira despairs at Bhishma's prowess, but Krishna encourages him, appoints Dhrishtadyumna as commander, and the Pandavas form the Krauncha formation.

beats.title: The Krauncha Formation and the Blowing of Conches
beats.summary: Duryodhana, seeing the Pandava army arrayed in the Krauncha formation, addresses his generals and warriors, urging them to protect Bhishma. The Kaurava army then arranges its own formation. Both sides blow conches, creating a tumultuous sound, and the battle is about to commence.

beats.title: The Beginning of the War: Bhishma vs Arjuna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the beginning of the Kurukshetra war, focusing on the initial confrontation between Bhishma and Arjuna. It includes Duryodhana's encouragement of his forces, the fierce exchange of arrows between the two great warriors, and the admiration of celestial beings for their skill.

beats.title: The Duel of Drona and Dhrishtadyumna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle between Drona and Dhrishtadyumna. Sanjaya describes the fierce duel, where Drona disables Dhrishtadyumna's chariot and weapons, but Bhima intervenes, rescues Dhrishtadyumna, and attacks Drona. Duryodhana then orders the Kalinga army to protect Drona, leading to a massive battle between Bhima and the Kalingas.

beats.title: Bhīma's Slaughter of the Kaliṅgas
beats.summary: Bhīmasena single-handedly decimates the Kaliṅga army, killing their king Śrutāyus, prince Ketumān, and Śakradeva, among many others. He fights with a sword after his chariot is disabled, displaying superhuman prowess. Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna and Sātyaki support him. Bhīṣma briefly engages but is driven off when his charioteer is killed. The Pāṇḍava forces celebrate Bhīma's victory.

beats.title: The Fourteenth Day: Abhimanyu's Duel and Arjuna's Rampage
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle on the fourteenth day, focusing on the duel between Abhimanyu and Lakṣmaṇa, Arjuna's devastating attack on the Kaurava army, and Bhīṣma's decision to sound the retreat at sunset.

beats.title: Battle Formations at Kurukshetra
beats.summary: Bhishma arranges the Kaurava army in the Garuda formation at dawn. The Pandavas, led by Arjuna and Dhrishtadyumna, counter with a crescent formation. A fierce battle ensues with great noise and chaos.

beats.title: Second Day of the Kurukshetra War
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the fierce battle on the second day of the Kurukshetra war. Arjuna attacks the Kaurava army, but the Kauravas resist. Both sides fight fiercely with various weapons. The battlefield becomes covered with dust and blood, and many warriors fall. Drona, Bhishma, Jayadratha, and others break the Pandava formations, while Bhima, Ghatotkacha, Satyaki, and the Draupadeyas counterattack. Duryodhana leads a thousand chariots against the Pandavas, and Arjuna attacks the enemy kings. The battle continues with great intensity.

beats.title: The Fourteenth Day: Arjuna's Valor and the Rout of the Kauravas
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the fierce battle on the 14th day of the Kurukshetra war. Arjuna is surrounded by thousands of enemy chariots but repels their weapons. Satyaki and Abhimanyu fight together against the Saubalas. Bhima and Ghatotkacha attack Duryodhana; Bhima wounds him, causing him to faint. The Kaurava army flees but is rallied by Duryodhana, who then reproaches Bhishma for not fighting properly. Bhishma promises to do his best.

beats.title: Bhishma's Rampage and Krishna's Intervention
beats.summary: Bhishma, enraged by Duryodhana's distress, launches a fierce attack on the Pandava army, causing massive destruction. Arjuna and Krishna counter him, but Arjuna hesitates due to respect for Bhishma. Krishna then takes up his discus to kill Bhishma, but Arjuna stops him, vowing to fulfill his promise. Arjuna uses the divine Aindra weapon, routing the Kaurava forces as night falls.

beats.title: The Dawn of Battle: Bhishma's Array and the Duel with Arjuna
beats.summary: Bhishma leads the Kaurava army at dawn, forming a formidable array. Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, advances with the Pandava forces. Abhimanyu attacks the Kaurava commanders. Bhishma and Arjuna engage in a fierce duel, witnessed by all.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Valor and Dhrishtadyumna's Intervention
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce battle where Abhimanyu (Saubhadra) fights valiantly against multiple Kaurava warriors including Drona's son, Bhurishravas, Shalya, and others. He is surrounded but fights bravely. Dhrishtadyumna, the Panchala prince, comes to aid Arjuna and Abhimanyu, killing the son of Samyamani in combat. The chapter highlights the prowess of Abhimanyu and the intervention of Dhrishtadyumna.

beats.title: The Slaughter of the Elephant Division
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the inevitable defeat of his sons despite their efforts, while Sanjaya describes a fierce battle where Bhima and Abhimanyu lead the Pandava attack against the Kaurava forces, including an elephant charge led by the Magadha king. Bhima, wielding a mace, decimates the elephant division, and Abhimanyu kills the Magadha king. The chapter highlights the Pandavas' superior combat prowess and the Kauravas' desperation.

beats.title: Bhima's Rampage and Satyaki's Intervention
beats.summary: Bhima single-handedly attacks the Kaurava army after the elephant division is killed. Duryodhana orders his forces to attack Bhima, but Bhima holds them back like a shore holds back the ocean. He kills many warriors with his mace, causing panic. Bhishma then advances to confront Bhima, but Satyaki intercepts him, and a fierce battle ensues between Satyaki and Bhurishravas.

beats.title: Bhima's Rampage and Ghatotkacha's Intervention
beats.summary: Bhima fights Duryodhana and his brothers, killing many of them. Ghatotkacha intervenes with his magical powers, causing the Kaurava army to retreat. Bhishma advises withdrawal, and the day ends with the Pandavas celebrating.

beats.title: Bhishma's Discourse on the Invincibility of the Pandavas
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, distressed by the Pandavas' success, asks Sanjaya why his sons are defeated despite having great warriors like Bhishma and Drona. Sanjaya explains that the Pandavas fight righteously and are protected by Krishna, while the Kauravas are unrighteous. Bhishma recounts a past event where Brahma praised Vishnu (Narayana) and revealed that Krishna is the supreme being who incarnates to establish dharma and destroy demons. Bhishma concludes that the Pandavas are invincible because Krishna is on their side.

beats.title: Bhishma's Discourse on the Divine Nature of Vasudeva
beats.summary: Bhishma narrates to Dhritarashtra the divine origin of Vasudeva (Krishna) as the supreme being, who incarnates with Nara (Arjuna) to destroy demons. He emphasizes that Krishna is not an ordinary human but the eternal lord, and warns against disrespecting him.

beats.title: Bhishma's Discourse on the Supreme Vasudeva
beats.summary: Bhishma describes the supreme nature of Vasudeva (Krishna) as the creator, sustainer, and ultimate reality, including his cosmic forms and deeds such as slaying the demon Madhu. He explains that Krishna is the source of all beings, elements, and social classes, and that surrendering to him leads to liberation.

beats.title: Bhishma's Counsel to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Bhishma praises Krishna as the supreme deity, recounting various sages' declarations of his divinity, and advises Dhritarashtra to make peace with the Pandavas, warning that disrespecting Nara-Narayana leads to destruction.

beats.title: The Deployment and Initial Clash
beats.summary: The chapter describes the initial deployment of armies on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, focusing on the formation of the Pandava army in a 'shyena' (eagle) formation and the Kaurava army in a 'makara' (crocodile) formation. It narrates the opening clashes, including Bhima's attack on Bhishma, Arjuna's counterattack, Duryodhana's appeal to Drona, and the fierce engagement involving key warriors like Abhimanyu, the Draupadeyas, and Shikhandi. The battle is compared to a conflict between gods and demons.

beats.title: The First Day's Battle
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the fierce battle on the first day, with Bhīṣma leading the Kauravas and the Pāṇḍavas countering. The scene is chaotic with elephants, horses, and warriors clashing, and Duryodhana advances with the Kaliṅgas while the Pāṇḍavas surround Bhīma to attack Bhīṣma.

beats.title: The Onset of Battle: Arjuna's Assault and General Engagement
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the beginning of the battle on day 1, focusing on Arjuna's attack on Bhīṣma, the confusion caused by Arjuna's weapons, and the general engagement of various warriors and armies.

beats.title: The Initial Clash of Armies
beats.summary: This chapter describes the initial deployment and clash of the Kaurava and Pandava armies on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. It details the specific warriors engaging each other, the fierce fighting, and the temporary setback of the Pandavas when Bhishma disables Satyaki's chariot. The battle intensifies as both sides charge at each other.

beats.title: The First Day's Battle: Duels and Slaughter
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle on the first day, focusing on duels between key warriors: Arjuna vs. Aśvatthāmā, Bhīmasena vs. Duryodhana, and Abhimanyu vs. Lakṣmaṇa. Bhīṣma slaughters the Pāṇḍava army with divine weapons, and the field becomes strewn with corpses.

beats.title: Bhūriśravasa's Duel and Day's End
beats.summary: Bhūriśravasa fights Sātyaki and kills his ten sons, then Sātyaki and Bhūriśravasa engage in a duel. Bhīmasena rescues Sātyaki by giving him a chariot, while a Kaurava prince gives Bhūriśravasa a chariot. Arjuna kills 25,000 warriors. As the sun sets, Bhīṣma orders withdrawal, and both armies return to their camps.

beats.title: The Second Day: Makara and Krauncha Formations
beats.summary: On the second day of the Kurukshetra war, both armies form complex battle formations: the Pandavas arrange a Makara (crocodile) formation under Dhrishthadyumna's command, while the Kauravas counter with a Krauncha (heron) formation led by Bhishma. Fierce fighting ensues, with notable duels between Bhima and Drona. The formations eventually break down into chaotic single combat.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament on the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra describes the vast and well-equipped Kaurava army, lamenting that despite its strength, it was destroyed in battle. He reflects on fate, the advice of Vidura, and the stubbornness of Duryodhana.

beats.title: Bhima's Solo Attack and Rescue
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the battle after Dhritarashtra's lament. Bhima enters the Kaurava army, fights alone, and is rescued by Dhrishtadyumna. Drona defeats Drupada and uses a celestial weapon to confuse the Kauravas, but later dispels it. Yudhishthira sends Abhimanyu and others to support Bhima and Dhrishtadyumna. Drona attacks Dhrishtadyumna, cutting his bow and killing his horses, but Dhrishtadyumna escapes to Abhimanyu's chariot. The Kaurava army rejoices at Drona's prowess.

beats.title: Bhima's Battle with Duryodhana and His Brothers
beats.summary: Bhima fights Duryodhana and his brothers, who attack him together. Abhimanyu and other Pandava warriors join the battle. Vikarna is wounded by Abhimanyu. Duryodhana's brothers are repelled. Bhishma attacks the Pandava army. Arjuna's Gandiva bow is heard. The battlefield becomes a river of blood with severed limbs and elephants.

beats.title: Bhima's Assault and the Evening Battle
beats.summary: Bhima attacks Duryodhana, cutting his bow, chariot, and umbrella, but Duryodhana is rescued by Jayadratha and Kripacharya. Abhimanyu fights eight Kaurava brothers, defeating Vikarna. Shrutakarma and Shrutakirti engage with Kaurava princes; Shrutakarma is rescued by Sutasoma. Shatanika kills Dushkarna, then five Kekaya brothers attack him but are countered by five Kaurava brothers. Bhishma destroys the Pandava army. The day ends with both sides returning to camp.

beats.title: Bhishma's Vow and the Kaurava Army's Assembly
beats.summary: After the day's battle, the warriors return to their camps, bathed in blood. Duryodhana, distressed, approaches Bhishma for counsel. Bhishma reassures him, vowing to fight the Pandavas with full might, though acknowledging their formidable allies. Encouraged, Duryodhana orders the army to march out again, and the Kaurava forces assemble for battle, creating a tumultuous and awe-inspiring spectacle.

beats.title: Bhishma's Mandala Formation and the Initial Onslaught
beats.summary: Bhishma, having promised Duryodhana to fight, arranges the Kaurava army in a mandala formation. The Pandavas counter with a vajra formation. A fierce battle ensues with various duels. Arjuna, aided by Krishna, unleashes the Aindra astra, causing havoc. Bhishma protects the fleeing Kaurava soldiers.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Bhīṣma Advances and Multiple Duels
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the battle after Suśarman's defeat. Bhīṣma advances to fight Arjuna, while Droṇa attacks Virāṭa, killing his son Śaṅkha. Aśvatthāmā fights Śikhaṇḍī, Sātyaki defeats the rākṣasa Alambusa, Dhṛṣṭadyumna attacks Duryodhana, and Bhīma wounds Kṛtavarmā.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Iravan, Ghatotkacha, and the Twins
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the defeat of his sons despite their efforts. Sanjaya describes the battle, focusing on the exploits of Iravan (son of Arjuna and a Naga princess) who defeats the Avanti princes, and Ghatotkacha (son of Bhima and Hidimba) who fights Bhagadatta but is forced to retreat. The Madra king Shalya fights his nephews Nakula and Sahadeva, who wound him and drive him from the field.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Yudhishthira Defeats Shrutayu, Duels and Rescues, Arjuna Advances
beats.summary: This chapter describes the battle events of the Mahabharata war on a particular day. Yudhishthira fights and defeats Shrutayu, causing Duryodhana's army to flee. Cekitana and Kripa engage in a fierce duel, both falling unconscious but rescued by allies. Abhimanyu fights multiple Kaurava warriors but spares them, remembering Bhima's words. Arjuna, urged by Yudhishthira, advances to protect Abhimanyu and confronts Suśarman and other kings guarding Bhishma.

beats.title: Bhima's Mace and Shikhandin's Vow
beats.summary: Arjuna defeats the Trigarta king and his forces, then moves toward Bhishma. Yudhishthira, Bhima, Nakula, and Sahadeva also engage Bhishma. Jayadratha cuts their bows. Duryodhana attacks them. Yudhishthira rebukes Shikhandin for not fulfilling his vow to kill Bhishma. Shikhandin attacks Bhishma but is intercepted by Shalya. Bhishma cuts Yudhishthira's bow and flag. Bhima, enraged, attacks Jayadratha on foot with a mace, killing his horses. Duryodhana advances to protect Jayadratha. Bhima throws his mace, which destroys Citrasena's chariot. The Kauravas cheer Duryodhana.

beats.title: The Eighth Day of the Kurukshetra War
beats.summary: This chapter describes the eighth day of the Kurukshetra war, focusing on the fierce battle between Bhishma and Yudhishthira, the intervention of Shikhandi, the exploits of Dhrishhtadyumna and Satyaki, and the eventual retreat of both armies at sunset. The chapter ends with the armies returning to their camps, tending to wounds, and celebrating briefly before resting.

beats.title: Second Day of the Kurukshetra War: Army Formations and Initial Clash
beats.summary: The chapter describes the formation of armies on the battlefield of Kurukshetra on the second day of the war. After a night's rest, both sides prepare for battle. Bhishma arranges the Kaurava army in a great ocean-like formation, with various kings and warriors in specific positions. Yudhishthira asks Dhrishtadyumna to counter it, who forms a śṛṅgāṭaka (horn-shaped) formation. The battle begins with fierce fighting involving elephants, chariots, and infantry.

beats.title: Bhīma's Vengeance: The Slaying of Duryodhana's Brothers
beats.summary: Bhīma kills eight of Duryodhana's brothers in battle, fulfilling his earlier vow. Duryodhana laments to Bhīṣma, who reminds him of the warnings given by Vidura, Droṇa, and Gāndhārī, and advises him to fight with resolve, acknowledging that the Pāṇḍavas are invincible.

beats.title: The Slaughter of the Kauravas
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the daily slaughter of his sons by Bhima, despite the presence of great warriors like Bhishma, Drona, and Kripa. Sanjaya recounts the fierce battle where Bhima wreaks havoc on the Kaurava army, and Arjuna's sons and allies also inflict heavy casualties. The chapter describes the carnage in vivid detail, with rivers of blood and the earth strewn with fallen warriors and equipment.

beats.title: The Death of Iravān
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle where Śakuni and Hārdikya attack the Pāṇḍava army. Iravān, Arjuna's son by a Nāga princess, fights valiantly but is slain by the Rākṣasa Ārśyaśṛṅgi through magical combat. The battle continues with Bhīṣma, Bhīma, and others fighting fiercely.

beats.title: Ghatotkacha's Rampage
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what the Pandavas did after Iravan was killed. Sanjaya describes Ghatotkacha's fierce roar that terrifies the Kaurava army, then his attack on Duryodhana's elephant division. Duryodhana fights back, killing several rakshasas. Ghatotkacha, enraged, confronts Duryodhana, reminding him of his misdeeds against the Pandavas and Draupadi, and showers him with arrows.

beats.title: Ghatotkacha's Rampage
beats.summary: Ghatotkacha, the rakshasa son of Bhima, attacks Duryodhana's army. He kills the Vaṅga king's elephant and then engages many Kaurava warriors, wounding several. Bhishma advises the Kaurava heroes to protect Duryodhana. A fierce battle ensues between Ghatotkacha and the Kaurava forces.

beats.title: The Rescue of Ghatotkacha
beats.summary: Ghatotkacha, the rakshasa son of Bhima, attacks Duryodhana but is surrounded by Kaurava forces. Yudhishthira sends Bhima to rescue him. A fierce battle ensues between the Pandava and Kaurava armies, with great slaughter on both sides.

beats.title: The Rout of the Kauravas by Ghatotkaca's Illusion
beats.summary: Duryodhana's army is being routed by Bhima. Duryodhana attacks Bhima, cutting his bow and wounding him. Ghatotkaca and other Pandava warriors rush to protect Bhima. Drona and other Kaurava warriors counterattack. Ghatotkaca uses his maya to create illusions, causing the Kaurava army to flee in panic. The Pandavas celebrate their victory at sunset.

beats.title: Bhagadatta's Battle and Ghatotkacha's Valor
beats.summary: Duryodhana, after being defeated by Ghatotkacha, approaches Bhishma for counsel. Bhishma advises him to focus on fighting Yudhishthira and assigns Bhagadatta to confront Ghatotkacha. A fierce battle ensues between Bhagadatta and the Pandava forces, including Bhima, Abhimanyu, and Ghatotkacha. Ghatotkacha breaks Bhagadatta's weapons, but Bhagadatta wounds many Pandava warriors. Bhima, disarmed, takes up a mace. Arjuna arrives, and Duryodhana orders a massive counterattack. Bhagadatta continues to attack Yudhishthira's forces.

beats.title: The Fourteenth Day: Bhima's Rampage and Nightfall
beats.summary: Sanjaya narrates to Dhritarashtra the events of the 14th day of the Kurukshetra war. Arjuna grieves over the death of his son Iravan, but resolves to fight. Bhima kills several of Duryodhana's sons. Abhimanyu disables King Ambastha. The battle becomes a chaotic melee with horrific carnage. As night falls, both armies withdraw to their camps.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Desperation and Karna's Proposal
beats.summary: Duryodhana, distressed by the inability of his generals to defeat the Pandavas, consults with Karna and Shakuni. Karna advises that Bhishma is not fighting wholeheartedly due to his affection for the Pandavas, and suggests that Duryodhana request Bhishma to lay down his arms so that Karna can single-handedly defeat the Pandavas. Duryodhana agrees and goes to Bhishma's camp, where he respectfully asks Bhishma to either fulfill his promise to destroy the Somakas and Pandavas or allow Karna to fight. Duryodhana then falls silent.

beats.title: Bhishma's Rebuke and Vow
beats.summary: Bhishma, wounded by Duryodhana's harsh words, rebukes him by recounting Arjuna's past feats and declares his intention to fight the Pandavas except Shikhandi, whom he refuses to harm due to her former female nature. Duryodhana then leaves.

beats.title: The Second Day of War: Bhīṣma's Reluctance and the Battle Formations
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the second day of the Kurukshetra war. Duryodhana, concerned about Bhīṣma's reluctance to fight Śikhaṇḍin, orders his forces to protect Bhīṣma. Bhīṣma forms the Sarvatobhadra formation, while the Pāṇḍavas counter with their own formation. The chapter ends with omens of disaster.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Rampage and the Duel with Alambusha
beats.summary: Abhimanyu, son of Arjuna, attacks the Kaurava army single-handedly, routing them and defeating many warriors including Kripa, Drona, and Ashvatthama. Duryodhana then orders the rakshasa Alambusha to confront Abhimanyu. Alambusha attacks the Pandava army and fights the five Draupadeya brothers, wounding them. Abhimanyu intervenes, and a fierce duel ensues between Abhimanyu and Alambusha, likened to Indra versus Vritra or Shambara.

beats.title: The Duel with Alambusa and the Clash of Armies
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle between his sons and the Pandavas. Sanjaya describes the duel between Abhimanyu and the rakshasa Alambusa, where Abhimanyu defeats him using the Bhaskara astra. Then Bhishma surrounds Abhimanyu, and Arjuna advances to rescue him. The Kaurava and Pandava forces clash, with notable duels between Satyaki and Kripacharya, and between Satyaki and Ashvatthama, until Drona intervenes and Arjuna confronts Drona.

beats.title: The Battle between Drona and Arjuna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya how Drona and Arjuna fought, given their mutual affection. Sanjaya describes the battle: Drona and Arjuna exchange arrows; Duryodhana sends Trigarta king Suśarman to attack Arjuna; Arjuna fights the Trigartas and uses the Vāyavya astra, which Drona counters with a mountain astra; Bhima attacks the elephant division, killing many elephants and causing a rout.

beats.title: The Midday Battle and the River of Blood
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the fierce battle at midday, where Bhīṣma attacks the Pāṇḍava army. The Pāṇḍava warriors, including Dhṛṣṭadyumna, Śikhaṇḍī, Virāṭa, and Drupada, counterattack but are wounded. Bhīṣma refrains from striking Śikhaṇḍī, remembering his former femininity. A chaotic melee ensues, with elephants, horses, and chariots causing destruction. The battlefield becomes a river of blood and gore. Warriors lament Duryodhana's fault for the carnage. Duryodhana urges his generals to fight without ego. Saṁjaya concludes that the destruction is due to Duryodhana's misdeeds and fate.

beats.title: The Battle Intensifies: Arjuna's Rout and Bhīṣma's Stand
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the battle where Arjuna kills many Trigarta warriors, causing a rout. Duryodhana rallies his forces with Bhīṣma. The Pāṇḍavas attack Bhīṣma. Individual duels occur: Sātyaki vs Kṛtavarman, Drupada vs Droṇa, Bhīmasena vs Bāhlika, Abhimanyu vs Citrasena, and Sātyaki vs Bhīṣma. Bhīṣma throws a spear at Sātyaki, who deflects it and throws his own, which Bhīṣma cuts. The battle becomes intense.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Orders and the Battle with Shalya
beats.summary: Duryodhana, seeing Bhishma surrounded by Pandavas, orders Duhshasana to protect him. Duhshasana surrounds Bhishma with a large cavalry force. The cavalry attacks the Pandava army but is repelled by Yudhishthira, Nakula, and Sahadeva. Duryodhana then requests Shalya to stop Yudhishthira. Shalya attacks, but Bhima intervenes, leading to a fierce battle.

beats.title: Bhishma's Rampage and Krishna's Intervention
beats.summary: Bhishma, enraged, attacks the Pandava army with a hail of arrows, causing massive destruction. He wounds many warriors including Bhima, Satyaki, Nakula, Sahadeva, Yudhishthira, and Dhrishtadyumna. The Pandava forces are thrown into chaos. Krishna urges Arjuna to fulfill his vow to kill Bhishma. Arjuna, though reluctant, agrees and engages Bhishma. Bhishma breaks Arjuna's bow twice, but Arjuna cuts his bowstring. Krishna, angered by Arjuna's hesitation, dismounts and rushes at Bhishma with a whip. Arjuna stops him, reminding him of his promise not to fight. Krishna returns to the chariot. Bhishma continues to decimate the Pandava army until sunset.

beats.title: The Night Counsel: Seeking Bhishma's Fall
beats.summary: After sunset, the battle pauses. Yudhishthira, distressed by Bhishma's onslaught, considers retreating to the forest. Krishna encourages him, offering to fight Bhishma himself. Yudhishthira refuses, citing Bhishma's past kindness and a vow not to fight. The Pandavas and Krishna then visit Bhishma's camp to seek advice on how to defeat him. Bhishma reveals that he will not fight if faced by Shikhandi, who was formerly a woman. Arjuna is reluctant to attack his grandfather, but Krishna reminds him of his kshatriya duty and the precedent set by Brihaspati. They decide to use Shikhandi as a shield, and Arjuna agrees to strike Bhishma from behind Shikhandi.

beats.title: Bhishma's Fall: The Tenth Day
beats.summary: Bhishma's fierce battle against the Pandavas on the tenth day, with Shikhandi confronting him and Arjuna supporting Shikhandi, leading to Bhishma's fall.

beats.title: Bhishma's Tenth Day Battle
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya how Shikhandi attacked Bhishma and who protected him. Sanjaya describes Bhishma's fierce fighting on the tenth day, his vow to kill ten thousand warriors daily, and Arjuna's counterattack. Duryodhana complains to Bhishma about the Pandava onslaught. Bhishma reassures him, recalls his vow, and engages the Pandava army, killing thousands. The Pandavas and Panchalas surround Bhishma, but he continues to fight like a smokeless fire.

beats.title: The Tenth Day: Arjuna's Assault on Bhīṣma
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle on day 10, where Arjuna, with Śikhaṇḍin as his vanguard, attacks Bhīṣma. The Pāṇḍava forces, encouraged by Dhṛṣṭadyumna, charge at Bhīṣma but are countered by the Kauravas. Duḥśāsana engages Arjuna in a fierce duel, but ultimately retreats to Bhīṣma's chariot for protection.

beats.title: The Tenth Day of Battle: Duels for Bhishma
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the fierce battle on day 10, focusing on duels between Kaurava and Pandava warriors, all centered around protecting or killing Bhishma. Key encounters include Sātyaki vs. Alambusa, Abhimanyu vs. Sudakṣiṇa, Sahadeva vs. Kṛpa, Nakula vs. Vikarṇa, Ghaṭotkaca vs. Durmukha, Dhṛṣṭadyumna vs. Kṛtavarman, Bhīmasena vs. Bhūriśravas, Yudhiṣṭhira vs. Droṇa, Cekitāna vs. Citrasena, and Arjuna vs. Duḥśāsana.

beats.title: Droṇa's Foreboding and Advice to Aśvatthāmā
beats.summary: Droṇa, seeing omens of destruction, warns his son Aśvatthāmā about the impending battle where Arjuna, supported by Kṛṣṇa, will attack Bhīṣma. He describes the prowess of the Pāṇḍavas and advises Aśvatthāmā to protect himself and engage carefully.

beats.title: Bhima's Single-Handed Battle Against Ten Kaurava Warriors
beats.summary: Bhima fights ten Kaurava warriors simultaneously, including Shalya, Kritavarma, Kripa, Bhagadatta, Vinda-Anuvinda, Citrasena, Vikarna, Durmarshana, and Jayadratha. He defeats them all, disables Jayadratha's chariot, and withstands their combined assault. Arjuna then joins Bhima, and Duryodhana orders Suśarman of the Trigartas to attack both Pandavas.

beats.title: The Battle Intensifies: Arjuna and Bhima vs. the Kauravas
beats.summary: Arjuna and Bhima engage in fierce combat against the Kaurava army, including Shalya, Suśarman, Kṛpa, Jayadratha, and others. Bhīṣma leads the Kaurava counterattack, and Śikhaṇḍī confronts him. The battle intensifies with heavy casualties on both sides.

beats.title: Bhishma's Fall: The Tenth Day
beats.summary: On the tenth day of the Kurukshetra war, Bhishma, weary of fighting the Pandavas, reveals his desire to die and instructs Yudhishthira to arrange for his fall with Shikhandi as the key. The Pandavas, led by Shikhandi and Arjuna, attack Bhishma while the Kauravas defend him. A fierce battle ensues.

beats.title: The Fourteenth Day: Bhishma's Rampage and Arjuna's Counter
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce battle on the 14th day of the Kurukshetra war, focusing on the duel between Abhimanyu and Duryodhana, the combat between Satyaki and Ashvatthama, the fight between Paurava and Dhrishtaketu, the clash between Citrasena and Susharma, the duel between Abhimanyu and Brihadbala, Bhima's rampage against elephants, Yudhishthira's fight with the Madra king, Virata's battle with Saindhava, the duel between Drona and Dhrishtadyumna, Arjuna's confrontation with Bhagadatta and then Bhishma, Bhishma's devastating archery, Dushasana's heroics, and Arjuna's counterattack leading to a river of blood.
