beats.title: The Tenth Day: Bhishma's Fall
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the tenth day of the Kurukshetra war, focusing on Bhishma's fierce rampage against the Pandava army. Despite heavy losses, the Pandavas, led by Arjuna and with Shikhandi as a shield, confront Bhishma. Krishna urges Arjuna to attack Bhishma, who is eventually overwhelmed by Arjuna's arrows.

beats.title: Fall of Bhishma
beats.summary: Bhishma, after a fierce ten-day battle, is finally brought down by Arjuna with Shikhandi as his shield. Despite being pierced by countless arrows, Bhishma does not die immediately but lies on a bed of arrows, awaiting the auspicious Uttarayana period to leave his body. The Kauravas are despondent, while the Pandavas rejoice.

beats.title: Bhishma's Fall and Bed of Arrows
beats.summary: Bhishma falls on the tenth day of battle, pierced by Arjuna's arrows. He lies on a bed of arrows, and both armies cease fighting to pay homage. Arjuna provides a pillow of arrows for his head. Bhishma declares he will wait until the sun turns north to give up his life, and refuses medical aid. Krishna and Yudhishthira discuss the victory.

beats.title: Bhishma's Last Counsel
beats.summary: After the battle, both armies visit the dying Bhishma on his bed of arrows. Bhishma asks Arjuna for water, and Arjuna pierces the earth with an arrow to bring forth a cool, fragrant stream. Bhishma praises Arjuna's divine weapons and advises Duryodhana to make peace with the Pandavas, offering half the kingdom. Duryodhana remains obstinate.

beats.title: Bhishma's Blessing to Karna
beats.summary: After Bhishma falls, Karna visits him on his bed of arrows. Bhishma reveals he knows Karna is Kunti's son and praises his valor, but urges him to reconcile with the Pandavas. Karna refuses, citing his loyalty to Duryodhana. Bhishma then blesses him to fight for heaven.

beats.title: After Bhishma's Fall
beats.summary: After Bhishma's fall, Dhritarashtra laments and asks Sanjaya what the Kauravas did next. Sanjaya describes the army's despair, the warriors' grief, and how they remembered Karna, who had vowed not to fight while Bhishma lived. The chapter ends with Dhritarashtra hoping Karna will fulfill their expectations.

beats.title: Karna's Vow After Bhishma's Fall
beats.summary: After Bhishma's fall, Karna arrives and laments the loss, then resolves to fight fiercely to protect the Kaurava army and defeat the Pandavas, vowing to either conquer or die.

beats.title: Karṇa's Lament over Bhīṣma
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the scene of Bhīṣma lying on a bed of arrows after being felled by Arjuna. Karṇa approaches Bhīṣma, laments his fall, and expresses fear that without Bhīṣma, the Kauravas will be destroyed by the Pāṇḍavas, comparing Arjuna and Kṛṣṇa to fire and wind.

beats.title: Bhishma's Praise and Command to Karna
beats.summary: Bhishma praises Karna for his past victories and declares him equal to Duryodhana in the Kuru family. He urges Karna to protect the Kuru army and go fight. Karna bows to Bhishma and proceeds to the battlefield, where the Kuru warriors cheer him.

beats.title: Appointment of Drona as Commander-in-Chief
beats.summary: After Bhishma's fall, Duryodhana consults Karna about the next commander-in-chief. Karna recommends Dronacharya, praising his wisdom and prowess. Duryodhana then requests Drona to lead the Kaurava army, comparing him to various deities. Drona accepts, and the kings consecrate him as senapati with great fanfare.

beats.title: The Battle Formation and Omens
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the deployment of the Kaurava army under Droṇa's command, with Karṇa leading the vanguard. The Pāṇḍava army, led by Arjuna and Kṛṣṇa, is arrayed in the Krauñca formation. Omens of disaster appear as the battle begins, with Droṇa attacking fiercely and Dhṛṣṭadyumna countering. The chapter ends with a poetic description of Droṇa's chariot and his assault on the enemy.

beats.title: Droṇa's Rampage and Fall
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle where Droṇa, despite being old, fights like a young warrior, causing havoc among the Pāṇḍava forces. Many warriors including Bhīma, Arjuna, and others try to stop him. Droṇa kills thousands, but eventually falls to Dhṛṣṭadyumna. The Pāṇḍavas rejoice, and the earth trembles.

beats.title: The Fall of Dronacharya
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the death of Dronacharya at the hands of Dhrishtadyumna, questioning how such a great warrior and teacher could be killed. He expresses disbelief and sorrow, and asks Sanjaya for details of the battle.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Inquiry
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, overwhelmed by grief and anxiety about the war, questions Sanjaya about the Kaurava army's performance against the Pandava forces, specifically focusing on how various Pandava heroes were countered by the Kauravas. He lists numerous warriors on both sides, expressing despair over the Pandavas' strength and the presence of Krishna as their ally.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Krishna's Glories
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the deeds of Krishna, recounting his divine exploits from childhood to the war, and expresses despair over the inevitable defeat of the Kauravas due to Krishna's alliance with the Pandavas.

beats.title: Droṇa's Promise to Capture Yudhiṣṭhira
beats.summary: Droṇa, appointed as commander-in-chief of the Kaurava army, promises Duryodhana to capture Yudhiṣṭhira alive, but with the condition that Arjuna be absent from the battlefield. Duryodhana agrees, and the plan is proclaimed throughout the army.

beats.title: The Battle Begins: Arjuna's Assurance
beats.summary: Duryodhana describes the Kaurava army to Dronacharya, then Bhishma blows his conch, followed by Krishna and Arjuna. In Parva 7, Chapter 12, Yudhishthira expresses fear of Drona's vow to capture him, but Arjuna reassures him that Drona will never succeed while Arjuna lives. The battle begins with fierce fighting, and Drona devastates the Pandava forces.

beats.title: The Great Battle: Drona's Rampage and Single Combats
beats.summary: Drona attacks the Pandava army like a forest fire. The Kaurava and Pandava warriors engage in duels: Shakuni vs Sahadeva, Viviṃśati vs Bhima, Shalya vs Nakula, Kṛpa vs Dhṛṣṭaketu, Kṛtavarman vs Sātyaki, Karṇa vs Virāṭa, Bhagadatta vs Drupada, Bhūriśravas vs Yājñaseni (Sahadeva?), Śikhaṇḍī vs Saumadatti, Alambusa vs Ghaṭotkaca, Anuvinda vs Cekitāna, Lakṣmaṇa vs Kṣatradeva, and Pauravas vs Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu defeats Pauravas, then fights Jayadratha. After a fierce sword duel, Jayadratha breaks Abhimanyu's sword, but Abhimanyu escapes. He then kills Shalya's charioteer with a spear. The Pandavas cheer, but the Kauravas surround Abhimanyu. Jayadratha attacks him.

beats.title: The Duel of Bhima and Shalya
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya to describe the duel between Bhima and Shalya, and the subsequent battle. Sanjaya narrates the fierce mace fight between Bhima and Shalya, which ends with Shalya being rescued by Kritavarma. The Pandavas then rout the Kaurava army.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Drona's Assault and Arjuna's Intervention
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata, Sanjaya describes the fierce battle where Vrishasena, son of Karna, initially wreaks havoc on the Pandava army. Nakula attacks him, but Vrishasena cuts his bow and banner. The Draupadeyas come to Nakula's aid, but are countered by Ashvatthama and others. Drona then leads a counterattack, wounding many Pandava heroes and advancing towards Yudhishthira. Yugandhara tries to stop Drona but is killed. Virata, Drupada, and others protect Yudhishthira. Drona kills Simhasena and Vyaghradatta. Arjuna then arrives, creating darkness with his arrows, causing Drona and Duryodhana to retreat. Arjuna withdraws his army and is praised.

beats.title: Parva 7, Chapter 16
beats.summary: Droṇa, dejected after the day's battle, devises a plan to capture Yudhiṣṭhira by luring Arjuna away. The Trigarta king Suśarman and his brothers vow to engage Arjuna in a separate combat, swearing a solemn oath. Arjuna, challenged by the Saṁśaptakas, seeks Yudhiṣṭhira's permission to fight them, which is granted. The two armies clash fiercely.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Samshaptakas
beats.summary: Arjuna confronts the Trigarta army (the Samshaptakas) who have sworn to defeat him. He blows his conch Devadatta, terrifying their forces. A fierce battle ensues where Arjuna kills many warriors including Sudhanva. The Trigarta king rallies his troops, and they return to fight, determined to die rather than face disgrace.

beats.title: Arjuna's Slaughter of the Saṁśaptakas
beats.summary: Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, confronts the Saṁśaptaka warriors who had sworn to defeat him. Using divine weapons, Arjuna creates illusions causing the Saṁśaptakas to fight and kill each other. He then unleashes the Vāyavyāstra to scatter them and slaughters many. Meanwhile, Dronācārya attacks Yudhiṣṭhira, who is defended by his forces.

beats.title: The Garuda Formation and the Battle
beats.summary: Drona arranges the Garuda formation; Yudhishthira counters with a half-moon formation. Dhrishhtadyumna attacks Drona but is intercepted by Duryodhana's son Durmukha. A fierce battle ensues with massive casualties. Drona eventually breaks through to attack Yudhishthira.

beats.title: Droṇa's Rampage
beats.summary: Droṇa, the Kaurava commander, unleashes a devastating assault on the Pāṇḍava army. He kills several prominent warriors including Satyajit, Vṛka, and Śatānīka, and forces Yudhiṣṭhira to flee. Despite counterattacks by Pāñcāla and other allies, Droṇa's rampage continues, creating a river of blood and carnage. The Pāṇḍava forces are thrown into disarray.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Boasting and Karna's Warning
beats.summary: Duryodhana, seeing the Pandava army routed by Drona, boasts to Karna that they will never fight again. Karna warns that Bhima, remembering past injustices, will not give up and that the Pandavas, led by Bhima and supported by Satyaki and others, are advancing towards Drona. Duryodhana then proceeds with his brothers to protect Drona.

beats.title: Description of the Pandava Army
beats.summary: Samjaya describes to Dhritarashtra the chariots, horses, and warriors of the Pandava army as they advance towards Drona. Each warrior is associated with specific horse colors and markings, highlighting the grandeur and diversity of the Pandava forces.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the deaths of Bhishma and Drona, reflecting on fate and the consequences of abandoning dharma. He asks Sanjaya to describe the battle, particularly the actions of Arjuna and the retreat of the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Great Battle: Duels and the Onslaught of Ghatotkacha
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the fierce battle where Duryodhana's warriors attempt to block the Pandava forces from reaching Drona. Numerous duels occur between Kaurava and Pandava heroes, culminating in Ghatotkacha's rampage and his duel with Alambusa. The chapter highlights the chaos and intensity of the multi-front combat.

beats.title: Bhagadatta's Elephant Rampage
beats.summary: Bhagadatta, king of Pragjyotisha, attacks the Pandava army with his elephant, causing havoc. Bhima and other Pandava warriors counterattack. Bhagadatta's elephant is wounded but continues to rampage. The chapter describes the fierce battle between Bhagadatta and the Pandavas, with Bhima ultimately killing Bhagadatta's son Ruciparvan.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Saṁśaptakas
beats.summary: Arjuna, seeing Bhagadatta's elephant causing havoc, asks Krishna to go confront him. The Saṁśaptakas challenge Arjuna, who initially hesitates but then decides to fight them. He kills many with his vajra astra, and after defeating them, proceeds to Bhagadatta.

beats.title: Arjuna's duel with Susharma and Bhagadatta
beats.summary: Arjuna and Krishna turn back to face the Trigarta army led by Susharma. Arjuna defeats Susharma and his brothers, then returns to the main Kaurava army. He attacks fiercely, causing the Kaurava forces to flee. Bhagadatta then charges at Arjuna on his elephant, and a fierce duel ensues. Krishna skillfully maneuvers the chariot, and Arjuna eventually kills the elephant and its rider.

beats.title: The Slaying of Bhagadatta
beats.summary: Bhagadatta, king of Prāgjyotiṣa, fights Arjuna and Kṛṣṇa. He uses the Vaiṣṇava astra, which Kṛṣṇa neutralizes by receiving it on his chest, turning it into a garland. Kṛṣṇa explains the astra's origin: he gave it to Earth for her son Naraka, making Naraka invincible. Now, to protect Arjuna, Kṛṣṇa nullified the astra's power. Arjuna then kills Bhagadatta and his elephant.

beats.title: Arjuna's Victory over the Gandhara Princes and Shakuni's Illusions
beats.summary: Sanjaya narrates to Dhritarashtra the battle events where Arjuna kills the Gandhara princes Vrishaka and Achala, then counters Shakuni's magical illusions with divine weapons, causing the Kaurava army to flee in disarray.

beats.title: The Battle After the Rout
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the state of his army after the Pandava forces broke through. Sanjaya describes the fierce battle where Drona and his son Ashvatthama fight the Panchalas, especially Dhrishtadyumna. The Kaurava warriors, though initially broken, rally around Drona. Ashvatthama kills the Pandava warrior Nila, causing panic among the Pandava ranks. The chapter ends with Ashvatthama attacking the remaining Samshaptakas and Narayana forces.

beats.title: The Great Slaughter: Arjuna's Return and the Clash with Karna
beats.summary: Bhima attacks the Kaurava army but is surrounded by Drona, Karna, and others. Yudhishthira sends reinforcements. A fierce battle ensues with chaotic fighting. Arjuna arrives after defeating the Samshaptakas and devastates the Kaurava forces. Karna counters with the Agneya astra, but Arjuna neutralizes it. Bhima, Dhrishtadyumna, and Satyaki attack Karna, cutting his bow. Duryodhana, Drona, and Jayadratha rescue Karna. The battle continues until sunset, when both sides withdraw.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Death and the Cakravyūha
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the events after Arjuna's earlier success, including the formation of a cakravyūha by Droṇa, Abhimanyu's penetration and death, and the subsequent grief of the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas.

beats.title: The Virtues of the Pāṇḍavas and the Cakravyūha
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the virtues of the Pāṇḍavas and the formation of the Kaurava army in a cakravyūha, leading to Dhr̥tarāṣṭra's inquiry about Abhimanyu's death.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Entry into the Cakravyūha
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍava army, led by Bhīmasena and others, attacks Droṇa's impenetrable formation. Yudhiṣṭhira, realizing Droṇa's invincibility, requests Abhimanyu to break the cakravyūha, as only Arjuna, Kṛṣṇa, Pradyumna, or Abhimanyu can do so. Abhimanyu agrees, despite not knowing how to exit, and enters the formation with support from Bhīma and others.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Heroic Charge
beats.summary: Abhimanyu, son of Subhadra and Arjuna, disregards the charioteer's warning about Drona's prowess and charges into the Kaurava army. He breaks through Drona's formation and single-handedly wreaks havoc, killing many warriors and causing panic among the Kauravas. The chapter describes his heroic feats and the devastation he inflicts.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Heroic Stand
beats.summary: Abhimanyu, the son of Arjuna, single-handedly attacks the Kaurava army after they break formation. He wounds many warriors, including Duhsasana, Karna, and Salya, but is eventually surrounded and overwhelmed by multiple Kaurava heroes. Despite his valiant efforts, he is killed after a prolonged battle.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Solo Attack
beats.summary: Saubhadra (Abhimanyu) single-handedly attacks the Kaurava army, killing Shalya's younger brother and demonstrating his archery skills learned from Krishna and Arjuna. He fights fiercely, causing the enemy to flee.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Solo Attack
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle. Sanjaya describes Abhimanyu's fierce attack on the Kaurava army, his prowess, and the reactions of Drona and Duryodhana. Duryodhana incites his warriors to kill Abhimanyu, and Duḥśāsana engages him in combat.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Valor and the Battle Against Duḥśāsana
beats.summary: Abhimanyu defeats Duḥśāsana in battle, then engages Karṇa and other warriors, showcasing his prowess. The Pāṇḍavas and their allies celebrate and advance against Droṇa's army.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Rampage
beats.summary: Abhimanyu, son of Arjuna, single-handedly attacks the Kaurava army, killing many warriors including a brother of Karna. He breaks through the formation, causing chaos, but is eventually surrounded. The chapter describes his valor and the devastation he wreaks.

beats.title: Jayadratha's Boon and Stand
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle. Sanjaya describes how Jayadratha, empowered by a boon from Shiva, single-handedly held back the Pandava army, causing fear among the Kauravas and then a counterattack.

beats.title: Jayadratha's Heroic Stand
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the valor of Jayadratha (Sindhu king) in battle against the Pāṇḍavas. Jayadratha single-handedly holds off the Pāṇḍava army, breaking their formation and repelling each warrior who tries to penetrate Droṇa's array. Despite losing his chariot and weapons, he continues fighting, earning praise from both sides.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Rampage
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the fierce battle where Abhimanyu (Arjuni, son of Arjuna) enters the Kaurava army, kills Vasatiya, and creates havoc among the enemy forces, cutting down many warriors and their equipment.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Rampage
beats.summary: Abhimanyu, Arjuna's son, single-handedly attacks the Kaurava army, killing many warriors including Satyaśravas and Rukmaratha, son of Śalya. He uses the Gāndharva weapon and ratha-māyā to confuse and slaughter hundreds of princes. Duryodhana, witnessing the carnage, becomes fearful and eventually engages Abhimanyu but is repelled.

beats.title: The Fall of Lakshmana and Abhimanyu's Heroism
beats.summary: Duryodhana's son Lakshmana is killed by Abhimanyu (Saubhadra). The Kaurava forces, initially routed, rally around Duryodhana and attack Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu defeats many warriors including Kratha's son, but is eventually surrounded and killed by multiple Kaurava heroes.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Heroic Entry
beats.summary: Abhimanyu enters the Kaurava formation and fights valiantly against six great warriors: Drona, Kripa, Karna, Ashvatthama, Kritavarma, and Brihadbala. He wounds them all and kills Brihadbala, the king of Kosala, before continuing to harass the Kaurava army.

beats.title: Abhimanyu's Heroic Stand
beats.summary: Abhimanyu, son of Arjuna, single-handedly fights many Kaurava warriors including Karna, Duryodhana, and others. He is eventually disarmed and his chariot destroyed, but continues fighting with sword and shield. Drona cuts his sword, Karna shatters his shield, and he is surrounded and killed by six maharathas.

beats.title: The Fall of Abhimanyu
beats.summary: Samjaya narrates to Dhritarashtra the death of Abhimanyu at the hands of multiple Kaurava warriors, including Dronacharya, Karna, and others. Abhimanyu, after slaying many enemies, is surrounded and killed. The Pandava army is demoralized, but Yudhishthira encourages them. The chapter ends with a vivid description of the battlefield at dusk, filled with corpses and scavengers.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament for Abhimanyu
beats.summary: After Abhimanyu's death, Yudhishthira laments deeply, blaming himself for sending the young hero into the enemy formation. He fears Arjuna's wrath and the destruction of the Kauravas.

beats.title: The Death of Abhimanyu
beats.summary: After a day of battle, Arjuna returns to camp with Krishna, troubled by omens and the subdued atmosphere. He learns that Abhimanyu has been killed in the Chakravyuha. Arjuna laments deeply, blaming his brothers for failing to protect his son. Krishna consoles him, explaining the dharma of warriors. Arjuna then asks for details of Abhimanyu's death.

beats.title: Arjuna's Vow to Kill Jayadratha
beats.summary: Yudhishthira recounts to Arjuna the tragic death of Abhimanyu in battle, describing how the young hero was surrounded and killed by six great warriors including Dronacharya, Kripacharya, Karna, Ashvatthama, Brihadbala, and Kritavarma, after Jayadratha blocked the Pandava forces. Arjuna, overwhelmed with grief and rage, vows to kill Jayadratha by the next day or enter fire, listing terrible fates he would accept if he fails.

beats.title: Jayadratha's Fear and Drona's Assurance
beats.summary: Jayadratha, terrified of Arjuna's vow to kill him, seeks protection from the Kaurava elders. Duryodhana reassures him, and together they approach Drona. Drona explains that while he taught both equally, Arjuna excels due to his dedication and practice. He promises to protect Jayadratha and urges him to fight without fear, emphasizing the impermanence of life and the glory of dying in battle.

beats.title: Arjuna's Vow to Kill Jayadratha
beats.summary: Arjuna vows to kill Jayadratha before sunset the next day, despite the protection of six great warriors (Karna, Bhurishravas, Ashvatthama, Vrishasena, Kripa, and the Madra king) arranged by Drona in a complex formation. Krishna warns of the difficulty, but Arjuna reaffirms his resolve, invoking his divine weapons and the support of Krishna as his charioteer.

beats.title: Krishna Consoles Subhadra
beats.summary: After the day's battle, Krishna and Arjuna are sleepless with grief. The gods are alarmed by their anger. Omens appear: harsh winds, a headless sun, lightning, earthquakes, seas churning, rivers flowing backward, and animals crying. Duryodhana's army is terrified by Arjuna's vow. Krishna consoles Subhadra and her daughter-in-law, promising that Arjuna will kill Jayadratha the next day.

beats.title: Subhadra's Lament for Abhimanyu
beats.summary: Subhadra laments the death of her son Abhimanyu in battle. Krishna consoles her and Draupadi, then departs.

beats.title: Krishna's Vow to Ensure Arjuna's Success
beats.summary: Krishna visits Arjuna's tent, performs evening rituals, and retires. The Pandava camp stays awake worrying about Arjuna's oath to kill Jayadratha by sunset the next day. Krishna wakes up, instructs his charioteer Daruka to prepare his chariot, and vows to ensure Arjuna's success, even if he must destroy the Kaurava army himself.

beats.title: Arjuna Obtains the Pashupata Weapon
beats.summary: Arjuna, distressed by his vow to kill Jayadratha by sunset, is consoled by Krishna. Krishna advises him to seek the Pashupata weapon from Shiva. They travel to Mount Mandara, where they meet Shiva, who grants them the weapon after they retrieve it from a lake guarded by serpents. Arjuna successfully obtains the weapon, and Shiva blesses him to fulfill his vow.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Morning Rituals and Krishna's Visit
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the morning of the battle: Yudhishthira wakes to music and praise, performs his daily rituals including bathing, japa, fire offerings, and charity, then receives Krishna who arrives at his tent.

beats.title: Krishna's Assurance to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Krishna about his well-being and expresses his dependence on Krishna for victory and happiness. Krishna reassures him, praising Arjuna's prowess and promising that Arjuna will kill Jayadratha (the slayer of Subhadra's son) and return victorious.

beats.title: Arjuna's Preparation and Instructions
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes Arjuna's visit to Yudhiṣṭhira before the battle, where he recounts his meeting with Śiva and receives blessings. After taking leave, Arjuna, Kṛṣṇa, and Sātyaki prepare for battle. Arjuna instructs Sātyaki to protect Yudhiṣṭhira while he goes to kill Jayadratha, expressing confidence in victory due to auspicious omens.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament After Abhimanyu's Death
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments to Sanjaya about the silence in his sons' camps after Abhimanyu's death, recalling the joyful sounds that once filled them. He blames Duryodhana for rejecting Krishna's peace offer and following evil counsel, leading to the war. He describes the mighty Pandava army and expresses despair, asking Sanjaya to recount what happened after Abhimanyu's fall.

beats.title: Samjaya's Rebuke to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Samjaya rebukes Dhritarashtra for his past failures to prevent the war and his current lamentation, emphasizing the inevitability of fate and the valor of the Pandavas and their allies.

beats.title: The Śakaṭa Formation
beats.summary: Droṇa arranges the Kaurava army in a śakaṭa (cart) formation with a padma (lotus) sub-formation and a sūcī (needle) formation at its center. Jayadratha is placed at the sūcī's mouth to protect it. The chapter describes the warriors and their positions, culminating in the king's joy upon seeing the formation.

beats.title: Arjuna's Onslaught
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the battle formation, omens, and Arjuna's fierce attack on Duryodhana's forces. Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, decimates the Kaurava army, causing panic and slaughter.

beats.title: Arjuna's Rout of Duḥśāsana's Elephant Force
beats.summary: Duryodhana's army is routed by Arjuna. Duḥśāsana leads an elephant charge but is repelled. Arjuna's archery devastates the Kaurava forces, causing panic. Duḥśāsana retreats to Droṇa's protection in the śakaṭavyūha formation.

beats.title: Arjuna's Advance and Drona's Duel
beats.summary: Arjuna seeks to fulfill his vow to kill Jayadratha before sunset. He approaches Drona with respect, asking for passage through the Kaurava formation. Drona tests him, and a fierce duel ensues. Krishna advises Arjuna to bypass Drona to focus on the greater goal. Arjuna circumambulates Drona and proceeds, but is blocked by many warriors. A tumultuous battle erupts.

beats.title: Arjuna's Victory over Shrutayudha and Sudakshina
beats.summary: Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, engages in fierce battle against Dronacharya, Krutavarma, Shrutayudha, and Sudakshina. Shrutayudha is killed by his own divine mace because he attacked Krishna who was not fighting. Sudakshina is slain by Arjuna. The Kaurava army flees in panic.

beats.title: Arjuna's Slaughter of the Kaurava Forces
beats.summary: Arjuna, after killing Sudakshina and Shrutayudha, faces a counterattack from the Kaurava army. He defeats Shrutayu and Acyutayu, then their sons Ayutayu and Dirghayu. He then slaughters many Mleccha warriors and elephants, creating a river of blood. Finally, he kills the Ambastha king who had struck Krishna with a mace.

beats.title: Drona's Armor and the Divine Mantra
beats.summary: Duryodhana, distressed by Arjuna's rampage, approaches Drona for counsel. Drona reassures him and bestows an invincible armor, recounting its divine origin from Shiva to Indra and its transmission through the lineage of sages. Duryodhana then advances against Arjuna with a vast army.

beats.title: The Fourteenth Day Battle: Drona's Rampage
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas on the fourteenth day. Duryodhana leads the Kaurava forces, while Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna leads the Pāṇḍava army. The battle is chaotic, with both sides attacking fiercely. Drona and Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna duel, and the army splits into three parts. Various warriors engage in single combat, including Bhīmasena, Sātyaki, and others. The Kaurava army is routed, but Drona fights like a fire of destruction.

beats.title: The First Day's Battle: Duels and Chaos
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the fierce battle on the first day, highlighting duels between key warriors: Vindā and Anuvinda attack Virāṭa; Yājñaseni (Draupadī's son) fights Bāhlīka; Śaibya battles Kāśyaputra; Bāhlīkarāja fights Draupadeyas; Duḥśāsana wounds Sātyaki, who recovers and strikes back; Alambusa fights Kuntibhoja; Śakuni is attacked by Mādrīputras (Nakula and Sahadeva); Ghaṭotkaca fights Alāyudha; Yudhiṣṭhira battles Madrarāja; Viviṁśati, Citrasena, and Vikarṇa attack Bhīmasena. The chapter emphasizes the chaos and heroism of the war.

beats.title: The Duel of Drona and Dhrishtadyumna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the intense battle between the Kauravas and Pandavas, focusing on the duel between Drona and Dhrishtadyumna. Dhrishtadyumna attacks Drona, but Drona counters fiercely. Satyaki intervenes to rescue Dhrishtadyumna, and both sides fight with great ferocity.

beats.title: The Duel of Drona and Satyaki
beats.summary: Duryodhana and Dhritarashtra inquire about the battle. Duryodhana points out the Pandava army and its commanders to Drona. Bhishma blows his conch, followed by others. Krishna and Arjuna blow their divine conches. The chapter then describes the duel between Yuyudhana (Satyaki) and Drona, where they display extraordinary archery, culminating in the use of divine weapons (Agneya and Varuna). The Pandava brothers and other allies rush to protect Satyaki, while the Kauravas surround Drona. A chaotic battle ensues.

beats.title: Arjuna's Advance and the Slaying of the Avanti Brothers
beats.summary: Arjuna and Krishna advance towards Jayadratha, facing the Avanti brothers Vinda and Anuvinda. After slaying them, Arjuna's horses become exhausted. He dismounts and single-handedly holds off the Kaurava army while Krishna tends to the horses. Arjuna creates a lake for the horses to drink and a shelter of arrows.

beats.title: Krishna Tends the Horses and Arjuna Advances
beats.summary: After Arjuna creates a protective shelter of arrows, Krishna dismounts to free the horses pierced by arrows. He then tends to the horses, removing arrows, wiping them, and giving them water. Re-harnessing them, they resume the chariot. The Kaurava army is astonished and demoralized. Some warriors urge haste to kill Krishna and Arjuna. Others blame Duryodhana for the destruction. Arjuna advances towards Jayadratha, scattering the army like a lion among deer. Krishna blows his conch, and the sight of the chariot with its monkey banner terrifies the warriors. Duryodhana confronts Arjuna.

beats.title: The Escape of Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna from Droṇa's Formation
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes to Dhṛtarāṣṭra how Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna break through the formidable army formations of Droṇa and the Bhojas, emerging unscathed. They are compared to the sun and moon freed from eclipse, or lions escaping a trap. Their escape causes despair among the Kauravas, who realize Jayadratha's death is imminent. Duryodhana, protected by Droṇa, confronts them, and Kṛṣṇa speaks to Arjuna at a crucial moment.

beats.title: Krishna's Exhortation and Duryodhana's Challenge
beats.summary: Krishna urges Arjuna to kill Duryodhana, recounting his past misdeeds. Arjuna agrees, and they advance. Duryodhana bravely faces them, causing a stir in the army. He challenges Arjuna to display his skills.

beats.title: The Ineffectiveness of Arjuna's Arrows and the Divine Armor
beats.summary: Arjuna's arrows prove ineffective against Duryodhana's divine armor given by Drona. Krishna questions Arjuna's strength. Arjuna explains the armor's origin and resolves to defeat Duryodhana. He uses a special astra, but Aśvatthāmā cuts it. Duryodhana attacks, Arjuna destroys his chariot and horses. The Kaurava army surrounds Arjuna and Krishna. Krishna encourages Arjuna, who blows his bowstring and conch, terrifying the enemy. The earth resounds.

beats.title: The Kaurava Counterattack and Arjuna's Response
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the Kaurava army's response to the sound of the Pāṇḍava conches. Eight great warriors (Bhūriśravas, Śala, Karṇa, Vṛṣasena, Jayadratha, Kṛpa, Madrarāja, and Drauṇi) surround Arjuna's chariot to protect Jayadratha. A fierce exchange of arrows ensues, with Arjuna countering each attack. The chapter highlights the chaos and valor on the battlefield.

beats.title: Description of Banners
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the various banners of the warriors on both sides in the Kurukṣetra war, focusing on the Kaurava army. He details the divine and fearsome banners of Arjuna, Droṇa's son Aśvatthāmā, Karṇa, Krpa, Vṛṣasena, Śalya, Jayadratha, Somadatta's son, Śala, and Duryodhana. The chapter ends with Arjuna stringing his bow Gāṇḍīva and engaging the enemy, causing a great tumult.

beats.title: Drona's Duel with Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Duryodhana describes the Kaurava army to Drona, then the battle begins with Drona leading the Kaurava forces against the Pandavas. Drona engages Yudhishthira in a fierce duel, disables his chariot, and forces him to flee to Sahadeva's chariot.

beats.title: The Slaughter of Trigarta and Magadha Warriors
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce battle between the Kauravas and Pandavas, focusing on the exploits of several warriors. Br̥hatkṣatra kills Kṣemadhūrti, Vīradhanvā is slain by Dhr̥ṣṭaketu, Sahadeva defeats Durmukha and kills Niramitra, Nakula overpowers Vikarṇa, Sātyaki kills Vyāghradatta and routs the Magadhas, and finally Droṇa charges at Sātyaki in anger.

beats.title: The Fall of Saumadatti and the Defeat of Alambusa
beats.summary: The chapter describes a fierce battle where the five sons of Draupadi (the Upapandavas) fight against Saumadatti (the son of Somadatta). After a prolonged exchange, Sahadeva beheads Saumadatti. Then the rakshasa Alambusa engages Bhima in combat, initially overpowering him. Bhima recovers and uses the Tvastra weapon to defeat Alambusa, who flees to Drona's army. The Pandavas celebrate Bhima's victory.

beats.title: Ghatotkaca Slays Alambusa
beats.summary: Ghatotkaca, the rakshasa son of Bhima, fights and kills the rakshasa Alambusa in a fierce magical battle, aided by the Pandava brothers. The Kauravas mourn Alambusa's death.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Plea to Satyaki
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, distressed by Arjuna's peril, implores Satyaki to rescue him from Drona's attack. He praises Satyaki's valor and loyalty, recalling Arjuna's words about him. Yudhishthira urges Satyaki to break through the Kaurava army and aid Arjuna, emphasizing the urgency as Drona overwhelms the Pandava forces.

beats.title: Satyaki's Assurance and Yudhiṣṭhira's Protection
beats.summary: Satyaki (Sātyaki) assures Yudhiṣṭhira that he will go to kill Jayadratha, but first entrusts the protection of Yudhiṣṭhira to Kṛṣṇa, fearing Droṇa's attack. Yudhiṣṭhira agrees and lists his other protectors, including Bhīma, Dhṛṣṭadyumna, and others, expressing confidence that Dhṛṣṭadyumna can counter Droṇa.

beats.title: Satyaki's Vow to Break Through the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: Satyaki (Sātyaki) vows to protect Arjuna and break through the Kaurava army to kill Jayadratha. He describes the enemy forces, prepares his chariot, and sets off after receiving Yudhiṣṭhira's blessings.

beats.title: Sātyaki's Advance Through the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the fierce battle where Yuyudhāna (Sātyaki) breaks through the Kaurava army, fights Droṇa and Kṛtavarmā, and advances toward Arjuna's position, causing great carnage.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament on the Battlefield
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle after Arjuna and Satyaki penetrate the Kaurava army. He describes his well-equipped forces and laments their inability to stop the two warriors, questioning the fate of his sons and the Kaurava army.

beats.title: Kritavarma's Stand
beats.summary: Samjaya describes to Dhritarashtra the battle between the Pandavas and the Kauravas, focusing on the duel between Kritavarma and the Pandava forces, including Bhima, Shikhandi, and others. Kritavarma single-handedly holds off the Pandava army, wounding many, until Shikhandi is injured and carried away. The Pandavas are routed.

beats.title: Sātyaki's Victory over Jalasandha and the Onset of Droṇa's Attack
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the battle scene where Sātyaki (Yuyudhāna) defeats Kṛtavarmā, then attacks the Magadha elephant division and kills its commander Jalasandha. Droṇa then advances to confront Sātyaki, leading to a fierce battle.

beats.title: Satyaki's Duel with Duryodhana and Krtavarma
beats.summary: Satyaki (Yuyudhana) fights valiantly against Duryodhana and other Kaurava warriors, then engages Krtavarma in a fierce duel, ultimately wounding him and breaking through the enemy formation.

beats.title: The Duel of Drona and Sātyaki
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between Drona and Sātyaki (Yuyudhāna). Both warriors display extraordinary archery, exchanging volleys of arrows. Sātyaki wounds Drona and cuts his bow, but Drona retaliates, killing Sātyaki's charioteer. Sātyaki fights on, even holding the reins himself, and eventually wounds Drona again. Drona's horses run wild, causing confusion. The Kaurava army is routed, but Drona regroups at the vyūha entrance, burning like a fire.

beats.title: Sātyaki Slays Sudarśana
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between Sātyaki (Śinipravīra) and Sudarśana, a Kaurava prince. Sātyaki kills Sudarśana after a prolonged exchange of arrows, then proceeds to join Arjuna.

beats.title: Sātyaki's Slaughter of the Mleccha Hosts
beats.summary: Saṁjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the fierce battle where Sātyaki, after killing the charioteer Sudarśana, encourages his own charioteer to drive him towards Arjuna. Sātyaki boasts of his prowess, vowing to destroy the Kāmbojas and other Mleccha warriors. The charioteer expresses unwavering confidence in Sātyaki. Sātyaki then attacks and annihilates the Kāmboja, Yavana, Śaka, and other Mleccha forces, creating a gruesome scene. After routing them, he proceeds towards Arjuna, praised by both celestial bards and his own troops.

beats.title: Sātyaki's Single-Handed Rout of the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes Yuyudhāna (Sātyaki) breaking through the Kaurava army, engaging multiple warriors including Duryodhana, Duḥśāsana, Śakuni, and others, and ultimately routing the Kaurava forces before proceeding to Arjuna's chariot.

beats.title: Satyaki's Single-Handed Battle
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about his sons' actions when Satyaki (Shaineya) attacked them. Sanjaya describes the fierce battle where Satyaki single-handedly fought and killed many warriors, including mountain-dwelling stone-fighters, causing panic in the Kaurava army. Drona hears the tumult and orders his charioteer to head towards Satyaki.

beats.title: Drona's Rebuke and Battle with the Panchalas
beats.summary: Dronacharya rebukes Duhshasana for fleeing from Satyaki, then engages in battle with the Panchalas. He kills Viraketu and other Panchala princes, but is challenged by Dhrishtadyumna. After a fierce duel, Drona cuts down Dhrishtadyumna's charioteer, causing his horses to bolt, and then routs the Panchala and Pandava forces. Drona re-forms his battle array and remains undefeated.

beats.title: Sātyaki's Battle with Duḥśāsana
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the fierce battle between Sātyaki (Yuyudhāna) and Duḥśāsana. Duḥśāsana attacks Sātyaki with arrows, but Sātyaki retaliates, killing many Trigarta warriors. Duḥśāsana's son also fights Sātyaki, who defeats Duḥśāsana but spares his life, remembering Bhīmasena's vow to kill all of Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons. Sātyaki then proceeds to join Arjuna.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Single-Handed Assault
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya how Satyaki could break through the Kaurava army alone. Sanjaya describes the massive battle formation, the fierce fighting, and Duryodhana's single-handed assault on the Pandava forces. Duryodhana wounds many Pandava heroes but is eventually challenged by Yudhishthira, and the battle intensifies with Drona protecting Duryodhana.

beats.title: Drona's Afternoon Onslaught
beats.summary: Drona, the Kaurava commander, unleashes a devastating assault on the Pandava forces in the afternoon battle. He kills several prominent warriors including Brihatkshatra, Dhrishtaketu, and Kshatradharma, causing panic among the Pandava ranks. Despite fierce resistance, Drona's prowess proves overwhelming, leading to a crisis for the Pandavas.

beats.title: Bhima's Quest for Arjuna
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, distressed by the absence of Arjuna and Krishna, sends Bhima to find them. Bhima fights through the Kaurava army, defeats many warriors including several of Dhritarashtra's sons, and confronts Drona before proceeding further.

beats.title: Bhīma's Rampage and Yudhiṣṭhira's Hopes
beats.summary: Bhima, having descended from his chariot, attacks Drona's chariot and lifts it by the pole, but Drona escapes to another chariot and moves to the vyūha entrance. Bhima then charges through the Kaurava army, defeating many warriors, and finally reaches Arjuna. Yudhiṣṭhira hears the roars of Bhima and Arjuna and rejoices, reflecting on Arjuna's past victories and hoping for the death of Jayadratha.

beats.title: Bhima's Duel with Karna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya who stood before the enraged Bhima in battle. Sanjaya describes the fierce duel between Bhima and Karna, where Bhima cuts Karna's bow, kills his horses and charioteer, forcing Karna to flee to Vrishasena's chariot. Bhima roars in victory, and the Pandava army rejoices.

beats.title: Duryodhana Confronts Drona; Battle with Yudhamanyu and Uttamaujas
beats.summary: Duryodhana, enraged that Arjuna, Bhima, and Satyaki have crossed Drona's defense and reached Sindhu king Jayadratha, confronts Drona. Drona explains that the battle is like a dice game where the stakes are Jayadratha's life, and urges Duryodhana to protect him. Duryodhana leaves to guard Jayadratha, while Drona stays to block the Panchalas. Meanwhile, Yudhamanyu and Uttamaujas, Arjuna's chariot protectors, fight Duryodhana; Duryodhana destroys their chariot, but they escape and join Arjuna.

beats.title: The Duel Between Karna and Bhima
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the duel between Karna and Bhima near Arjuna's chariot. Sanjaya describes the fierce battle, with both warriors exchanging volleys of arrows. Bhima, remembering past enmity, fights with rage, while Karna initially fights mildly but then retaliates. Bhima cuts Karna's bow and kills his horses and charioteer, forcing Karna to retreat to another chariot.

beats.title: The Duel Between Bhima and Karna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle after seeing Bhima's fury. Sanjaya describes the fierce duel between Bhima and Karna, where Bhima, remembering past grievances, attacks Karna with great rage. The battle is intense, with both warriors exchanging volleys of arrows, causing chaos and casualties. The Kaurava army is terrified, and even Krishna and Arjuna are concerned about Bhima's burden.

beats.title: The Duel of Karna and Bhima
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the duel between Karna and Bhima. Sanjaya describes their fierce battle, including Karna's broken bow, Bhima's cutting of Karna's chariot, and the intervention of Duryodhana's son Durjaya, who is killed by Bhima. The chapter highlights the intensity of the conflict and Dhritarashtra's despair.

beats.title: Bhima and Karna Duel
beats.summary: Bhima and Karna engage in a fierce duel. Bhima disables Karna's chariot, but Karna continues fighting on foot. Duryodhana sends Durmukha to provide Karna a new chariot. Bhima kills Durmukha. Karna, grieving, then wounds Bhima with arrows. Bhima retaliates, wounding Karna and his charioteer. Karna flees the battlefield, and Bhima stands victorious.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and the Battle with Karna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments Karna's defeat by Bhima, recalling Duryodhana's boasts. Samjaya describes the battle where Bhima kills five Kaurava princes and engages Karna.

beats.title: The Duel of Bhima and Karna
beats.summary: Bhima and Karna engage in a fierce duel. Bhima kills several of Duryodhana's sons who come to Karna's aid. The battle continues with both warriors displaying great skill and ferocity.

beats.title: Bhima Slays Seven of Duryodhana's Brothers
beats.summary: Bhima kills seven of Duryodhana's brothers in a fierce battle with Karna, prompting Duryodhana to recall Vidura's warnings and the insults to Draupadi.

beats.title: The Duel of Karna and Bhima
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle, and Sanjaya describes the fierce duel between Karna and Bhima, where both warriors unleash torrents of arrows, causing massive destruction and a river of blood. The Kaurava army is routed, and the battlefield becomes strewn with corpses and weapons.

beats.title: The Duel of Bhima and Karna
beats.summary: Bhima and Karna engage in a fierce duel. Bhima cuts Karna's bow and earring, but Karna retaliates, destroying Bhima's chariot and horses. Karna taunts Bhima, calling him a glutton unfit for war. Arjuna then intervenes, driving Karna away with his arrows. Ashvatthama protects Karna by cutting Arjuna's arrow, but is forced to retreat. Arjuna continues to decimate the Kaurava army.

beats.title: Satyaki's Heroic Deed
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle, expressing grief over the death of his warriors and the prowess of Arjuna, Krishna, Bhima, and Satyaki. Sanjaya describes Satyaki's fierce combat with Alambusa, whom he kills, and then Satyaki's advance to support Arjuna, facing a counterattack led by Duhshasana.

beats.title: Sātyaki's Arrival and Arjuna's Concern
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra how Sātyaki, after defeating many warriors including the Trigartas, Śūrasenas, Kaliṅgas, and even Duryodhana and his brothers, finally reaches Arjuna's chariot. Kṛṣṇa praises Sātyaki's valor, but Arjuna expresses concern that Sātyaki left King Yudhiṣṭhira unprotected, especially with Droṇa still alive and Bhūriśravā advancing. Arjuna worries about the safety of both Sātyaki and Yudhiṣṭhira.

beats.title: The Duel of Bhurishravas and Satyaki
beats.summary: Bhurishravas and Satyaki engage in a fierce duel. After a prolonged fight, Bhurishravas overpowers Satyaki and drags him by the hair. Krishna urges Arjuna to intervene, and Arjuna cuts off Bhurishravas' arm, saving Satyaki.

beats.title: The Death of Bhurishravas
beats.summary: Bhurishravas, having lost his arm to Arjuna while fighting Satyaki, criticizes Arjuna for attacking him when he was engaged with another. He then sits down in prayopavesha (fast unto death). Satyaki, enraged, beheads him despite objections from many warriors. Arjuna defends Satyaki's action, citing the killing of Abhimanyu by multiple warriors as a precedent. The chapter ends with Bhurishravas attaining heaven.

beats.title: The Origin of the Vr̥ṣṇi-Kaurava Enmity
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates the origin of the enmity between Śini and Somadatta, leading to Bhūriśravas's boon to kill Śini's son. He extols the Vr̥ṣṇis' invincibility and virtues, concluding that Duryodhana's hostility towards them is a great mistake.

beats.title: The Battle for Jayadratha
beats.summary: After Bhurishravas's death, Arjuna urges Krishna to drive towards Jayadratha before sunset to fulfill his vow. Duryodhana and Karna strategize to protect Jayadratha until sunset. Karna assures Duryodhana he will fight Arjuna. Arjuna attacks the Kaurava army, cutting down many warriors. He is surrounded by Duryodhana, Karna, Vrishasena, the Madra king, Ashvatthama, Kripa, and Jayadratha. A fierce battle ensues. Arjuna wounds Karna and cuts his bow, but Ashvatthama saves Karna. Arjuna kills Karna's horses and charioteer, but Ashvatthama rescues Karna. The Kauravas continue to protect Jayadratha as the sun declines.

beats.title: The Slaying of Jayadratha
beats.summary: Arjuna, guided by Krishna, kills Jayadratha in battle, fulfilling his vow before sunset. Krishna recounts the boon and curse of Vriddhakshatra, Jayadratha's father, who had declared that whoever causes Jayadratha's head to fall to the ground will have his own head shattered. Arjuna uses a divine arrow to decapitate Jayadratha and carries the head to the meditating Vriddhakshatra, dropping it in his lap. When Vriddhakshatra stands, the head falls to the ground, causing his own head to shatter. The Pandavas celebrate, and the battle continues against Drona.

beats.title: The Battle After Jayadratha's Death
beats.summary: After Arjuna kills Jayadratha, Karna and others attack him. Karna fights Satyaki, who unseats him from his chariot. Karna retreats to Duryodhana's chariot. Satyaki spares Karna's life due to Bhima's vow. Karna gets a new chariot and continues fighting.

beats.title: Arjuna's Vow and the Slaying of Vrishasena
beats.summary: In this chapter, Bhima complains to Arjuna about Karna's insults, prompting Arjuna to vow to kill Karna's son Vrishasena. Arjuna fulfills his vow, and Krishna praises him. Krishna then shows Arjuna the battlefield strewn with fallen warriors, and blows his conch.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Joy and Praise of Krishna
beats.summary: After the death of Jayadratha, Yudhishthira embraces Krishna and Arjuna, praising Krishna's grace as the source of victory. He extols Krishna as the supreme lord who grants success, citing examples of Indra and the gods. Krishna and Arjuna respond that Yudhishthira's wrath has destroyed Jayadratha and the Kaurava army. Bhima and Satyaki then approach Yudhishthira, who rejoices at their survival and victory over Drona and others. The Pandava army rejoices and prepares for further battle.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Lament
beats.summary: Duryodhana laments to Drona after Jayadratha's death, blaming himself for the loss of many warriors and expressing despair. He resolves to either kill the Pandavas or die in battle.

beats.title: Drona's Lament and Resolve
beats.summary: Duryodhana reproaches Drona for failing to protect Jayadratha and Bhurishravas. Drona responds with a lament, blaming Duryodhana's past misdeeds—the dice game, the humiliation of Draupadi, and the exile of the Pandavas—for the current catastrophe. He declares his resolve to fight the Panchalas and advises Duryodhana to uphold dharma.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Lament and Karna's Counsel on Fate
beats.summary: Duryodhana, incited by Drona, resolves to fight and laments to Karna about the death of Jayadratha, blaming Drona for allowing Arjuna to break the formation. Karna defends Drona, attributing events to fate, and urges Duryodhana to fight with effort while accepting divine will. The battle resumes.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Rampage and Yudhiṣṭhira's Counterattack
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas. Duryodhana, grieving the death of Jayadratha, charges into the Pāṇḍava army, causing great destruction. He wounds many Pāṇḍava heroes. Yudhiṣṭhira cuts Duryodhana's bow and wounds him, leading to a roar from the Pāñcālas. Droṇa then arrives to support Duryodhana, and a great battle ensues.

beats.title: The Night Attack on Drona
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya how the Pandavas surrounded Drona during the night battle after the fall of Jayadratha. Sanjaya describes the fierce night battle, with both sides attacking Drona, the terrifying omens, and the chaos of the nocturnal combat.

beats.title: The Night Battle: Bhima's Rampage
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the night battle after the fall of Jayadratha and Bhurishravas. Sanjaya describes the fierce nocturnal combat: Drona kills many Pandava allies; Bhima slays the Kalinga prince, Dhruva, Jayarata, and crushes Dushkarna and Durmada; Karna's spear is intercepted; the Kaurava army panics; Bhima honors Yudhishthira; the Pandavas rejoice; the Kauravas surround Bhima again.

beats.title: The Night Battle: Aśvatthāmā vs Ghaṭotkaca
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce night battle between Aśvatthāmā and Ghaṭotkaca, including the death of Ghaṭotkaca's son Añjanaparvan at Aśvatthāmā's hands, the duel between Aśvatthāmā and Ghaṭotkaca, and the eventual slaying of Ghaṭotkaca by Aśvatthāmā. It also covers Duryodhana's orders to Śakuni and others to attack the Pāṇḍavas, and the widespread destruction caused by Aśvatthāmā's archery.

beats.title: The Slaughter of Bāhlīka and the Divine Weapon Duel
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the fierce battle where Bhīma and Sātyaki fight Somadatta and Bāhlīka, resulting in Bāhlīka's death. Yudhiṣṭhira slaughters many Kaurava allies, prompting Droṇa to engage him with divine weapons. Yudhiṣṭhira counters all, but Droṇa unleashes the Brahmāstra, which Yudhiṣṭhira neutralizes. Droṇa then decimates the Pāñcālas until Arjuna and Bhīma rally the troops.

beats.title: Karna's Boast and Kripa's Rebuke
beats.summary: Karna boasts to Duryodhana that he will defeat the Pandavas, but Kripa criticizes him for his past failures and empty boasts. Karna defends himself, citing divine fate and his divine spear from Indra. The chapter highlights the tension between Karna's confidence and Kripa's skepticism.

beats.title: Karna's Duel with Arjuna and Duryodhana's Intervention
beats.summary: Karna fights the Pandava army, then duels Arjuna. Duryodhana intervenes and is restrained by Aśvatthāman and Kṛpa, who urge caution. Duryodhana accuses Aśvatthāman of negligence and demands he destroy the Pāñcālas.

beats.title: Aśvatthāmā's Vow and Battle with Dhṛṣṭadyumna
beats.summary: Aśvatthāmā (Drauṇi) responds to Duryodhana's concerns, vowing to fight for him. He engages in a fierce battle with Dhṛṣṭadyumna, the Pāñcāla prince. After a prolonged duel, Aśvatthāmā gains the upper hand, killing many Pāñcāla warriors and routing their army, causing panic among the Pāṇḍava forces.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Drona's Stand
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata, the battle continues with intense fighting. Yudhishthira and Bhima attack Ashvatthama, while Duryodhana and Drona counter. Arjuna and Bhima slaughter many enemy warriors, causing chaos. Drona uses the Vayavya weapon to scatter the Panchalas, but Arjuna and Bhima regroup and attack Drona. The Kaurava army, despite Drona's efforts, begins to flee in fear.

beats.title: Satyaki kills Somadatta; Yudhishthira confronts Drona
beats.summary: Satyaki kills Somadatta in battle. Yudhishthira attacks Drona's army, but Drona fights back and uses the Vayavya astra, which Arjuna neutralizes. Krishna advises Yudhishthira to avoid Drona and join Bhima.

beats.title: The Night Battle with Torches
beats.summary: During the night battle of the Mahabharata, a dust storm and darkness cause confusion. Duryodhana orders torches to be lit, illuminating the battlefield. Both armies fight fiercely under torchlight, with divine beings witnessing the spectacle.

beats.title: The Night Battle: Duryodhana's Strategy to Protect Droṇa
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the night battle on the 14th day of the Kurukshetra war. Duryodhana instructs his brothers and allies to protect Droṇa from the Pāñcālas, especially Dhṛṣṭadyumna. The battle is fierce and chaotic, with Arjuna attacking the Kauravas and Droṇa decimating the Pāñcālas. Duryodhana expresses confidence in victory if Droṇa is protected.

beats.title: Night Battle: Yudhishthira's Assault on Drona
beats.summary: In the night battle of the Kurukshetra war, Yudhishthira orders the Pandava forces to attack Drona. Various warriors engage in duels: Kritavarma fights Yudhishthira and defeats him, forcing him to flee. Kritavarma then returns to protect Drona's chariot wheel.

beats.title: The Night Battle: Satyaki vs Bhurishravas, Ghatotkacha vs Ashvatthama, Bhima vs Duryodhana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce battle between Satyaki and Bhurishravas, followed by the duel between Ghatotkacha and Ashvatthama, and the clash between Bhima and Duryodhana. Bhima defeats Duryodhana by killing his horses and charioteer with a mace, causing Duryodhana to flee. Yudhishthira then leads the Pandava forces against Drona.

beats.title: Sahadeva's Defeat and Arjuna's Victory over Alambusa
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 7, Chapter 142), Sahadeva attacks Karna but is defeated and spared due to Karna's promise to Kunti. Virata fights Shalya and is wounded, leading to a rout of the Pandava army. Arjuna and Krishna then confront the rakshasa Alambusa, whom Arjuna defeats, after which they proceed to face Drona.

beats.title: Night Battle: Vṛṣasena's Rampage
beats.summary: Saṃjaya describes the night battle where Vṛṣasena, son of Karṇa, defeats the Somakas and then faces Yudhiṣṭhira, while Duḥśāsana fights Prativindhya. The battle is fierce and chaotic.

beats.title: Night Battle: Nakula and Shikhandin
beats.summary: In this night battle of the Mahabharata, Nakula fights Shakuni, defeating him, then proceeds towards Drona. Shikhandin engages Kripacharya in a fierce duel. The battle becomes chaotic in the darkness, with soldiers killing friends and relatives indiscriminately.

beats.title: The Night Battle: Dhrishtadyumna vs Drona and the Intervention of Karna and Satyaki
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 7, Chapter 145), the night battle continues as Dhrishtadyumna attacks Drona. Drona cuts his bow, but Dhrishtadyumna takes another and shoots a deadly arrow. Karna intercepts and cuts it into twelve pieces. Multiple warriors on both sides engage, with Satyaki coming to Dhrishtadyumna's aid. Karna and Satyaki duel fiercely; Satyaki wounds Vrishasena, Karna's son, causing Karna to rage. Meanwhile, Arjuna's Gandiva bow is heard, and Karna urges Duryodhana to focus on killing Satyaki and Dhrishtadyumna. Duryodhana sends Shakuni with a large force to engage the Pandavas. The battle intensifies.

beats.title: Night Battle: Satyaki and Arjuna's Feats
beats.summary: In the night battle of the Mahabharata, Yuyudhana (Satyaki) fights fiercely against Duryodhana and the Kaurava forces, causing great destruction. Arjuna engages with Shakuni and Uluka, defeating them. Dhrishtadyumna attacks Drona, and the Pandava forces rout the Kaurava army, leading to a river of blood. The chapter ends with the Pandavas blowing their conches in victory.

beats.title: Night Battle: Duryodhana's Reproach and the Counterattack
beats.summary: Duryodhana, seeing his army routed by the Pandavas, angrily confronts Karna and Drona, accusing them of not fighting to their full potential. Stung by his words, they launch a fierce counterattack, causing heavy casualties among the Panchalas and Pandava forces. As night falls, the battle continues in darkness, with warriors fighting by torchlight and calling out names. Krishna urges Arjuna to join Bhima in confronting Drona and Karna.

beats.title: Karna's Night Battle with Ghatotkacha
beats.summary: Karna devastates the Pandava army, prompting Yudhishthira to urge Arjuna to confront him. Krishna advises that only Ghatotkacha can counter Karna at night, and summons the rakshasa. Ghatotkacha accepts the challenge and engages Karna in a fierce nocturnal battle.

beats.title: The Slaying of Alambala
beats.summary: Saṁjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the night battle between Ghaṭotkaca and Alambala (son of Jaṭāsura). Duryodhana orders Alambala to fight Ghaṭotkaca. After a fierce duel with magical transformations, Ghaṭotkaca kills Alambala, cuts off his head, and presents it to Duryodhana before proceeding to attack Karṇa.

beats.title: The Night Battle Between Karna and Ghatotkacha
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the night battle between Karna and Ghatotkacha. Sanjaya describes Ghatotkacha's terrifying appearance, chariot, weapons, and magical powers. The fierce duel involves divine weapons and illusions, with Ghatotkacha creating various forms and Karna countering with his astra. Despite Ghatotkacha's might, Karna withstands the assault and destroys his chariot and horses. Ghatotkacha vanishes after declaring he will bring death to Karna.

beats.title: Alayudha's Arrival and Challenge
beats.summary: Alayudha, a powerful rakshasa king, arrives to aid Duryodhana, seeking revenge on Bhima for killing his relatives (Hidimba, Baka, Kirmira). He boasts of destroying the Pandavas and their army. Duryodhana welcomes him, and Alayudha charges at Bhima with a formidable chariot and weapons, causing chaos in the Pandava ranks.

beats.title: The Battle with Alayudha
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 7, Chapter 152), the demon Alayudha arrives on the battlefield and engages in fierce combat with Ghatotkacha and Bhima. Duryodhana encourages Alayudha to attack Bhima, while Krishna directs the Pandava forces to counter the demonic assault. The battle intensifies with both sides using various weapons, culminating in a brutal mace duel between Bhima and Alayudha. Krishna, concerned for Bhima's safety, urges Ghatotkacha to intervene.

beats.title: The Slaying of Alāyudha
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates the night battle between Ghaṭotkaca and Alāyudha, a rākṣasa ally of Duryodhana. Kṛṣṇa instructs Ghaṭotkaca to leave Karṇa and attack Alāyudha. After a fierce duel with various weapons and magical illusions, Ghaṭotkaca beheads Alāyudha and throws his head before Duryodhana, causing dismay among the Kauravas.

beats.title: The Slaying of Ghatotkacha
beats.summary: Karna kills the rakshasa Ghatotkacha using the divine Shakti weapon given by Indra, after Ghatotkacha creates havoc in the Kaurava army with his magical powers.

beats.title: Krishna's Joy at Ghatotkacha's Death
beats.summary: After Ghatotkacha's death, Krishna rejoices excessively, confusing Arjuna. Krishna explains that Karna's divine spear, which was destined to kill Arjuna, has been used on Ghatotkacha, and Karna's armor and earrings have been taken by Indra, making him vulnerable. Krishna reveals that Karna can now be killed only by Arjuna when his chariot wheel is stuck.

beats.title: Krishna Explains the Killing of Jarasandha and Others
beats.summary: Krishna explains to Arjuna how he and others killed powerful kings and demons through various stratagems (yogas) to protect dharma, and promises to help defeat Karna and Duryodhana. The chapter emphasizes that divine intervention and cunning are necessary to overcome invincible foes.

beats.title: The Neutralization of Karna's Infallible Spear
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra questions why Karna did not use his infallible spear against Arjuna, and Sanjaya explains that Krishna deliberately had Ghatotkacha engage Karna to neutralize the spear. The chapter reveals the strategic thinking behind the events and Krishna's protective role.

beats.title: The Slaying of Ghatotkacha and Yudhishthira's Despair
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya why Karna did not use the divine Shakti weapon against Krishna or Arjuna earlier. Sanjaya explains that fate and divine intervention prevented it; the weapon was used to kill Ghatotkacha instead. After Ghatotkacha's death, Yudhishthira becomes despondent, but Krishna and Vyasa console him, urging him to focus on dharma and continue fighting.

beats.title: The Night Battle and Truce
beats.summary: After Ghatotkacha's death, Yudhishthira orders Dhrishthadyumna to attack Drona. The Pandava army charges, but Drona holds them back. A fierce night battle ensues, with warriors exhausted and sleep-deprived. Arjuna calls for a truce to rest, which both sides accept. They sleep on the battlefield. At moonrise, they awaken and resume fighting.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Complaint and Drona's Rebuke
beats.summary: Duryodhana complains to Drona that the Pandavas are resting and growing stronger, and urges Drona to act. Drona defends Arjuna's prowess, recounting his past feats, and rebukes Duryodhana for his foolish talk. Duryodhana insists he and his allies can defeat Arjuna. Drona sarcastically suggests Duryodhana face Arjuna himself, then withdraws, and the battle begins.

beats.title: The Dawn Battle and Drona's Rampage
beats.summary: The chapter describes the night battle that continues into dawn, with Drona leading the Kaurava forces. Arjuna, urged by Bhima and Krishna, attacks the enemy. Drona kills many warriors including Virata, Drupada, and Drupada's grandsons. Dhrishtadyumna curses that Drona or anyone who flees from him will lose their merits. Bhima rebukes Dhrishtadyumna and charges into Drona's army. A fierce battle ensues until sunset.

beats.title: The Dawn Battle and Nakula's Duel
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce battle that resumed at sunrise after a night of fighting. The battlefield becomes chaotic with dust and blood, causing confusion where warriors cannot distinguish friend from foe. Key duels include Nakula vs Duryodhana. The chapter highlights the valor of warriors like Arjuna and Drona, who remain steadfast amidst the turmoil.

beats.title: The Duel of Drona and Arjuna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fierce battle between Duryodhana's forces and the Pandavas, focusing on duels between Sahadeva and Dushasana, Bhima and Karna, and a spectacular duel between Drona and Arjuna. The duel between Drona and Arjuna showcases their mastery of divine weapons, with celestial beings witnessing the battle. The chapter ends with the battle becoming chaotic and obscured by arrows.

beats.title: The Fall of Droṇa
beats.summary: Duryodhana and Sātyaki engage in a fierce but chivalrous duel, reminiscing about their childhood friendship. Bhīma kills an elephant named Aśvatthāmā, and Yudhiṣṭhira falsely reports its death to Droṇa, causing Droṇa to lose heart. Droṇa slaughters thousands of Pāñcālas and Matsyas. Sages intervene, urging Droṇa to lay down arms. Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna attacks Droṇa, who fights back but is eventually killed by Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna after a prolonged battle.

beats.title: The Fall of Dronacharya
beats.summary: Dronacharya is killed by Dhrishtadyumna after laying down his weapons upon hearing the false news of his son Ashvatthama's death. The Kaurava army flees in panic, and the Pandavas celebrate victory.

beats.title: Ashvatthama's Vow
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the reaction of Ashvatthama upon hearing of his father Drona's unjust killing by Dhrishtadyumna. Sanjaya describes Ashvatthama's grief and rage, his vow to kill Dhrishtadyumna and destroy the Panchalas, and his preparation to use the divine Narayana astra.

beats.title: Aśvatthāmā's Vengeance and Arjuna's Lament
beats.summary: After Drona's death, the Kaurava army flees in panic. Aśvatthāmā, enraged by his father's deceitful killing, unleashes the Nārāyaṇa astra, causing cosmic disturbances. Arjuna identifies Aśvatthāmā's roar and rebukes Yudhiṣṭhira for the adharmic act of killing Drona unarmed, expressing deep remorse.

beats.title: Bhima and Dhrishtadyumna Rebuke Arjuna
beats.summary: Bhima and Dhrishtadyumna rebuke Arjuna for his hesitation and pacifist speech, reminding him of the kshatriya dharma to fight and avenge injustices. They defend their actions in killing Drona and other enemies, arguing that adharma must be met with appropriate force.

beats.title: The Quarrel Between Satyaki and Dhrishtadyumna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the reaction of the Pandavas and other kings when Drona was insulted by Dhrishtadyumna. Sanjaya describes that the kings remained silent, but Arjuna, Yudhishthira, Bhima, the twins, Krishna, and Satyaki were distressed. Satyaki rebukes Dhrishtadyumna for killing Drona unethically. Dhrishtadyumna defends himself, citing the adharmic acts of the Kauravas. The argument escalates, and Satyaki attacks Dhrishtadyumna but is restrained by Bhima and Sahadeva. Krishna and Yudhishthira pacify both warriors.

beats.title: The Nārāyaṇa Astra Unleashed
beats.summary: Aśvatthāmā unleashes the Nārāyaṇa astra, causing panic among the Pāṇḍava army. Yudhiṣṭhira despairs and orders retreat, but Kṛṣṇa instructs everyone to dismount and lay down weapons, as the astra only harms those who fight. Bhīma refuses and attacks Aśvatthāmā, but the astra grows stronger. Ultimately, the Pāṇḍava army disarms, and the astra strikes Bhīma's head, leaving him enveloped in energy.

beats.title: The Neutralization of the Narayana Astra and Ashvatthama's Counterattack
beats.summary: Arjuna uses the Varuna weapon to protect Bhima from Ashvatthama's astra. Krishna and Arjuna intervene, pulling Bhima from his chariot and disarming him, causing the Narayana astra to subside. Ashvatthama then attacks Dhrishtadyumna, killing several warriors including the Cediya prince and Paurava, and routs the Panchalas.

beats.title: The Countering of the Āgneya Astra and the Revelation of Nara-Nārāyaṇa
beats.summary: After Drona's fall, Aśvatthāmā unleashes the āgneya astra, causing devastation. Arjuna counters with the brāhma astra. Aśvatthāmā, despondent, meets Vyāsa, who reveals the identity of Kṛṣṇa as Nārāyaṇa and Arjuna as Nara, and explains the boons obtained from Rudra. Aśvatthāmā then worships Rudra and honors Kṛṣṇa, leading to a ceasefire.

beats.title: Vyāsa's Revelation of Mahādeva's Glory
beats.summary: Vyasa explains to Arjuna that the fiery being he saw before him is Mahadeva (Shiva), who protects him in battle. Vyasa then narrates the glory of Shiva, including his destruction of Daksha's sacrifice and the three cities of the Asuras, and teaches the Śatarudrīya hymn. He assures Arjuna of victory with Krishna as his guide.

beats.title: The Fall of Karna and the Grief of Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: After the fall of Drona, King Dhritarashtra, grieving the loss of Bhishma and Drona, questions Sanjaya about the fate of his sons and the Kaurava army. Sanjaya recounts the events leading to Karna's death and the subsequent despair of the Kauravas. Dhritarashtra expresses his anguish and asks for a detailed account of the battle.

beats.title: Karna's Vow and Fall
beats.summary: After the fall of Drona, Duryodhana's army is disheartened. Duryodhana rallies them by praising Karna's prowess and urging them to fight. Karna then engages in fierce battle, causing great slaughter among the enemy, but is eventually slain by Arjuna.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Grief and Inquiry
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, upon hearing of the death of his sons, collapses in grief. The women of the palace lament. Vidura revives him. Dhritarashtra then questions Sanjaya about the fate of Duryodhana and others. Sanjaya reports that Karna and Dushasana have been killed.

beats.title: The Slaughter of the Heroes
beats.summary: Samjaya reports to Dhritarashtra the massive slaughter of the Kaurava army by the Pandavas, listing many fallen heroes. He then describes the surviving Kaurava warriors ready to fight. Dhritarashtra, overwhelmed by grief, asks for the list of Pandava casualties, which Samjaya provides, including Abhimanyu's death. The chapter ends with Dhritarashtra lamenting.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament for Karna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the death of Karna, praising his valor and questioning how such a great warrior could be slain. He expresses despair and considers suicide, but Sanjaya consoles him. Dhritarashtra then asks for details of the battle, including the reactions of various warriors and the fate of his army.

beats.title: Installation of Karṇa as Commander-in-Chief
beats.summary: After Drona's death, Duryodhana's army is in disarray. Duryodhana consults his generals, and Aśvatthāmā advises appointing Karṇa as commander-in-chief. Duryodhana agrees and ceremonially installs Karṇa as senāpati, who then prepares for battle.

beats.title: Karna's Command and the Battle Formations
beats.summary: Karna is appointed as commander-in-chief of the Kaurava army. He arranges the army in a Makara (crocodile) formation. Yudhishthira points out Karna's invincibility to Arjuna and urges him to kill Karna. Arjuna counters with an Ardha-chandra (half-moon) formation. Both armies prepare for battle, and a tumultuous sound arises as they clash.

beats.title: The Elephant Duel and Rout of the Kauravas
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Kaurava and Pāṇḍava armies, focusing on the elephant duel between Bhīmasena and Kṣemadhūrti, king of Kulūta, which ends with Bhīma killing Kṣemadhūrti and routing the Kaurava forces.

beats.title: The Duel of Sātyaki and the Kekaya Brothers
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas, focusing on the duel between Sātyaki and the Kekaya brothers. Sātyaki kills Anuvinda and then defeats the other Kekaya brother in a sword fight, causing the Kekaya army to flee.

beats.title: The Slaying of Citrasena and Citra
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 8, Chapter 10), Saṁjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the fierce battle between the Pāṇḍava and Kaurava forces. Key events include the duel between Śrutakarmā (Pāṇḍava) and Citrasena (Kaurava), where Śrutakarmā kills Citrasena. Then Prativindhya (Pāṇḍava) fights Citra (Kaurava), ultimately killing him. The Kaurava army flees, but Aśvatthāmā (Drauṇi) alone advances to confront Bhīmasena.

beats.title: The Duel of Bhīmasena and Aśvatthāmā
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce duel between Bhīmasena and Aśvatthāmā (Drauṇi) in the Mahābhārata war. Both warriors display extraordinary skill, exchanging volleys of arrows, performing complex maneuvers, and using divine weapons. The battle is so intense that celestial beings (siddhas) praise it as unprecedented. Eventually, both are wounded and rendered unconscious, and their charioteers withdraw them from the field.

beats.title: Arjuna's Victory over the Magadha Forces
beats.summary: Arjuna, guided by Krishna, attacks the Magadha army led by Daṇḍadhāra. After a fierce battle, Arjuna kills Daṇḍadhāra and his elephant, then defeats another elephant-riding warrior named Daṇḍa. The enemy forces are routed, and Arjuna is praised by his soldiers before proceeding to fight the Saṃśaptakas.

beats.title: Arjuna's Slaughter of the Samshaptakas and the Battlefield Scene
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes Arjuna's fierce battle against the Samshaptakas, his slaughter of many warriors, and Krishna's guidance to hasten to kill Karna. Krishna then shows Arjuna the gruesome battlefield, pointing out the carnage and the valor of the Pandya king who is decimating the Kaurava army.

beats.title: The Fall of the Pandya King
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya to describe the valor of the Pandya king in battle. Sanjaya narrates how the Pandya king, a great archer, decimates the Kaurava forces, but is eventually slain by Ashvatthama after a fierce duel.

beats.title: Karna's Rampage and the Pandava Response
beats.summary: After the fall of the Pandya king, Karna wreaks havoc on the Pandava army, killing many warriors. Arjuna and Krishna rush to the battlefield, and a fierce battle ensues between Karna and the Pandavas, with massive casualties on both sides.

beats.title: The Elephant Charge and the Duel of Nakula and Karna
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 8, Chapter 17), Sanjaya describes the fierce battle between the Pandava and Kaurava forces, focusing on the elephant charge led by Duryodhana's mahamatra warriors against Dhrishtadyumna. The Pandava heroes, including Nakula, Sahadeva, Satyaki, and others, counterattack, killing many elephants and enemy warriors. Nakula and Sahadeva engage in duels with Kaurava princes and Karna. Nakula fights Karna but is defeated and spared due to Kunti's plea. Karna then routs the Panchala army, causing great slaughter.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Duels and Rout
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas, focusing on duels involving Yuyutsu, Ulūka, Śatānīka, Sutasoma, Śakuni, Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna, Kr̥pa, Śikhaṇḍin, and Kr̥tavarmā. The Pāṇḍava army is routed after Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna is wounded by Kr̥tavarmā.

beats.title: The Slaughter of the Saṃśaptakas and the General Battle
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas, focusing on Arjuna's slaughter of the Saṃśaptakas and the general carnage. Duryodhana is disarmed by Yudhiṣṭhira but rescued by Karṇa and others. The chapter ends with mutual slaughter and a grim scene of the battlefield.

beats.title: Duel between Yudhishthira and Duryodhana
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle after Duryodhana was made chariotless. Sanjaya describes the duel between Yudhishthira and Duryodhana, where they exchange arrows, break each other's bows, and Yudhishthira strikes Duryodhana with a spear. Duryodhana faints, but Kritavarma rescues him. Bhima then attacks Kritavarma.

beats.title: The Eighth Day: Karna's Fury and Arjuna's Counter
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the eighth day of the Kurukshetra war, focusing on the fierce battle between Karna and Sātyaki, Arjuna's rampage, and the retreat of the Kaurava army at sunset. The Pāṇḍavas celebrate their victory.

beats.title: Karna's Boast and Request for Shalya
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the defeat of his sons and asks Sanjaya about Karna's actions in battle. Sanjaya recounts Karna's boastful speech to Duryodhana, where he claims superiority over Arjuna due to his divine bow Vijaya and requests Shalya as his charioteer. Duryodhana agrees to provide all needed resources.

beats.title: The Request for Shalya as Charioteer
beats.summary: Duryodhana requests King Shalya to serve as Karna's charioteer, praising his skills. Shalya initially refuses, offended by being asked to serve a suta's son, but Duryodhana appeases him by acknowledging his superiority over Krishna in horsemanship. Shalya agrees under a condition to speak freely.

beats.title: The Destruction of Tripura and the Urging of Shalya
beats.summary: Duryodhana narrates to Shalya the story of Tripura's destruction by Shiva, drawing parallels to the current battle. He recounts how the three sons of Taraka obtained boons, built three cities, and terrorized the worlds. The gods sought Brahma's help, who directed them to Shiva. After propitiating him, Shiva, with Brahma as his charioteer, destroyed the three cities with a single arrow. Duryodhana then urges Shalya to be Karna's charioteer, comparing him to Brahma and Karna to Shiva, and recounts how Parasurama obtained divine weapons from Shiva and later taught Karna.

beats.title: Shalya Agrees to be Karna's Charioteer
beats.summary: Duryodhana requests Shalya to be Karna's charioteer, praising his skill. Shalya agrees but warns he may speak harsh truths. Karna welcomes Shalya. Shalya then boasts of his own abilities, comparing himself to Matali, Indra's charioteer, and promises to drive Karna's horses skillfully in battle.

beats.title: Karna's Advance with Shalya as Charioteer
beats.summary: Duryodhana appoints Shalya as Karna's charioteer, praising his skill. Karna mounts his chariot and boasts of defeating the Pandavas. Shalya warns him against underestimating them, recalling past defeats. Karna dismisses the omens and expresses grief over Bhishma and Drona's deaths, but resolves to fight. Shalya taunts Karna, reminding him of his earlier flights and defeats. Karna retorts and advances toward the Pandava army.

beats.title: Karna's Boast and Shalya's Rebuke
beats.summary: Karna boasts before the battle, offering rewards to anyone who shows him Arjuna. King Shalya mocks Karna's arrogance, comparing him to a jackal challenging a lion. Karna retaliates by insulting Shalya and the Madra people, then reaffirms his resolve to fight Arjuna and Krishna.

beats.title: Shalya's Fable of the Crow and the Swan
