beats.summary: Shalya rebukes Karna for his arrogance by narrating the fable of a crow who boasted of many flight patterns but was humbled by a swan. He compares Karna to the crow, fed by the Kauravas, and Arjuna to the swan, superior in battle. Shalya recounts Karna's past failures and warns him not to underestimate Krishna and Arjuna.

beats.title: Karna's Vow and Curse
beats.summary: Karna, in a conversation with Shalya, recounts his past failures and curses, expresses his determination to fight Arjuna despite omens, and justifies his loyalty to Duryodhana. He boasts of his prowess and reveals a curse that his chariot wheel will sink in battle.

beats.title: Karna's Insults and Shalya's Retort
beats.summary: Karna insults Shalya by recounting a series of derogatory stories about the Bahlika and Madra peoples, as told by various brahmanas and others, to humiliate Shalya. Shalya retorts by pointing out Karna's own flaws and the hypocrisy of criticizing others. The chapter ends with Karna remaining silent and Shalya turning to face the enemy.

beats.title: Karṇa's Vyūha and the Pāṇḍava Response
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the battle formation (vyūha) arranged by Karṇa on the twelfth day of the Kurukṣetra war. Karṇa positions various warriors including Kṛpa, Māgadha, Kṛtavarmā, Śakuni, Ulūka, and the Saṁśaptakas on the flanks, with himself at the center. Yudhiṣṭhira instructs Arjuna to counter the formation, assigning specific opponents to each Pāṇḍava. Arjuna agrees and advances. Śalya warns Karṇa of Arjuna's approach, describing ominous omens. Karṇa dismisses the warnings, and the two armies clash.

beats.title: The Battle of Karna and the Pandavas
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle formation and the actions of Arjuna and Karna. Sanjaya describes the fierce fighting between the Pandavas and Kauravas, focusing on Karna's prowess and the counter-attacks by Bhima, Nakula, Sahadeva, Satyaki, and others. The chapter ends with both sides regrouping.

beats.title: Karna's Victory Over Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Karna attacks Yudhishthira's army, defeats him in combat, and taunts him for fleeing. The Pandava forces rally and a fierce battle ensues with massive casualties, described as a river of blood. Apsaras carry fallen heroes to heaven.

beats.title: Bhima's Duel with Karna
beats.summary: Bhima charges towards Karna's army, entrusting Yudhishthira to Satyaki and Dhrishtadyumna. Karna and Bhima engage in a fierce duel; Bhima shatters Karna's bow and wounds him, causing Karna to collapse unconscious. Shalya then carries Karna away in his chariot. Bhima routs the Kaurava army.

beats.title: Bhima's Rampage and the Duel with Karna
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the battle after Karna's fall. Sanjaya describes Bhima's fierce fight against Karna and the Kaurava army, including the slaying of many Kaurava warriors and elephants. Karna and Bhima engage in a duel, with Karna disabling Bhima's chariot. Bhima fights on foot, destroying hundreds of elephants and chariots. The battle intensifies as both armies clash, with Arjuna and others also involved. The chapter ends with a general melee.

beats.title: The Fierce Battle
beats.summary: Saṁjaya describes the fierce and chaotic battle of the Kurukshetra war, with vivid imagery of elephants, horses, chariots, and foot soldiers clashing, rivers of blood flowing, and the Kauravas' army being overwhelmed.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with the Saṃśaptakas
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes Arjuna's fierce battle against the Saṃśaptakas and Suśarman. Arjuna uses divine weapons, including the Nāga and Aindra astras, to defeat them. Kṛṣṇa aids him by shaking off attackers. The enemy army is terrified by Arjuna's prowess and the sound of their conches.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Kṛpa, Kṛtavarmā, and Others
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the battle where Kṛtavarmā, Kṛpa, Aśvatthāmā, and Karṇa try to rally the Kaurava army. Kṛpa fights Śikhaṇḍī, nearly killing him, but Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna intervenes. Kṛtavarmā engages Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna. Aśvatthāmā blocks Yudhiṣṭhira, and Karṇa holds off Bhīma and others. Kṛpa kills Suketu, son of Citraketu. Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna kills Kṛtavarmā's charioteer, then attacks the Kauravas.

beats.title: Ashvatthama's Onslaught and Yudhishthira's Rebuke
beats.summary: In this chapter, Ashvatthama (son of Drona) attacks Yudhishthira's army, displaying extraordinary archery. He is countered by Satyaki, Yudhishthira, and the Draupadeyas. After a fierce exchange, Yudhishthira rebukes Ashvatthama for attacking him despite being a Brahmin, then withdraws to attack the Kaurava forces.

beats.title: The Great Slaughter: Karna and Bhima's Rampage
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 8, Chapter 40), the battle intensifies as Karna and Bhima lead fierce attacks. Karna defeats many Panchalas and Chedis, while Bhima devastates the Kaurava army. Duryodhana fights Nakula and Sahadeva, but is rescued by his brothers after being disarmed by Dhrishtadyumna. Arjuna, urged by Krishna, destroys the Samshaptakas and later overcomes Ashvatthama's assault. The chapter ends with widespread slaughter on both sides.

beats.title: Krishna's Battlefield Guidance
beats.summary: Krishna urges Arjuna to observe the battlefield, pointing out key warriors on both sides, including Karna and Drona's son Ashvatthama, as the Kaurava army retreats and the Pandava forces advance under Dhrishtadyumna.

beats.title: The Battle Between Ashvatthama and Dhrishtadyumna
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 8, Chapter 42), the battle between the Kauravas and Pandavas intensifies. Karna confronts the Pandava forces, while Dronacharya's son Ashvatthama attacks Dhrishtadyumna, seeking revenge for his father's death. Arjuna and Krishna intervene to save Dhrishtadyumna, and after a fierce exchange, Arjuna wounds Ashvatthama, who is then carried away by his charioteer. The Pandavas celebrate their success.

beats.title: Krishna's Urging and Bhima's Counterattack
beats.summary: Krishna describes to Arjuna the dire situation of Yudhishthira being pursued by Duryodhana and Karna, and urges Arjuna to attack Karna. He then reports that Bhima has turned the tide, slaughtering the Kaurava army, and Arjuna finishes off the remaining enemies.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Karna's Counterattack
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what happened after Bhima and Yudhishthira withdrew and the Kaurava army was being slaughtered. Sanjaya describes Karna rallying the Kaurava forces and engaging the Pandava warriors. The chapter details individual duels: Shikhandi vs Karna, Dhrishtadyumna vs Duhshasana, Nakula vs Vrishasena, Sahadeva vs Uluka, Satyaki vs Shakuni, Bhima vs Duryodhana, Yudhamanyu vs Kripa, and Uttamaujas vs Kritavarma. The battle is fierce with many casualties.

beats.title: The Battle with Ashvatthama and Karna's Rampage
beats.summary: In this chapter, Arjuna fights a fierce battle with Ashvatthama, who uses divine weapons. Karna then unleashes the Bhargavastra, devastating the Pandava army. Arjuna and Krishna seek out Yudhishthira, who has been wounded by Karna and retreated. They find him and pay respects.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament and Inquiry about Karna's Death
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, overwhelmed by Karna's prowess and humiliation, recounts his suffering and repeatedly asks Arjuna how he killed Karna, expressing relief and seeking confirmation of Karna's death.

beats.title: Arjuna's Report to Yudhiṣṭhira
beats.summary: Arjuna recounts to Yudhishthira his fierce battle with Aśvatthāmā and Karna, describing the chaos and his determination to kill Karna. He urges Yudhishthira to witness his fight and promises victory.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Rebuke of Arjuna
beats.summary: Yudhishthira rebukes Arjuna for fleeing from Karna in battle, recalling past boasts and prophecies about Arjuna's prowess, and questioning his courage.

beats.title: Krishna's Counsel and the Reconciliation of Arjuna and Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Krishna dissuades Arjuna from killing Yudhishthira for insulting him, using the stories of Balaka and Kaushika to illustrate the complexity of dharma. Arjuna then harshly rebukes Yudhishthira, who offers to abdicate and go to the forest. Krishna intervenes, explaining that the insult was a ruse to uphold Arjuna's vow, and reconciles the brothers.

beats.title: Arjuna's Reconciliation and Vow to Kill Karna
beats.summary: Arjuna, after harsh words to Yudhishthira, is consoled by Krishna and reconciles with his brother. He vows to kill Karna, receives Yudhishthira's blessing, and prepares for battle with Krishna's encouragement.

beats.title: Krishna's Exhortation to Arjuna to Kill Karna
beats.summary: Samjaya recounts to Dhritarashtra the events of the war, focusing on Krishna's exhortation to Arjuna to kill Karna. He describes the destruction of both armies, the deaths of Bhishma and Drona, and the role of Arjuna in protecting the Pandava forces. Krishna emphasizes Karna's evil deeds and urges Arjuna to fulfill his vow.

beats.title: Arjuna's Vow to Kill Karna
beats.summary: Arjuna, encouraged by Krishna's words, becomes determined to kill Karna. He boasts of his prowess, vows to fulfill his promises, and declares that Karna's death will bring joy to his allies and grief to the Kauravas.

beats.title: The Eighth Parva, Chapter 53: The Battle Array and Single Combats
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the Kuru army arrayed for battle, with various warriors engaging in combat. Notable events include Bhīma's fierce attack on Duryodhana's sons, the slaying of Suṣeṇa by Uttamaujas, and Karna's grief-stricken retaliation. The chapter depicts the chaos and brutality of war.

beats.title: Bhima's Rage and Arjuna's Arrival
beats.summary: Bhima, surrounded by enemy forces, instructs his charioteer to drive into the Kaurava army, vowing to send them to Yama. He fights fiercely, causing chaos. Later, he asks his charioteer Viśoka about the remaining arrows and the identity of a powerful force causing the enemy to flee. Viśoka reveals it is Arjuna, with his monkey banner and divine conch, and Kṛṣṇa. Bhima rejoices and offers rewards.

beats.title: Bhima's Victory over Shakuni and the Kaurava Retreat
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes Arjuna's attack on the Kaurava army, Bhima's fierce combat, and the eventual retreat of Duryodhana's forces after Bhima defeats Shakuni. The chapter highlights the valor of Arjuna and Bhima, and the role of Karna as a refuge for the fleeing Kauravas.

beats.title: Karna's Counterattack
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about Karna's actions after Bhimasena broke the Kaurava army. Sanjaya describes Karna's fierce counterattack, slaughtering Somakas and Panchalas, and the ensuing chaotic battle where both sides suffer heavy losses.

beats.title: The Approach of Arjuna and Karna's Vow
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the fierce battle where Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, confronts Karna and the Kaurava forces. Karna, encouraged by Shalya, prepares to fight Arjuna. Arjuna attacks the Kaurava army, causing massive destruction. The chapter highlights the valor of both sides and the inevitability of the duel between Arjuna and Karna.

beats.title: Arjuna's Rout of the Kaurava Army
beats.summary: Arjuna, with his divine bow Gāṇḍīva, decimates the Kaurava army, causing a rout. He rescues Bhīmasena from the battlefield, consults with him, and then proceeds to attack and kill ten of Duryodhana's younger brothers.

beats.title: Arjuna's Onslaught and Bhima's Rescue
beats.summary: Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, attacks Karna's army. He kills ninety warriors and then faces a massive assault from elephants and Mlecchas. Bhima comes to his aid, destroying the remaining enemy forces with his mace. The Kaurava army is routed and seeks refuge with Karna, who then attacks the Panchalas.

beats.title: Karna's Rampage and Bhīma's Duel with Duryodhana
beats.summary: Karna dominates the battlefield, killing many Pāñcāla warriors and defeating five great chariot-warriors. Sātyaki and Bhīma come to aid, and Bhīma engages Duryodhana in a fierce duel.

beats.title: Bhima kills Dushasana
beats.summary: Bhima kills Dushasana in battle, drinks his blood, and vows to kill Duryodhana next.

beats.title: The Fall of Vrishasena
beats.summary: After the fall of Dushasana, Karna enters the battlefield and is encouraged by Shalya. Vrishasena, Karna's son, attacks Bhima but is intercepted by Nakula. A fierce battle ensues involving the Pandava brothers, the Kauravas, and their allies, with many warriors and elephants slain. Arjuna ultimately kills Vrishasena, enraging Karna.

beats.title: The Duel of Karna and Arjuna
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes the dramatic duel between Karna and Arjuna, with cosmic beings and natural elements taking sides. The gods, led by Brahma and Shiva, declare that victory belongs to Krishna (Nara-Narayana), and Karna will attain heavenly realms. The chapter ends with Arjuna vowing to kill Karna for past insults.

beats.title: The Duel of Arjuna and Karna and Aśvatthāmā's Peace Attempt
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 8, Chapter 64), the battle between the Kauravas and Pandavas intensifies. Arjuna and Karna engage in a fierce duel, displaying extraordinary archery. The celestial beings witness the battle, and flowers rain down. Aśvatthāmā attempts to negotiate peace with Duryodhana, urging him to reconcile with the Pandavas, but Duryodhana refuses, citing Bhīma's killing of Duḥśāsana as an unforgivable act. The chapter ends with Duryodhana ordering his troops to attack.

beats.title: The Duel of Arjuna and Karna
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce duel between Arjuna and Karna on the battlefield. Both warriors display extraordinary prowess, exchanging volleys of arrows. Bhīmasena and Vāsudeva urge Arjuna to kill Karna. Arjuna invokes the Brahmāstra, but Karna fights back fiercely, wounding Arjuna and Kṛṣṇa. Arjuna retaliates, killing many warriors including Suṣeṇa and Śalya, and finally Karna's army flees, but Karna alone charges at Arjuna.

beats.title: The Duel of Karna and Arjuna
beats.summary: Karna and Arjuna engage in a fierce duel. Karna uses a divine serpent arrow to knock off Arjuna's crown (kirīṭa). Arjuna retaliates, cutting down the serpent and wounding Karna. Karna's chariot wheel sinks into the earth due to a brahmin's curse, and he asks Arjuna for a pause, citing dharma. Arjuna hesitates but is urged by Krishna.

beats.title: The Fall of Karna
beats.summary: Krishna reminds Karna of his past sins, especially the humiliation of Draupadi, and declares that his death is imminent. Arjuna, enraged, engages in a fierce duel with Karna, ultimately decapitating him with the Anjalika arrow. Karna's fall causes the Kaurava army to flee.

beats.title: The Fall of Karna
beats.summary: After Karna's fall, Shalya describes the devastated battlefield to Duryodhana, who grieves deeply. The Kauravas flee, while Arjuna and Krishna blow their conches in triumph. The chapter ends with the Kaurava army retreating.

beats.title: The Slaying of Karna and the Aftermath
beats.summary: After Karna is slain by Arjuna, Krishna and Arjuna embrace. Krishna suggests they inform Yudhishthira. They go to Yudhishthira's tent, where he rejoices at the news. Yudhishthira praises Krishna and Arjuna, recalling that they are the divine Nara-Narayana, and declares that with Krishna as Arjuna's charioteer, victory is assured. He then goes to see Karna's body. The Pandava brothers and allies celebrate. Dhritarashtra, hearing the news, collapses in grief, but Vidura and Sanjaya revive him.

beats.title: The Fall of Shalya and Duryodhana
beats.summary: After Karna's fall, Duryodhana, overwhelmed with grief, appoints Shalya as commander and resumes battle. Shalya is killed by Yudhishthira. Duryodhana hides in a lake but is challenged and slain by Bhima. The surviving warriors (Kripa, Kritavarma, Ashvatthama) attack the Pandava camp at night. Sanjaya returns to Hastinapura and reports the devastating losses to Dhritarashtra, who collapses in sorrow. Vidura consoles him and sends away the grieving women.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the death of his sons and allies in the Kurukshetra war, questioning fate and recalling past warnings. He asks Sanjaya to narrate the details of the battle, including the fall of key warriors like Bhishma, Drona, Karna, and Duryodhana.

beats.title: Kripa's Counsel to Duryodhana
beats.summary: Kripa, seeing the Kaurava army demoralized after the fall of Karna and other heroes, advises Duryodhana to seek peace with the Pandavas, emphasizing the futility of continued war and the superiority of Arjuna and Krishna.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Rejection of Peace
beats.summary: Duryodhana responds to Kṛpa's advice for peace, rejecting it due to deep enmity with the Pāṇḍavas. He recounts past grievances, including the dice game, Draupadī's humiliation, and Abhimanyu's death, and resolves to fight to the death, seeking glory and heaven through battle. The Kaurava army then marches and camps near the Sarasvatī river.

beats.title: Appointment of Śalya as Commander-in-Chief
beats.summary: After Karna's death, the Kaurava army, terrified, takes refuge on the Himavat mountain. Duryodhana, urged by his generals, appoints a new commander-in-chief. Aśvatthāmā recommends Śalya, praising his qualities. Duryodhana formally requests Śalya to lead, and Śalya accepts.

beats.title: Shalya Appointed Commander
beats.summary: After Karna's death, Duryodhana appoints Shalya as the commander of the Kaurava army. Shalya boasts of his prowess and vows to defeat the Pandavas. On the Pandava side, Yudhishthira consults Krishna, who praises Shalya's strength and advises Yudhishthira to kill him. Krishna compares Shalya to a well after crossing the ocean of Bhishma, Drona, and Karna. Yudhishthira and the Pandava army rest joyfully, relieved after Karna's fall.

beats.title: Shalya Takes Command
beats.summary: After Bhishma, Drona, and Karna have fallen, King Duryodhana appoints Shalya as the commander-in-chief. The Kaurava army forms a sarvatobhadra formation with Shalya at the front. The Pandavas divide their forces and attack. Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the remaining forces and the battle that ensues.

beats.title: The Fierce Battle and the Rout of the Kauravas
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Kauravas and the Śrñjayas (Pāṇḍavas), with vivid imagery of elephants, chariots, horses, and foot soldiers clashing. The fighting is chaotic and brutal, with a river of blood forming. Arjuna and Bhīmasena cause confusion among the Kaurava forces, and the Pāṇḍava army, led by Dhṛṣṭadyumna and Śikhaṇḍin, attacks the Madra king Śalya. The Kaurava army is routed and flees.

beats.title: The Battle Continues: Nakula's Valor and the Clash of Armies
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 9, Chapter 9), the battle continues with Shalya, the king of Madra, rallying the Kaurava forces after a rout. Nakula fights and kills Citrasena, then faces and kills Karna's sons Satyasena and Sushena. The Pāṇḍava army, led by Yudhiṣṭhira, advances, and a fierce battle ensues between the two sides, with Arjuna joining after defeating the Saṃśaptakas.

beats.title: Shalya's Onslaught and Bhima's Counterattack
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 9, Chapter 10), the battle intensifies as Shalya, the king of Madra, attacks the Pandava army. He wounds many warriors including Yudhishthira, Bhima, and the Draupadeyas. The Pandavas retaliate, and Bhima kills Shalya's horses with his mace. The chapter describes the fierce combat, omens, and the valor of both sides.

beats.title: The Mace Duel between Bhima and Shalya
beats.summary: Bhima and Shalya engage in a fierce mace duel, each displaying great skill. After a mutual fall, Shalya is rescued by Kripa. The battle escalates into a general melee, with Duryodhana and others attacking the Pandava forces. Shalya wounds Yudhishthira, who retaliates, and Arjuna also wounds Shalya. The chapter ends with Yudhishthira overwhelmed by Shalya's arrows.

beats.title: The Pandavas Attack Shalya
beats.summary: Shalya, the king of Madra, is attacked by Yudhishthira, Bhima, Nakula, Sahadeva, and Satyaki. Despite being outnumbered, Shalya fights valiantly, cutting their weapons and arrows. He creates a dark cloud of arrows, causing confusion. The Pandavas struggle but persist. Duryodhana believes the Pandavas are defeated. Yudhishthira recalls Krishna's words about Shalya's fury.

beats.title: Arjuna's Battle with Ashvatthama and Others
beats.summary: Arjuna fights against Drona's son Ashvatthama and other warriors. He kills many enemies, and after a fierce duel, Ashvatthama is wounded but continues fighting. Suratha, a Panchala warrior, attacks Ashvatthama but is killed. The battle intensifies as Arjuna faces multiple foes.

beats.title: The Battle with Śalya
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Mahabharata (Parva 9, Chapter 14), Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between Duryodhana and Dhṛṣṭadyumna, followed by the engagement of Śalya against the Pāṇḍavas. Śalya fights valiantly, wounding Nakula, Yudhiṣṭhira, Bhīmasena, Sahadeva, and Sātyaki, and even cuts Nakula's bow. Sātyaki mounts another chariot and attacks Śalya, leading to a tumultuous duel reminiscent of the battle between Śambara and Indra. The Pāṇḍavas then surround Śalya, but he continues to fight alone against many, creating a scene of chaos and darkness from arrows.

beats.title: The Duel of Yudhiṣṭhira and Śalya
beats.summary: Sañjaya describes the fierce battle between the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas, focusing on the duel between Yudhiṣṭhira and Śalya. Yudhiṣṭhira, enraged by the retreat of his troops, resolves to fight Śalya personally. He arranges his chariot with protectors and engages Śalya. The battle is intense, with both sides suffering. Bhīma fights Duryodhana, cutting his bow and flag, but Duryodhana is rescued by Aśvatthāmā, Kṛpa, and Kṛtavarmā. Yudhiṣṭhira then attacks Śalya, causing great slaughter. Śalya cuts Yudhiṣṭhira's bow, but Yudhiṣṭhira retaliates, cutting Śalya's bow and killing his horses. Aśvatthāmā rescues Śalya, who later returns with a new chariot.

beats.title: The Death of Shalya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the death of King Shalya of Madra at the hands of Yudhishthira, followed by the killing of Shalya's younger brother and the subsequent rout of the Kaurava army. It also includes the duel between Satyaki and Kritavarma, and Duryodhana's brief stand before the Pandavas.

beats.title: The Fall of the Madra Army
beats.summary: After Shalya's death, the Madra army attacks Yudhishthira. Duryodhana tries to stop them but fails. Shakuni urges Duryodhana to fight unitedly. The Pandavas counterattack, leading to a fierce battle with heavy casualties. The Madra forces are annihilated, and the Kaurava army turns back.

beats.title: The Fall of Shalya and the Rout of the Kauravas
beats.summary: After the fall of King Shalya, the Kaurava army flees in panic. Duryodhana rallies his troops, but Bhima slaughters 21,000 foot soldiers. The Pandavas pursue Duryodhana, who stands firm. Duryodhana delivers a speech on kshatriya dharma, inspiring his men to turn back and fight.

beats.title: Shalva's Elephant Attack and Death
beats.summary: Shalva, king of the Mlecchas, attacks the Pandava army with a mighty elephant. He routs the Pandava forces, but Dhrishtadyumna, son of Drupada, confronts him. Shalva's elephant crushes Dhrishtadyumna's chariot, forcing him to dismount. Bhima, Shikhandi, and Satyaki then engage the elephant. Ultimately, Dhrishtadyumna kills the elephant with a mace, and Satyaki beheads Shalva with an arrow.

beats.title: Satyaki defeats Kritavarma; Duryodhana rallies the army
beats.summary: After the fall of Shalva, the Kaurava army breaks. Kritavarma rallies them, but Satyaki attacks, kills Kshemadhurti, and engages Kritavarma in a fierce duel. Satyaki cuts Kritavarma's bow, then kills his horses and charioteer. Kritavarma hurls a trident, but Satyaki shatters it and strikes him in the heart, leaving him chariotless. Kripa rescues Kritavarma. Duryodhana then single-handedly stops the Pandava advance, like a sacrificial fire. Kritavarma later mounts another chariot.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Heroic Stand and the General Battle
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes to Dhritarashtra the fierce battle where Duryodhana single-handedly attacks the Pandava army, wounding many warriors. Despite being surrounded, Duryodhana stands firm. Various duels ensue: Ashvatthama vs Bhima, Shakuni vs Yudhishthira, Uluka vs Nakula, Kritavarma vs Satyaki, Duryodhana vs Dhrishtadyumna, and Kripa vs the Draupadeyas. The battlefield becomes chaotic with dust and blood, but eventually the dust settles revealing the duels.

beats.title: The Eighteenth Day: The Slaughter of Shakuni's Army
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the fierce battle on the 18th day, focusing on the clash between the Pandavas and Kauravas. Yudhishthira attacks, but Duryodhana sends 700 chariots against him. The Pandavas and Panchalas counterattack. Shakuni attacks from the rear, causing the Pandava army to break. Yudhishthira sends Sahadeva and the Draupadeyas to stop Shakuni. A chaotic melee ensues with heavy casualties. Shakuni retreats with 6,000 horses. The battle continues with indiscriminate slaughter, including kinsmen killing each other. Ominous portents appear. The chapter ends with the ongoing carnage.

beats.title: Arjuna's Final Onslaught
beats.summary: Samjaya narrates to Dhritarashtra how after the fall of Bhishma, Drona, and many other warriors, the Kaurava army is decimated. Arjuna, urged by Krishna, enters the battlefield and single-handedly destroys the remaining Kaurava forces with his Gandiva bow, fulfilling the prophecy of the destruction of the Kshatriyas due to Duryodhana's folly.

beats.title: The Slaughter of the Elephant Division
beats.summary: Samjaya describes the battle where Arjuna, Bhima, and other Pandavas decimate the Kaurava elephant division. Duryodhana is wounded and retreats. Aśvatthāmā, Kr̥pa, and Kr̥tavarmā search for Duryodhana. Samjaya himself fights and is captured by Sātyaki. The Pandavas prevail.

beats.title: Bhima Slays the Kaurava Brothers
beats.summary: Bhima kills many of Duryodhana's brothers and other Kaurava warriors in a fierce battle, including Durmarshana, Shrutanta, Jayatsena, Jaitra, Ravibhuri, Durvimocana, Dushpradharsha, Sujata, Durvishaha, and Shrutarva. After slaying them, he routs the remaining Kaurava army.

beats.title: The Slaying of the Remaining Kaurava Warriors
beats.summary: Sanjaya describes to Dhritarashtra the final battle where Arjuna and Bhima kill the remaining Kaurava warriors, including Sudarshana and Susharma, and Duryodhana is left alone. Krishna urges Arjuna to finish Duryodhana.

beats.title: Sahadeva Slays Shakuni
beats.summary: Sahadeva kills Uluka and then beheads Shakuni in battle, avenging the dice game. The Pandava forces celebrate victory.

beats.title: The Aftermath of the Kaurava Defeat
beats.summary: Sañjaya narrates to Dhṛtarāṣṭra the final destruction of the Kaurava army after the fall of Śakuni. Duryodhana, seeing his forces annihilated, flees the battlefield and hides in a lake, using his māyā to make the water solid. Sañjaya is spared by the Pāṇḍavas due to the intervention of Vyāsa. The three surviving Kaurava warriors—Kṛpa, Aśvatthāmā, and Kṛtavarman—meet Sañjaya and lament. The camp is abandoned, and the women and ministers flee to Hastinapura. Yuyutsu, the only Kaurava to side with the Pāṇḍavas, returns to the city and reports to Vidura.

beats.title: Duryodhana Hides in the Lake
beats.summary: After the Kurukshetra war, Duryodhana hides in a lake using his magical powers. The three survivors—Kripa, Ashvatthama, and Kritavarma—find him and urge him to fight. Duryodhana refuses, wanting to rest. Hunters overhear their conversation and inform Bhima, who tells Yudhishthira. The Pandavas march to the lake, and the three warriors flee. Duryodhana remains in the lake, awaiting battle.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Lake Refuge
beats.summary: After the war, Duryodhana hides in a lake using magic. Yudhishthira and Krishna find him. Krishna advises using counter-magic (kriyā) to defeat him. Yudhishthira taunts Duryodhana, who emerges but refuses to fight, offering the kingdom instead. Yudhishthira rejects the offer, insisting on battle.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Challenge from the Water
beats.summary: Duryodhana, after being taunted by the Pandavas while standing in water, emerges with a mace and challenges them to single combat. Yudhishthira agrees and offers him a choice of weapon and opponent. Duryodhana chooses the mace and demands to fight one of the Pandavas. After further exchanges, he arms himself and prepares for battle.

beats.title: Bhima's Vow and the Challenge to Duryodhana
beats.summary: In this chapter, Vasudeva (Krishna) rebukes Yudhishthira for agreeing to a single combat with Duryodhana, arguing it is reckless. Bhima then vows to kill Duryodhana, recalling past injustices. Duryodhana boasts of his prowess, and both sides prepare for the duel.

beats.title: Arrival of Balarama
beats.summary: Samjaya narrates to Dhritarashtra the arrival of Balarama (Halayudha) on the battlefield of Kurukshetra to witness the mace duel between his disciples Duryodhana and Bhima. Balarama is welcomed by the Pandavas and other kings, exchanges greetings, and takes his seat among them. The chapter sets the stage for the climactic mace fight.

beats.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage and the Story of Soma's Curse
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about Balarama's return to Kurukshetra after his pilgrimage. Vaishampayana narrates how Krishna failed to negotiate peace, Balarama refused to participate in the war and went on a pilgrimage along the Sarasvati river, performing generous charity. The story of the moon god Soma's curse by Daksha and his redemption at the Prabhasa tirtha is recounted. Balarama visits various tirthas including Camasodbheda and Udapana.

beats.title: The Story of Trita and His Brothers
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the sage Trita, who was abandoned by his brothers Ekata and Dvita in a dry well. Despite being trapped, Trita performed a Soma sacrifice using his mental powers, invoking the gods. The gods, led by Brihaspati, came and granted him a boon. Trita was rescued by the river Sarasvati and later cursed his brothers to become wild animals. The chapter ends with Balarama visiting the well and praising it.

beats.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage along the Sarasvati
beats.summary: Balarama travels along the Sarasvati river, visiting various tirthas (sacred sites), performing rituals, and giving gifts to brahmins. He witnesses the miraculous turning of the river at the Naimiṣeya kuñja, where the river reversed its course to accommodate the sages. The chapter describes the sages' austerities and the divine presence at these tirthas.

beats.title: The Seven Sarasvatis and the Sage Mankanaka
beats.summary: This chapter from the Mahabharata (Parva 9, Chapter 37) narrates the story of the seven Sarasvati rivers and the sage Mankanaka. It describes how the Sarasvati river appeared in seven forms at different sacrificial sites, and then relates the tale of Mankanaka's accidental injury, his ecstatic dance upon seeing plant sap, and his subsequent humbling by Shiva, who demonstrated greater detachment by producing ash from his own wound. The chapter concludes with Shiva's blessing and promise to reside at the Saptasārasvata tīrtha.

beats.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage to Kapālamocana and Other Tīrthas
beats.summary: Balarama travels to various tirthas, including the Auśanasa tirtha (Kapālamocana), where the sage Mahodara was freed from a skull stuck to his leg. He then visits Ruṣaṅgu's ashrama and hears about the merit of dying at Pr̥thūdaka on the northern bank of Sarasvatī. Finally, he goes to the place where Viśvāmitra attained brāhmaṇyahood and where Arṣṭiṣeṇa also achieved it.

beats.title: The Attainment of Brāhmaṇahood by Viśvāmitra and Others
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṃpāyana about the austerities of Ārṣṭiṣeṇa, Sindhudvīpa, and Devāpi, and how Viśvāmitra attained brāhmaṇahood. Vaiśaṃpāyana narrates: Ārṣṭiṣeṇa, unable to master the Vedas in his guru's house, performed severe penance at a tīrtha on the Sarasvatī and attained siddhi, granting boons. Later, Sindhudvīpa and Devāpi also attained brāhmaṇahood at the same tīrtha. Viśvāmitra, son of Gādhi, after his father's death, faced a threat from rākṣasas. He sought help from Vasiṣṭha, but his army was routed by Vasiṣṭha's divine cow. Realizing the power of penance, Viśvāmitra undertook severe austerities at the Sarasvatī tīrtha, overcoming divine obstacles, and finally obtained brāhmaṇahood from Brahmā. Rāma later performed dāna at that tīrtha and then went to Baka's āśrama.

beats.title: The Curse and Forgiveness of Baka Dālbhya
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of sage Baka Dālbhya, who, insulted by King Dhṛtarāṣṭra, performs a ritual to destroy the king's kingdom. Later, the king repents and seeks forgiveness, and the sage relents, restoring the kingdom. The chapter also describes the sacredness of Sarasvatī river and the deeds of Yayāti and Bṛhaspati.

beats.title: The Carrying Away of Vasiṣṭha by Sarasvatī
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks about the story of Vasiṣṭha being carried away by Sarasvatī. Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates the rivalry between Viśvāmitra and Vasiṣṭha, born of competition in austerity. Viśvāmitra, enraged, commands Sarasvatī to bring Vasiṣṭha to him so he can kill him. Sarasvatī, fearing both sages, carries Vasiṣṭha but then diverts him away, saving him. Viśvāmitra curses her to flow with blood for a year. The gods and sages grieve, but eventually she returns to her normal course.

beats.title: The Purification of Sarasvati and Indra's Liberation from Brahmahatyā
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the Sarasvati river being cursed by Viśvāmitra to flow with blood, attracting rākṣasas who drink it. Sages later purify the river and help the rākṣasas attain liberation by bathing in the Aruṇā river. It also recounts Indra's slaying of Namuci and his subsequent purification from brahmahatyā by bathing in the Aruṇā tīrtha.

beats.title: The Birth and Anointment of Skanda
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṃpāyana about the birth and anointment of Skanda (Kārttikeya). Vaiśaṃpāyana narrates how Śiva's seed fell into fire, then into Gaṅgā, and was deposited on a mountain. Skanda was born in a Śara-stamba, suckled by the Kṛttikās, and grew up. He later manifested four forms (Skanda, Śākha, Viśākha, Naigameṣa) to approach Rudra, Pārvatī, Agni, and Gaṅgā simultaneously. The gods requested Brahmā to grant him leadership, and Brahmā appointed him as Senāpati of all beings. The anointment ceremony took place on the bank of Sarasvatī at Samantapañcaka.

beats.title: Parva 9, Chapter 44
beats.summary: This chapter describes the grand consecration of Skanda (Kārttikeya) as the commander-in-chief of the gods. Various deities, sages, celestial beings, and natural entities gather, bringing auspicious items. Brahmā performs the coronation, and numerous divine beings bestow attendants and weapons upon Skanda. The chapter lists the names and forms of Skanda's countless attendants, highlighting their diverse appearances and weaponry.

beats.title: The Anointment and Victory of Skanda
beats.summary: This chapter describes the army of the Mothers (Matrganas) accompanying Skanda (Karttikeya), their diverse forms and attributes, the gifts of weapons and boons from various deities to Skanda, his anointment as commander of the divine army, and his subsequent battle against the Daityas, including the slaying of Taraka and the piercing of Mount Kraunca.

beats.title: The Anointment of Varuṇa and the Disappearance of Agni
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṃpāyana about the anointment of Varuṇa as lord of waters and the disappearance of Agni. Vaiśaṃpāyana narrates how the gods anointed Varuṇa, then Agni hid in a śamī tree due to Bhṛgu's curse, was found by the gods, and later bathed at various tīrthas. Kubera's penance and boons are also described, including his anointment as lord of wealth and his divine vehicle.

beats.title: The Story of Srucāvatī and the Tīrtha Badarapācana
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Srucāvatī, daughter of sage Bharadvāja, who performed severe penance to win Indra as her husband. Indra tested her by asking her to cook badara fruits without firewood; she used her own body as fuel. Pleased, Indra granted her wish and established the tīrtha Badarapācana. The chapter also recounts a parallel story of Arundhatī, who similarly cooked badara fruits for Śiva during a drought, earning a boon that the tīrtha would grant great merit.

beats.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage to Sarasvati Tirthas
beats.summary: Balarama visits various tirthas along the Sarasvati river, including Indratirtha, Ramatirtha, Yamunatirtha, and Adityatirtha. He performs rituals, gives donations, and hears stories of past deeds by Indra, Parashurama, Varuna, and Vishnu. The chapter highlights the sanctity of these tirthas and the spiritual attainments of sages like Vyasa and Asita Devala.

beats.title: The Renunciation of Asita Devala
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the sage Asita Devala, who lived a householder's life dedicated to dharma. He encounters the wandering ascetic Jaigīṣavya, who possesses extraordinary yogic powers, including the ability to be in multiple places simultaneously and traverse various celestial realms. Asita, initially skeptical, witnesses Jaigīṣavya's powers and seeks his guidance. Jaigīṣavya teaches him the path of renunciation and yoga. Asita then decides to abandon household life for mokṣa, despite the lamentations of beings dependent on him. Ultimately, he attains supreme siddhi through yoga.

beats.title: The Story of Dadhīca and Sārasvata
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the sage Dadhīca, whose bones were used by Indra to create weapons to defeat the demons. It also tells of the birth of Sārasvata, son of Dadhīca and the river Sarasvatī, who during a twelve-year drought taught the Vedas to sixty thousand sages who had forgotten them due to hunger.

beats.title: The Tale of the Old Maiden and the Merit of Kurukṣetra
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks about the old maiden who performed severe penance. Vaiśaṃpāyana narrates: Sage Kuṇi Gārgya created a daughter mentally, then died. She performed tapas, refusing marriage. Nārada told her that without marriage, her penance was incomplete. She offered half her penance to a husband. Sage Śṛṅgavān (son of Gālava) married her on condition of one night together. She became young and beautiful, left after one night, and then died, attaining heaven. Śṛṅgavān, grieving, followed her path. Later, Balarāma heard of Śalya's death, gave donations, and asked sages about the merit of Kurukṣetra.

beats.title: The Origin of Kurukṣetra
beats.summary: The sages narrate to Rama the origin of Kurukṣetra as a sacred field plowed by King Kuru, who sought to grant heaven to those dying there. Indra initially mocked him but later granted the boon that anyone dying in battle or through austerity in that field attains heaven. The chapter establishes Kurukṣetra as the 'uttaravedi' of Prajāpati, a supremely holy place.

beats.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage and Narada's Report
beats.summary: Balarama visits holy sites along the Sarasvati river, hears from Narada about the Kurukshetra war's aftermath, and proceeds to witness the final duel between Duryodhana and Bhima.

beats.title: The Mace Duel: Duryodhana and Bhima Prepare
beats.summary: Duryodhana, seeing Balarama present for the mace duel, asks Sanjaya how his son fought Bhima. Sanjaya describes the preparations: the Kuru army moves to Samantapanchaka, the sacred battlefield. Both Duryodhana and Bhima, armed with maces and armored, are compared to gods, demons, and natural forces. Duryodhana challenges Bhima, and the kings assemble to watch. Balarama sits among them, radiant like the moon.

beats.title: The Mace Duel: Bhima vs Duryodhana
beats.summary: Duryodhana challenges Bhima to a mace duel, recalling past grievances. Bhima vows to kill Duryodhana, listing his crimes. Ominous omens appear. Duryodhana responds defiantly, and the duel begins.

beats.title: The Mace Duel between Bhima and Duryodhana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the intense mace duel between Bhima and Duryodhana during the Kurukshetra war. Both warriors display extraordinary skill and ferocity, exchanging powerful blows. Duryodhana strikes Bhima on the head, but Bhima remains steady. Bhima then knocks Duryodhana down, but Duryodhana recovers and strikes Bhima again, causing him to fall. The gods and celestial beings witness the battle, and a shower of flowers falls when Duryodhana knocks Bhima down. Bhima eventually regains consciousness and stands firm.

beats.title: The Mace Duel and Fall of Duryodhana
beats.summary: Krishna advises Arjuna that Bhima must use deceit to defeat Duryodhana, as Duryodhana is stronger in fair combat. Bhima then breaks Duryodhana's thighs with a mace blow, fulfilling his oath. The fall of Duryodhana causes cosmic disturbances.

beats.title: Bhima Kills Duryodhana
beats.summary: After Bhima kills Duryodhana in a mace duel, he kicks Duryodhana's head with his left foot and taunts him for the Draupadi humiliation. Yudhishthira rebukes Bhima for this unrighteous act, then laments over Duryodhana's death, blaming fate and Duryodhana's own faults.

beats.title: Balarama's Anger and Departure
beats.summary: After Bhima kills Duryodhana by striking below the waist, Balarama (Rama) becomes furious and attacks Bhima. Krishna intervenes, pacifying Balarama by citing the prior oath, the sage Maitreya's curse, and the advent of Kali Yuga. Balarama departs for Dvaraka. Yudhishthira is distressed by Bhima's act, but Krishna and Bhima console him, and Yudhishthira ultimately accepts the victory.

beats.title: The Death of Duryodhana and Krishna's Justification
beats.summary: After Duryodhana is slain by Bhima in the mace duel, the Pandavas and their allies celebrate. Krishna rebukes Duryodhana's corpse, listing his sins. Duryodhana, though dying, defends his life and is honored by celestial beings. Krishna then explains to the Pandavas that their enemies could not be defeated by fair means, justifying the use of stratagems.

beats.title: The Pandavas Enter the Kaurava Camp and the Chariot Burns
beats.summary: After the war, the Pandavas enter Duryodhana's camp, which is now desolate. Krishna advises Arjuna to dismount, and then the divine chariot of Arjuna, previously protected by Krishna, burns down due to the Brahmastra weapons used by Drona and Karna. Krishna explains that he had been protecting the chariot, but now that Arjuna's mission is complete, he has withdrawn his protection. Krishna and Yudhishthira exchange words about victory and Krishna's role. The Pandavas then take possession of the Kaurava treasury and spend the night by the river Oghavati. Krishna sends Daruka to console Gandhari and departs for Hastinapura.

beats.title: Krishna's Mission to Pacify Gandhari
beats.summary: After the Kurukshetra war, Yudhishthira fears Gandhari's wrath due to Duryodhana's unfair death. He sends Krishna to pacify her. Krishna visits Hastinapura, consoles Dhritarashtra and Gandhari, and then leaves to protect the Pandavas from Ashvatthama's plot.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Lament and Death
beats.summary: Duryodhana, after being struck down by Bhima in a mace fight, laments his defeat and criticizes the Pandavas for violating dharma. He instructs messengers to convey his words to his surviving allies and family, then dies. The earth trembles and the onlookers flee.

beats.title: Duryodhana's Fall and Aśvatthāmā's Vow
beats.summary: After hearing of Duryodhana's death, Aśvatthāmā, Kr̥pa, and Kr̥tavarmā rush to the battlefield and find Duryodhana fallen. Aśvatthāmā laments, but Duryodhana consoles them, accepting his fate and praising their efforts. He then appoints Aśvatthāmā as commander-in-chief, and Kr̥pa performs the consecration. Aśvatthāmā vows to destroy the Pāñcālas.

beats.title: The Night Raid: Ashvatthama's Vow
beats.summary: After the death of Duryodhana, the three surviving Kaurava warriors—Kritavarma, Kripa, and Ashvatthama—retreat to a forest. Ashvatthama, unable to sleep due to grief and rage, observes an owl attacking and killing crows at night. Inspired by this, he resolves to launch a night raid on the Pandava camp, justifying it through a distorted interpretation of dharma. He wakes his companions and they proceed with the plan.

beats.title: Kripa's Counsel on Fate and Effort
beats.summary: Kripa advises Ashvatthama on the interplay of divine will (daiva) and human effort (puruṣakāra), emphasizing that success arises from their combination. He criticizes Duryodhana's reckless actions and suggests seeking counsel from elders like Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Vidura.

beats.title: Aśvatthāmā's Vow of Revenge
beats.summary: Aśvatthāmā, grieving and angry over his father's death, rejects Kṛpa's advice and resolves to attack the Pāñcālas and Pāṇḍavas at night while they sleep. He justifies his plan by reflecting on the variability of human intellect and the duties of a kṣatriya, and vows to kill them all to avenge his father and allies.

beats.title: Ashvatthama's Vow of Revenge
beats.summary: Kripa and Kritavarma try to console Ashvatthama after the death of his father Drona, urging him to rest and fight the next day. Ashvatthama refuses, expressing his grief and rage, and declares his intent to attack the Pandava camp that very night.

beats.title: The Night Raid: Ashvatthama's Vow
beats.summary: Kripa tries to dissuade Ashvatthama from attacking the sleeping Pandava camp, arguing that killing sleeping, unarmed, or surrendered warriors is adharmic. Ashvatthama rejects this, citing the adharmic killings of his father Drona, Karna, Bhishma, and Duryodhana by the Pandavas. He resolves to kill Dhrishtadyumna and the Panchalas. Kripa and Kritavarma join him, and they proceed to the camp.

beats.title: Aśvatthāmā Encounters the Divine Guardian
beats.summary: Aśvatthāmā, accompanied by Kr̥pa and Kr̥tavarmā, approaches the Pāṇḍava camp at night. At the entrance, he encounters a terrifying divine being (a vision of Śiva) that blocks his path. Despite using various weapons, Aśvatthāmā fails to overcome it. Realizing his actions are adharmic, he repents and finally surrenders to Mahādeva (Śiva) for protection.

beats.title: Aśvatthāmā's Self-Sacrifice to Rudra
beats.summary: Aśvatthāmā (Drauṇi) performs a self-sacrifice to propitiate Rudra (Śiva) for victory. He invokes Śiva with many names, offers himself as oblation into a fire, and Śiva appears, enters his body, and grants him a divine sword. The chapter describes the terrifying attendants of Śiva who witness the rite.

beats.title: The Night Massacre of the Panchalas
beats.summary: Duryodhana's army is described, including warriors like Bhishma, Karna, and others. Bhishma blows his conch, and Krishna and Arjuna blow theirs. The chapter then shifts to the night massacre by Ashvatthama, who kills Dhrishtadyumna and other Panchala warriors in their sleep, aided by Kripa and Kritavarma. The camp is set ablaze, and a horrific slaughter ensues, with rakshasas feasting on the dead. Ashvatthama reports to Duryodhana, who questions why he didn't act earlier. Sanjaya explains it was due to the absence of the Pandavas and Krishna.

beats.title: The Death of Duryodhana
beats.summary: After the night massacre, the three survivors—Aśvatthāmā, Kr̥pa, and Kr̥tavarmā—find Duryodhana dying on the battlefield. They lament his fate, blaming Bhīma's unfair tactics and Kr̥ṣṇa's inaction. Aśvatthāmā reveals he killed Dhr̥ṣṭadyumna and the Pāñcālas in revenge. Duryodhana, pleased, dies peacefully. Saṁjaya reports to Dhṛtarāṣṭra, who grieves.

beats.title: The Aftermath of the Sauptika Massacre
beats.summary: After the night of the Sauptika massacre, Dhrishtadyumna's charioteer informs Yudhishthira that the Pandava camp was attacked by Kritavarma, Kripa, and Ashvatthama, killing all the sons of Draupadi and the Panchala princes. Yudhishthira laments the loss, reflecting on the irony of victory turning into defeat due to carelessness. He sends Nakula to bring Draupadi and then proceeds to the battlefield to see the slain.

beats.title: Draupadi's Lament and Bhima's Vow
beats.summary: After the war, Dhritarashtra's sons and allies are dead. Yudhishthira is grief-stricken. Draupadi, having heard of her sons' murder by Ashvatthama, confronts Yudhishthira and then urges Bhima to avenge them. Bhima sets out to kill Ashvatthama.

beats.title: Krishna's Discourse on Ashvatthama's Arrogance
beats.summary: Krishna recounts to Yudhishthira how Ashvatthama, driven by grief over his father Drona's death, sought to acquire Krishna's divine discus. Despite his efforts, Ashvatthama failed to even lift it. Krishna rebukes him for his arrogance and warns Yudhishthira to protect Bhima from Ashvatthama, who possesses the Brahmashira weapon.

beats.title: Bhima's Pursuit of Ashvatthama and the Unleashing of the Brahmashira Astra
beats.summary: Bhima, enraged by the death of his sons, pursues Ashvatthama to the banks of the Ganga, where he finds him seated near Vyasa. Bhima challenges him, and Ashvatthama, in desperation, unleashes the divine Brahmashira astra, causing a catastrophic fire.

beats.title: The Confrontation of Divine Astras
beats.summary: Krishna perceives Drona's son's intention and urges Arjuna to use the divine astra taught by Drona to neutralize the enemy's astra. Arjuna recites a mantra for protection, then releases the astra. Both astras blaze fiercely, causing cosmic disturbance. Sages Narada and Vyasa appear between them to pacify the conflict, reminding that no human has ever used such astras before.

beats.title: The Withdrawal and Release of the Divine Astras
beats.summary: After the war, Arjuna withdraws his divine astra (Brahmashira) at the request of the sages, but Aśvatthāmā, unable to retract his own astra due to anger and lack of self-control, releases it into the wombs of the Pāṇḍava women, causing a curse. Vyāsa intervenes, advising Aśvatthāmā to give up his gem and cease hostilities.

beats.title: The Curse of Aśvatthāmā and the Birth of Parikṣit
beats.summary: After the war, Aśvatthāmā attacks the Pāṇḍava camp and kills the sons of Draupadī. He then uses a celestial weapon to destroy the unborn child in Uttarā's womb. Kṛṣṇa intervenes, revives the stillborn child (Parikṣit), and curses Aśvatthāmā to wander the earth for 3000 years. The Pāṇḍavas retrieve the jewel from Aśvatthāmā and present it to Draupadī, who forgives him. Yudhiṣṭhira wears the jewel.

beats.title: Krishna Explains Ashvatthama's Boon and the Origin of Rudra
beats.summary: After the night massacre, King Yudhishthira laments to Krishna how Ashvatthama killed all his sons and warriors. Krishna explains that Ashvatthama sought refuge in Mahadeva (Shiva), who grants boons. He then narrates the story of Rudra's creation, his penance, and his anger upon seeing the world created by Brahma, leading to him breaking his own linga and retreating to Mount Munjavata.

beats.title: The Disruption of the Sacrifice by Rudra
beats.summary: Vasudeva narrates to Yudhishthira the story of Rudra's exclusion from a sacrifice by the gods, his anger, disruption of the yajna, and eventual appeasement, drawing a parallel to the death of the Pandava sons by Ashvatthama, which is attributed to Rudra's grace.

beats.title: The Lament of Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: After the Kurukshetra war, Dhritarashtra laments the death of his sons and allies. Sanjaya consoles him, reminding him of his past mistakes and the inevitability of the war's outcome. Vidura also offers wisdom.

beats.title: Vidura's Consolation
beats.summary: Vidura consoles the grieving Dhritarashtra after the Kurukshetra war, teaching the inevitability of death and the futility of grief, urging him to accept the fate of warriors who died nobly.

beats.title: Vidura's Discourse on Impermanence and Karma
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura how the wise are freed from mental sorrows. Vidura explains the impermanence of the body and world, the role of karma in determining happiness and suffering, and the importance of detachment. He uses analogies like the banana plant, changing clothes, and clay pots to illustrate the transient nature of life. The wise seek the ultimate goal beyond the cycle of birth and death.

beats.title: Vidura's Discourse on the Suffering of Samsara
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura about the nature of the world and the cycle of birth and death. Vidura describes the suffering of beings from conception through life, driven by desire and ignorance, and emphasizes that only by following dharma can one attain liberation.

beats.title: The Parable of the Brahmin in the Forest
beats.summary: Vidura narrates a parable to Dhritarashtra about a brahmin trapped in a dangerous forest, symbolizing the cycle of samsara. The brahmin falls into a well, clings to a vine, and faces multiple threats: a snake, a mad elephant, bees, and mice. Despite his peril, he continues to drink honey, unable to let go of his attachment to life.

beats.title: The Allegory of the Forest of Samsara
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Vidura about the nature of a person living in a difficult situation and how to be liberated from great fear. Vidura responds with an allegory of the forest of samsara (worldly existence), explaining that the forest represents samsara, the snakes are diseases, the giant woman is old age, the well is the body, the great serpent is time/death, the creeper is the hope of life, the elephant with six faces is the year (with months and seasons), the mice are days and nights, the bees are desires, and the flowing honey is the pleasures of the senses. Those who understand the revolving wheel of samsara cut its bonds.

beats.title: Vidura's Discourse on the Path of Samsara
beats.summary: Vidura explains to Dhritarashtra the nature of samsara as a difficult path, using metaphors of a journey, a chariot, and wild animals. He teaches that self-control, discipline, and detachment lead to liberation, while ignorance binds one to suffering.

beats.title: Vyasa's Consolation to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Vyasa consoles Dhritarashtra, who is grief-stricken over the death of his sons in the Kurukshetra war. Vyasa reveals that the war was divinely ordained to relieve the earth's burden, and that Duryodhana was an incarnation of Kali. He urges Dhritarashtra to overcome his sorrow through wisdom and compassion.

beats.title: The Lament of the Kuru Women
beats.summary: After Vyasa departs, King Dhritarashtra, overwhelmed by grief, orders Vidura to bring Gandhari and the Kuru women, including Kunti. The women, in deep sorrow, follow the king as he leaves the city for the battlefield. The entire city mourns, and the women, disheveled and weeping, lament their fallen kin. The scene is compared to the end of an epoch.

beats.title: The Survivors' Report
beats.summary: After the war, the three survivors—Kripa, Kritavarma, and Ashvatthama—approach Dhritarashtra and Gandhari to report the death of Duryodhana and the massacre of the Pandava camp. They describe how they killed the sleeping Panchalas and Draupadi's sons in revenge for Duryodhana's unfair killing. They then seek permission to leave, and depart in different directions.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Grief and Krishna's Intervention
beats.summary: After the war, Yudhishthira approaches the grieving Dhritarashtra, who in his anger tries to kill Bhima but is tricked by Krishna into destroying an iron statue instead. Krishna then pacifies Dhritarashtra and advises him to accept the inevitable.

beats.title: Krishna's Admonition and Dhritarashtra's Repentance
beats.summary: Krishna admonishes Dhritarashtra for his foolishness in siding with Duryodhana despite knowing the righteousness of the Pandavas. Dhritarashtra acknowledges his fault, blaming his attachment to his sons, and expresses a desire to embrace Bhima. He then touches the Pandava brothers, weeping and comforting them.

beats.title: Gandhari's Anger and Vyasa's Intervention
beats.summary: Gandhari, grieving the death of her sons, is about to curse the Pandavas. Sage Vyasa intervenes, reminding her of her earlier words 'where dharma is, there is victory' and advises her to control her anger. Gandhari acknowledges that the war was caused by Duryodhana, Shakuni, Karna, and Duhshasana, but is angered by Bhima's unfair blow below the waist in the mace fight.

beats.title: Bhīmasena's Justification and Gāndhārī's Lament
beats.summary: Bhīmasena defends his actions to Gāndhārī, explaining that he killed Duryodhana and drank Duḥśāsana's blood due to past injustices, including the humiliation of Draupadī. Gāndhārī rebukes him for cruelty and lack of mercy, questioning why he spared none of her sons.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament and Consolation
beats.summary: Gandhari, grieving the death of her sons and grandsons, confronts Yudhishthira. He admits his guilt and asks for her curse. She sees his toenails and they become deformed. Then she calms down and consoles the Pandavas. They approach Kunti, who weeps seeing her sons wounded. Draupadi laments her slain sons. Gandhari consoles her, reflecting on the inevitability of fate and recalling Vidura's advice.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament on the Battlefield
beats.summary: Gandhari, using her divine sight granted by Vyasa, witnesses the horrific aftermath of the Kurukshetra war. She laments the destruction, describing the battlefield covered with corpses, being devoured by scavengers. She addresses Krishna, expressing her grief over the deaths of heroes like Bhishma, Karna, Abhimanyu, Drona, and others, and the sorrow of the widowed women.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament
beats.summary: Gandhari laments over the fallen Duryodhana, expressing her grief and reflecting on the reversal of fortune. She recalls her earlier advice that victory lies with dharma, and mourns the death of her son and the destruction of the Kaurava army.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of her sons in the Kurukshetra war, describing the grief of the widows and the gruesome scene. She recalls her advice to Duryodhana to avoid conflict and blames his actions for the tragedy. She points out Dushasana's corpse, killed by Bhima who drank his blood.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament for Her Fallen Sons
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the deaths of her sons on the battlefield, describing each fallen Kaurava warrior—Vikarna, Durmukha, Citrasena, Vivimsati, and Duhsaha—and their gruesome fates, while questioning how such valiant heroes could be slain.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament for Abhimanyu
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of Abhimanyu, describing the grief of his wife Uttara and the tragic scene of the battlefield. She criticizes the warriors who killed the young hero and expresses her own sorrow.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament for Karna
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of Karna, describing his fall in battle against Arjuna, his wife's grief, and the fulfillment of a curse.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament for the Fallen
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of heroes on the battlefield, describing the gruesome fate of warriors like Avantya, Bahlika, Jayadratha, and others, and the grief of their widows, including her daughter Duhshala.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament for the Fallen Warriors
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of Shalya, Bhagadatta, Bhishma, and Drona, describing their heroic deeds and the sorrow of their families.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament for the Fallen
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of her sons and relatives, particularly focusing on the deaths of Bhurishravas, Somadatta, Shala, and Shakuni. She criticizes Arjuna and Satyaki for their actions in battle, and reflects on the destruction of the Kuru dynasty.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament and Curse
beats.summary: Gandhari laments the death of her sons and other warriors on the battlefield, then curses Krishna for allowing the destruction, leading to Krishna's acceptance of the curse and the Pandavas' fear.

beats.title: The Aftermath: Lament and Funerals
beats.summary: After the war, Vasudeva rebukes Gandhari for her role in the destruction, then Dhritarashtra asks Yudhishthira about the fate of the fallen warriors. Yudhishthira describes their destinations based on their conduct in battle. Dhritarashtra then inquires about the funeral rites for the dead. Yudhishthira arranges for the cremation of all warriors, including both Kauravas and Pandavas, and then proceeds to the Ganga with Dhritarashtra.

beats.title: Kunti's Revelation and Karna's Funeral Rites
beats.summary: The women of the Kuru clan, led by Kunti, perform water rites for the fallen warriors on the banks of the Ganga. Kunti reveals that Karna was her firstborn son, causing grief to the Pandavas. Yudhishthira laments the concealment of this truth and performs Karna's funeral rites.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Lament for Karṇa
beats.summary: After the war, Yudhiṣṭhira laments the death of his elder brother Karṇa, whom he unknowingly killed. He recounts how Karṇa was actually the son of Kuntī, born before her marriage, and how he was bound by his promise to Duryodhana. Yudhiṣṭhira expresses deep remorse and seeks to understand the circumstances of Karṇa's death.

beats.title: The Story of Karna's Past
beats.summary: Vaishampayana narrates to Janamejaya the story of Karna's past, including his birth as a Kshatriya, his training under Drona and Parashurama, and the curse he received from a Brahmana for accidentally killing a cow.

beats.title: Karna's Deception and Curse
beats.summary: Narada narrates how Karna, under the tutelage of Parasurama (Jamadagnya), endures a painful insect bite without disturbing his sleeping guru. Parasurama awakens, kills the insect, and a demon named Grtsa emerges, explaining he was cursed to become that insect. Parasurama then questions Karna's true identity; Karna falsely claims to be a Brahmana. Parasurama curses him that the Brahmastra will fail him when he needs it most, but also predicts he will be unmatched in battle. Karna then goes to Duryodhana.

beats.title: Karna's Exploit at the Swayamvara
beats.summary: Narada narrates how Karna, after obtaining a divine weapon from Bhargava (Parashurama), accompanied Duryodhana to a swayamvara in the kingdom of Kalinga. Many kings assembled, including Shishupala, Jarasandha, Bhishmaka, and others. The princess bypassed Duryodhana, who forcibly abducted her with Karna's help. Karna defeated the pursuing kings by cutting their bows and causing chaos, allowing Duryodhana to take the princess to Hastinapura.

beats.title: The Fall of Karna
beats.summary: Narada recounts the story of Karna's battle with Jarasandha, his victory, and the subsequent events leading to his death in the Kurukshetra war, highlighting the various curses and boons that contributed to his downfall.

beats.title: Kunti's Revelation and Yudhishthira's Grief
beats.summary: After Narada's departure, Yudhishthira is overwhelmed with grief over the war's destruction. His mother Kunti consoles him, revealing that she had previously tried to reconcile with Karna but failed due to his deep-seated enmity. She also discloses that Karna was her firstborn, making him Yudhishthira's elder brother. This revelation deepens Yudhishthira's sorrow, leading him to curse women for their inability to keep secrets.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Lament and Desire for Renunciation
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira, overwhelmed with grief after the war, laments the death of his relatives and friends, especially Karṇa. He criticizes the Kṣatriya code of conduct and expresses a desire to renounce the kingdom and retire to the forest. Arjuna responds to him.

beats.title: Arjuna's Rebuke to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Arjuna rebukes Yudhishthira for considering renunciation after winning the war, arguing that wealth is the foundation of dharma, kama, and svarga, and that poverty is worse than death. He cites examples of kings and gods who acquired wealth through conflict, urging Yudhishthira to perform the Ashvamedha sacrifice.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Renunciation
beats.summary: Yudhishthira describes to his teacher the ascetic life he intends to lead, renouncing worldly pleasures and attachments, practicing severe austerities, and seeking liberation from the cycle of samsara. He emphasizes detachment, equanimity, and the pursuit of the eternal, immutable state.

beats.title: Bhima's Rebuke of Yudhishthira's Pacifism
beats.summary: Bhima rebukes Yudhishthira for his pacifist stance after the war, arguing that renunciation is not appropriate for a king and that action (karma) is necessary for success. He uses analogies to criticize inaction and defends the kshatriya duty to fight enemies.

beats.title: The Dialogue of Indra and the Ascetics
beats.summary: Arjuna narrates an ancient story of a dialogue between Indra (as a golden bird) and ascetics who abandoned household life. The bird criticizes their renunciation and extols the householder's path of performing Vedic rituals, sharing food with guests, ancestors, and gods, and eating only leftovers (vighasāśin). The ascetics accept the teaching and adopt the householder dharma. Arjuna is then advised to rule the earth with firmness.

beats.title: Nakula's Discourse on Renunciation and Duty
beats.summary: Nakula advises King Yudhishthira on the importance of performing Vedic sacrifices and householder duties, arguing that true renunciation (tyāga) is not abandoning the world but fulfilling one's dharma with detachment. He warns against hypocrisy and emphasizes that the householder path, when followed properly, leads to the highest goal.

beats.title: Sahadeva's Discourse on Renunciation and Liberation
beats.summary: Sahadeva speaks to Yudhishthira about the nature of liberation, attachment, and the path of the wise. He argues that true siddhi (perfection) does not come from abandoning external objects while still craving bodily pleasures, nor from renouncing the body while ruling the earth. He contrasts mortality (mr̥tyu) with the eternal Brahman, stating that both reside within the self and cause conflict among beings. He advises following the path trodden by the virtuous of old, and warns that even a king who conquers the whole world but does not enjoy it properly lives in vain. Attachment to objects leads to death's mouth. Seeing the true nature of both external and internal beings frees one from great fear. Finally, he asks forgiveness for his words spoken in distress.

beats.title: Draupadi's Rebuke to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Draupadi rebukes Yudhishthira for his despondency after the war, reminding him of his duty as a king to rule and enjoy the fruits of victory, and urging him to cheer his brothers and rule the earth.

beats.title: The Necessity of Daṇḍa
beats.summary: Arjuna explains to Yudhishthira the necessity of daṇḍa (punishment/rule) for maintaining order in society. He argues that daṇḍa protects dharma, artha, and kāma; it is feared by all, including ascetics and animals. Without daṇḍa, the strong would devour the weak, and chaos would prevail. He cites examples from nature and scripture, concluding that a king must wield daṇḍa righteously, and that killing an aggressor is not sinful.

beats.title: Bhima's Counsel to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Bhima rebukes Yudhishthira for his grief and hesitation after the war, urging him to overcome mental afflictions by understanding the dual nature of physical and mental diseases, and to rule the kingdom rightfully. He reminds Yudhishthira of past sufferings and the need to conquer the inner battle.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Discourse on Renunciation
beats.summary: Yudhishthira advises a king to overcome desire, anger, and attachment, emphasizing renunciation and inner peace over worldly conquest. He illustrates that true victory lies in controlling the stomach (desire) and cites the example of King Janaka who, despite losing everything, remained content. The chapter contrasts the paths of pitryana (ancestral) and devayana (divine), advocating for wisdom and detachment.

beats.title: The Dialogue of Janaka and His Wife
beats.summary: Arjuna, distressed by Yudhishthira's grief, recounts a dialogue between King Janaka and his wife. The queen criticizes Janaka for abandoning his kingdom and taking up a beggar's life, arguing that true renunciation is not in external acts but in inner detachment while fulfilling one's duties. She emphasizes the importance of householder life as the foundation for society and spiritual seekers, and condemns hypocritical ascetics who beg without true renunciation.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Discourse on Renunciation
beats.summary: Yudhishthira criticizes Arjuna for his narrow focus on martial prowess and lack of understanding of deeper dharma. He explains that true wisdom comes from renunciation (tyāga), not wealth or action, and that the path of knowledge and detachment leads to liberation. He warns against being misled by ritualistic interpretations and emphasizes the superiority of renunciation over action.

beats.title: The Importance of Wealth for Sacrifice
beats.summary: Vaiśaṁpāyana advises Yudhiṣṭhira on the importance of wealth for performing sacrifices, citing examples of Indra, Mahādeva, Marutta, and Hariścandra who attained greatness through yajñas. He emphasizes that wealth should be used for sacrificial purposes, not hoarded or misused.

beats.title: The Supreme Dharma of Non-Violence and Contentment
beats.summary: Devasthana narrates the ancient dialogue between Indra and Brihaspati on the supreme dharma of non-violence, contentment, and self-control. The chapter contrasts various paths (sacrifice, renunciation, charity, etc.) and concludes that the true dharma for a king is to protect his subjects without enmity, following the example of ancient kings and gods who attained heaven through righteous rule.

beats.title: Arjuna's Consolation to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Arjuna consoles Yudhishthira, who is grieving after the war, by reminding him of the kshatriya dharma: that death in battle is superior to many sacrifices, and that he should perform great sacrifices like Indra. Arjuna cites Indra's example of killing his own kin and attaining Indrahood.

beats.title: Vyasa's Counsel to Yudhishthira on Kingly Duties
beats.summary: Vyasa instructs Yudhishthira on the duties of a king, emphasizing the householder stage as the highest and the importance of wielding the rod of punishment (danda) for a kshatriya.

beats.title: The Story of Shankha and Likhita
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Vyasa how King Sudyumna attained supreme success. Vyasa narrates the story of two brothers, Shankha and Likhita, who were ascetics. Likhita ate fruits from Shankha's hermitage without permission. Shankha accused him of theft and sent him to King Sudyumna for punishment. Sudyumna, after hearing Likhita's confession, cut off his hands. Likhita returned to Shankha, who forgave him and told him to bathe in the Bāhudā river, where his hands were restored. Vyasa concludes that Sudyumna attained success through this act of justice, and that a king's duty is to protect his subjects, not to shave his head (renounce).

beats.title: The Duties of a King and the Story of Hayagrīva
beats.summary: Vyasa advises Yudhishthira on the duties of a king, emphasizing righteous rule, protection of subjects, and the importance of action. He narrates the story of King Hayagriva, who attained heaven by fighting enemies and ruling justly, illustrating that a king who performs his duties with courage and wisdom achieves glory.

beats.title: The Consolation of Time
beats.summary: Vyasa consoles Yudhishthira, who is grieving after the war, by teaching about the power of time (kāla) and the inevitability of change. He cites the ancient king Senajit to illustrate that happiness and sorrow alternate, and advises detachment and equanimity. The chapter concludes with the duties of a king and the rewards of righteous rule.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament and Vyasa's Consolation
beats.summary: Yudhishthira laments the deaths of his relatives and allies in the Kurukshetra war, including Abhimanyu, the sons of Draupadi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, Drupada, Vasushena (Karna), Dhrishtaketu, Bhishma, Drona, and Karna. He confesses his guilt for deceitful acts, such as lying about Ashvatthama's death and causing Bhishma's fall. Overcome with grief, he resolves to fast unto death. Vyasa then consoles him, teaching that all unions end in separation, all accumulations in destruction, and life ends in death; he advises Yudhishthira to fulfill his ordained duties.

beats.title: The Dialogue of Aśma and Janaka
beats.summary: Vyasa consoles Yudhishthira, who is grieving over the death of his kinsmen, by narrating the ancient dialogue between King Janaka and the sage Aśma. Aśma teaches that all beings are subject to time, fate, and the cycle of pleasure and pain; one should not grieve for what is inevitable. The chapter emphasizes acceptance of destiny, the transient nature of relationships, and the importance of performing one's duties without attachment.

beats.title: Narada's Consolation to Srnjaya
beats.summary: Narada consoles King Srnjaya, who is grieving his son's death, by recounting the stories of many great kings who also died, emphasizing the inevitability of death and the futility of excessive grief. He then revives Srnjaya's son.

beats.title: The Tale of Narada and Parvata
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Vasudeva about the story of Srnjaya's son who was said to be a spitter of gold and died young. Vasudeva narrates the tale of the sages Narada and Parvata, who made a pact to share all thoughts. Parvata cursed Narada to become a monkey-faced husband of Sukumari, and Narada cursed Parvata to never attain heaven. Later, they reconciled and Parvata regained heaven, while Narada remained on earth as a sage.

beats.title: The Story of Suvarnaṣṭhīvin
beats.summary: Narada narrates to Yudhishthira the story of King Srñjaya and his son Suvarnaṣṭhīvin, who was granted a boon by the sages Narada and Parvata but was killed by Indra's tiger due to fear of his power. Narada later revived the prince, and he ruled for a thousand years. The chapter concludes with Narada advising Yudhishthira to overcome grief and follow the path of dharma.

beats.title: Vyasa's Consolation to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Vyasa consoles Yudhishthira after the war, explaining that a king's duty is to protect dharma and punish wrongdoers. He discusses the nature of action and its fruits, advising Yudhishthira to perform expiation and not to grieve.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament
beats.summary: Yudhishthira laments to Bhishma about the immense loss of life in the Kurukshetra war, including sons, grandsons, brothers, fathers, and many kings. He expresses guilt for causing the deaths out of greed for the kingdom and fears the consequences, including the suffering of women and his own fall into hell. He asks Bhishma for guidance on atonement through severe penance.

beats.title: Vyasa's Consolation
beats.summary: Vyasa consoles Yudhishthira after the war, explaining that all beings are subject to Time (Kala), which is the ultimate agent of death. He advises Yudhishthira to perform the Ashvamedha sacrifice as expiation and to rule righteously.

beats.title: Expiation for Sins
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Bhishma about expiation for sins. Vyasa lists various sinful actions and explains that some actions, when done under specific circumstances (e.g., self-defense, for a guru, in emergencies), do not incur sin. He also outlines expiatory rites.

beats.title: Expiations for Sins
beats.summary: Vyasa explains various penances and expiations for sins such as brahminicide, drinking liquor, violating the guru's bed, stealing, and other offenses. He emphasizes that through specific austerities, donations, and rituals, one can be purified. The chapter also discusses the relative severity of sins committed knowingly versus unknowingly, and the importance of faith and following the conduct of the virtuous.

beats.title: Vyasa's Discourse on Dharma: Edible and Gift
beats.summary: Vyasa instructs Yudhishthira on dharma, focusing on what is edible, what is fit for gift, and who is a worthy recipient. He recounts a dialogue between sages and Manu, who explains the duality of dharma and adharma, the importance of intention, and the rules of purity for householders. The chapter lists forbidden foods and unworthy recipients of gifts, emphasizing that gifts to unworthy brāhmaṇas lead to ruin.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Counsel and Entry into Hastinapura
beats.summary: Yudhishthira asks Vyasa about rajadharma and the conflict between dharma and kingship. Vyasa advises him to seek Bhishma, who is omniscient in dharma. After initial reluctance due to guilt over killing Bhishma, Yudhishthira is consoled by Krishna and Vyasa, overcomes his sorrow, and enters Hastinapura in a grand procession.

beats.title: The Return of Yudhiṣṭhira and the Destruction of Cārvāka
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira returns to Hastinapura after the war and is welcomed by citizens and brahmins. A rākṣasa named Cārvāka, disguised as a mendicant and friend of Duryodhana, curses him for killing his kinsmen. The brahmins reveal his true nature and destroy him with their power. Vāsudeva then narrates the story of Cārvāka's boon from Brahmā and his destined death at the hands of brahmins.

beats.title: The Coronation of Yudhishthira
beats.summary: After the war, Yudhishthira sits on the throne, surrounded by family and allies. A grand coronation ceremony is performed by Dhaumya, with offerings and blessings from brahmins. Yudhishthira accepts the kingdom and honors all present.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Governance Assignments
beats.summary: Yudhishthira responds to the citizens' praise, emphasizing his duty to serve Dhritarashtra as king. He then assigns various administrative roles to his brothers and ministers, ensuring proper governance.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Post-War Duties
beats.summary: After the war, King Yudhishthira performs funeral rites for all the slain warriors, including his relatives and friends. He also honors Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, Vidura, and the widows, and rules the kingdom righteously, free from enmity.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Praise of Krishna
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, after being crowned king, praises Krishna with a long list of names and attributes, acknowledging him as the supreme being, the creator and destroyer of the universe. Krishna, pleased, responds with words of encouragement.

beats.title: Distribution of Residences after the War
beats.summary: After the war, King Yudhishthira allows his subjects to return home. He then addresses his brothers, acknowledging their hardships and urging them to rest.
