beats.title: Preparation for the Ashvamedha Sacrifice
beats.summary: Yudhishthira learns of Arjuna's safe return and success in the horse sacrifice campaign. He then instructs Bhima to prepare the sacrificial ground for the Ashvamedha yajna. Bhima oversees construction of the yajna hall, residences for kings and brahmins, and sends invitations. Many kings arrive with gifts. Sages and brahmins gather, and Yudhishthira humbly receives them.

beats.title: The Grandeur of Yudhishthira's Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter describes the grand scale and opulence of Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice, as seen by the assembled kings. It details the golden structures, abundant food, and diverse animals brought for the ceremony, highlighting the prosperity and unity of the kingdom.

beats.title: Arrival of Kings and Kṛṣṇa's Message
beats.summary: King Yudhiṣṭhira instructs Bhīmasena to honor the assembled kings and sages. Kṛṣṇa arrives with the Vṛṣṇis and relays a message from Arjuna regarding the proper reception of guests and the upcoming arrival of his son Babhruvāhana from Maṇipūra. Yudhiṣṭhira approves.

beats.title: Arjuna's Return and the Defect in His Ankles
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira asks Kṛṣṇa why Arjuna, despite his virtues, always suffers. Kṛṣṇa explains it is due to a slight defect in Arjuna's ankles. Draupadi shows displeasure at this, but Kṛṣṇa accepts her affection. Later, Arjuna returns victorious from his horse-sacrifice campaign, is welcomed by all, and then Babhruvāhana arrives with his mothers.

beats.title: The Arrival of Babhruvahana and the Initiation of the Ashvamedha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the arrival of Babhruvahana, the son of Arjuna and Chitrangada, at the court of the Pandavas after the war. He pays respects to Dhritarashtra, Yudhishthira, Bhima, and others, and is warmly received. Krishna gifts him a divine chariot. On the third day, Vyasa instructs Yudhishthira to perform the Ashvamedha sacrifice to purify himself from the sin of killing his kinsmen. Yudhishthira initiates the sacrifice, which is conducted flawlessly by learned Brahmins. Bhima oversees the distribution of food. The sacrificial ground is elaborately constructed with golden bricks and posts, and the ritual includes the proper offering of animals. The assembly is graced by sages, Gandharvas, and Apsaras.

beats.title: The Completion of the Horse Sacrifice and Distribution of Wealth
beats.summary: After the horse sacrifice, Yudhishthira gives away the entire earth as dakshina to the priests. Vyasa returns it, asking for gold instead. Yudhishthira then distributes immense wealth, including gold, to the Brahmins and others. The chapter describes the grandeur of the sacrifice, the distribution of riches, and the purification of Yudhishthira.

beats.title: The Mongoose and the Horse Sacrifice
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about a wonder at Yudhishthira's horse sacrifice. Vaishampayana narrates that after the sacrifice, a mongoose with golden sides emerged from a hole, criticized the sacrifice as inferior to a gift of barley-meal by a poor brahmin who lived by gleaning, and then vanished. The brahmins questioned the mongoose, who explained that the brahmin's gift was superior because it was given with faith and without expectation. The mongoose then revealed that half his body had turned golden from contact with the barley-meal, and he had come to compare the sacrifice.

beats.title: The Story of Kapoti and the Power of Sincere Giving
beats.summary: Nakula narrates the story of a poor brahmin (Kapoti) who, despite extreme poverty and hunger during a famine, offers his entire family's meager meal of barley flour to a guest. The guest is revealed to be Dharma in disguise, who blesses the family and takes them to heaven. Nakula then explains that the merit of this small, sincere gift surpasses that of grand sacrifices, and that the horse sacrifice of Yudhishthira is not equal to it. He concludes by stating that non-violence, contentment, good conduct, simplicity, austerity, self-control, truth, and charity are all equal in merit.

beats.title: The Dispute over Animal Sacrifice
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks why Nakula criticized Yudhishthira's horse sacrifice. Vaisampayana recounts a past dispute between Indra and sages over animal sacrifice in Indra's yajna. The sages argue that non-violence is the true dharma, advocating sacrifice with seeds and plants. Indra, deluded by pride, rejects their advice. The sages then consult King Vasu, who wrongly advises animal sacrifice and is cursed to fall to the underworld. The chapter concludes with teachings on righteous conduct: giving only what is lawfully obtained, non-violence, truth, and compassion are the roots of eternal dharma.

beats.title: Agastya's Sacrifice and the Power of Mental Resolve
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the fruits of charity given with righteousness, especially the great reward of giving a handful of parched grain. Vaishampayana narrates the ancient story of sage Agastya's twelve-year sacrifice, during which Indra withheld rain. Agastya declares he will perform a 'mental sacrifice' (cintāyajña) and sustain the sacrifice through his own power, even threatening to become Indra himself. The assembled sages advise him to avoid violence and adhere to non-injury (ahiṃsā). Impressed by Agastya's tapobala, Indra eventually rains and the sacrifice concludes successfully.

beats.title: The Story of Krodha and the Nakula
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks about the being who appeared in Nakula's form with a golden head and spoke like a human. Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates the story of Krodha (Anger) personified, who was cursed by the Pitṛs to become a nakula (mongoose) after disrupting Jamadagni's śrāddha. The mongoose was freed from the curse when it insulted Yudhiṣṭhira's dharma during a sacrifice.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Rule and Their Respect for Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana how the Pandavas ruled after the war, treating Dhritarashtra with respect. Vaishampayana describes that the Pandavas, having conquered their enemies, ruled the earth with Dhritarashtra as their head. They consulted him in all matters and acted with his permission for fifteen years. They honored him with luxurious items and services. Vidura, Sanjaya, and Yuyutsu attended the blind king. Kunti served Gandhari, and Draupadi and other wives followed suit. Yudhishthira ensured that Dhritarashtra, bereft of sons, did not suffer. However, Bhima alone could not forget the dice game incident.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Respect for Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: After the war, King Dhritarashtra is honored by the Pandavas and lives in peace. Yudhishthira instructs his brothers and ministers to respect Dhritarashtra and follow his commands. Dhritarashtra performs shraddha ceremonies for his sons, giving gifts to Brahmins. The Pandavas, out of compassion, ensure Dhritarashtra's comfort and happiness.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Reconciliation with the Pandavas
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after the war, is treated with respect by the Pandavas, especially Yudhishthira. He becomes affectionate towards them, forgetting his own sons' misdeeds. However, Bhima remains resentful, though he outwardly follows Yudhishthira's lead.

beats.title: Bhima's Resentment and Dhritarashtra's Despondency
beats.summary: After fifteen years of peace, Bhima still harbors resentment towards Dhritarashtra for the past war. He speaks harshly, reminding Dhritarashtra of his sons' deaths. Dhritarashtra, pained by Bhima's words, becomes despondent and calls a meeting of friends.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Lament and Request for Forest Retirement
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra laments the destruction of the Kuru dynasty due to his own fault in crowning Duryodhana and ignoring Krishna's advice. He describes his penance for fifteen years, eating sparingly, sleeping on the ground, and reciting japa. He tells Yudhishthira that he does not grieve for his slain sons as they died nobly in battle, and he asks permission to retire to the forest with Gandhari to perform severe austerities, noting that it is the custom of their family to renounce in old age.

beats.title: Dhṛtarāṣṭra's Retirement
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses deep distress over Dhṛtarāṣṭra's suffering and offers to renounce the kingdom, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra insists on retiring to the forest. Dhṛtarāṣṭra collapses in grief, and Yudhiṣṭhira revives him with a cool touch.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Request for Asceticism
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, weakened by a fast, asks Yudhishthira to touch him, and upon being touched, he revives. He then requests permission to go into the forest for asceticism. Yudhishthira urges him to eat first, but Dhritarashtra insists on being allowed to fast. Vyasa arrives and speaks.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Retirement
beats.summary: Vyasa advises Yudhishthira to allow Dhritarashtra to retire to the forest, as he is old and grieving. Yudhishthira agrees, and after Vyasa departs, he respectfully informs Dhritarashtra of the decision, requesting him to take food before leaving.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Return and Advice to Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, after being permitted by Yudhishthira, returns to his palace with Gandhari, Vidura, Sanjaya, and Kripa. After performing morning rituals and dining, he advises Yudhishthira on the duties of a king, emphasizing vigilance, respect for elders, control of senses, proper selection of ministers, secrecy in counsel, and care for the city and personal safety.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Counsel on Kingship
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra instructs Yudhishthira on the duties of a king, emphasizing justice, punishment, espionage, and statecraft. He advises on managing ministers, punishing criminals, maintaining the treasury, and dealing with enemies. The chapter is a discourse on righteous governance.

beats.title: The Mandala Theory of Statecraft
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the principles of statecraft, specifically the mandala theory of foreign relations. Sanjaya explains the sixfold policy (ṣāḍguṇya) and the various strategies for dealing with allies, enemies, and neutrals, including war, peace, and retreat.

beats.title: Dhr̥tarāṣṭra's Advice on Statecraft
beats.summary: Dhr̥tarāṣṭra advises Yudhiṣṭhira on statecraft, including strategies for peace and war, types of armies, handling of calamities, and the importance of righteous rule for both worldly and heavenly rewards.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Decision to Retire to the Forest
beats.summary: Yudhishthira agrees to Dhritarashtra's request to retire to the forest. Dhritarashtra, with Gandhari, announces his decision to the assembled court and people, seeking their permission. He expresses his grief over his sons' death and his desire to live in the forest, having seen Yudhishthira's rule established.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Farewell and Entrustment
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra addresses the citizens and courtiers, recounting the righteous rule of his ancestors and his own service, apologizing for Duryodhana's misdeeds that led to the war, and entrusting Yudhishthira as their new king, asking for forgiveness and blessings as he and Gandhari prepare to retire to the forest.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Request for Forest Exile
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, grieving the loss of his sons, requests permission from the citizens to retire to the forest with Gandhari. The citizens, initially speechless with sorrow, eventually respond through a Brahmana named Sambaka, expressing their deep respect for Dhritarashtra and his lineage, and affirming that neither he nor Duryodhana ever wronged them. They praise the rule of the Pandavas and reluctantly accept his decision.

beats.title: Consolation of Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: A brahmana (likely Vyasa) consoles Dhritarashtra after the war, explaining that the destruction was due to fate (daiva) and not the fault of Duryodhana, Dhritarashtra, or others. He praises Yudhishthira's virtues and assures Dhritarashtra that the Pandavas will rule righteously and protect the people. Dhritarashtra accepts the counsel and dismisses the assembly.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Request for Shraddha
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra, having resolved to retire to the forest, sends Vidura to Yudhishthira to seek permission to perform a shraddha for Bhishma, Drona, Somadatta, Bahlika, and other slain relatives. Yudhishthira and Arjuna welcome the request, but Bhima initially refuses due to lingering anger over past wrongs. Arjuna persuades Bhima to forgive, and Yudhishthira endorses the plan.

beats.title: Arjuna's Counsel and Yudhishthira's Generosity
beats.summary: Arjuna advises Yudhishthira to honor Dhritarashtra and offer him wealth and gifts, despite Bhima's resentment. Yudhishthira agrees and instructs Vidura to convey his generosity to the king.

beats.title: Vidura's Report and Dhritarashtra's Resolution
beats.summary: Vidura reports to Dhritarashtra that Yudhishthira and Arjuna have agreed to give him wealth and honor, while Bhima is reluctant due to past grievances but is persuaded. Dhritarashtra decides to perform a great charity on Karttika.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Shraddha Sacrifice
beats.summary: King Dhritarashtra, after hearing Vidura's words, becomes pleased and performs a grand shraddha sacrifice to honor the departed souls of his sons, grandsents, and others who died in the war. He distributes vast amounts of wealth, food, and gifts to Brahmins and all classes, culminating in a ten-day festival of charity.

beats.title: The Departure of Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra and Gandhari, accompanied by the Pandavas and other family members, depart from Hastinapura to begin their vanaprastha (forest retirement). The scene is filled with grief and lamentation as they leave the city.

beats.title: Arjuna's Despondency
beats.summary: King Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas are doing on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes the armies, listing warriors on both sides, and narrates the blowing of conches signaling the start of the war.

beats.title: Kunti's Explanation of Her Past Encouragement
beats.summary: Kunti addresses her sons, explaining that she previously encouraged them during their hardships to ensure the survival of the Pandava lineage and their honor. She recounts the suffering of Draupadi and the humiliation at the dice game, and reveals that her motivation was not for personal gain but for the preservation of the dynasty and the attainment of her own spiritual goals through service and austerity.

beats.title: Kunti's Departure and the Pandavas' Return
beats.summary: After hearing Kunti's words, the Pandavas turn back from their departure to the forest, but Kunti remains resolute in her decision to go. Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Vidura also proceed to the forest. The Pandavas return to Hastinapura in grief. Dhritarashtra establishes a hermitage on the Ganges bank, performs rituals, and the group spends the night there. The next morning, they continue their journey northward, fasting.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Renunciation and Ascetic Life
beats.summary: King Dhritarashtra, accompanied by Gandhari, Kunti, Vidura, and Sanjaya, goes to the banks of the Ganga, performs rituals, then proceeds to Kurukshetra. There he meets King Shatayupa, who has retired to the forest, and together they go to Vyasa's ashram. Dhritarashtra receives initiation and begins a severe ascetic life, controlling his senses, while Gandhari and Kunti follow similar vows.

beats.title: Narada's Consolation to Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Narada visits Dhritarashtra in the forest and recounts stories of kings who attained heaven through tapas, encouraging Dhritarashtra that he too will achieve liberation. He also predicts the fates of Kunti, Vidura, and Sanjaya.

beats.title: The Future Abode of Dhṛtarāṣṭra
beats.summary: The sage Vaiśampāyana narrates that after Nārada spoke, the sages praised him. King Śatayūpa asks Nārada about the future abode of Dhṛtarāṣṭra. Nārada reveals that he saw Pāṇḍu in Indra's court and learned that Dhṛtarāṣṭra has three years left to live, after which he will enjoy Kubera's realm with Gāndhārī, then travel freely through various worlds, purified by his severe penance. The sages and the king are pleased.

beats.title: Grief of the Pandavas after the Elders' Departure
beats.summary: After Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, Kunti, and Vidura depart to the forest, the Pandavas and the citizens of Hastinapura grieve deeply. They lament the old king's exile, the loss of sons and relatives in the war, and the desolate state of the earth. The Pandavas, especially Yudhishthira, are overwhelmed with sorrow and remorse, unable to find joy in kingdom, women, or study. Draupadi and Subhadra also mourn their sons. Only the presence of Parikshit, son of Abhimanyu, gives them a reason to live.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas' Journey to See Their Mother
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, deeply distressed by the exile of their mother Kuntī and the blind king Dhṛtarāṣṭra, decide to visit them in the forest. Yudhiṣṭhira orders a grand procession to Kurukṣetra, and they set out with their retinue.

beats.title: The Journey to the Hermitage
beats.summary: King Yudhishthira, accompanied by his brothers, army, and citizens, sets out to visit Dhritarashtra in his hermitage. The army is described in detail, and they cross the Yamuna to reach the hermitage of the royal sage Shatayupa and Dhritarashtra.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Visit Dhṛtarāṣṭra in the Forest
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas, along with citizens and women, approach Dhṛtarāṣṭra's hermitage. They find it empty and learn he has gone to the Yamunā for flowers and water. They follow and meet him, Gāndhārī, and Kuntī. There is an emotional reunion, with Sahadeva rushing to his mother Kuntī. Yudhiṣṭhira introduces the people, and Dhṛtarāṣṭra feels as if he is back in his palace. The chapter ends with the hermitage being compared to the sky filled with stars.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra and the Forest Visit
beats.summary: Samjaya describes to Dhritarashtra the scene at Kurukshetra, including the armies, warriors, and the blowing of conches. Then, in a later scene, Dhritarashtra visits the Pandavas in the forest, and Samjaya identifies each family member to visiting ascetics.

beats.title: Vidura's Yoga and Merging with Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Dhritarashtra asks Yudhishthira about his well-being and the state of his kingdom. Yudhishthira inquires about his mother Gandhari and Vidura. Vidura appears as an ascetic, and Yudhishthira follows him. Vidura merges his body and consciousness into Yudhishthira through yoga, leaving his own body lifeless. Yudhishthira is about to cremate the body but is stopped by a divine voice, which declares Vidura's attainment of the eternal dharma and the Santanaka worlds. Yudhishthira returns and reports to Dhritarashtra, who offers fruits and roots, which Yudhishthira accepts.

beats.title: The Pāṇḍavas Visit the Hermitage of Śatayūpa
beats.summary: The Pāṇḍavas spend a night in the hermitage of Śatayūpa and other sages in Kurukṣetra. The next morning, Yudhiṣṭhira tours the āśrama, which is filled with Vedic chants, peaceful animals, and sacrificial altars. He distributes gifts to the sages. Then he returns to Dhṛtarāṣṭra and Gāndhārī, and Kuntī. Vyāsa arrives with the sages, and they all sit together.

beats.title: Vyasa's Visit to Dhritarashtra in the Forest
beats.summary: Vyasa visits Dhritarashtra in the forest, inquires about his well-being and spiritual progress, explains that Vidura was an incarnation of Dharma due to a curse, and offers to grant any boon.

beats.title: Dhritarashtra's Grief and the Sages' Visit
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the events after Dhritarashtra's retirement to the forest. Vaishampayana describes how the Pandavas, with their armies and households, spent a month in the forest with Dhritarashtra. Vyasa and other sages (Narada, Parvata, Devala, Vishvavasu, Tumburu, Citrasena) visit. Dhritarashtra expresses his grief over the war and his son Duryodhana's actions, and asks about the fate of those who died in battle.

beats.title: Gandhari's Lament and Vyasa's Intervention
beats.summary: Gandhari, overwhelmed by grief for her slain sons, requests the sage Vyasa to use his yogic powers to show the deceased warriors to the grieving king Dhritarashtra and the other women. Vyasa, perceiving Kunti's hidden sorrow, prompts her to speak her mind.

beats.title: Kunti's Confession to Vyasa
beats.summary: Kunti reveals to Vyasa the secret of Karna's birth: she was granted a boon by the sage Durvasa to summon any god, and in her youth, she invoked the sun god Surya, who fathered Karna. She abandoned the newborn in the river. Vyasa reassures her that she is blameless due to her subsequent virginity and the nature of divine beings.

beats.title: The Revelation of Divine Incarnations
beats.summary: Vyasa reveals to Gandhari and the others that the warriors who died in the Kurukshetra war were divine beings incarnated on earth for a cosmic purpose. He identifies each major figure as an incarnation of a god or celestial being, and then leads the group to the Ganges where they will see the dead warriors again.

beats.title: The Resurrection of the Fallen Warriors
beats.summary: Vyasa performs a ritual to summon the spirits of all warriors who died in the Kurukshetra war. Dhritarashtra and Gandhari, granted divine sight, see their sons and others rise from the Ganges, free from enmity and adorned with celestial forms.

beats.title: The Reunion of Warriors and Their Families
beats.summary: After the war, all warriors and their families reunite in harmony, free from anger and enmity. Vyasa grants boons, and the departed souls attain their desired realms. The chapter concludes with the blessings of hearing this narrative.

beats.title: The Eternal Nature of the Self and Karma
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks how beings who have left their bodies can be seen again. The sage (Vyasa's disciple) explains that karma determines the body and form; the kṣetrajña (knower of the field) is eternal and unchanging. As long as karma is not exhausted, the embodied form persists. Through sacrifice and knowledge, one transcends attachment to the body and attains liberation.

beats.title: Janamejaya's Vision and Astika's Praise
beats.summary: King Janamejaya, having regained sight of his deceased father Parikshit through the grace of Vyasa, performs an ablution ceremony. He then praises Astika, who recounts the greatness of Vyasa and the serpent sacrifice, emphasizing the power of hearing sacred narratives to destroy sin and loosen heart-knots.

beats.title: The Departure of the Pandavas
beats.summary: After seeing their sons and grandsons, Dhritarashtra and Gandhari decide to renounce the world and take up severe penance. Vyasa advises Dhritarashtra to let Yudhishthira return to rule the kingdom. Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Kunti persuade Yudhishthira and his brothers to leave them to their ascetic life. The Pandavas, with Draupadi, depart for Hastinapura.

beats.title: The Death of Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Kunti
beats.summary: Narada visits Yudhishthira and informs him of the death of Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Kunti in a forest fire. Dhritarashtra had been performing severe penance at Gangadvara, subsisting on air, while Gandhari lived on water and Kunti on occasional fasts. A forest fire broke out, and Dhritarashtra, unable to flee due to weakness, chose to remain and be consumed by the fire, along with Gandhari and Kunti. Sanjaya escaped and later went to the Himalayas. The Pandavas grieve upon hearing this news.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Lament for Kunti and Dhritarashtra
beats.summary: Yudhishthira laments the death of his mother Pritha (Kunti) and his father Dhritarashtra in a forest fire, reflecting on the cruel twists of fate and the irony that the mighty king who once had a hundred sons and was fanned by beautiful women now lies burned. He criticizes Arjuna for setting the Khandava forest ablaze, which he sees as ungrateful, and mourns the helplessness of his brothers in saving their mother. The chapter ends with the Pandavas weeping together.

beats.title: The Passing of Dhritarashtra and the Rites Performed by Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Narada tells Yudhishthira that Dhritarashtra was not burned by an ordinary fire but by his own sacrificial fire, which he had kindled before entering the forest. The sages on the Ganga bank confirmed this. Yudhishthira performs the water rites and shraddha ceremonies for Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Kunti, giving generous gifts. After twelve days, he returns to Hastinapura. Narada then departs. The chapter notes that Dhritarashtra spent three years in the forest and fifteen in the city, always giving gifts despite his grief. Yudhishthira rules the kingdom without joy, having lost his relatives.

beats.title: The Mausala Parva Begins
beats.summary: Yudhishthira observes ominous portents and learns of the Vrishnis' destruction in the Mausala incident, including the deaths of Vasudeva and Rama. The Pandavas are grief-stricken.

beats.title: The Curse of the Sages and the Destruction of the Vr̥ṣṇis
beats.summary: King Janamejaya asks Vaiśaṁpāyana how the Vr̥ṣṇis and Andhakas perished in front of Vāsudeva. Vaiśaṁpāyana narrates that in the 36th year, a great calamity befell the Vr̥ṣṇis: they killed each other with clubs, impelled by time. Janamejaya inquires about the curse that caused their destruction. Vaiśaṁpāyana explains that Sāmba and other Vr̥ṣṇi heroes, driven by fate, dressed Sāmba as a woman and asked the sages Viśvāmitra, Kaṇva, and Nārada what the 'woman' would give birth to. The sages, angered by the mockery, cursed that Sāmba would give birth to an iron club that would destroy the Vr̥ṣṇis and Andhakas, except Rāma and Janārdana. They also predicted that Halāyudha would go to the ocean and that Jarā would kill Kr̥ṣṇa. The sages then went to Keśava, who accepted the inevitable. The next day, Sāmba gave birth to a huge iron club. The king had it ground into powder and thrown into the sea, and prohibited liquor in the city under penalty of impalement.

beats.title: Omens of Doom for the Vrishnis
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ominous portents and moral decay among the Vrishnis and Andhakas, foretelling their destruction. Krishna recognizes these as signs of the curse of Gandhari and the passage of time, and orders a pilgrimage to the sea.

beats.title: The Destruction of the Vrishnis
beats.summary: The chapter describes the destruction of the Vrishni and Andhaka clans at Prabhasa. After ominous portents, the Yadavas go on a pilgrimage to Prabhasa, where they indulge in drinking. A quarrel erupts between Satyaki and Kritavarma over past grievances, leading to a massacre. Krishna, knowing it is time for the curse to take effect, picks up a blade of eraka grass that becomes a mace and kills many. The entire clan annihilates itself with the grass-maces. Krishna then stands by as the destruction is completed.

beats.title: The Death of Krishna and Balarama
beats.summary: After the destruction of the Yadava clan due to a brahmin's curse, Krishna sends Daruka to inform Arjuna. He instructs Babhru to protect the women, but Babhru is killed by a hunter's iron bolt. Krishna then goes to the forest, where he sees Rama (Balarama) in yoga, with a great white serpent emerging from his mouth and entering the ocean, worshipped by divine serpents. Krishna, knowing the time has come, enters yoga and is mistaken for a deer by a hunter named Jara, who shoots him in the foot. Krishna ascends to heaven, welcomed by gods and sages.

beats.title: Arjuna's Visit to Dvaraka After the Mausala
beats.summary: After hearing about the destruction of the Vrishnis in the mausala incident, Arjuna goes to Dvaraka to console the widows and see his uncle. He finds the city desolate and the women grieving. He comforts them and then proceeds to meet his maternal uncle.

beats.title: Vasudeva's Lament and Krishna's Prophecy
beats.summary: Vasudeva laments the destruction of the Vrishnis to Arjuna, recounting Krishna's prophecy and instructing Arjuna to perform the funeral rites and abandon the city to the sea.

beats.title: Arjuna's Lament and the Aftermath of the Vrishni Massacre
beats.summary: After the destruction of the Vrishnis, Arjuna arrives in Dwaraka, consoles the grieving, and arranges the funeral of Vasudeva. He then leads the survivors, including women and children, to Indraprastha. On the way, they are attacked by Abhira bandits; Arjuna's divine weapons fail, and many women are abducted. He eventually settles the remaining survivors in various places, installs Vajra as king of Indraprastha, and meets Vyasa.

beats.title: Arjuna's Grief and Vyasa's Consolation
beats.summary: Arjuna, grieving the death of Krishna and the Vrishnis, visits sage Vyasa. Vyasa consoles him, explaining that the destruction was due to a brahmin's curse and that Krishna, as the eternal Vishnu, has returned to his supreme abode after fulfilling his mission. Vyasa advises Arjuna to accept the flow of time and that his own time for departure has come.

beats.title: The Great Departure
beats.summary: After hearing of the Vrishni massacre and Krishna's departure, Yudhishthira decides to renounce the kingdom and embark on a final journey. He installs Parikshit on the throne, appoints Vajra as king of the Yadavas, performs funeral rites for the Vrishnis, and then with his brothers and Draupadi, sets out eastward, eventually reaching the sea. There, Agni appears and demands that Arjuna return the Gandiva bow and inexhaustible quivers to Varuna. Arjuna complies, and the group continues their pilgrimage around the earth.

beats.title: The Fall of the Pandavas on Mount Meru
beats.summary: As the Pandavas ascend Mount Meru after renouncing the world, Draupadi and each brother except Yudhishthira fall dead due to their hidden flaws. Yudhishthira explains each fall: Draupadi's partiality to Arjuna, Sahadeva's pride in wisdom, Nakula's pride in beauty, Arjuna's boastfulness, and Bhima's gluttony and boasting. Only Yudhishthira and the dog continue.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Test of Compassion
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, after ascending to heaven in his mortal body, is distressed that his brothers and Draupadi are not there. He refuses to enter without them and insists on bringing his loyal dog. Indra tries to persuade him to abandon the dog, but Yudhishthira refuses, equating abandonment of a devotee with great sins. Dharma, appearing in his true form, praises Yudhishthira's compassion and reveals the test. Yudhishthira then asks to be taken to where his brothers and Draupadi are, rejecting the heavenly realm without them.

beats.title: The Arrival in Heaven
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the destinations of his ancestors after reaching heaven. Vaishampayana narrates that Yudhishthira, upon entering heaven, sees Duryodhana seated in splendor and becomes angry, refusing to stay with him. Narada advises Yudhishthira to accept Duryodhana's presence, explaining that Duryodhana attained the warrior's heaven by dying in battle. Yudhishthira then asks about the whereabouts of his brothers and other fallen warriors, expressing a desire to see them.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Journey Through Hell
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira, grieving for his fallen brothers and Karṇa, expresses his desire to join them in the afterlife. The gods send a divine messenger to guide him through a horrific hellish realm, where he hears the voices of his loved ones suffering. He initially refuses to proceed, but eventually stays to alleviate their pain, questioning the justice of their fate.

beats.title: Yudhishthira's Ascension to Heaven
beats.summary: After Yudhishthira's brief experience of hell, the gods arrive, led by Indra. Dharma appears in person and explains that the hell was an illusion created by Indra to test Yudhishthira. Yudhishthira is then purified by bathing in the celestial Ganga, sheds his mortal body, and ascends to heaven in his divine form, accompanied by Dharma and the gods.

beats.title: The Celestial Vision of Yudhishthira
beats.summary: Yudhishthira, after death, ascends to heaven and sees his relatives and allies in divine forms. Indra explains that Draupadi is the goddess Shri, her sons are Gandharvas, and others like Karna, Bhima, Nakula, Sahadeva, and Abhimanyu have attained their celestial identities. The chapter reveals the divine nature of the characters and their post-mortem states.

beats.title: The Fates of the Warriors
beats.summary: Janamejaya asks Vaishampayana about the fates of the warriors who died in the Kurukshetra war. Vaishampayana explains that each attained a celestial state according to their deeds, merging with various deities or cosmic elements. The chapter concludes with the completion of the snake sacrifice and praises of the Mahabharata.
